ecom_wishlist.key_characteristics: Small table (~100 rows). wishlist_id appears unique across rows (100 distinct). customer_id has moderate cardinality (65 distinct) indicating repeat customers. sku is low-cardinality (7 distinct) with skew: TOM001 (20%), POT002 (16%), CAR004 (15%), ONI003 (13%), APP009 (12%). added_at is stored as text but appears to be a full timestamp in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format and is unique per row. priority_flag is a binary-like nvarchar with values '0' and '1' (54%/46%). notes is completely NULL (100%). No declared primary or foreign keys.
ecom_wishlist.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can be used to analyze wishlist volume over time, item popularity (top SKUs), customer-level wishlist activity (counts, recency), and priority-tagged items. It can surface behavioral signals for personalization and detect active wishlist usage. Because timestamps are text, time-based queries require casting/normalization. Enrichment with product or customer tables enables revenue or segmentation analyses.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most frequently added to wishlists (top-N products)?
Which customers add the most items to their wishlist and who are repeat wishlist users?
How does wishlist activity trend over time (daily/weekly/monthly)?
What proportion of wishlist items are flagged as priority and which products are prioritized?
Which wishlist items have not been purchased (requires join to orders) and could be targeted for campaigns?
ecom_wishlist.relationships: Logical joins: customer_id -> ecom_customers.customer_id for customer attributes (name, segment, registration date); sku -> products or product_variants (or b2b_products / product_pricing) for product meta (category, price); joined to ecom_orders/order_items to measure conversion (wish -> order). These joins enable funnel conversion, personalized marketing, SKU-level revenue potential, and segmentation by customer cohorts. Because no FK constraints exist, NL2SQL should treat joins as best-effort and validate existence of matching keys in target tables.

inventory_items.purpose: Represents raw inventory item receipts and per-batch stock metadata for seeded inventory. It documents each physical batch/lot of items received (item-level records) including supplier, quantity, costs and date metadata. In the larger schema it is a source of truth for on-hand stock details used by product, order, returns and shipment systems.
inventory_items.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar (text) despite containing numeric and datetime semantics. Row count is small (100). item_id and batch_id are near-unique (item_id distinct=100, batch_id distinct=99). sku and name have low cardinality (10 distinct) while supplier has very low cardinality (3). total_stock_units and cost_per_unit contain numeric values encoded as strings; several date-like columns store ISO-formatted strings (YYYY-MM-DD and YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). No declared primary or foreign keys.
inventory_items.analysis_capabilities: Can compute current on-hand quantities, per-batch cost valuations (inventory value = total_stock_units * cost_per_unit), supplier share of inventory, identify near-expiry / expired batches, receipt timelines by day/hour, and batch-level traceability. Because types are strings, analytical queries must CAST/CONVERT values into numeric/datetime types before aggregations, comparisons or range filters.
business_questions: What is the total on-hand units and inventory value by SKU or product name?
Which batches (batch_id) are expiring within X days or are already expired?
How much stock does each supplier currently provide (units and value)?
What were the daily/weekly receipt volumes (units and value) over a date range?
Which batches correspond to a particular order/return/shipment (when joined to order_items/returns/shipments via SKU or batch_id)?
inventory_items.relationships: No declared foreign keys, but practical joins are expected: join on sku (or product name) to products/product_variants/product_inventory/product_sales tables; join batch_id and item_id to shipment/returns/order_item or product_inventory tables if they track batch-level flows. These joins enable end-to-end traceability (which batch fulfilled which order), inventory valuation across systems, and supplier performance analysis. Recommended join keys: sku (common), batch_id (batch-level tracing), item_id (if external systems reference it).

order_items.purpose: Represents individual order line items for orders ingested into the zuna_seed_raw schema. Each row corresponds to a single SKU/variant on an order with quantity, price, tax, batch and timestamps. It is the granular sales/picking record used for revenue, fulfillment and inventory analyses.
order_items.key_characteristics: Small table (~217 rows) with mostly high-cardinality id-like columns stored as nvarchar. Numeric and datetime information is stored as strings (nvarchar). sku/product_name/variant_id have low cardinality (10 values), while order_item_id, order_id, batch_id, picked_at, created_at are high-cardinality. discount_amount is constant (all zeros) in current snapshot. line_total and tax_amount have moderate cardinality. Many columns are suitable for aggregation after casting.
order_items.analysis_capabilities: Can compute per-line revenue and tax, SKU-level sales and quantities, picking fulfillment metrics by batch and picked_at, time-series of order item creation, order-level rollups (when grouped by order_id), and join enrichment with orders/products for customer-level, product master or inventory analyses.
business_questions: Which SKUs and variants generated the most revenue and quantity sold over a time window?
What is the average unit price, tax and line_total by SKU/variant or batch?
Which batches had the most items picked and what was the picking time distribution (picked_at)?
How many order items and total revenue were created per day (created_at) and how quickly were they picked?
Are there order items with unexpected tax, price, or line_total discrepancies (after casting) indicating data issues?
order_items.relationships: Primary join keys: order_id to orders/ecom_orders/b2b_orders; sku/variant_id to products/product_variants/product_pricing; batch_id to picking_batches or b2b_picking_batches; timestamps can join to time-dimension tables. These joins enable customer-level revenue, product master enrichment, inventory allocation and fulfillment performance analyses.

pos_daily_sales_summary.purpose: Daily aggregated point-of-sale summary records per store/date. It captures precomputed daily metrics (transactions, sales, tax, refunds, avg transaction value) and metadata for each summary row. Intended for reporting, trend analysis, and high-level KPI checks rather than transaction-level queries.
pos_daily_sales_summary.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows sample) with summary granularity. No primary key or foreign keys declared. Many numeric measures are stored as nvarchar (strings) and need casting for arithmetic. summary_id is unique per row (distinct=50) but not declared PK. store_id has low cardinality (4 distinct) and date has moderate cardinality (24 distinct). created_at contains timestamps (string). notes is constant ('Daily summary').
pos_daily_sales_summary.analysis_capabilities: Can produce per-store and per-date reports, time-series trends (sales, refunds, tax), aggregated KPIs (sum, average, growth rates), refund and tax ratios, per-transaction averages and anomaly detection of summaries (by recomputing metrics from base tables if available). Good for quick dashboards and joins to store metadata for enrichment.
business_questions: What were daily total sales, tax and refunds by store for the last N days?
Which store had the highest average transaction value or highest total transactions on a given day?
How have total sales and refunds trended week-over-week or month-over-month per store?
What is the refund rate (refunds / total_sales) or tax rate (total_tax / total_sales) per store or overall?
Are there outlier dates (very high/low sales or avg_txn_value) that need investigation?
pos_daily_sales_summary.relationships: Logical joins: store_id can join to pos_stores (store metadata), pos_sales_daily/pos_sales_monthly for comparison/rollups, pos_transactions/pos_transaction_lines for drilldown to transactions, and ecom/b2b daily/monthly tables for cross-channel comparisons. date can be joined to calendar or snapshot tables for time dimension. summary_id is unique but not used as a foreign key. Because types are string, joins on date may require consistent formatting or casting when other tables use DATE/TIMESTAMP types.

pos_inventory_adjustments.purpose: Records manual inventory adjustments that occurred at POS stores. Each row captures a single adjustment event including which SKU and store were affected, the timestamp of the adjustment, quantity delta, reason, who adjusted and approved it, and inventory levels before and after the adjustment. This table is used for inventory auditing, discrepancy analysis, and operational controls.
pos_inventory_adjustments.key_characteristics: Small table (~50 rows). adjustment_id is distinct for all rows (50) and appears to uniquely identify events though no PK is declared. Many columns are stored as nvarchar instead of native types: timestamps (adjustment_ts, created_at) and numeric fields (qty_change, inventory_before, inventory_after) are textual and must be cast for numeric/time operations. pos_sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) and store_id very low cardinality (4 distinct) — good for grouping/aggregation. reason, adjusted_by, approval_by also low-cardinality categorical fields. inventory_before is nearly unique (48 distinct) and inventory_after unique across all rows (50).
pos_inventory_adjustments.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: (1) compute the frequency and magnitude of adjustments by SKU, store, reason, or user; (2) detect suspicious patterns (repeated adjustments by same user or large negative qty_change); (3) compute day/hour-level adjustment volumes after casting timestamps; (4) validate that inventory_after = inventory_before + qty_change after converting types; (5) audit approval coverage and identify adjustments without approver (if any).
business_questions: Which SKUs and stores have the highest number or magnitude of inventory adjustments over a given period?
What are the primary reasons for inventory adjustments (Shrinkage, Damage, Stock Count Correction) and how much quantity is affected per reason?
Are there users who frequently make adjustments that are subsequently approved or rejected — i.e., what is the distribution of adjustments by adjusted_by and approval_by?
Do inventory_after values match inventory_before + qty_change for all events (data integrity check), and which events violate this?
What are the temporal patterns of adjustments (daily/hourly peaks) and are some stores more active at particular times?
pos_inventory_adjustments.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Practical joins: pos_sku can join to product/product_variants/pos_products/product_inventory tables to enrich adjustments with product names, categories, pricing, and master inventory; store_id can join to pos_stores for location metadata; adjusted_by and approval_by can join to pos_users or HR/employee tables to get user names and roles. Linking to product_sales or pos_transactions enables root-cause analysis (e.g., match adjustments to returns or sales). Important: joins must account for type consistency (pos_sku and store_id are nvarchar here) and potential naming differences across tables.

pos_loyalty_redemptions.purpose: Records of point-based loyalty redemptions at point-of-sale. Each row documents a redemption event tying a customer and a POS transaction to loyalty points consumed and the resulting discount. This table is useful for measuring loyalty program usage, financial impact of redemptions, and per-store/staff redemption behavior.
pos_loyalty_redemptions.key_characteristics: Small sample table (50 rows). redemption_id and pos_txn_id are almost unique (high cardinality). customer_id has moderate cardinality (39 distinct). store_id and created_by are low-cardinality (4 and 3 distinct respectively). loyalty_points_used and discount_amount are stored as strings (nvarchar) though they represent numeric values; discount_amount shows floating representation artifacts in strings. redeemed_at is stored as nvarchar but follows an ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern in the sample and should be treated as a datetime. notes is constant ('Loyalty redemption'). No declared primary/foreign keys in DDL. Because fields are stored as strings, NL2SQL must perform type casting and trimming. For optimization: add proper types (INT/DECIMAL/DATETIME), an index on redeemed_at and customer_id (or a surrogate PK), and consider a unique constraint on redemption_id to support id-based lookups.
pos_loyalty_redemptions.analysis_capabilities: Alone, the table can answer usage and impact metrics for loyalty redemptions: counts over time, total/average discount amounts and points redeemed, redemption frequency by customer/store, temporal patterns (hour/day/month), and staff/approver activity. It cannot enrich demographics or product-level context without joins to customer, transactions, or store tables.
business_questions: How many loyalty redemptions occurred in a given period and what is the total discount amount applied?
Which customers redeemed the most points or received the highest total discount?
Which stores (or staff) have the highest number or value of loyalty redemptions?
What is the time-of-day / day-of-week pattern for loyalty redemptions?
What is the average discount and average points used per redemption?
pos_loyalty_redemptions.relationships: Logical joins: customer_id -> ecom_customers.customer_id or b2b_customers.customer_id to get customer profile and segmentation; pos_txn_id -> pos_transactions.pos_txn_id for full transaction context (items, totals); store_id -> pos_stores.store_id or pos_terminals for store-level attributes; created_by -> pos_users.user_id for staff-level analysis; approval_id may join to an approvals/audit table if present elsewhere. These joins enable analyses like redemption as a share of transaction value, customer lifetime impact, store performance, and staff approval bottlenecks.

pos_product_wise_sales.purpose: Daily product-level point-of-sale (POS) sales ledger: each row records sales metrics for a product on a specific date as ingested into the raw schema. It is a raw ingestion table used as the source for reporting, transformation and enrichment with product master and transactional tables.
pos_product_wise_sales.key_characteristics: Small table (~22k rows) with nullable columns and no declared keys. 'date' is stored as nvarchar (ISO-like strings) with high cardinality (~731 distinct). 'product_id' has low cardinality (10 distinct values). All monetary and quantity columns are nvarchar and appear to be numeric strings (each has high cardinality, often unique per row). No primary/foreign keys declared; possible natural composite key (date + product_id) but duplicates should be checked. Data quality risks: nulls, non-numeric characters, inconsistent formats, and duplicate rows.
pos_product_wise_sales.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports time-series sales analysis per product, simple aggregations (sum/avg) of quantities and amounts, margin/discount/tax rate calculations (after numeric conversion), top-N product ranking, trend detection, and daily/weekly/monthly roll-ups once date and numeric columns are converted and cleaned.
business_questions: Which products generated the highest net sales in a given date range?
How many kilograms of each product were sold per day/week/month?
What is the average discount and effective discount rate applied per product over a period?
What are daily gross vs net sales and tax collected per product?
Which products show growing or declining sales trends over the last N days
pos_product_wise_sales.relationships: Primary join key is product_id to product master tables (e.g., pos_products, b2b_products, ecom_products, product_variants). Date enables joins to calendar or time-dimension tables and to daily/monthly summary tables (pos_sales_daily, pos_sales_monthly). Enriching with order-level or transaction tables (pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines, order_items) enables per-transaction breakdowns, store- or terminal-level analysis, and calculation of unit prices or margins. These relationships enable product metadata enrichment (category, vendor), cross-channel comparison, and reconciling raw POS sales with invoice/order records.

pos_products.purpose: POS product master snapshot for a single store (STORE_AHD_001). Contains SKU-level product metadata, POS pricing, stock and reorder information as ingested in raw form. Likely used as a source for POS product catalogs, inventory reconciliation and price lookups.
pos_products.key_characteristics: Small table (13 rows). Many columns stored as nvarchar though containing numeric or datetime-like values. Several columns are low-cardinality or constant: store_id has 1 distinct value, tax_rate has 1 distinct value, active_flag has 1 distinct value. pos_sku and sku each have 10 distinct values. Several rows (23.1%) are null across most columns. There are three completely empty unnamed columns and one mostly-empty unnamed column.
pos_products.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can be used for per-SKU price and inventory reporting for the specific store, identifying items below reorder point, simple price lists, and auditing updated timestamps. It cannot support cross-store comparisons (only one store present) or robust joins without normalization/casting. It is useful as a lookup for SKU metadata and for generating alerts for low stock.
business_questions: What are the current POS prices for each SKU at this store?
Which SKUs are at or below their reorder point and need restocking?
What is the current stock quantity per SKU and the total stock value (price * qty)?
When was each SKU last updated in the POS product table?
Which SKUs are active in the POS catalog?
pos_products.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Logical joins: join on sku or pos_sku to transaction tables (pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines), sales aggregations (pos_product_wise_sales, pos_products_sales), product master tables (products, b2b_products, product_variants) and inventory/pricing tables (product_inventory, product_pricing). These joins enable sales vs stock reconciliation, margin/price analysis, and cross-referencing product metadata. Note: joins require data type normalization and potential trimming because many keys are nvarchar and may include formatting differences (e.g., plucode with decimal).

pos_products_sales.purpose: This table appears to be a raw POS (point-of-sale) product-level sales extract that stores aggregated monetary metrics per product SKU. It is likely an ingestion/staging table (note schema zuna_seed_raw) used to capture Gross Sales, Discount Amount, Tax Amount and Net Sales for SKUs before normalization/cleaning and joining to canonical product or transactional tables.
pos_products_sales.key_characteristics: Small sample (10 rows) in the provided extract. All monetary columns are stored as nvarchar (string) with decimal-like numeric values (many with long fractional representation). SKU is nvarchar with max length 6 observed; distinct SKUs = 10 (high cardinality relative to sample size). Columns have spaces in their names (e.g., 'Gross Sales') which requires quoted identifiers or aliasing in SQL. There are no primary or foreign keys declared. Data patterns: monetary strings are well-formed numeric text (period as decimal separator) but may contain variable precision; nullability is allowed across columns. Because numeric data is string-typed, any numeric aggregation requires casting/cleaning.
pos_products_sales.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table enables product-level revenue analysis such as: total gross/net sales by SKU, average discount or discount rate per SKU (if discount percent derived), tax contribution by SKU, ranking top/bottom SKUs by any monetary metric, and simple integrity checks (e.g., Gross - Discount + Tax ≈ Net if that is intended). It cannot provide time-series, store-level, or customer-level breakdowns without joining to other tables. Useful for validating ingestion and for quick SKU revenue summaries before enrichment.
business_questions: Which SKUs generated the highest net sales in this extract?
What is the total gross sales, total discounts, total tax and total net sales across all SKUs?
Which SKUs have the highest discount amounts (absolute) and which have the highest discount as a percent of gross?
Is Net Sales consistent with Gross Sales minus Discount plus Tax (data integrity check) for each SKU?
How much of total gross sales is consumed by tax (tax share) per SKU?
pos_products_sales.relationships: No explicit foreign keys are declared. The natural join key is sku which can be linked to canonical product tables in the schema such as pos_products, products, product_variants, b2b_products or product_sales. Joining by sku enables enrichment with product attributes (name, category, vendor), inventory and pricing history, and allows analyses broken down by category/store/brand when combined with other tables. Because this is a raw staging table, the typical relationship is a many-to-one join from this table to the product master (sku -> products.sku).

pos_returns.purpose: This table records point-of-sale return events (individual returned line items) captured in a raw ingestion schema. Each row represents a returned SKU from a POS transaction, with metadata about quantity, reason, refund amount, who processed the return, and simple flags for approval and restocking. It serves as the source of truth for return counts, refund totals, return reasons, and operational metrics (e.g., processor performance, restock behaviour) for POS returns.
pos_returns.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows) in provided snapshot. Identifier columns (pos_return_id, pos_txn_id) have high cardinality relative to table size. SKU cardinality is low (10 distinct). Several fields are stored as nvarchar despite representing dates, numeric quantities, or amounts: return_ts and created_at look like 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'; qty and approval/restock flags are small integer-like strings; refund_amount is a decimal-like string. Categorical columns (reason, processed_by, flags) have low cardinality and clear top-value distributions. No primary key or foreign key constraints are declared in DDL.
pos_returns.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can: compute return counts and refund totals by SKU, reason, processor, time window (after casting), and flags; identify top-returned SKUs and top refund amounts; calculate average refund per return or per SKU; examine distribution of reasons and restock/approval rates; do basic time-based analyses using return_ts or created_at after casting to timestamp. More advanced customer or product-enriched analyses require joins to other tables.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the highest total refund amount and number of returns in a given period?
What are the most common reasons for returns and how do they impact refund totals?
Which POS users (processed_by) handle the most returns and which generate the highest refund amounts?
What proportion of returns require approval and/or result in restocking, and how does that vary by reason or SKU?
How do returns and refunds trend over time (daily/weekly/monthly) based on return_ts or created_at?
pos_returns.relationships: No explicit FKs are declared, but natural joins are possible: pos_txn_id -> pos_transactions or pos_transactions/pos_transactions.pos_txn_id; sku -> pos_products/products/product_variants (sku or variant_id); processed_by -> pos_users (user id/code). Joining to order/invoice tables (if present) can link returns back to original sales; joining to product tables enriches product metadata (category, brand, price); joining to customers (via transaction/order tables) allows customer-level return analysis. Because keys are strings and not constrained, rely on naming conventions and data-cleaning (trim/case) when joining.

pos_sales_daily.purpose: Daily point-of-sale (POS) sales summary at the store level. Each row appears to capture aggregated sales metrics (bills, quantity, gross/discount/tax/net amounts) for a given store and date. Likely ingested from POS exports/raw pipeline and intended as a daily snapshot used for reporting and downstream aggregations.
pos_sales_daily.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar (text) despite many containing numeric/date values. High-cardinality numeric-like columns (gross_sales, net_sales, tax_amount, discount_amount, total_qty_kg) indicate near-unique values per row. The date column is textual with ~731 distinct values across ~2,193 rows (so roughly two years of daily data for 3 stores). store_id has very low cardinality (3 stores). created_at is constant for all rows (ingestion timestamp). Three Unnamed columns are mostly null or constant; two are 100% null, one is 99.8% null with 4 distinct values in a tiny fraction of rows — likely artifacts from source.
pos_sales_daily.analysis_capabilities: With minimal cleaning this table supports time-series analysis (daily trends), store-level comparisons, KPIs such as daily gross/net sales, average bill value (gross or net divided by total_bills), sales per kg, discount/tax rate calculations, and rolling aggregates (7/30 day). It can feed monthly/yearly rollups and join with store metadata to add location/region. However, numeric and date conversions are required before accurate aggregations.
business_questions: What are daily gross/net sales for each store and how have they trended over time?
Which store has the highest average bill value or sales per kg over a period?
How much discount and tax was given per day/store and what is the discount rate (discount/gross)?
What are 7-day and 30-day rolling averages for net sales per store?
Identify days with unusually high/low total_qty_kg or number of bills for anomaly detection.
pos_sales_daily.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Natural joins: store_id can join to pos_stores or pos_vendors (store metadata) to enrich location, region, or store type. date can join to other daily tables in the schema (pos_sales_daily variants in other schemas, pos_sales_monthly/ yearly, pos_transactions aggregated by date) for reconciliation and drill-through to transactions or product-wise sales. Because there is no primary key, joins to transactional tables must use (date, store_id) and additional disambiguation if multiple snapshots per day exist. The Unnamed columns appear to be unused and can be dropped to simplify joins.

pos_sales_monthly.purpose: Monthly aggregated point-of-sale (POS) sales metrics per store. It stores one row per store-month with counts (bills), volumes (kg), and monetary aggregates (gross, discount, tax, net). Used for high-level revenue and performance reporting.
pos_sales_monthly.key_characteristics: Small table (36 rows). All rows have year = '2024'. month has 12 distinct values (1-12) and store_id has 3 distinct stores. Numeric measures are stored as nvarchar strings (decimals and integers). created_at is also nvarchar and appears constant (likely ETL timestamp). No primary/foreign keys defined. High cardinality only in numeric fields (36 distinct each).
pos_sales_monthly.analysis_capabilities: Time series (month-over-month) and cross-store comparisons for 2024, KPI computations (monthly totals, averages, rates like discount% or tax%), simple trend detection, per-store ranking, and combining with daily/product level tables for drill-down if joined by store_id+year+month. Quick sanity checks (e.g., net_sales vs gross_sales minus discount + tax).
business_questions: What is the monthly net sales trend for each store for 2024?
Which store had the highest gross sales and total quantity sold (kg) in a given month?
What is the discount percentage of gross sales by month and store (discount/gross)?
How many bills were generated per store each month and how does bill count correlate with average ticket size?
What is the monthly tax collected per store and its share of gross sales?
pos_sales_monthly.relationships: No explicit foreign keys, but logically joins on store_id and year/month to related tables: pos_stores (store metadata), pos_sales_daily/pos_sales_yearly (drill-down/roll-up by time), pos_product_wise_sales or pos_products (to break down by product), b2b/ecom sales tables for cross-channel comparisons. Those joins enable cross-granularity analysis (daily ↔ monthly ↔ yearly) and enrichment with store attributes (region, city) or product-level metrics.

pos_sales_yearly.purpose: Per-store aggregated POS sales metrics for a given year. This table stores yearly rollups (bills, quantities, gross/discount/tax/net figures) at the store level and is intended to provide a high-level view of store performance for reporting and KPI calculations.
pos_sales_yearly.key_characteristics: Very small table (3 rows). All columns are stored as nvarchar despite numeric semantics for most columns. 'year' and 'created_at' have single distinct values in the sample (year = '2024', created_at = '2025-12-02 10:42:54'). store_id has 3 distinct values (one per row). Numeric-looking columns (total_bills, total_qty_kg, gross_sales, discount_amount, tax_amount, net_sales) each have 3 distinct string values representing numbers, some with decimal precision. Max string lengths vary (up to nvarchar(48) for created_at). No primary key or foreign key constraints defined.
pos_sales_yearly.analysis_capabilities: Can produce store-level yearly summaries and comparisons across stores for metrics like bills, quantity, gross/net sales, discounts and taxes. Supports simple ranking of stores by any metric, computing per-bill averages (if numeric casting applied), and deriving margins/discount percentages. Limited temporal analysis from this table alone because 'year' has a single value in the sample; richer time-series requires joining to monthly/daily tables (pos_sales_monthly / pos_sales_daily).
business_questions: Which stores had the highest net sales in 2024?
How many bills were processed per store in 2024 and what is the average ticket (net_sales / total_bills)?
What was the total quantity sold (kg) across all stores in 2024 and which store contributed the most?
How much discount and tax were applied per store in 2024 and what percentage of gross_sales do they represent?
How do store-level yearly aggregates compare to monthly/daily aggregates (when joined with pos_sales_monthly/pos_sales_daily)?
pos_sales_yearly.relationships: Connects naturally to pos_stores (on store_id) for store metadata (name, region, type), and to pos_sales_monthly / pos_sales_daily / pos_product_wise_sales for lower-level rollups or drill-downs. Joins enable: (a) enriching yearly metrics with store attributes, (b) validating aggregates by comparing sums of monthly/daily tables, and (c) combining with product-level sales to compute store-level product mix. Because there are no declared FKs, joins should use explicit equality on store_id and matching year columns in other tables. Also useful to join to accounting tables (tax/discount details) for reconciliation.

pos_shift_closures.purpose: POS shift closure records: one row per closed shift capturing shift identifiers, terminal and user, shift start/end timestamps, monetary totals (sales, cash, card, refunds), expected vs counted cash and any notes about discrepancies. Serves as the canonical source for shift-level cash reconciliation and shift-level performance in the POS reporting domain.
pos_shift_closures.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows). Many numeric-looking columns stored as nvarchar; timestamps stored as nvarchar in ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format. shift_id appears unique (distinct=50, equals row count) and is a strong candidate key. terminal_id and user_id have low cardinality (4 and 3 distinct values respectively). discrepancies_note is 100% NULL. No declared PKs or FKs. Typical patterns: numeric values include many decimal values with long precision; timestamps are parseable; cash fields include expected and actual for reconciliation.
pos_shift_closures.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can perform per-shift reconciliation (expected vs counted cash), compute shift-level KPIs (total sales, card vs cash mix), user/terminal performance (aggregations by user_id or terminal_id), detect shifts with large cash discrepancies, and build time series of shift volumes when shift timestamps are parsed to proper datetime types.
business_questions: Which shifts had a discrepancy between expected_cash and counted_cash, and what is the distribution of discrepancy amounts?
What is total sales, cash collected and card collected by user (cashier) or by terminal over a period?
Which user or terminal has the highest average shift sales or the highest incidence of cash discrepancies?
How do shift start/end times distribute across days and what are peak shift revenue periods?
What is the ratio of refunds to total_sales per shift and which shifts had refunds above a threshold?
pos_shift_closures.relationships: No declared foreign keys. Natural joins likely to: pos_transactions (to reconcile transaction-level totals with shift totals), pos_terminals (to enrich terminals with location/store), pos_users or pos_users-like table (to get user/cashier metadata), pos_daily_sales_summary / pos_sales_daily (to roll up shift-level values to daily totals). These joins enable reconciliation between transaction-level and shift-level data, enrich shift records with user/terminal attributes, and support multi-level aggregations (shift->day->store).

pos_stores.purpose: Holds point-of-sale (POS) store master / metadata (store identifier, location and activity timestamps). It is used as a reference for store-level reporting, filtering and joining to transaction, inventory, sales and terminal tables.
pos_stores.key_characteristics: Very small table (4 rows) with 1:1 mapping between store_id and location fields. All columns are nullable nvarchar types; date/time values are stored as strings in a consistent ISO-like format (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). store_id has low cardinality but is unique across rows (distinct=4). city and region mirror each other in values shown (likely region == city or region is a city-level region). There are no declared primary or foreign keys. Because date columns are strings, time-based queries require casting to a datetime type for proper range/filter/sorting.
pos_stores.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports location-level cataloging, recent-activity queries (last seen online), lifecycle queries (first activation date), small aggregations by city/region, and serves as a dimension to join with sales/transactions/inventory to produce store-level metrics. It cannot provide sales or transactional metrics itself but can annotate those datasets.
business_questions: Which stores are currently active or were last online within a given date range?
How many stores are in each city/region and what are their activation dates?
Which stores have been recently active (e.g., last 7/30/90 days) to target operational checks?
List the store IDs and their first activation date to audit new store rollouts.
Which stores should be joined to POS transactions and inventory to compute store-level KPIs?
pos_stores.relationships: No foreign keys declared, but logically store_id is the natural join key to many POS and retail tables in the schema (pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines, pos_terminals, pos_sales_daily, pos_products_sales, pos_inventory_adjustments, pos_shift_closures, pos_users). Joining on store_id enables mapping transactional and inventory events to location and time metadata (first_active_date, last_online) to analyze per-store performance and recency of activity.

pos_sync_log.purpose: Stores point-of-sale (POS) synchronization events between terminals and a central system. Each row records a sync attempt including timestamps, counts of records exchanged, status, errors and retry metadata. Used to monitor health, throughput and reliability of terminal syncs.
pos_sync_log.key_characteristics: Small table (50 rows sample). Many columns are stored as nvarchar including timestamps and numeric counters. Cardinalities: sync_id and timestamps are high-cardinality (~50 distinct); terminal_id low-cardinality (4 distinct; TERM-001-* dominates); sync_status is binary-like (SUCCESS 58%, FAILED 42%); records_sent/received show many distinct numeric values but stored as strings; error_message largely NULL (58%) with a single non-null value 'Network error'; retry_count small set of values (0-3) but stored as string. No declared PK or FKs in DDL.
pos_sync_log.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can compute sync success rates, per-terminal sync frequency and volume, retry distributions, failure counts and basic SLA metrics (time since last success if parsed). Time-series trends (if strings cast to datetime) and aggregate metrics (avg/min/max records_sent/received) are possible. You can also identify recurring error patterns and problematic terminals.
business_questions: What is the overall success rate of POS syncs over a period (daily/weekly/monthly)?
Which terminals have the most failed syncs and highest retry counts?
What is the average and peak number of records sent and received per sync and per terminal?
When was the last successful sync for each terminal (to detect offline terminals)?
Which syncs failed due to network errors and how often do they occur over time?
pos_sync_log.relationships: No foreign keys declared, but terminal_id logically relates to pos_terminals (terminal metadata) and possibly to pos_transactions/pos_transaction_lines (to reconcile counts). Joining to pos_terminals enables human-friendly terminal names, store mapping and region-level aggregation. Joining to transactions or daily summaries allows validating records_sent/received against actual transaction volumes and detecting data-loss or duplication.

pos_terminals.purpose: Inventory of point-of-sale (POS) terminals: identity, location, assignment, software/version and recent connectivity metadata. It is a device registry used to manage terminals across stores and to relate terminals to transactions, shifts and operational users.
pos_terminals.key_characteristics: All columns stored as nvarchar (strings). Low distinct counts in sample for pos_software_version and status (single value each), small sample cardinality (8 rows) but likely larger in production. terminal_id and store_id are not declared keys but behave like identifiers; terminal_code and device_serial appear unique-per-terminal. Timestamps (last_online_ts, created_at) and coordinates (location_lat, location_long) are text and should be cast to datetime/decimal for accurate time and spatial queries. Many NULL-able columns; no declared PK/FK even though logical joins exist (store_id -> pos_stores, assigned_user_id -> pos_users).
pos_terminals.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports device inventory counts, distribution of terminals per store, monitoring recency (last online), software roll-out coverage, mapping terminals geographically, identifying assigned users, and computing onboarding timelines (created_at). It enables operational queries (which terminals are offline/active, who is responsible) and preparation for joins to sales/transactions tables for terminal-level sales analysis.
business_questions: How many terminals are deployed per store and what are their identifiers?
Which terminals have not checked in within a given time window (potentially offline)?
Which software versions are running on terminals and which stores still need upgrades?
What are the geolocations of our terminals (for mapping/region coverage)?
Which user (operator) is assigned to each terminal and when was each terminal provisioned?
pos_terminals.relationships: Although no foreign keys are declared, common joins are: store_id -> pos_stores(store_id) to get store metadata (name, region); assigned_user_id -> pos_users(user_id) to get operator details; terminal_id or terminal_code -> pos_transactions/pos_transaction_lines/pos_shift_closures to attribute transactions or shifts to a terminal; device_serial may be used to link to hardware inventory or vendor tables. These relationships enable linking device operational state to sales performance, shift activity, returns and support workflows.

pos_transaction_lines.purpose: Represents individual POS transaction lines (items) sold in point-of-sale transactions. Each row is one line item on a POS receipt containing SKU, quantity, pricing, tax, batch and creation timestamp. It is a detailed sales activity table used to compute revenue, tax, SKU-level sales and inventory/batch traceability.
pos_transaction_lines.key_characteristics: Small table (125 rows). Many numeric fields are stored as nvarchar (strings) and require casting for numeric/date operations. pos_line_id is unique per row (125 distinct), pos_txn_id has ~50 distinct values (multiple lines per transaction). sku has low cardinality (10 distinct) with clear top SKUs (BRN007, ONI003, TOM001, LIM008, BTR006). batch_id is almost unique (124 distinct) indicating tight batch tracking. discount_amt is constant '0' for all rows. variant_info and expiry_date are 100% null. created_at appears as a datetime string (50 distinct values). tax_amt and line_total contain decimal-like strings with floating representations (e.g., 4.7999999999999998).
pos_transaction_lines.analysis_capabilities: On its own this table can support SKU-level sales analytics (units sold, revenue, avg price), per-transaction breakdowns, tax aggregation, batch-level item traceability, and time-based trends using created_at after casting. It cannot provide customer, store or full transaction metadata without joins (e.g., to pos_transactions, pos_stores, products). Because several columns are strings, correct type casting is necessary for accurate numeric/date aggregations.
business_questions: Which SKUs generated the most revenue and units sold in this dataset?
What is total revenue and tax collected for the recorded POS lines over a given time range?
Which batches were sold and how many units per batch (batch traceability)?
What is the average unit price and average line_total per SKU?
How many line items are associated with each POS transaction (lines per receipt)?
pos_transaction_lines.relationships: Natural joins: pos_txn_id -> pos_transactions (to get store, terminal, cashier, transaction-level status); sku -> pos_products / products / product_variants (to enrich with product name, category); batch_id -> inventory_items or product_inventory (to link batch manufacture/expiry info). These relationships enable full sales analytics by store/customer, product dimension enrichment, inventory/batch expiry traceability and mapping sales to orders/payments.

pos_transactions.purpose: Point-of-sale transactions master table capturing one row per POS transaction (sales) with basic financials, payment mode, terminal and user metadata. It serves as the canonical source for transaction-level sales analytics in the POS domain.
pos_transactions.key_characteristics: Small sample (50 rows) but representative characteristics: many columns are stored as nvarchar rather than native numeric/datetime types; txn_type is constant (SALE), status is constant (COMPLETED), discount_total is constant (0), synced_flag is constant (1). txn_ts and created_at are present and appear to be ISO-like timestamp strings (yyyy-mm-dd hh:mi:ss). txn_total and tax_total contain numeric values stored as strings with varying decimal representations. Cardinalities: pos_txn_id and external_txn_ref are unique-ish (distinct=50), terminal_id and pos_user_id low cardinality, payment_mode low-cardinality (CASH/UPI/CARD), shift_id small set.
pos_transactions.analysis_capabilities: With this table alone you can compute transaction-level revenue, tax sums, payment-mode mix, per-terminal/per-user sales counts and totals over time (after casting txn_ts/txn_total). You can analyze shift-level performance, trends by created_at/txn_ts, and identify data-quality issues (missing cashier notes, inconsistent data types).
business_questions: What is total sales (sum of txn_total) over a given period?
How do sales break down by payment_mode (CASH/UPI/CARD) for the last N days?
Which terminals and POS users generated the most transactions and revenue?
What are the shift-wise sales totals and average ticket size?
Are there any transactions not synced or with abnormal tax/total values (data quality checks)?
pos_transactions.relationships: No declared foreign keys, but likely joins: pos_transactions.pos_txn_id -> pos_transaction_lines (transaction details/lines); pos_user_id -> pos_users for user metadata; terminal_id -> pos_terminals for terminal metadata; shift_id -> pos_shift_closures or shift dimension for shift metadata; external_txn_ref -> ecom_payments or pos_payments/external payment systems for reconciliation. Those joins enable item-level rollups, cashier performance, terminal uptime/usage, and payment reconciliation.

pos_users.purpose: Holds point-of-sale (POS) user accounts (cashiers, supervisors) and basic profile/access metadata used by POS systems and audits. It is a small user directory used to attribute transactions, shifts and activities to specific store employees.
pos_users.key_characteristics: Very small table (3 rows in sample). All columns stored as nvarchar (strings). pos_user_id appears to be a stable identifier (3 distinct). role has low cardinality (Cashier, Supervisor). active_flag currently only '1' (string). created_at and last_login are stored as strings in a datetime format and will require casting for time-based calculations. pin_hash contains hashed PINs (sensitive). No declared primary or foreign keys in DDL, but store_id likely references pos_stores. Current dataset shows uniform distribution across three users; production expected to have many more rows.
pos_users.analysis_capabilities: By itself this table can be used to: list and filter POS users by store or role; compute counts of users per role/store; identify recent/active users via last_login; audit user creation dates; join to transactions, shifts or audits (if joined by pos_user_id) to attribute activity to users.
business_questions: How many POS users are assigned to each store?
How many employees have the role 'Cashier' vs 'Supervisor'?
Which users have not logged in within the last N days (potentially inactive)?
When was each POS user account created and who are recent hires?
Which users are associated with a specific transaction or shift (when joined to transactions or shifts tables)?
pos_users.relationships: Though no explicit foreign keys exist in the DDL, store_id should be treated as a foreign key to pos_stores (enables lookups of store metadata and region). pos_user_id should be used to join to POS activity tables such as pos_transactions, pos_transaction_lines, pos_shift_closures, pos_sales_daily, pos_sync_log to attribute actions to users. Joining enables cross-table analyses like sales per user, returns by user, shift performance and audit trails.

pos_vendors.purpose: Master list of point-of-sale (POS) vendors/suppliers used by the system. It stores vendor identifiers, contact details, address, tax identifier (GST), business category and a creation timestamp. The table is used as a lookup for vendor metadata in procurement, invoicing, payments and sales-related joins and reporting.
pos_vendors.key_characteristics: Small table (40 rows). vendor_id and gst_no are high-cardinality and unique across rows (distinct 40). city and business_type are low-cardinality (9 and 5 distinct) with clear skew (few cities cover most rows). created_at currently has a single identical string value for all rows (likely imported as text). All columns are nvarchar and nullable; there are no declared keys or foreign keys. Data values show consistent patterns (phone with country code, email domain vendor.in, multi-part addresses).
pos_vendors.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table supports vendor counts, distribution by city or business type, contact lists for outreach, basic data quality checks (missing values, duplicate vendor_name or gst_no), and creation-time profiling. It can be joined with transactions, invoices, payments, products and order tables to analyze supplier spend, lead times, and vendor performance when combined with those tables.
business_questions: How many unique vendors do we have in the POS system?
What is the distribution of vendors by city and by business type?
Which vendors are missing contact information (phone/email) or GST numbers?
Provide a vendor contact list (name, phone, email, address) for outreach.
Which cities or business types account for the majority of our supplier base?
pos_vendors.relationships: No explicit foreign keys are declared, but vendor_id (and vendor_name/gst_no) is the natural join key to other vendor-related tables in the schema such as b2b_vendor_partners, ecom_vendors, b2b_invoices, b2b_payments, b2b_orders and POS transaction tables (pos_transactions/pos_transaction_lines). Joining enables linking vendor metadata to spending (invoices/payments), procurement events (orders), and product/vendor performance metrics. Recommend adding foreign key constraints and indexes where vendor_id is used in transactional tables to optimize joins.

product_inventory.purpose: Per-centre product inventory snapshot: tracks inventory quantities (units and kg), reserved/available stock, reorder parameters, lead times, safety stock, expiry and storage temperature per inventory record (SKU x centre). Used for operations (replenishment, expiry/risk monitoring) and inventory reporting.
product_inventory.key_characteristics: Small table (40 rows). Many numeric measurements stored as NVARCHAR/text (qty, reserved, available, reorder_point, lead_time_days, safety_stock_kg) and timestamps also stored as NVARCHAR (expiry_ts). No declared primary/foreign keys. inventory_id appears unique per row (distinct=40) but not enforced. sku cardinality is low (10 distinct). centre_id has 4 distinct centres. lead_time_days has very low cardinality (3 values). storage_temp_c has 2 values. Pattern: inventory_id encodes sku and centre (e.g., INV-APP009-AHMEDABAD).
product_inventory.analysis_capabilities: Can compute stock summaries per SKU or centre, identify low/available/reserved stock, flag items below reorder_point, compute days-to-expiry and expiry risk, compare available stock vs safety_stock_kg, and estimate replenishment window using lead_time_days. Limited longitudinal analysis — snapshot only (no history).
business_questions: Which SKUs at which centres are below their reorder point and need replenishment?
What is total available stock (kg and units) per centre or per SKU?
Which inventory items are expiring within X days and must be prioritized?
How much of the current stock is reserved vs available for sale per SKU/centre?
What is the expected replenishment lead time distribution by SKU or centre (to plan orders)?
product_inventory.relationships: Natural join keys: sku can join to product tables (products, product_variants, b2b_products, ecom_products, pos_products, product_pricing) to enrich product metadata. centre_id can join to centre/warehouse/store tables (pos_stores, delivery_slots?) or any centre master for location attributes. inventory_id could be used as unique identifier for inventory-level joins to inventory_items or pos_inventory_adjustments if mapped. No declared FKs — joins should be performed on sku and centre_id after type/format alignment.

product_pricing.purpose: Stores time-bounded pricing records for products (by SKU). Each row represents a price version (with start and end timestamps), unit price, currency and minimum order quantity, plus audit metadata. It is used to determine historical and current pricing for SKUs and to support pricing-related analyses and joins to sales/orders to compute revenue or apply the correct price at order time.
product_pricing.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar (strings). Row count ~100. Distinct counts: price_id 100 (appears unique per row), sku 10 (high reuse), start_ts and end_ts are distinct per row (100), price_per_unit distinct per row (100). currency and created_by are constant (single value each). min_order_qty has low cardinality (values '1','5','10'). Timestamp-like values use 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' string pattern. Prices are stored with many decimal digits as strings. No declared primary key or foreign keys. All columns are nullable in DDL but dataset shows populated values.
product_pricing.analysis_capabilities: By itself this table supports timeline-based pricing analysis: price history per SKU, active price at a point in time (when parsed to timestamp), price change frequency, overlapping/coverage checks for price intervals, aggregates (min/max/avg price_per_unit) per SKU or time window, counts of price versions, and min_order_qty analysis. When joined to orders/sales by sku and time (order timestamp between start_ts and end_ts), it enables correct revenue attribution, price validation, and price list reconciliation.
business_questions: What is the price history for a specific SKU (all price versions with start/end and min order qty)?
What was the effective unit price for SKU X at a given order timestamp?
Which SKUs had the largest price increase or decrease in a given date range?
Are there any overlapping price intervals or gaps in pricing for any SKU?
What is the distribution (min/avg/max) of price_per_unit by SKU and over time?
product_pricing.relationships: Primary natural join key is sku — joinable to product/catalog tables (products, product_variants, b2b_products, ecom_vendors' product views, product_sales, order_items, etc.) to map price records to product metadata and transactions. start_ts/end_ts combined allow temporal joins to orders or order_items by checking order_ts BETWEEN start_ts AND end_ts (after casting to timestamp). price_id can be used as a stable identifier for a price version if other tables reference it (none shown). created_by could link to user or admin tables if present. Because types are strings, NL2SQL queries must cast start_ts/end_ts and price_per_unit to appropriate types to ensure correct comparisons and aggregations.

product_sales.purpose: This table appears to be a raw ingestion of product-level sales metrics (gross sales, discounts, delivery fee, tax and net sales) keyed by SKU. It likely serves as a staging or analytical input to compute product revenue, discounts and unit-level financial metrics prior to joining with master product, orders or time-dimension tables for richer analytics.
product_sales.key_characteristics: Small sample (10 rows). All columns are nvarchar and hold numeric values as strings for the monetary columns. sku has max length 22 but observed max length 6 and 10 distinct values (which equals the row count). Monetary fields show decimal numbers stored as text with up to 18 characters in length. All columns are nullable and there is no declared primary key or foreign keys. Column names include spaces (e.g., 'Gross Sales') which requires quoting/bracketing in SQL. Because values are strings, numeric comparisons/aggregations require casting. No time dimension or count/quantity columns are present, so analyses are limited to snapshot monetary aggregations.
product_sales.analysis_capabilities: Can perform per-SKU aggregations (sum, avg) after converting strings to numeric types, compute derived KPIs such as discount percentage (Discount / Gross Sales), contribution of delivery fee/tax to gross/net sales, and simple validations (Gross Sales - Discount + Delivery Fee + Tax ?= Net Sales) if intended formula is known. Can produce top-N SKU revenue lists and per-sku margin approximations. Limited for time-series, cohort, or transaction-level analytics due to missing date and quantity fields.
business_questions: Which SKUs generate the highest gross sales and net sales (top-N products)?
What is the discount rate per SKU (discount as a percentage of gross sales)?
How much of gross sales is consumed by delivery fees and taxes per SKU?
Are there inconsistencies between Gross Sales, Discount, Delivery Fee, Tax and reported Net Sales (simple reconciliation / data quality check)?
Which SKUs have the largest absolute or relative discounts and how does that impact net sales rank?
product_sales.relationships: Most useful joins are to product master tables (products, b2b_products, ecom_products, product_variants) using sku to enrich with name, category, brand, cost and product hierarchy. Joining to order/line or sales tables (b2b_order_lines, ecom_orders, pos_transaction_lines, etc.) would provide time, quantities, and transactional context enabling time-series, unit-level, and profitability analyses. Because sku is currently just a text column with no FK constraint, NL2SQL systems should treat joins as heuristic matches on sku and allow downstream users to validate join quality.

product_variants.purpose: Holds product variant-level master data (SKU-level variants) describing packaging, units, weight, barcodes and pricing for product variants. It is used as a reference/lookup for variant attributes and to join with sales, inventory and pricing tables to support SKU/variant-level analytics.
product_variants.key_characteristics: Small table (20 rows in sample). Many columns stored as nvarchar (strings) though several represent numeric or datetime concepts (price_retail, price_wholesale, units_per_pack, weight_kg, created_at). variant_id and variant_sku appear unique across rows (distinct 20). variant_name and uom are low-cardinality (variant_name: 2 values, uom: 3 values). active_flag has a single value '1' in sample (no inactive rows present). Price columns include floating point artifacts in string form (e.g., 13.199999999999999). created_at values are ISO-like datetime strings. No declared primary or foreign keys in DDL.
product_variants.analysis_capabilities: Alone, this table enables: SKU/variant attribute lookup; price comparisons between retail and wholesale; packaging and weight-based aggregations (once numeric types are cast); tracking when variants were created (after casting created_at); filtering active variants; detecting duplicate barcodes. When joined to sales/orders/inventory tables it enables variant-level sales, margin and inventory analyses.
business_questions: What packaging variants exist for a given product SKU (e.g., Loose vs Packed 500g)?
What are the retail and wholesale prices for each variant and how do they compare?
Which barcodes map to which product variants (to support scanning and fulfillment)?
What are the unit/weight attributes (units_per_pack, weight_kg) per variant for logistics and labeling?
When were variants created (to track new variant introductions) and which are active?
product_variants.relationships: Although no explicit foreign keys are declared, this table is typically joined to product master and transactional tables by sku or variant_sku (or variant_id if present in other tables). Typical joins: products.sku = product_variants.sku to enrich product-level data; order_lines/pos_transaction_lines/order_items may join via variant_sku or barcode to attribute sales to variants. These joins enable variant-level sales, pricing, inventory and returns analyses. Because types are strings, casts may be required for numeric/date joins and comparisons. The absence of declared PK/FK means NL2SQL systems should infer join keys (sku, variant_sku, variant_id) and prefer explicit JOIN hints from users.

products.purpose: This table is a raw product catalog snapshot (zuna_seed_raw.products). It holds product master attributes (SKU, name, classification, weight, packaging and perishability metadata) intended to describe items sold across channels. In the broader schema it functions as a product reference that should be joined to sales, inventory, pricing and order tables to enable commercial and operational analyses.
products.key_characteristics: Small sample (10 rows) in provided stats. SKU is unique across sample (distinct 10). Many categorical columns have very low cardinality: category (1), brand (1), product_type (2). UOM has 3 values. Several numeric-looking fields (gross_weight_kg, net_weight_kg, shelf_life_days, storage_temp_c, is_perishable_flag) are stored as nvarchar and must be cast for numeric operations. Two columns (hs_code, default_image_url) are 100% null in this snapshot. created_at is stored as nvarchar but contains timestamp strings. No primary key or constraints declared. pack_size is constant (single value).
products.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table supports: product catalog queries (list/lookups), distribution of product types/subcategories, perishable classification and shelf-life profiling, inventory handling rules derivation (storage temp, shelf life), and basic time-of-creation audits. Richer analyses (sales, turnover, inventory aging, returns) require joining to sales/inventory/order tables via sku.
business_questions: What products exist in the catalog and what are their SKUs, names and unit-of-measure?
Which products are perishable and what are their shelf lives and recommended storage temperatures?
How many distinct subcategories and types of products do we have and what is their distribution?
Which SKUs are missing regulatory or media metadata (e.g., HS code or image URL)?
When were products added to the catalog (created_at) and which recent products might need enrichment?
products.relationships: This table should be joined to multiple tables in the schema using sku as the likely join key (schema contains b2b_products, ecom_products, pos_products, product_inventory, product_pricing, product_sales, order_items, product_variants, returns, shipments, etc.). Those joins enable sales/performance analyses (join to sales/order tables), inventory and stocklevel calculations (join to product_inventory), pricing and margin analysis (join to product_pricing and invoicing), and supply chain/regulatory checks (HS code with shipments/returns). Because this table has no declared PK or FK, enforce/assume sku uniqueness and add constraints or surrogate keys in production for reliable joins.

returns.purpose: Records of product returns initiated against orders. Each row captures a return request lifecycle (request, approval, pickup scheduling, centre receipt, refund and credit note linkage). The table is a raw ingest (all columns are nvarchar) intended to be joined with orders, credit notes, return items and invoices for business analytics and operational reporting.
returns.key_characteristics: Small table (40 rows). All columns stored as nvarchar (strings) even where numeric or datetime semantics exist. return_id is effectively unique (40 distinct). order_id repeats (35 distinct) — some orders have multiple returns. Timestamps (request_ts, pickup_scheduled_ts, received_at_centre_ts, created_at) are ISO-like strings but not typed; consistent formatting observed. refund_amount is a string but contains decimal numeric values. reason_code has low cardinality (4 values). status has 4 states with skew towards COMPLETED and APPROVED. requested_items_json is always '[]' in this sample and resolution_notes are fully null. approved_by has two distinct non-null values and ~52% nulls.
returns.analysis_capabilities: Alone this table can support analyses of return volumes, reason distributions, status distributions, refund totals, per-approver counts and timing metrics between request/ pickup/ centre receipt (after casting timestamps). It cannot attribute returns to customers or product-level metrics without joins to orders or return-items tables. It can link refunds to credit notes via credit_note_id for reconciliation.
business_questions: How many returns were created in a given date range and how are they distributed by reason_code and status?
What is the total and average refund_amount for returns (and by reason_code or status)?
Which approvers processed the most returns and what are their approval rates or average refund amounts?
What are the average times from request_ts -> pickup_scheduled_ts -> received_at_centre_ts (operational latency) for completed returns?
Which orders have multiple returns (and how many returns per order)?
returns.relationships: Natural join keys: order_id can join to orders tables (b2b_orders, ecom_orders, pos_transactions/order_items depending on domain) to attribute returns to customers, order totals and product lines. credit_note_id can join to b2b_credit_notes (or ecom_refunds/ecom_return_items depending on naming) to reconcile refund documents. Detailed product-level analysis requires joining to a return-items table (not present here) such as ecom_return_items or order_lines. Joins enable computing return rate per SKU, customer-level return behavior, revenue impact and reconciliation with invoices/payments.

shipments.purpose: Records of shipments for orders: each row represents one shipment event/record including identifiers, carrier, dates, status, weight and charges. Serves as the source of truth for shipment-level analytics, carrier performance, delivery timing and cost analysis in the logistics/shipping domain.
shipments.key_characteristics: All columns are stored as nvarchar/text. Row count small (100) in sample. shipment_id and order_id are fully distinct (100 distinct values). carrier_id is low-cardinality (3 values). shipment_status has 4 categories. actual_delivery_date has ~41% nulls (incomplete deliveries). package_dimensions_json is constant ('{}') in this sample. package_weight_kg and shipping_charge are stored as text but represent numeric values (package_weight_kg distinct ~99). Datetime-like columns are nvarchar and require casting for temporal analysis. There are no declared primary or foreign keys.
shipments.analysis_capabilities: On-time delivery and SLA compliance (expected vs actual); carrier and pickup-centre performance (delivery time, statuses distribution, average shipping_charge, weight vs charge); shipment volume over time; identifying late or missing deliveries; cost-per-weight and cost-per-order analyses. Can compute transit time (after casting), carrier share, pickup centre workloads and simple auditing using created_at/last_update.
business_questions: What percentage of shipments were delivered on or before the expected_delivery_date (on-time delivery rate)?
Which carrier has the highest average shipping_charge and how does that relate to package_weight_kg?
What is the average transit time (shipment_date to actual_delivery_date) by pickup_centre_id and by carrier?
How many shipments are currently in each shipment_status (IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, PICKED, DELIVERED)?
Which orders have no actual_delivery_date (pending/undelivered) and how long have they been outstanding?
shipments.relationships: Logical join keys: order_id can join to order tables (e.g., b2b_orders, ecom_orders) to connect shipments to order-level data; carrier_id can join to vendor/partner tables (b2b_vendor_partners, ecom_vendors) or dispatch tables (b2b_dispatches) for carrier metadata; tracking_no and shipment_id can join to b2b_shipment_tracking_events for event timelines. These joins enable end-to-end order-to-delivery analysis, cost allocation to orders/customers, carrier benchmarking, and enriched routing/status timelines.

contract_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for selecting and grouping contract-level records, de-duplication, and joining back to contract detail tables or logs (if present). Useful for contract-level aggregations, lookups and auditing.
business_questions: Show full contract details for contract_id = 'CON3001'.
How many orders/invoices are tied to a specific contract (after joining orders by customer and SKU)?
contract_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string in WHERE/JOIN predicates. There is no enforced PK — consider enforcing uniqueness or using it as primary key. Use equality or IN lists for identification. Watch for trailing spaces; trim values when matching external keys.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer the contract applies to.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group or filter contracts by customer, join to customer master (b2b_customers) to enrich with account attributes, and join to orders/invoices for compliance and revenue attribution.
business_questions: List all active contracts for customer CUST1001.
Which customers have more than one open contract or overlapping contract periods?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Acts as the natural FK to b2b_customers.customer_id (no declared constraint). Ensure consistent formatting/casing when joining. Use as a primary join key when reconciling contracts to orders/invoices by customer.

start_date.description: Contract effective start timestamp (stored as nvarchar).
start_date.analysis_use: Used to compute contract duration, determine whether a contract is in force at a given date, cohort analysis (contracts started in a time window), and filtering by period. Must be CAST/CONVERT to datetime for accurate comparisons and date-based grouping.
business_questions: How many contracts started in Q1 2025?
Which contracts are already in effect as of today (start_date <= today and end_date >= today)?
start_date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type before date arithmetic. Guard against malformed or null strings; specify format in conversion if needed (e.g., 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'). Prefer storing as native datetime in future schema changes.

end_date.analysis_use: Used to determine expiry, compute remaining duration, filter expiring contracts, and classify ACTIVE vs EXPIRED when combined with start_date and current date. Required for renewal and expiration analyses.
business_questions: Which contracts will expire within the next 30/90 days?
How many contracts have already expired as of today?
end_date.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string — CAST/CONVERT before comparisons. Use combined logic (start_date <= date AND end_date >= date) to identify active contracts instead of relying solely on status. Handle open-ended/null end_date as ongoing contracts.

sku_list_json.description: Serialized list of SKUs covered by the contract. In the current data it looks like array text with single quotes (e.g., ['SKU1','SKU2']).
sku_list_json.analysis_use: Explode to rows to answer SKU-level coverage queries, join to product or order line tables to compute contracted vs actual sales, and filter contracts that include a given SKU. Also used to compute SKU penetration and portfolio overlap across contracts.
business_questions: Which contracts cover SKU 'LIM008'?
What percentage of SKUs sold in the last month are under an active contract?
sku_list_json.nl2sql_hints: Current format may not be valid JSON (single quotes). Preprocess: replace single quotes with double quotes or use string-splitting functions to create a normalized (contract_id, sku) table. If DB supports JSON functions, ensure proper JSON array format (double quotes) before using JSON_QUERY/OPENJSON. Indexing will be much more effective after normalizing SKUs into a separate child table.

pricing_model.description: Categorical field indicating the contract's pricing strategy (e.g., Dynamic, Fixed, Tiered).
pricing_model.analysis_use: Group and filter contracts by pricing model, compare performance (revenue, margins, compliance) across models, and segment contract analytics by pricing type.
business_questions: How many contracts use the Dynamic pricing model vs Fixed or Tiered?
Which pricing model corresponds to the highest average monthly invoice amount (when joined with invoice data)?
pricing_model.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Watch for inconsistent casing/typos — normalize values in queries (UPPER/TRIM). Consider refactoring to a code table for cleaner joins and to support additional model metadata.

min_monthly_volume.description: Minimum monthly purchase volume required by the contract (stored as nvarchar but represents a numeric quantity).
min_monthly_volume.analysis_use: Numeric threshold used to check customer compliance (actual monthly sales vs min requirement), to flag high-volume contracts, and to aggregate total committed volume across contracts. Requires casting to integer/decimal for aggregation and comparison.
business_questions: Which contracts have a min_monthly_volume > 1000 and are currently undersupplied?
What is the total committed minimum monthly volume across all active contracts?
min_monthly_volume.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — CAST/CONVERT to INT or DECIMAL before numeric operations. Handle non-numeric or null values defensively (TRY_CAST or CASE). Normalize units if multiple unit conventions might exist.

discount_schema_json.description: Structured discount rules for the contract (JSON). Current sample shows a consistent JSON object {"vol_discount":"5%"}.
discount_schema_json.analysis_use: Extract discount rates/tiers for pricing calculations, apply discounts when computing expected revenue, and compare discount generosity across contracts. If more complex (multi-tier) it can drive pricing logic.
business_questions: What is the volume discount for contract CON3001?
Which contracts provide a volume discount of 5% or greater?
discount_schema_json.nl2sql_hints: This appears to be valid JSON — use JSON parsing functions (JSON_VALUE/OPENJSON) to extract fields. Percent values are strings (e.g., '5%') — strip '%' and cast to numeric for arithmetic. Given low variance in current data, consider normalizing if additional discount schemas are introduced.

delivery_windows_json.description: Structured delivery window information for the contract (JSON). Current value example: {"mon-fri":"08:00-12:00"}.
delivery_windows_json.analysis_use: Filter or match deliveries to allowed windows, compute delivery compliance, and plan logistics. Useful for operational scheduling and SLA checks when joined with delivery or dispatch events.
business_questions: What delivery window applies to contract CON3001?
How many contracts restrict deliveries to '08:00-12:00' on weekdays?
delivery_windows_json.nl2sql_hints: Valid JSON — extract keys/values using JSON functions. If the format is simple and constant, consider storing normalized delivery window identifiers for efficient filtering. If windows vary, ensure parsing covers multiple keys/days.

penalty_terms.description: Human-readable penalty clause text describing contract penalties (string). Example: 'Late delivery penalty applies'.
penalty_terms.analysis_use: Used for reference and reporting, to flag contracts that include penalty conditions, and as a join filter when computing penalty-triggering events from delivery/dispatch logs.
business_questions: Which contracts include a late delivery penalty?
List contracts and their penalty terms for commercial review.
penalty_terms.nl2sql_hints: Free text field — not ideal for predicate logic. If penalty types must be queried frequently, normalize into structured columns (e.g., penalty_type, penalty_rate). For text filtering, use LIKE/ILIKE or full-text search depending on DB capabilities.

status.description: Lifecycle status of the contract (PENDING, ACTIVE, EXPIRED).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for lifecycle analytics (counts by status), segmentation of active vs non-active agreements, and candidate selection for renewal/activation processes.
business_questions: How many contracts are currently PENDING and require activation?
How does contract status distribution change month-over-month?
status.nl2sql_hints: Use in WHERE and GROUP BY; standardize casing with UPPER/TRIM to avoid mismatches. Do not rely solely on status to determine 'active' — cross-check start_date/end_date to protect against stale status values.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the contract record was created (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and lineage (when contract was added), cohort and trend analysis (contracts created per period), and for ordering results by creation time. Use for SLA or onboarding metrics.
business_questions: How many contracts were created each month in 2025?
What is the average time from created_at to start_date for contracts?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — CAST/CONVERT to datetime before use in time-based GROUP BY or date arithmetic. Be mindful of inconsistent formats/timezones. Use created_at for creation-time filters but verify with start_date for effective contract timelines.

credit_note_id.description: Unique identifier for the credit or debit note (e.g., CN17001).
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, point lookups, grouping by note, and counting unique notes. Useful for joins when referencing specific notes.
business_questions: How many credit notes were issued in a given period (distinct credit_note_id)?
Show details for credit note CN17001.
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. It appears unique—use it as the primary key in queries (WHERE credit_note_id = 'CN17001'). Indexing this column improves point lookups. No casting required.

invoice_id.description: Identifier of the invoice against which this credit/debit note was issued (e.g., INV13001).
invoice_id.analysis_use: Join key to invoices to attribute credits to customers/orders; grouping by invoice to compute net adjustments; filtering to examine notes for a particular invoice or order cycle.
business_questions: List all credit notes issued against invoice INV13001.
What is the total credit value applied to a specific invoice or customer (after joining invoices to customers)?
invoice_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; use string equality for joins (e.g., JOIN b2b_invoices i ON cn.invoice_id = i.invoice_id). Consider indexing invoice_id for faster joins/filters. Watch for whitespace or case differences; trim values if necessary.

note_type.description: Indicates whether the record is a CREDIT or a DEBIT note.
note_type.analysis_use: Dimension for grouping and aggregation (sum/avg/ count by type). Filter to separate credits and debits and compute net adjustments.
business_questions: What is the total amount issued as CREDIT vs DEBIT in the last month?
How many debit notes were raised compared to credit notes?
note_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field with values like 'CREDIT' and 'DEBIT'. Use simple WHERE note_type = 'CREDIT' or GROUP BY note_type. Normalize case if inputs are inconsistent.

amount.description: Monetary value of the credit/debit note stored as a string (examples: '104.13', '1120.3699999999999').
amount.analysis_use: Primary numeric measure for aggregation (SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX). Required for financial totals, impact analysis, and rank by amount.
business_questions: What is the total value of credit notes in the last quarter?
Which credit notes are the largest (top N by amount)?
amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — always CAST/TRY_CAST to a numeric type (DECIMAL(18,2) or FLOAT) before aggregation: e.g., TRY_CAST(amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)). Be cautious of precision artifacts (many decimals). Normalize thousands separators or currency symbols if present. Use COALESCE/NULLIF to handle empty strings.
