reason.description: Categorical reason for issuing the note (Damage, Price Adjustment, Return).
reason.analysis_use: Dimension for grouping and segmentation to identify root causes and prioritize operational improvements. Useful in filters and pivot analyses.
business_questions: Which reason accounts for the highest total credit value?
How has the distribution of reasons changed over time?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality values; use WHERE reason = 'Damage' or GROUP BY reason. Consider normalizing values (trim/case) and treating unknown/blank as a separate category. Map to return records when reason = 'Return'.

issued_by.description: Name of the person who created/issued the credit note.
issued_by.analysis_use: Group or filter to see issuer activity (counts, sums), identify staff generating most credits, and detect anomalous patterns by issuer.
business_questions: Who issued the most credit notes by count and by total amount?
Are there issuers with unusually high average credit amounts?
issued_by.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar full names. Joins to personnel/user tables require name matching or a separate user id; prefer joining to a users table if present. Trim and standardize name formatting; consider splitting into identifiers if available elsewhere.

issued_at.description: Timestamp when the credit note was issued, stored as an nvarchar string (format appears 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
issued_at.analysis_use: Temporal filtering and grouping (daily/weekly/monthly trends), compute issuance rates, and calculate time-to-approval by diffing with approved_at.
business_questions: How many credit notes were issued per day/week/month?
What is the distribution of issuing activity during business hours?
issued_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — CAST/CONVERT to datetime (e.g., TRY_CAST(issued_at AS DATETIME) or use format 120 if needed) before date functions. Handle null/invalid strings with TRY_CAST and filter out unparsable rows. Normalize timezone if relevant.

approved_by.description: Name of the person who approved the credit note.
approved_by.analysis_use: Used for approval accountability, grouping by approver to find bottlenecks or outliers, and join to user tables if available.
business_questions: Who approves the most credit notes and what is the total value they approve?
Which approvers have the highest rate of voided notes after approval?
approved_by.nl2sql_hints: Similar considerations as issued_by: string name field, possible joins to user/approval audit tables, normalize whitespace/case, and handle nulls for unapproved notes.

approved_at.description: Timestamp when the credit note was approved, stored as an nvarchar string (format appears 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
approved_at.analysis_use: Calculate approval latency (approved_at - issued_at), identify overdue approvals, and trend approvals over time.
business_questions: What is the average time between issuance and approval of credit notes?
How many credit notes remain unapproved after X days?
approved_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime before arithmetic (e.g., DATEDIFF with TRY_CAST). Handle nulls for pending approvals. Make sure to use the same parsing logic as issued_at; watch for timezone or inconsistently formatted values.

related_return_id.description: Reference to a return record associated with this credit note (e.g., RET15025).
related_return_id.analysis_use: Join key to returns to reconcile credit amounts to returned items, attribute root causes to products/orders, and filter credits that are return-driven.
business_questions: How many credit notes are tied to product returns and what is the total credited amount?
List credit notes for return RET15025 and show their amounts and statuses.
related_return_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string join key to b2b_returns (or returns table). No declared FK — expect missing matches. Consider indexing if frequent joins. Normalize values before joining (trim/case).

status.description: Processing state of the credit note (VOID, APPLIED, ISSUED).
status.analysis_use: Filter by lifecycle state, compute effective applied credits vs voided, and track operational throughput (issued -> applied).
business_questions: What portion (count and amount) of credit notes are APPLIED vs VOID vs ISSUED?
How many issued notes never moved to APPLIED within 30 days?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical low-cardinality field — use as a WHERE filter or GROUP BY. Ensure normalization of values; treat unknowns consistently. Consider indexing for status-based reporting.

notes.description: Free-text notes field; currently 100% null in this dataset.
notes.analysis_use: When populated, used for qualitative context or free-form comments; could be searched for keywords (e.g., 'fraud', 'customer request'). With current data it's not useful.
business_questions: Are there notes explaining why certain high-value credits were issued?
Filter credit notes where notes contain the keyword 'dispute' (if populated).
notes.nl2sql_hints: Text field — consider full-text indexing if used for search. Because it's null for all rows here, ignore in numeric aggregations. Be defensive in NL2SQL: allow for NULL and empty-string checks.

address_id.description: Unique identifier for the address record (alphanumeric).
address_id.analysis_use: Use as the primary address identifier for filtering and joining; group by address_id to get address-level metrics; deduplication and uniqueness checks.
business_questions: Which orders were shipped to a specific address_id (after joining to orders/dispatches)?
How many unique addresses exist in the system and are any duplicated?
address_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — treat as a string in queries. Many rows are unique; consider adding/using as primary key in schema. When matching, use exact equality; trim/upper if inconsistent formatting is possible.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer owning or associated with the address (alphanumeric).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to customer and transactional tables; group by customer_id to count addresses; filter by customer to fetch all their addresses.
business_questions: How many addresses does customer CUST1001 have?
Which customers have addresses in city X?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — use as join key to b2b_customers and orders. Ensure consistent casing/whitespace. Consider indexing for faster joins. Expect one-to-many relationship (customer -> addresses).

addr_line1.description: Primary street address line (e.g., building number, street).
addr_line1.analysis_use: Used for display, text-based grouping/deduplication, and filtering (e.g., find addresses on a given street). Low cardinality of some values; useful for fuzzy matching and cleansing.
business_questions: Which addresses are on 'MG Road' or another specific street?
Are there duplicate addr_line1 values across different customers indicating shared locations?
addr_line1.nl2sql_hints: Text field — use LIKE, ILIKE (DB dependent) or full-text for fuzzy matches. Normalize whitespace and case in NL2SQL generation. Beware of abbreviations and variants when matching (e.g., 'Rd' vs 'Road').

addr_line2.description: Secondary address line (landmark or additional directions). Nullable and low-cardinality with many repeated values like landmarks.
addr_line2.analysis_use: Useful for delivery instructions and filtering for addresses with/without landmarks; can help cluster addresses by common landmarks.
business_questions: How many addresses include 'Near Bus Stop' as a landmark?
Which addresses lack addr_line2 and may need additional directions?
addr_line2.nl2sql_hints: Nullable text field — account for NULLs in filters (IS NULL / IS NOT NULL). Because values repeat, use exact matching for common landmarks. Trim and normalize text for reliable comparisons.

city.analysis_use: Common grouping/filtering field for aggregations, regional reporting, and routing. Low cardinality makes it efficient for GROUP BY and distribution queries.
business_questions: How many addresses are in Vadodara vs Gandhinagar?
Which cities contain customers with more than N addresses?
city.nl2sql_hints: Text field with limited distinct values — safe to GROUP BY. Normalize casing in NL2SQL. Consider mapping city names to canonical values if free-text variants exist.

state.description: State/province of the address. In this dataset, it is constant (Gujarat).
state.analysis_use: Useful for high-level regional filters and ensures compliance/regulatory segmentation. Because it is constant here, it has limited discriminative analytic value but still important for completeness.
business_questions: Are all customer addresses in the same state?
Filter addresses by state for region-specific analyses.
state.nl2sql_hints: Text field and currently constant. Still include in SELECTs for completeness. If expecting different states, treat as GROUP/BY filterable string.

pincode.description: Postal code (pincode) for the address, stored as text.
pincode.analysis_use: Key for delivery zone grouping, aggregation by postal areas, join/lookup to area-level metadata, and clustering addresses for delivery runs.
business_questions: How many addresses per pincode and which pincodes have the most customers?
Which pincodes correspond to late or failed deliveries (when joined with dispatch data)?
pincode.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — treat as string (preserve leading zeros if any). Good candidate for indexing and grouping. Normalize formatting (trim) before comparisons and joins to any pincode reference tables.

country.description: Country of the address. In this dataset, constant (India).
country.analysis_use: High-level filter and validation; useful for multi-country deployments but currently not discriminative.
business_questions: Are there any non-India addresses in the dataset?
Filter addresses by country for region-specific business rules.
country.nl2sql_hints: Text field and constant here. Include in WHERE clause only if relevant. When building NL2SQL, consider country as optional if always the same.

latitude.description: Latitude coordinate for the address, stored as text (nvarchar) with high precision.
latitude.analysis_use: Enable geospatial calculations after casting to numeric (distance calculations, clustering, mapping). Useful for routing, proximity searches and mapping addresses to service areas.
business_questions: Which addresses are within 10 km of a given warehouse (requires numeric casting and Haversine calculation)?
What is the nearest address to a given coordinate?
latitude.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast to numeric/float in SQL for calculations (e.g., CAST(latitude AS DECIMAL(12,8))). Be mindful of nulls and malformed strings; add TRY_CAST or equivalent where supported. For performance, consider storing numeric lat/long in dedicated numeric columns or a geospatial type and indexing them.

longitude.description: Longitude coordinate for the address, stored as text (nvarchar) with high precision.
longitude.analysis_use: Paired with latitude for geospatial queries (distance, bounding boxes, clustering). Required for map visualizations and route optimization.
business_questions: Which addresses fall inside a specific rectangular bounding box or cluster?
Which delivery stops are closest to each other for route planning?
longitude.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast to numeric/float for geospatial operations. Use TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC checks in NL2SQL to handle bad data. Prefer storing as native geospatial type for production NL2SQL systems to leverage indexes and spatial functions.

customer_id.description: Surrogate business identifier for each customer (string). Appears unique across rows in this snapshot (50 distinct values).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup key for joins to transactional tables (orders, invoices, payments, addresses). Filtering and grouping by customer for per-customer metrics. De-duplication and identity checks.
business_questions: Show invoices/orders/payments aggregated by customer_id.
Which customers have outstanding balances greater than X?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text in SQL predicates and joins. Use equality joins (customer_id = ...) and IN lists. Consider enforcing/changing to a proper PK index if available. NL2SQL should prefer inner joins to transactions when customer_id is present; allow LEFT JOIN if the presence is optional.

name.description: Customer display name or business name.
name.analysis_use: Human-readable label for reporting, grouping at a summarized level (e.g., top customers). Useful for fuzzy matching and de-duplication with other name variants.
business_questions: List top 10 customers by revenue with their names.
Are there likely duplicate customers (names with similar text)?
name.nl2sql_hints: Use for SELECT and ORDER BY, avoid grouping by raw name for large datasets (use customer_id as canonical key). Consider using LOWER/trim or fuzzy-matching functions in NL2SQL when user asks for 'customers named X' or approximate matches.

account_type.description: Categorical business classification (Retailer, Wholesale, Hotel/Catering, Exporter).
account_type.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering dimension for segmentation analysis and rollups (counts, avg credit, revenue when joined to transactions). Low cardinality -> good for GROUP BY and filter pushdown.
business_questions: How many customers and what is total credit exposure by account_type?
Compare monthly order volume for Retailer vs Wholesale customers.
account_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; exact match filters or IN lists (e.g., account_type = 'Retailer'). Map synonyms (e.g., 'hotel' -> 'Hotel/Catering') in NL2SQL layer. Because values are few, the query planner should push down filters easily.

gst_number.description: Tax identifier (GST) for the customer. Unique per customer in this dataset.
gst_number.analysis_use: Used for compliance reporting, uniqueness checks, and grouping customers by tax ID. Useful to detect duplicates and support invoicing rules.
business_questions: Which customers are missing a GST number?
Show transactions grouped by gst_number for tax reporting.
gst_number.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string, but often required for equality/exists checks. NL2SQL should support IS NULL checks and pattern matching (LIKE) for partial GST lookups. Avoid numeric casting.

primary_contact_name.description: Name of the main contact person at the customer.
primary_contact_name.analysis_use: Contact-level reporting, CRM tasks, and joins to activity logs. Useful for data quality and outreach lists.
business_questions: List customers and their primary contact names for outreach.
How many contacts are missing names?
primary_contact_name.nl2sql_hints: Use for SELECT and WHERE with equals or LIKE for partial name searches. Consider case-insensitive matching. Not reliable as a join key.

primary_contact_phone.description: Primary contact phone number (string, includes country code).
primary_contact_phone.analysis_use: Contactability checks, filtering customers with valid phone numbers, and integration for SMS/outbound dialing lists.
business_questions: How many customers have no phone number on record?
Export phone numbers for customers in Tier-1 segment.
primary_contact_phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; normalize/strip formatting if doing numeric comparisons. Use LIKE or regexp to validate patterns. NL2SQL rules should treat phone as contact attribute, not a join key.

email.analysis_use: Filtering for email campaigns, data quality checks (missing/invalid), and contact-based reporting. Can be used to detect duplicates across customers.
business_questions: List customers missing an email address.
Which customers have corporate (non-personal) email domains?
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text. Use pattern matching (LIKE '%@domain.com') and NULL checks. Consider case-insensitive comparisons. Avoid joining on email unless explicitly indicated.

billing_address_id.description: Pointer to the billing address (string id). Logical FK to b2b_customer_addresses table.
billing_address_id.analysis_use: Join key to fetch billing address details, grouping by address (e.g., city/state) for billing region analysis and tax jurisdiction checks.
business_questions: Show customers grouped by billing city/state.
Which customers have missing or identical billing and delivery addresses?
billing_address_id.nl2sql_hints: Use LEFT JOIN to b2b_customer_addresses on billing_address_id. Treat as string key; expect duplicates across addresses. NL2SQL should check for NULL and use IS NOT NULL when filtering for valid addresses.

delivery_address_id.description: Pointer to the delivery/shipping address (string id). Logical FK to b2b_customer_addresses.
delivery_address_id.analysis_use: Join for delivery performance, routing, and fulfillment analytics. Compare delivery and billing addresses to detect drop-shipping or consolidation opportunities.
business_questions: How many customers have different delivery and billing addresses?
Which delivery locations receive the highest order volume (after joining orders)?
delivery_address_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to b2b_customer_addresses. Use join conditions like delivery_address_id = addr.address_id. NL2SQL should allow comparing billing_address_id and delivery_address_id directly.

customer_segment.description: Tier classification (Tier-1, Tier-2, Tier-3) used for prioritization and segmentation.
customer_segment.analysis_use: Primary grouping for prioritized reporting, sampling, and segmentation-based quotas/discounts. Low cardinality beneficial for grouping and filter pushdown.
business_questions: Distribution of customers across segments and associated average credit_limit.
Segment-based performance: revenue per customer in each tier (join to sales).
customer_segment.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Support exact matches and IN lists. Map synonyms (e.g., 'Tier 1' -> 'Tier-1') in preprocessing. Good candidate for WHERE/GROUP BY in NL2SQL templates.

credit_limit.description: Customer credit limit stored as nvarchar (numeric values like decimals).
credit_limit.analysis_use: Numeric aggregations (sum, avg, max) for exposure calculations and credit risk. Compare against outstanding balances from invoices/payments to compute utilization.
business_questions: Total credit limit exposure across all customers and by account_type.
Which customers have credit_limit below/above a threshold?
credit_limit.nl2sql_hints: Must be CAST/CONVERT to numeric (DECIMAL) before aggregation or comparison. NL2SQL should generate SAFE_CAST to avoid errors on malformed values and handle NULL/empty strings. Consider trimming and replacing commas prior to cast.

payment_terms_days.description: Number of days for payment terms, stored as nvarchar but represents small integer values (e.g., 7, 15, 30, 45, 60).
payment_terms_days.analysis_use: Grouping for AR aging policies, credit policy enforcement, and filtering customers by payment terms. Useful to compute due dates when joined with invoice date.
business_questions: How many customers are on 30-day payment terms vs 45-day?
Average payment_terms_days by account_type or segment.
payment_terms_days.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to integer for numeric filters and grouping. NL2SQL should allow exact numeric comparisons and handle non-numeric/missing values gracefully (NULL or fallback).

assigned_sales_rep_id.description: Identifier of the sales representative assigned to the customer (string).
assigned_sales_rep_id.analysis_use: Join key to b2b_sales_agents to get rep names and territories. Used to compute rep-level KPIs (customer counts, revenue, credit exposure).
business_questions: How many customers and total credit_limit are assigned to each sales rep?
Which sales reps have the highest number of Tier-1 customers?
assigned_sales_rep_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string join key. NL2SQL should allow grouping by assigned_sales_rep_id and joining to b2b_sales_agents on exact match. Because most values are unique-ish, consider recommending an index on this column for joins.

created_at.description: Customer creation timestamp stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
created_at.analysis_use: Time-based analyses: cohort creation by month/week/day, growth rate, onboarding lag. Trend analysis when joined to transactions to build customer lifetime metrics.
business_questions: Number of new customers created each month.
How long between customer creation and first order?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse/cast to proper DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (STR_TO_DATE or CAST depending on DB). NL2SQL should normalize timezone assumptions if present and support date_bucket/grouping (MONTH(created_at), DATE(created_at)). Use safe parsing to handle malformed strings.

dispatch_id.description: Unique identifier for a dispatch event (external business ID, e.g., DISP11001).
dispatch_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, grouping by dispatch, counting dispatches, joining with external logs. Can be used to detect duplicate inserts or missing updates.
business_questions: How many dispatch records exist for a given date range?
Are there duplicate dispatch_id values or multiple dispatches for the same order?
dispatch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string identifier. If expected to be unique, prefer using it as primary key in schema (index). When constructing NL2SQL, map 'dispatch id' queries to exact equality filters (WHERE dispatch_id = '...') and use for joins when external logs reference it.

order_id.description: Business order identifier associated with the dispatch (e.g., B2BORD4001).
order_id.analysis_use: Key join column to b2b_orders for linking order details, customer, order totals; grouping by order to aggregate freight or status; filtering by order to fetch dispatch events.
business_questions: Which orders have been dispatched but not delivered?
What is the total freight charged for a particular order or set of orders?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; commonly used to join to b2b_orders. NL2SQL should prefer exact matches and support IN lists. Add an index on order_id for faster joins/filters.

vehicle_id.description: Identifier of the vehicle used for the dispatch (e.g., VH106).
vehicle_id.analysis_use: Group by vehicle to compute counts, freight handled, delays per vehicle; filter to investigate vehicle-specific incidents.
business_questions: Which vehicles handle the most dispatches or freight value?
Which vehicles have the highest rate of delayed deliveries?
vehicle_id.nl2sql_hints: String token often used in equality/group-by. If referenced in a fleet table, NL2SQL should join on vehicle_id. Consider normalizing to a vehicle dimension for richer queries.

driver_id.description: Identifier for the driver assigned to the dispatch (e.g., DR104).
driver_id.analysis_use: Used to attribute performance metrics (on-time rate, freight delivered) to drivers; group/filter by driver to find outliers.
business_questions: Which drivers have the highest number of late deliveries?
What freight value is each driver responsible for over a period?
driver_id.nl2sql_hints: String identifier; use equality/group-by. If driver metadata exists, join to driver table for names and contact info. Ensure driver_id comparisons are exact (no fuzzy matching).

carrier_partner_id.description: Identifier for the carrier or logistics partner (e.g., LOCAL_TRUCKERS, PRIVATE_LOGISTICS).
carrier_partner_id.analysis_use: Categorical aggregation: compare cost, delivery performance, and volume across carriers. Good for contracting and vendor KPIs because cardinality is low.
business_questions: Which carrier partner has the best on-time delivery percentage?
How much freight cost is being spent per carrier partner?
carrier_partner_id.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; ideal for GROUP BY. NL2SQL should treat this as a discrete dimension; consider mapping to vendor tables for enrichment. Be careful with variations in naming; canonicalize before analysis.

scheduled_dispatch_ts.description: Scheduled dispatch timestamp stored as NVARCHAR in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
scheduled_dispatch_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for planned scheduling analysis: compute scheduled vs actual delay, bin by date/hour/day-of-week, filter ranges for time-window queries.
business_questions: How many dispatches were scheduled per day/hour?
What is the average scheduled lead time in a given period?
scheduled_dispatch_ts.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as string, format is ISO-like; cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons, ordering and time arithmetic (e.g., CAST(scheduled_dispatch_ts AS DATETIME)). NL2SQL should detect and cast before doing >,< or date_trunc operations. Indexing as proper datetime column improves performance.

actual_dispatch_ts.description: Actual dispatch timestamp (when the dispatch occurred) stored as NVARCHAR 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'.
actual_dispatch_ts.analysis_use: Used to compute delays (actual - scheduled), delivery timelines, throughput metrics and SLA compliance; filter by actual dispatch windows.
business_questions: What is the average delay between scheduled and actual dispatch?
Which dispatches occurred during peak hours?
actual_dispatch_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME for arithmetic (e.g., DATEDIFF). NL2SQL should support implicit casting or include CAST operations. Consider indexing if frequent time-range filters are used.

seal_no.description: Seal number affixed to the shipment (security/consignment seal).
seal_no.analysis_use: Quality control and audit (detect missing/duplicate seals), join to inspection/quality tables if available, filter to find consignments requiring investigation.
business_questions: Which dispatches are missing a seal number?
Are there duplicate seal numbers across dispatches?
seal_no.nl2sql_hints: String identifier; equality and uniqueness checks common. NL2SQL should treat blank/null values explicitly. Consider indexing if used to validate frequently.

dispatch_status.description: Dispatch lifecycle status (categorical): SCHEDULED, IN_TRANSIT, DELIVERED.
dispatch_status.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate to compute counts and proportions per status; used in dashboards, SLA reporting and alerts.
business_questions: What proportion of dispatches are currently IN_TRANSIT vs DELIVERED?
How many dispatches are SCHEDULED for a given date?
dispatch_status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical string; ideal for GROUP BY and WHERE filters. NL2SQL should treat status comparisons as exact string matches; consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'delivered' lowercase) when parsing natural language.

freight_amount.description: Freight charge amount for the dispatch stored as NVARCHAR representing decimals (e.g., '1097.49').
freight_amount.analysis_use: Monetary aggregation (SUM freight), average freight per order/carrier/driver, outlier detection. Must be cast to numeric/decimal for accurate calculations.
business_questions: What is total freight spend by carrier or in a time period?
Which dispatches exceed a freight cost threshold?
freight_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—NL2SQL should CAST(freight_amount AS DECIMAL) before SUM/AVG or numeric comparisons. Be mindful of localization (decimal separators). Add numeric column or computed column to avoid repeated casting.

tracking_no.description: Logistics tracking number for the dispatch (carrier tracking reference).
tracking_no.analysis_use: Join key to detailed tracking events, filter to find tracking-less dispatches, group for tracking status summaries.
business_questions: Which dispatches do not have tracking numbers assigned?
Show tracking history for a given tracking number (via tracking events join).
tracking_no.nl2sql_hints: String identifier; used for joins to shipment-tracking tables. NL2SQL should support exact match and pattern searches (LIKE) when users ask for partial tracking numbers.

created_at.description: Timestamp when this dispatch record was created in the system stored as NVARCHAR 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit trail, data-entry lag analysis (created_at vs scheduled), filtering by ingestion/creation time, identifying late data entries.
business_questions: When were dispatch records created relative to the scheduled dispatch?
How many dispatch records were created by day/by creator?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME for temporal queries. Distinguish created_at (data insertion time) from scheduled_dispatch_ts (business planned time). Useful for queries like 'created in last 7 days'—ensure proper casting.

remarks.description: Free text remarks or handling instructions (e.g., 'Keep refrigerated', 'Fragile'). Low-cardinality in sample.
remarks.analysis_use: Categorical grouping of special-handling requirements, filter to find perishable/fragile items, quality and compliance reporting.
business_questions: How many dispatches require 'Keep refrigerated' handling this week?
What special handling instructions are most common for delayed deliveries?
remarks.nl2sql_hints: Free-text field but shows repeated phrases—NL2SQL can map common phrases to categories. Use exact match or full-text/LIKE depending on requirement. Normalize or create a handling_type column for consistent querying.

created_by.description: User who created the dispatch record (human-readable name).
created_by.analysis_use: Audit and accountability: group by creator to measure activity, detect anomalous creators, link to user metadata for contact/role.
business_questions: Which user created the most dispatch records in the last month?
Are there creators with higher rates of missing data or corrections?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string name; prefer joining to a users table (if present) via a stable user_id instead of name to avoid ambiguity. NL2SQL should support both exact name filters and fuzzy searches for natural language queries.

event_uuid.description: Unique identifier for each event message (string).
event_uuid.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, counting unique events, dedup joins, and correlation when tracing retries or reprocessed messages.
business_questions: How many distinct events were ingested in the time window?
Are there duplicate event_uuid values indicating duplicate deliveries?
event_uuid.nl2sql_hints: Treat as unique key; consider creating a primary key or unique index. In queries, use event_uuid for exact equality joins and deduplication (GROUP BY event_uuid). No cast required.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the event occurred in source system, stored as text in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' pattern.
event_ts.analysis_use: Filter and group events by occurrence time, compute latency (event_ts → processed_at), build time series and windowed aggregations.
business_questions: How many events occurred per hour/day by event_type?
Which events were generated outside business hours?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to native datetime in queries (e.g., CAST(event_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST) for accurate time predicates and grouping. Use date_trunc/DATE() after casting for aggregates. Watch for timezone considerations if present.

aggregate_type.description: High-level domain type the event relates to (inventory, order, shipment).
aggregate_type.analysis_use: Primary filter or group key to separate event domains. Useful for routing, partitioning logic, and targeted metrics per domain.
business_questions: What volume of order vs shipment vs inventory events are produced daily?
Is one aggregate_type generating disproportionate processing failures?
aggregate_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality: good candidate for filtered indexes or partitioning predicates. Use for conditional joins (JOIN to orders table only when aggregate_type='order'). Consider normalizing values or mapping synonyms in NL2SQL (e.g., 'inventory' ↔ INVENTORY_UPDATED).

aggregate_id.description: Identifier of the domain entity the event concerns (e.g., order id, shipment id, inventory id).
aggregate_id.analysis_use: Join key to domain tables to enrich events, group by entity to find hot entities, deduplicate multiple events for same aggregate_id.
business_questions: Which orders (aggregate_id) had the most related events in the last week?
Are there particular aggregate_ids that repeatedly fail processing?
aggregate_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; when joining to domain tables, ensure type alignment (e.g., CAST aggregate_id to domain id type if needed). Use aggregate_type to disambiguate target table for joins. Consider indexing aggregate_id for fast lookups.

event_type.description: Specific event action name (INVENTORY_UPDATED, SHIPMENT_CREATED, ORDER_CREATED).
event_type.analysis_use: Filter and group by specific event actions for event-level metrics, funnel analyses, and triggering downstream processes.
business_questions: How many ORDER_CREATED events were generated per day?
Are INVENTORY_UPDATED events correlated with downstream shipment events?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality; treat as categorical filter. Use exact string matches. Normalize queries to account for case or synonyms if user language varies.

payload_json.description: Payload body of the event as JSON text; currently contains '{}' for all rows.
payload_json.analysis_use: When populated, use JSON extraction to enrich metrics or filter by payload fields. Currently not useful (empty) but reserved for future enrichment.
business_questions: Which events contain order line details in payload for downstream processing?
Can we extract customer_id or product_id from payload_json for enrichment?
payload_json.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, treat as JSON when populated. Use JSON_VALUE/JSON_EXTRACT functions supported by the DB. Guard against empty/invalid JSON (payload_json='{}'). Avoid parsing on every row unless payload is non-empty; add a WHERE payload_json <> '{}' filter to reduce cost.

source_system.description: Origin system of the event (B2B_PORTAL, ERP).
source_system.analysis_use: Segment events by producer to measure system reliability, latency, and volumes per system. Useful for SLA tracking and source-specific routing.
business_questions: Which source_system produces more failed/unprocessed events?
What is the average processing latency for events from ERP vs B2B_PORTAL?
source_system.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality; good for GROUP BY and WHERE filters. Consider joining to an ingestion metadata table keyed by source_system for richer context. Normalize values if synonyms exist.

processed_flag.description: Indicator whether the event has been processed by downstream consumers ('1'/'0' stored as text).
processed_flag.analysis_use: Compute processing completion rates, identify backlogs and unprocessed events, and trigger reprocessing logic.
business_questions: How many events remain unprocessed today?
What fraction of events are processed within X minutes of creation?
processed_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean; CAST to INT/BOOLEAN in queries for numeric aggregation (SUM/CNT). Use WHERE processed_flag = '0' to find unprocessed. Consider normalizing to true boolean type in schema for easier NL2SQL.

processed_at.description: Timestamp when the event was processed, stored as text in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
processed_at.analysis_use: Compute processing latency (processed_at - event_ts), analyze processing time distributions, and detect delayed processing or backfills.
business_questions: What is the median time-to-process events by event_type?
Which events were processed outside expected SLAs (e.g., > 1 hour)?
processed_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for latency calculations and time windowing. Many rows may have NULL/empty processed_at for unprocessed events — handle with COALESCE or IS NOT NULL checks. Use TRY_CAST to avoid errors on malformed values.

retry_count.description: Number of times processing has been retried for this event, stored as text but representing small integers.
retry_count.analysis_use: Measure processing resilience, identify flapping events and entities that cause repeated retries, and set thresholds for alerts/poison-pill handling.
business_questions: Which events have retry_count > 3 indicating persistent failures?
What is the distribution of retry_count by source_system or event_type?
retry_count.nl2sql_hints: CAST to INT for numeric aggregates and thresholds. Because domain of values is small, consider histogram buckets (0,1,2,3+) in NL2SQL. Use retry_count in WHERE clauses to filter heavy-retry events.

partition_key.description: Partitioning label (pk1/pk2) used for physical partitioning or routing in ingestion pipeline.
partition_key.analysis_use: Partition-level counts for performance diagnostics, targeted reprocessing, and efficient queries using partition pruning.
business_questions: Which partition contains most failed events or retries?
Are partitions balanced in event volume across days?
partition_key.nl2sql_hints: Use partition_key in predicates to exploit partition pruning. Treat as a small categorical filter. Ensure NL2SQL maps user-level partition requests to these keys only when intended (avoid exposing internal partitioning details unless necessary).

created_at.description: Timestamp when the event row was created in this table (ingestion time), stored as text.
created_at.analysis_use: Monitor ingestion delays (created_at vs event_ts), identify late-arriving events, and support retention/cleanup queries based on ingestion time.
business_questions: How many events arrived more than 24 hours after event_ts?
What is the daily ingestion volume (by created_at)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons and windowing. Use in data retention queries (DELETE or archive older than X days) and when correlating with processing times. If created_at ≠ event_ts, surface both in latency calculations.

invoice_id.description: Unique identifier for each invoice (nvarchar). Appears to uniquely identify rows (50 distinct values).
invoice_id.analysis_use: Primary key for invoice-level aggregations, de-duplication and joins to payments/credit notes. Useful for selecting single-invoice details and linking to external tables.
business_questions: Retrieve full details for a specific invoice_id.
How many payments/adjustments are linked to this invoice?
invoice_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key for joins. Consider promoting to primary key or unique index. When matching, use exact equality; trim whitespace. Ensure engine uses same collation and case-sensitivity as joined tables.

order_id.description: Identifier for the related order that generated the invoice (nvarchar).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders and order_lines to attribute invoice totals to orders, compute order-level revenue vs invoiced amount, and analyze order-to-invoice mapping.
business_questions: Which orders are invoiced more than once or missing invoices?
Aggregate invoice_amount by order or find orders with discrepancies vs order totals.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use exact equality to join to b2b_orders.order_id. Cast/trim both sides if whitespace or type mismatches exist. Be cautious: one-to-many relationships may exist (credit notes, partial invoices).

invoice_number.description: Human/business-facing invoice number (nvarchar) often used in reports and communications.
invoice_number.analysis_use: Filtering and display in reports; join key for external systems/human lookups; grouping if invoice numbering encodes series or year.
business_questions: Find invoice by invoice_number used in external communication.
Count invoices per invoice series/prefix (if invoice_number encodes series).
invoice_number.nl2sql_hints: String comparisons important; support LIKE or prefix matching for series. Normalize formatting (leading zeros, dashes). When joining to external systems, ensure exact match and consistent formatting.

invoice_date.description: Date/time invoice was issued, stored as nvarchar in YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS format.
invoice_date.analysis_use: Primary temporal field for time-series revenue, period-based grouping, aging and month/quarter analysis. Used for filtering date ranges and cohorting.
business_questions: Total invoiced value by month using invoice_date.
How many invoices were issued last quarter?
invoice_date.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to DATE/TIMESTAMP in NL2SQL queries (e.g., TRY_CAST(invoice_date AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Accept both equality and range predicates. Support truncation to month/quarter for grouping. Handle nulls and malformed strings using TRY_ functions to avoid errors.

due_date.description: Date by which invoice payment is due, stored as nvarchar.
due_date.analysis_use: Used for accounts receivable aging, overdue calculations (current date > due_date), and days-to-pay metrics (when paired with payment date).
business_questions: How many invoices are past due as of today?
What is the distribution of days-to-due across invoices?
due_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP for arithmetic. Use DATE_DIFF or equivalent to compute aging. Handle empty/NULLs and invalid formats with TRY_CAST. Range filters perform better on typed columns.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer being invoiced (nvarchar).
customer_id.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregating revenue by customer, joining to customer master for segmentation, computing AR per customer and top customers by invoice_amount.
business_questions: Which customers contributed the most invoice_amount in the period?
Which customers have the most overdue invoice value?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to b2b_customers.customer_id. Trim and ensure consistent formatting. Consider indexing for frequent group-by queries. Beware of customers with multiple IDs; map to canonical id if needed.

taxable_value.description: Invoice taxable base amount (stored as nvarchar representing decimal values).
taxable_value.analysis_use: Aggregate taxable base for tax reports and revenue calculations before taxes. Used in reconciliations with order line totals.
business_questions: What is total taxable_value by month or customer?
How much of revenue is taxable vs non-taxable (if applicable)?
taxable_value.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., TRY_CAST(taxable_value AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle inconsistent precision and malformed values. Use COALESCE/ISNULL for missing values in aggregations.

igst.description: Integrated GST amount (nvarchar). In this snapshot it is constant '0', indicating no IGST applied.
igst.analysis_use: Included in tax composition; typically aggregated to compute total IGST collected (often zero for intra-state B2B in dataset).
business_questions: How much IGST was charged over the period?
Are there invoices with IGST > 0 (cross-state sales)?
igst.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric if used in calculations but confirm constants to avoid unnecessary casts. If always zero, queries can skip this field or treat as constant. Use TRY_CAST and handle NULLs.

cgst.description: Central GST component charged on the invoice (nvarchar decimal).
cgst.analysis_use: Aggregate for tax reporting, compute tax composition (cgst+sgst+igst), and reconcile total_tax.
business_questions: Total CGST collected by month/quarter?
Which invoices have unusually high CGST relative to taxable_value?
cgst.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for SUM and comparisons. Validate that cgst + sgst + igst equals total_tax (use TRY_CAST to avoid exceptions). Account for rounding differences when comparing.

sgst.description: State GST component charged on invoice (nvarchar decimal).
sgst.analysis_use: Same uses as cgst: tax reporting and reconciliation. Useful to split tax by jurisdiction.
business_questions: Total SGST collected by state (when joined to customer address)?
What is the average SGST per invoice?
sgst.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric for aggregation. When joining to location tables, ensure customer address/state mapping exists. Use rounding-aware comparisons when reconciling totals.

total_tax.description: Sum of tax components on invoice (nvarchar decimal).
total_tax.analysis_use: Aggregate to report total tax liability, validate vs components, and compute net revenue (invoice_amount - total_tax) if required.
business_questions: What is total tax liability for the period?
Are there invoices where total_tax does not equal sum(cgst, sgst, igst)?
total_tax.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC. When checking component sums, use tolerance for floating rounding (ABS(sum_components - CAST(total_tax AS DECIMAL)) < 0.01). Use TRY_CAST and NULL-safe arithmetic.

invoice_amount.description: Total amount billed on invoice including taxes (nvarchar decimal).
invoice_amount.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for aggregations, KPIs, ranking customers, and AR ageing value calculations.
business_questions: Total invoiced amount by period, customer, or status?
Which invoices contribute to 80% of revenue (Pareto analysis)?
invoice_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to DECIMAL for SUM, AVG, ORDER BY. Use appropriate precision (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)). When filtering by ranges, ensure conversion to numeric is applied to parameter or column consistently to avoid full table scans; better to store as numeric in schema.

status.description: Invoice lifecycle status (PAID, ISSUED, OVERDUE) stored as nvarchar low-cardinality field.
status.analysis_use: Filter/group AR by status, compute outstanding balances (sum of invoice_amount for OVERDUE or ISSUED), and measure collection performance (PAID ratio).
business_questions: What is the value and count of invoices in each status?
How has the proportion of OVERDUE invoices changed over time?
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string for equality filters. Normalize case and trim whitespace. Consider mapping to enum or small lookup table for robust NL2SQL parsing and faster filters. Use IN (...) for multi-status filters.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp (nvarchar). May mirror invoice_date or reflect ingestion time.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ETL troubleshooting, time-based filtering of newly created records, and comparing created_at vs invoice_date to detect late ingestion or backdated invoices.
business_questions: How many invoices were created in the last 7 days?
Are there invoices where created_at is significantly later than invoice_date?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for range queries and ordering. Distinguish semantic difference between created_at and invoice_date when generating NL2SQL (user intent: issue date vs database insert date). Use TRY_CAST and handle malformed strings.

snapshot_id.description: Identifier for the snapshot record (string). Appears to uniquely identify each row in current sample.
snapshot_id.analysis_use: Record-level identity for deduplication, tracking when a snapshot was created or referenced; not used for numeric aggregation. Useful when comparing multiple snapshots for the same date/centre if duplicates exist.
business_questions: Are there duplicate snapshots for the same centre/date (compare snapshot_id counts vs centre+date)?
Which snapshot IDs correspond to yesterday's KPI export?
snapshot_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. When checking uniqueness use COUNT(DISTINCT snapshot_id). If enforcing PK/uniqueness, consider ALTER TABLE to set as primary key. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace. Use equality joins on snapshot_id only if you expect it to be globally unique.

centre_id.description: Fulfilment centre identifier (string), low cardinality (~4 centres).
centre_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering dimension for most queries (group by centre_id, filter to single centre, aggregate across centres). Join key to centre metadata in other tables.
business_questions: Which centre has the highest average gross_sales over the last month?
How do delivery performance metrics (on_time_pct, avg_delivery_time_min) vary by centre?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a categorical filter or GROUP BY column. Index or partitioning by centre_id+date is recommended for frequent queries. Normalization: ensure case/whitespace consistency when joining to other tables (trim/upper/lower if necessary).

date.description: Snapshot date stored as string in ISO-like format (yyyy-mm-dd) for most rows.
date.analysis_use: Time dimension for trend analysis (group by date, filter by date range). Cast to DATE/DATE/TIMESTAMP type for date arithmetic, range filtering, and time-based window functions.
business_questions: What are the weekly/monthly trends of total_orders and gross_sales?
On which dates did on_time_pct drop below a threshold (e.g., 70%)?
date.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to DATE before comparisons (e.g., CAST(date AS DATE) or PARSE_DATE depending on dialect). Use inclusive/exclusive ranges carefully. If date values include time or inconsistent formats, apply robust parsing and fallbacks. When writing NL2SQL, recognize 'last N days' or 'between X and Y' and translate to date casts.

total_orders.description: Total number of orders captured in the snapshot (stored as string, small integer-like values).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate/count metric: SUM/AVG over time, rate calculations per centre/day, correlation against gross_sales and total_kg. Also filter for days with low/high order volume.
business_questions: How many orders did Centre CTR_VDR_001 process last week?
Which date had the maximum total_orders across all centres in the dataset?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for numeric operations (CAST(total_orders AS INT) or TRY_CAST). Handle NULL/empty strings and non-numeric gracefully (use TRY/SAFE_CAST). When aggregating, prefer SUM(CAST(... AS INT)). Beware of string-sorting vs numeric-sorting when ordering results.

total_kg.description: Total weight (kilograms) moved/processed in the snapshot (string representing decimal values).
total_kg.analysis_use: Key numeric metric for volume-based KPIs; used in SUM/AVG, kg per order calculations (total_kg / total_orders), trend analysis, and correlating weight to revenue.
business_questions: What is the total weight moved by each centre this month?
What is the average kg per order per centre?
total_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(total_kg AS DECIMAL(12,3))). Use TRY_CAST/SAFE_CAST to avoid failures on malformed strings. For kg-per-order, protect against division by zero (NULLIF(total_orders,0)).

gross_sales.description: Gross sales value for the snapshot (string representing monetary decimal).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Monetary KPI for revenue reporting; used in SUM, AVG, month-to-date, centre comparisons, and revenue per kg/order calculations.
business_questions: Which centre produced the highest gross_sales in October 2025?
What is the gross_sales per kg for each centre averaged over the last 30 days?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC with appropriate precision (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)). Handle thousands separators or currency symbols if present (clean strings first). For money aggregations use SUM(CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(...))). Consider currency normalization if multi-currency exists (not evident here).

returns_kg.description: Weight of returns (kilograms) recorded on that day (string decimal).
returns_kg.analysis_use: Monitor return volumes and changes over time; compute return rate relative to total_kg or total_orders; identify problematic dates/centres.
business_questions: Which dates had highest returns_kg for CTR_AHD_001?
What percentage of total_kg were returned on a given day?
returns_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL. Compute rates using returns_kg / NULLIF(total_kg,0). Use TRY_CAST to handle malformed values. Consider threshold filters (e.g., returns_kg > x) after numeric cast.

wastage_kg.description: Weight lost to wastage in kilograms (string decimal).
wastage_kg.analysis_use: Track spoilage/wastage trends, compute wastage rate (wastage_kg/total_kg), and identify operational issues in centres.
business_questions: What is the wastage rate (%) this month for each centre?
Are there days with unusually high wastage_kg that need investigation?
wastage_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric type. Use NULLIF(total_kg,0) for rate computations. Aggregate with SUM and use per-centre/per-day groupings. Clean any non-numeric text before casting.

avg_delivery_time_min.description: Average delivery time in minutes (string, small integer-like).
avg_delivery_time_min.analysis_use: Performance metric for delivery speed; group by centre/date for SLA monitoring, compute averages over windows, compare against targets or percentiles.
business_questions: What is the 7-day rolling average delivery time for each centre?
Which centre consistently meets the target average delivery time of X minutes?
avg_delivery_time_min.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INT or SMALLINT for aggregations (CAST(avg_delivery_time_min AS INT)). Handle non-numeric or extreme outliers. Use window functions for rolling averages once cast to numeric. Watch for text values like 'NA' — use TRY_CAST or filter them out.

on_time_pct.description: On-time delivery percentage (string representing decimal percent, e.g., '70.34').
on_time_pct.analysis_use: Service-level metric for on-time performance; used in averages, threshold-based alerts, and centre comparisons. Can be used to compute counts of days below SLA.
business_questions: How often does each centre exceed 75% on-time delivery in a month?
What is the average on_time_pct across centres last quarter?
on_time_pct.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL(5,2) or FLOAT. Interpret as percent already (70.34 -> 70.34%). For calculations converting to fraction use on_time_pct / 100. Handle precision differences and use TRY_CAST/SAFE_CAST for robustness.

created_at.description: Timestamp string when the snapshot row was created (e.g., '2025-11-02 09:36:36').
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/ingestion timing: helps determine staleness, identify latest snapshot per date/centre, filter by ingestion window, and debug data refresh issues.
business_questions: When was the latest snapshot created for CTR_RJT_001?
How long after the snapshot date are rows typically created?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME for time comparisons (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Use MAX(created_at) to find most recent snapshot. When comparing created_at vs date, ensure timezone handling if present. Use TRY_CAST to avoid failures on malformed strings.

notes.description: Free text notes field (currently 100% NULL in sample).
notes.analysis_use: Ad-hoc annotations or exceptions; rarely used for aggregations. Useful for human-readable comments about snapshot anomalies or corrections when present.
business_questions: Are there any manual notes recorded for snapshots in the selected period?
Which snapshots have audit notes indicating corrected data?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as optional string; filter IS NOT NULL to find annotated rows. Because current sample is entirely NULL, anticipate sparse usage. When used in NL2SQL, allow queries for 'has notes' or keyword search using LIKE or full-text if enabled.

allocation_id.description: Unique identifier for the allocation event/record.
allocation_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier when deduplicating or selecting single allocation rows; grouping by allocation_id isn't typically necessary but useful for tracing. Can be used to identify allocation history when combined with timestamps.
business_questions: What is the allocation record for a specific allocation_id?
Are there duplicate allocation_id values (data integrity check)?
allocation_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Often unique per row — can be used in WHERE clauses for direct retrieval. No numeric casting required. Consider indexing if queries frequently filter by allocation_id.

order_id.description: Identifier of the B2B order associated with the allocation.
order_id.analysis_use: Group or filter allocations by order to compute per-order allocated quantities and to join with orders table for customer/date/context attributes.
business_questions: How much inventory (kg/units) is allocated per order?
Which orders have active allocations versus released allocations?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; used as a natural join to b2b_orders(order_id). Cast not required. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace or case-sensitivity if joining across systems. Prefer exact equality filters (WHERE order_id = 'B2BORD4001').

order_line_id.description: Identifier for the order line (specific line item) the allocation maps to.
order_line_id.analysis_use: Important for fine-grained joins to b2b_order_lines to compare ordered quantity vs allocated quantity and to discover multiple allocations per line.
business_questions: Which order lines are under-allocated or over-allocated (allocated vs ordered quantity)?
Which order lines have multiple allocations or allocation adjustments?
order_line_id.nl2sql_hints: String field used to join to b2b_order_lines(order_line_id). Because distinct < rowcount, expect repeats; aggregate allocations by order_line_id. Consider casting/cleaning if white-space issues. Use joins rather than substring matching.

inventory_id.description: Identifier for the inventory item (location/SKU instance) reserved for the allocation.
inventory_id.analysis_use: Group by inventory_id to find hotspots, join to inventory or product tables for SKU-level reporting, and calculate inventory strain by allocated quantities.
business_questions: Which inventory items have the highest allocated quantities?
Are allocations concentrated on specific inventory locations or SKUs?
inventory_id.nl2sql_hints: String field — join to product_inventory or inventory_items(inventory_id) to retrieve product metadata. Normalize joins to exact equality. Consider indexing inventory_id for inventory-centric queries.

allocated_qty_kg.description: Allocated quantity expressed in kilograms, stored as a string (decimal).
allocated_qty_kg.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (SUM, AVG), comparisons against order line required kg, and trend/percentile calculations after casting to numeric/decimal.
business_questions: What is the total allocated kg across all allocations or per inventory/product?
What is the average allocation size in kg and how does it vary by allocator or status?
allocated_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal (e.g., CAST(allocated_qty_kg AS decimal(18,4))) before aggregation or numeric comparisons. Watch for non-numeric or null values. Preserve precision due to long decimal strings. Use safe casting with TRY_CAST or equivalent to avoid failures.

allocated_qty_units.description: Allocated quantity expressed in units (integer-like), stored as a string.
allocated_qty_units.analysis_use: Aggregate counts (SUM) or counts per order/inventory; used alongside allocated_qty_kg for multi-metric allocation reporting.
business_questions: How many units are currently allocated by product or order?
Are there inconsistencies between unit counts and kg allocations for the same SKU?
allocated_qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer (e.g., CAST(allocated_qty_units AS INT)) for SUM/ORDER BY. Use TRY_CAST to handle malformed strings. Treat NULL or empty strings as zero or exclude depending on business rule.

allocated_at.description: Timestamp when the allocation was created, stored as an nvarchar in ISO-like format.
allocated_at.analysis_use: Filter allocations by time windows, compute allocation lead times, order of allocations, and time-based trends. Useful for time series grouping after casting to datetime.
business_questions: When were allocations created (per day/week) and how has allocation volume changed over time?
Which allocations were created within a specific SLA window or during an outage?
allocated_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse/cast to proper datetime using appropriate function (e.g., CAST or CONVERT to datetime). Validate format consistency (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). Index or partition on parsed timestamp for large datasets. Use timezone awareness if required by downstream joins.

allocated_by.description: Name/identifier of the person or system that made the allocation.
allocated_by.analysis_use: Group or filter by allocator to measure productivity, errors, or allocation patterns per user/system; identify high-volume allocators or unusual behavior.
business_questions: Which allocators create the most allocations or allocate the most quantity?
Are certain allocators associated with a higher proportion of 'Shortage' reasons?
allocated_by.nl2sql_hints: String field; watch for inconsistent naming (case, spelling). May need normalization/join to user table if available (e.g., pos_users or b2b_portal_api_clients). Use exact matches or normalized lower() comparisons if necessary.

expiry_ts.description: Timestamp when the allocation expires (reservation window ends), stored as string.
expiry_ts.analysis_use: Filter allocations that will expire in a time window, compute time-to-expiry, and identify allocations that should be manually extended or released.
business_questions: Which allocations will expire within the next X hours/days and how much quantity will be freed?
What is the distribution of time-to-expiry for active allocations?
expiry_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons. Handle null or missing expiry values. Be careful with time zone semantics. Use safe casting (TRY_CAST) and consistent formats to avoid parsing errors.

status.description: Current state of the allocation (e.g., ACTIVE or RELEASED).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational vs historical allocations; group and aggregate quantities by status to determine reserved vs available inventory impacts.
business_questions: What proportion and total quantity of allocations are ACTIVE vs RELEASED?
How quickly do allocations move from ACTIVE to RELEASED after expiry?
status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; use equality filters (WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'). Consider mapping to boolean or enum in downstream models. Use status in combination with release_ts/expiry_ts to infer state transitions (e.g., status='RELEASED' and release_ts not null).

reason.description: Reason for the allocation (e.g., Normal allocation, Shortage).
reason.analysis_use: Categorize allocations for root-cause analysis — quantify shortage-driven allocations vs normal allocations and correlate with product or allocator.
business_questions: What share of allocations are due to shortage versus normal operations?
Which products or allocators have the most 'Shortage' allocations?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Use exact-match filters or GROUP BY reason. Consider normalizing into code/enum for ease of querying. Watch for variant values or typos.

release_ts.description: Timestamp when the allocation was released; stored as string. In this dataset it appears constant (one distinct value).
release_ts.analysis_use: Identify when allocations were released to compute hold duration (release_ts - allocated_at) and to audit release timings. If constant or mostly null, indicates incomplete data capture.
business_questions: When were allocations released and what was the average hold duration?
Are releases clustered at particular times (e.g., nightly jobs) and do they correlate with inventory refreshes?
release_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for duration calculations. Note dataset shows a single distinct value — verify whether this column is populated reliably in production. Use NULL-aware logic: release_ts may be absent for ACTIVE allocations. Avoid assuming uniqueness or variability without data validation.

event_id.description: Unique identifier assigned to each event record in the event stream.
event_id.analysis_use: Used for de-duplication, tracing specific events, and linking back to source logs. Useful as a grouping key when deduping or auditing events.
business_questions: Are there duplicate event_ids for the same order (indicating ingestion issues)?
Can we fetch the full audit trail for a specific event_id for troubleshooting?
event_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; use DISTINCT or GROUP BY to detect duplicates. Not a declared PK — don't assume uniqueness unless validated. Use equality filters when users provide exact event_id.

order_id.description: Identifier of the B2B order that this event pertains to.
order_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to b2b_orders and related tables. Use for grouping events per order, reconstructing timelines, counting events per order, and filtering events for particular orders.
business_questions: Show all events for order_id = 'B2BORD4001' ordered by event_ts.
Which orders have more than N events in the last X days?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as join key when linking to b2b_orders. Use equality filters or IN lists for small sets; consider indexes on order_id for performance. Cast event_ts when ordering (see event_ts hints).

event_type.description: Categorical label for the event action (e.g., CREATED, ALLOCATED, PICKED, DISPATCHED, CONFIRMED).
event_type.analysis_use: Group-by and filters for event counts, funnels, and distributions. Useful for segmentation by workflow stage and sequence analysis.
business_questions: How many PICKED events occurred today vs yesterday?
What percent of events are CONFIRMED vs DISPATCHED over the last month?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — pivot or GROUP BY is efficient. Support synonyms and pluralization in NL queries (e.g., 'pick' -> 'PICKED'). Use explicit filtering on event_type when generating counts or funnels.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the event occurred (stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary field for ordering events, computing durations between events, bucketing by hour/day/week, and time-series analyses. Must be cast to datetime for interval math.
business_questions: What is the average time between CREATED and DISPATCHED events per order?
How many events occurred per hour on 2025-10-06?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for filtering, ordering and arithmetic (e.g., CAST(event_ts AS DATETIME)). Normalize timezone if mixing sources. When users specify natural language dates, convert to the same format before comparing.

event_by.description: The user or system actor who triggered the event (human name or system identifier).
event_by.analysis_use: Used to attribute actions to agents/systems; group and rank actors by event volumes and types; detect outliers or suspicious activity.
business_questions: Which agents performed the most DISPATCHED events last week?
Are there any event_by values with a high rate of errors/unprocessed events?
event_by.nl2sql_hints: Possible joins to b2b_sales_agents or portal API client metadata if normalized. Treat as string; consider exact-match filters and top-N aggregations. Map variations of names to canonical identifiers if available.

prev_status.description: Order status before the event was applied.
prev_status.analysis_use: Used together with new_status to analyze status transitions, build transition matrices, and find illegal or unexpected transitions.
business_questions: What are the most common status transitions (prev_status -> new_status)?
How many events moved orders from PLACED to DISPATCHED directly?
prev_status.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — ideal for GROUP BY. When reconstructing transitions, ensure ordering by event_ts per order. Use pairs (prev_status, new_status) as composite grouping columns.

new_status.description: Order status after the event was applied.
new_status.analysis_use: Key for measuring current status distributions, computing conversion rates to DELIVERED, and detecting failures to reach expected status.
business_questions: How many orders reached DELIVERED status in the past month?
Which orders reverted from DISPATCHED back to CONFIRMED?
new_status.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality; use in GROUP BY and WHERE clauses. For time-windowed current-state queries, pick latest event per order by event_ts and inspect new_status.

details_json.description: JSON blob with additional event details; in this snapshot it is empty ('{}').
details_json.analysis_use: Potentially holds structured metadata (line-level info, quantities, error codes). Currently unused because values are empty; if populated, parse JSON for richer filters and aggregations.
business_questions: Are there events with additional error details to investigate (non-empty details_json)?
Can we extract item-level info or reason codes from details_json for failed events?
details_json.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; if JSON data exists, use JSON functions (JSON_VALUE/JSON_EXTRACT) after ensuring valid JSON. Guard queries for empty/NULL values to avoid runtime errors. Consider promoting frequently accessed JSON keys to physical columns.

source_system.description: Originating system for the event (ERP, MOBILE_APP, PORTAL).
source_system.analysis_use: Segment events by origin to analyze system-specific behavior, ingestion latency, and source-based error rates.
business_questions: Which source_system generates the most unprocessed events?
Is there a difference in time-to-dispatch for orders created via MOBILE_APP vs ERP?
source_system.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — use GROUP BY. Map synonyms (e.g., 'MOBILE' -> 'MOBILE_APP') in NL2SQL parsing. Use as a filter when users ask about events from a specific system.

ip_address.description: IP address of the actor/system when the event was generated (string).
ip_address.analysis_use: Security/audit use: detect suspicious activity, cluster events by source IP, and join to network logs. Can be used to geo-locate or rate-limit analysis.
business_questions: Which IPs generated the most events in the last 24 hours?
Are there IPs with unusually high failed/unprocessed events that indicate automation errors?
ip_address.nl2sql_hints: Store as string; apply string filters or pattern matching for subnet analysis. For geo-enrichment, join to IP-to-location tables. Watch for IPv6 formats — normalize if needed.

processed_flag.description: Indicator whether the event has been processed by downstream consumers ('0' or '1' stored as nvarchar).
processed_flag.analysis_use: Filter to find unprocessed events, monitor ingestion/processing backlogs, compute processing rates and latencies.
business_questions: How many events are currently unprocessed (processed_flag = '0')?
What is the age distribution of unprocessed events (requires event_ts/created_at)?
processed_flag.nl2sql_hints: Cast to boolean/integer for comparisons (e.g., CAST(processed_flag AS INT) = 0). Use as an efficient filter for operational queries; consider indexing for high-volume tables. In NL2SQL parse 'processed'/'unprocessed' to underlying '1'/'0'.

created_at.description: Ingestion timestamp when the event row was created in this table (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Measure ingestion latency by comparing event_ts to created_at, detect delays, and monitor freshness of the event stream.
business_questions: What is the average ingestion delay between event_ts and created_at?
List events ingested in the last hour that are still unprocessed.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons. When calculating latency, ensure consistent timezone handling. Use created_at for queries about ingestion windows (e.g., 'new events in last X minutes').

order_line_id.description: Unique identifier for the order line (text). Appears to be the natural primary key but not declared as PK.
order_line_id.analysis_use: Row-level identity for deduplication, joining back to order_line-level events or allocations, grouping for line-specific metrics.
business_questions: How many unique order lines exist in a time period?
Which order_lines have not been allocated or have missing allocation_id?
order_line_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Use equality filters (WHERE order_line_id = '...') or IN lists. Consider trimming/normalizing whitespace. If uniqueness matters, use DISTINCT on this column. Not suitable for numeric casts.

order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders table for customer, order status, invoice and shipment-level analyses; aggregate lines to order-level metrics (order totals, number of lines).
business_questions: What is the total value per order (sum of line_total grouped by order_id)?
Which orders have the most lines or largest line totals?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Primary join to b2b_orders. Use equality/IN and GROUP BY order_id. Normalize types (string) and trim. When aggregating order totals, cast line_total to numeric before SUM.

sku.description: Product stock keeping unit code for the line item.
sku.analysis_use: Group/filter by product for sales, inventory allocation and performance metrics; join to product master tables for attributes like category, weight, vendor.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest revenue or sold quantity?
Which SKUs are most frequently allocated to a given warehouse?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Join to products/b2b_products on sku. It's low cardinality (10 distinct) — good for GROUP BY. Use exact matches or prefix matching if variant codes exist. NOT NULL not guaranteed; handle nulls in queries.

item_description.description: Human readable description of the SKU at time of order (may include name/pack size).
item_description.analysis_use: Display label with SKU in results; sometimes used for grouping if SKU absent; useful for reporting and QA. May be used for free-text filters.
business_questions: What are the most common item descriptions sold?
Are there discrepancies between item_description and master product description?
item_description.nl2sql_hints: Prefer joining to product master for canonical description. Treat as text; use LIKE for fuzzy searches. Beware of inconsistent naming; do not rely on it as a stable join key.

qty_units.description: Quantity expressed in units (stored as nvarchar). Likely integer count of units/crates/etc.
qty_units.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG) to compute total units sold; used to compute average sell-through, pack-level metrics and conversion to weight if needed.
business_questions: What is total units sold per SKU or per order?
What is average units per order line or per order?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer (e.g., CAST(qty_units AS INT) or SAFE_CAST in some dialects). Handle non-numeric/null values and trimming. When writing NL2SQL, prefer numeric comparisons after cast (e.g., WHERE CAST(qty_units AS INT) > 10).

qty_kg.description: Quantity expressed in kilograms (stored as nvarchar, decimal-like).
qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate weight sold per SKU/warehouse; convert to standard units for inventory reconciliation and logistics planning.
business_questions: What is total kg sold per SKU and per time period?
Which orders have the highest weight (for shipping/dispatch optimization)?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal/float (e.g., CAST(qty_kg AS DECIMAL(12,3))). Handle commas/locale and invalid strings. Use numeric comparisons and aggregations only after casting.

unit_price.description: Unit selling price for the line (stored as nvarchar, decimal-like).
unit_price.analysis_use: Compute revenue, average price, price variance across time/SKUs, detect pricing anomalies and discounts.
business_questions: What is the average and median unit price per SKU over a period?
Are there outlier unit prices for given SKUs indicating data issues or special pricing?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before aggregations (e.g., SUM, AVG). Watch for values stored with excessive precision; normalize scale. Use COALESCE for nulls. Consider rounding in presentation.

line_total.description: Total amount for the order line (stored as nvarchar) — should equal unit_price * quantity (after correct numeric types and potential discounts).
line_total.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for line-level financial reporting, order totals and revenue by SKU/warehouse/time. Validate against computed unit_price * qty for data quality checks.
business_questions: What is total revenue over a period by SKU or warehouse?
Do any line_total values deviate from unit_price * qty (indicating discounts or data errors)?
line_total.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for SUM and comparisons. For integrity checks, compare CAST(line_total AS DECIMAL(...)) to CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL(...)) * CAST(qty_units AS INT) (allow epsilon for floating rounding).

allocation_id.description: Identifier for the allocation record that reserved inventory for this line (text).
allocation_id.analysis_use: Join to order_allocations or allocation events to analyze fulfillment, allocation success/failure, and allocation timing; group by allocation to analyze batch behavior.
business_questions: Which allocations account for the most revenue or quantity?
How many lines are unallocated (NULL allocation_id) or share the same allocation?
allocation_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to b2b_order_allocations on allocation_id. Use equality filters and GROUP BY allocation_id. Null/empty values indicate unallocated lines; treat appropriately.

promised_by.description: Promised fulfillment/delivery datetime for the line (stored as nvarchar).
promised_by.analysis_use: Compute SLA/lead time between created_at and promised_by, schedule fulfillment windows, measure late fulfillment when compared to actual dispatch/invoice dates (via joins).
business_questions: What is lead time distribution between created_at and promised_by?
How many lines have promised_by within X days and were fulfilled on time (after joining dispatch/invoice)?
promised_by.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., CAST(promised_by AS TIMESTAMP)). Watch formatting and invalid values. Use date functions to compute intervals. When parsing, use explicit format if dialect requires (e.g., TO_TIMESTAMP).

warehouse_allocated_id.description: Identifier of the warehouse/fulfillment centre allocated to fulfill this line.
warehouse_allocated_id.analysis_use: Group and filter by warehouse to measure performance, stock allocation patterns, throughput and revenue per center.
business_questions: Which warehouses fulfil the most revenue/quantity?
Does SKU distribution differ significantly across warehouses?
warehouse_allocated_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — effective for GROUP BY and GROUPING SETS. Join to warehouse/dispatch tables on this id. Treat as text key; normalize for case/whitespace. Consider using this as a filter in NL2SQL (e.g., warehouse_allocated_id = 'CTR_SUR_001').

batch_id.description: Batch or container identifier used for picking/dispatch tied to the line.
batch_id.analysis_use: Trace lines to picking batches, analyze batch-level throughput, batch-level quality issues and batch-to-shipment mappings.
business_questions: Which batches contain the most high-value lines?
Are there batch-level anomalies in quantities or weights?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to picking_batches or dispatch batch tables on batch_id. Group by batch_id to get aggregate metrics. Handle nulls for unbatched lines.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the order line was created/inserted (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis (sales over time), partitioning, computing lead times vs promised_by, hourly/daily patterns in orders.
business_questions: How many order lines were created per day/week/month?
What are peak ordering hours by created_at?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for time grouping (e.g., DATE(created_at) or TO_DATE). Use timezone awareness if required. For performance, consider storing a proper datetime type or precomputed date columns for NL2SQL.

remarks.description: Short categorical remark about packaging/handling (e.g., 'Bulk crate', 'Standard pack', 'Prewashed').
