Compare average commission rates across specializations.
specialization.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — good for GROUP BY and WHERE. Normalize values (trim, consistent case). Consider joining to a specialization dimension if additional metadata is needed.

commission_rate_pct.description: Agent commission rate stored as text. Values appear as floating numbers (e.g., '3.23', '0.5899999999999999') representing percentage-like rates.
commission_rate_pct.analysis_use: After converting to numeric, use for aggregations (avg/min/max), filtering (e.g., rate > X), and calculating payouts when joined with sales amounts. Also useful for identifying outliers and consistency checks.
business_questions: What is the average commission rate across all active agents?
Which agents have commission rates above 3%?
commission_rate_pct.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to a numeric type before comparisons/aggregations (e.g., CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(commission_rate_pct),'') AS DECIMAL(10,4))). Handle floating-point artifacts and empty/null strings. Clarify whether values are percentages (3.23 means 3.23%) or decimals (0.0323). Normalize scale consistently (e.g., store as decimal fraction or percentage) to avoid misinterpretation in NL2SQL prompts.

join_date.description: Date agent joined the program, stored as string in YYYY-MM-DD format for most rows.
join_date.analysis_use: Tenure calculations, cohort analysis, filtering by join date ranges, time-based segmentation (e.g., new hires in last 30/90/365 days).
business_questions: How many agents joined in the last 12 months?
Which agents joined before/after a particular date?
join_date.nl2sql_hints: Parse to DATE (e.g., CAST(join_date AS DATE) or TO_DATE(join_date,'YYYY-MM-DD')). Handle nulls and inconsistent formats; some values may be missing. Use date conversions in WHERE/GROUP BY for time-window queries.

status.analysis_use: Filter by current operational status; group counts by status; segment active workforce vs inactive for monitoring and outreach.
business_questions: How many agents are ACTIVE vs INACTIVE overall and by region?
List inactive agents who joined within the last 6 months for potential reactivation.
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with few values — use equality checks (status = 'ACTIVE'). Normalize case and trim when matching. Since status heavily skewed, consider using it in WHERE or GROUP BY. Treat missing/null as a separate bucket.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp as string (appears to be YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS format).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and recency checks, order of record ingestion, join timing with other datasets, filtering records by creation time.
business_questions: When were agent records created and which records were added in the last 30 days?
Are there agents created long before their join_date (data quality check)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Convert to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP(created_at,'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS')). Handle timezone considerations if any. Use for sorting and time-based filters. Null or malformed strings should be cleaned prior to temporal queries.

date.description: Business date for the aggregated metrics (string in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format).
date.analysis_use: Primary time axis for filtering, grouping and trend analysis. Use for WHERE date range, GROUP BY date (or date_trunc to week/month after casting).
business_questions: What were net_sales per day over the past 90 days?
Which dates show spikes in discount_amount or tax_amount?
date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE when available: CAST(date AS DATE) or TRY_PARSE(date, 'yyyy-MM-dd'). Lexicographic comparisons are safe if format is consistently YYYY-MM-DD. Ensure queries that filter ranges cast the string to date for index usage if column is converted to DATE.

customer_type.description: Type/category of customer (categorical: Retail Chain, HORECA, Caterer, Wholesaler).
customer_type.analysis_use: Dimension for segmentation and GROUP BY. Good low-cardinality grouping column for rollups and breakdowns.
business_questions: Which customer_type drives the highest average order value?
How do discount rates vary by customer_type?
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use exact match or IN lists. Consider normalizing to a reference table for joins. Watch for trailing spaces/casing; use TRIM/UPPER if necessary.

total_orders.description: Count of orders on that date for the customer_type (stored as string/int-like).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) or average computations (AVG) and denominator for per-order metrics (net_sales / total_orders). Use as integer after casting.
business_questions: How many orders were placed per day by customer_type?
What is the average number of orders per day in a given month?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER (e.g., CAST(total_orders AS INT) or TRY_CAST). Handle nulls/empty strings. When dividing by this value handle zero to avoid division-by-zero errors.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the date and customer_type (stored as string representing a numeric float).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM), average order quantity (total_qty_kg / total_orders), and unit-based KPIs. Useful for yield and volume trend analyses.
business_questions: How does sold quantity (kg) trend by customer_type over time?
What is the average kg per order per customer_type?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC with appropriate precision (e.g., CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Watch for thousand separators, non-numeric tokens and nulls; use TRY_CAST/NULLIF where supported.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (pre-discount and pre-tax) stored as string representing a numeric value.
gross_sales.analysis_use: SUM for total revenue, GROUP BY date/customer_type, and used to compute discount % and tax ratios.
business_questions: What are daily gross_sales and how do they compare across customer_types?
What percentage of gross_sales is given away as discount?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Beware of currency formatting or non-numeric values. Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST to handle invalid entries.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied for the aggregated date/customer_type (string numeric).
discount_amount.analysis_use: SUM and ratio to gross_sales to evaluate discounting behavior. Track trends and outliers.
business_questions: Which days/customer_types receive the highest absolute discount amounts?
What is the discount as % of gross_sales by customer_type?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL. Compute discount rate as discount_amount / gross_sales after casting and guarding against zero gross_sales. Watch for negative values or anomalies stored as strings.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the day/customer_type (string numeric).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liabilities, tax rate checks (tax_amount / net or gross) and compliance monitoring.
business_questions: How much tax was collected daily and by customer_type?
Are there days with tax_amount inconsistent with expected tax rates?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL. Use consistent rounding and guard against non-numeric entries. When comparing to gross_sales or net_sales, ensure both sides are cast to numeric.

net_sales.description: Net sales after discounts and taxes (string numeric).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for revenue. Aggregate, trend analysis, and computation of average order value. Often used as the target metric for dashboards.
business_questions: What are daily net_sales trends and month-over-month growth?
Which customer_type has the highest average net_sales per order?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL. Validate that net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount +/- tax logic (if applicable) for reconciliation. Because this column is high-cardinality, avoid string comparisons; treat as numeric in aggregations.

created_at.description: Ingestion or row creation timestamp (string), appears constant across rows—likely the import time.
created_at.analysis_use: Metadata for lineage/debugging (when the snapshot was created). Not useful as a business time dimension unless it varies.
business_questions: When was this sales snapshot created/loaded?
Are there multiple ingestion batches or only a single snapshot?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP when needed. Expect many identical values; treat as audit field. Do not use as the primary time axis for business reporting (use 'date').

Unnamed: 9.description: Empty artifact column (all nulls). Likely from source CSV/ETL header artifact.
Unnamed: 9.analysis_use: None for analysis — safe to drop or ignore.
business_questions: Is this column used by any processes or is it an artifact?
Can this column be removed to simplify the schema?
Unnamed: 9.nl2sql_hints: Ignore/drop. If present in NL2SQL prompts, clarify it contains only nulls to prevent confusion.

Unnamed: 10.description: Empty artifact column (all nulls). Likely an unused import column.
business_questions: Is there historical data in this column or is it always empty?
Can downstream models ignore or remove this column?
Unnamed: 10.nl2sql_hints: Ignore/drop. Do not include in SELECT lists unless investigating ETL source integrity.

Unnamed: 11.description: Sparse categorical column (4 small-code values observed: 'da','gs','ns','ta') but ~99.9% null — likely a metadata code or import artifact.
Unnamed: 11.analysis_use: Only useful if populated consistently—could be a region/code flag. Otherwise treat as sparse and investigate source before using.
business_questions: What do the codes in this column represent and when are they populated?
Does this code correlate with any sales patterns on the rows where it is present?
Unnamed: 11.nl2sql_hints: Treat as NULLable categorical. When present, use as GROUP BY only after documenting meaning. Watch for case/spacing; use TRIM/UPPER. For NL2SQL, clarify that this column is mostly null to avoid misleading aggregations.

Unnamed: 12.description: Mixed-content sparse column: some large numeric-looking strings and one code 'ns'. ~99.9% null. Likely an ETL artifact or aggregated field from another system.
Unnamed: 12.analysis_use: Not reliable without source validation — avoid using for core KPIs. Investigate and clean before analytical use.
business_questions: What is the origin and meaning of values in Unnamed: 12?
Can the numeric values here be reconciled with other monetary aggregates, or are they unrelated metadata?
Unnamed: 12.nl2sql_hints: Do not assume numeric type; values are mixed. Use TRY_CAST to numeric and filter out non-numeric tokens. Prefer to exclude this column in NL2SQL unless schema docs map it to a known metric.

year.description: Calendar year for the aggregated row (stored as nvarchar). Currently contains only '2024'.
year.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for yearly drills and filtering. Used for WHERE filters, GROUP BY when aggregating across years, and as a join key to other time-partitioned tables.
business_questions: Show monthly sales for year = 2024 by customer_type.
Compare yearly totals across different years (if table extended).
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as integer or smallint for comparisons and range filters. In NL2SQL, when users say '2024' treat it as numeric or string depending on target DB; prefer CAST(year AS INT) or normalize column to INT. Beware that the table currently only has 2024 so year filters for other years will return no rows.

month.description: Month number (1-12) for the aggregated row, stored as nvarchar.
month.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for monthly trend analysis, ordering, filtering (e.g., month = 3), and grouping. Combine with year to form a date or period for time series functions.
business_questions: Show net sales trend by month for a given customer_type.
What were the total orders in month 10 (October) across all customer types?
month.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for numeric ordering and range filters (e.g., ORDER BY CAST(month AS INT)). When users ask for 'March' or '03' map month names to integers. Be careful with zero-padding: values appear as '1'..'12'. Use safe casting and handle non-numeric/null values.

customer_type.description: Categorical bucket indicating customer segment (e.g., Caterer, HORECA, Retail Chain, Wholesaler).
customer_type.analysis_use: Dimension for GROUP BY and filtering to compare segments. Good for stacking/segmentation in charts and for computing percent contributions across metrics.
business_questions: Which customer_type had the highest net sales in June?
How does average order size differ between HORECA and Retail Chain?
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Use as a string dimension in GROUP BY and filters. There is no FK so do not assume referential integrity — mapping may be required to join to customer master. Normalize/canonicalize categories if queries use synonyms (e.g., 'Hotel/Restaurant' vs 'HORECA').

total_orders.description: Count of orders in the month for the customer_type, stored as nvarchar (integer-like).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) for totals across types or months, AVG for average orders per period, and for computing orders-based KPIs such as avg value per order (net_sales / total_orders).
business_questions: Total number of orders for Wholesaler in August.
What is the average monthly order count per customer_type in 2024?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Must be CAST/CONVERT to INT (e.g., CAST(total_orders AS INT)) before SUM/AVG. Handle nulls and non-numeric strings (use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT where available). Guard against division by zero when computing per-order metrics.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the month and customer_type, stored as nvarchar (decimal-like).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: SUM for total weight sold, AVG or SUM/total_orders for kg per order, and joins to inventory/fulfillment metrics for yield and stock planning.
business_questions: How many kilograms did the Retail Chain buy in March?
What is the average kg per order for Caterer across the year?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,3))). Watch for localization/formatting (commas/periods). Use TRY_CAST to avoid query failures on dirty data. When computing kg per order, ensure total_orders cast and zero-checks.

gross_sales.description: Sum of gross sales (pre-discount) for the month and customer_type, stored as nvarchar (decimal-like).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for SUM, trend analysis, contribution share, and as denominator for discount/tax rate calculations (discount_amount/gross_sales, tax_amount/gross_sales).
business_questions: What were gross sales by customer_type in July?
Which month had the highest gross sales for HORECA?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before aggregations. Use consistent precision (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)). Protect against zero/NULL when calculating rates. Prefer normalized numeric type in source to avoid repeated casting in queries.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied in the month for the customer_type, stored as nvarchar (decimal-like).
discount_amount.analysis_use: SUM for total discount exposure; compute discount rate (discount_amount/gross_sales); analyze promotional impact and margin leakage per segment.
business_questions: What total discounts were given to Retail Chain in September?
What is the average discount rate by customer_type across months?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for SUM and rate calculations. When gross_sales is zero or null, avoid dividing; use NULLIF(CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(...)), 0) or CASE to handle. Check for negative values or unusual formatting.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the month and customer_type, stored as nvarchar (decimal-like).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Sum to compute total tax liability, compute effective tax rate (tax_amount/gross_sales or tax_amount/net_sales), and for regulatory reporting.
business_questions: How much tax did Wholesaler pay in May?
What is the effective tax rate by customer_type for the year-to-date?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for aggregations and rate calculations. Decide whether tax is included in gross or computed separately; confirm business definitions before deriving rates. Use safe casts (TRY_CAST) for robustness.

net_sales.description: Net sales after discounts and tax adjustments for the month and customer_type, stored as nvarchar (decimal-like).
net_sales.analysis_use: Key bottom-line revenue metric for SUM, trend analysis, and computing contribution margins. Used to compute average order value (net_sales/total_orders) and to compare against gross_sales to validate discount/tax arithmetic.
business_questions: Which customer_type achieved the highest net sales in the year?
What is the average net sales per order for HORECA in Q3?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for aggregation and per-order calculations. When users request 'revenue' clarify if they mean gross_sales or net_sales. Verify that net_sales ≈ gross_sales - discount_amount +/− tax_amount as a data quality check.

created_at.description: Load or snapshot timestamp for the row (string). In this dataset it is constant (one ingestion timestamp).
created_at.analysis_use: Used for auditing, ETL lineage, and freshness checks. Useful to filter/select the latest snapshot if multiple loads exist; not used for business time-series (use year/month instead).
business_questions: When was this monthly snapshot loaded?
Show only rows from the latest load.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as TIMESTAMP/DATETIME after casting (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). If dataset contains multiple created_at values across loads, use MAX(created_at) to get latest snapshot. Currently constant, so avoid using it as a time-series axis.

year.description: Calendar year for which the aggregated metrics are reported (stored as nvarchar). In this dataset all rows are '2024'.
year.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filter key for yearly aggregations and comparisons. Use in WHERE clauses (e.g., year = '2024') and GROUP BY when comparing across years. Should be cast/normalized if stored as text (e.g., CAST(year AS INT)).
business_questions: Which years have the highest net_sales for each customer type?
How did orders and quantities change year-over-year for a given customer type?
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical/text in queries unless numeric comparisons needed. Prefer CAST(year AS INT) for numeric comparisons or BETWEEN, and normalize whitespace. Expect few distinct values; index or partition on year if table grows. When generating SQL, prefer explicit equality or IN lists (e.g., WHERE year = '2024').

customer_type.description: Categorical label describing the B2B customer segment (e.g., Caterer, HORECA, Retail Chain, Wholesaler).
customer_type.analysis_use: Primary dimension for grouping and segmentation. Use in GROUP BY and ORDER BY to produce breakdowns. Useful for joins to segmentation or customer master tables when enriched data is available.
business_questions: Which customer type generates the highest gross sales in the year?
Which customer type receives the largest total discount amount?
customer_type.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match string filters are common (e.g., WHERE customer_type = 'HORECA'). Beware of inconsistent capitalization/whitespace; consider LOWER/TRIM normalization in comparisons. Not a join key by default — joins to other tables require domain knowledge mapping.

total_orders.description: Total number of orders in the year for the customer_type (stored as nvarchar representing integer values).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation metric used for counts and rate calculations (e.g., average order value = gross_sales / total_orders). Use in arithmetic after casting to integer. Useful for ranking customer segments by activity.
business_questions: How many orders did each customer type place in 2024?
Which customer type has the highest orders per kg (total_orders / total_qty_kg)?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: This is textual numeric data — CAST(total_orders AS INT) or TRY_CAST depending on SQL dialect. Watch for NULLs or non-numeric characters. Use in DIVIDE operations with appropriate NULL/zero guards (e.g., CASE WHEN CAST(total_orders AS INT)=0 THEN NULL ELSE ... END).

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the year and customer_type (stored as nvarchar with decimal precision).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregation metric for volume-based analysis; used to compute yield per order, unit price per kg, trend of volume across segments. Must be cast to numeric (DECIMAL/DOUBLE) before aggregation/ratio calculations.
business_questions: What is the total kg sold to each customer type in 2024?
What is the average kg per order for each customer type?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to DECIMAL(18,6) or FLOAT before arithmetic. Watch for localization/decimal separators and trailing text. Use safe casting functions (TRY_CAST) to avoid query failures on dirty data.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount and pre-tax) for the year and customer_type (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Core revenue metric for ranking and share calculations. Use in aggregations, percentage-of-total computations, and deriving average order value. Cast to numeric for correct summation and arithmetic.
business_questions: Which customer type contributed the largest portion of gross sales in 2024?
What is the average gross sales per order for each customer segment?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — require CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL) or FLOAT. Use rounding for display and handle NULLs. When generating NL2SQL, transform expressions like SUM(CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))).

discount_amount.description: Total discount value applied across orders for the year and customer_type (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute discount as a percent of gross sales, total discount exposure by segment, and to examine promotional impact. Cast to numeric for arithmetic and percent calculations.
business_questions: What percentage of gross sales was given as discounts for each customer type?
Which customer type received the highest absolute discount amount?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL before arithmetic. For percent calculations, use (CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL)/NULLIF(CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL),0))*100 to avoid division by zero. Consider negative values or refunds in data validation.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected/charged for the year and customer_type (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used for tax liability reporting and to compute tax as percent of gross sales. Cast to numeric and aggregate as needed.
business_questions: How much tax was collected from each customer type in 2024?
What is the average tax per order or tax percentage of gross sales by customer type?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Treat as numeric after CAST. Use NULLIF for dividing by gross_sales. If tax rules differ by customer_type, joins to tax rules or invoice-level data may be required for deeper analysis.

net_sales.description: Total net sales (likely gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount or another reconciliation) for the year and customer_type (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary profitability/revenue metric for comparison. Use in share calculations, ranking, growth calculations and as the target metric in dashboards. Cast to numeric for aggregations and arithmetic.
business_questions: Which customer type generated the highest net sales in 2024?
How does net_sales compare to gross_sales after discounts and taxes by segment?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Verify how net_sales is derived from other columns; avoid double counting if recalculating. CAST to DECIMAL for SUM/AVG operations. Use consistency checks like SUM(CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL) - CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL) + CAST(tax_amount AS DECIMAL)) = SUM(CAST(net_sales AS DECIMAL)).

created_at.description: Ingestion or snapshot timestamp when the row was created in the raw table (stored as nvarchar). In this dataset it is identical across rows (2025-12-02 10:46:16).
created_at.analysis_use: Used to identify the data snapshot time for freshness checks, ETL provenance, or to filter rows by ingestion time if multiple snapshots exist. Not useful as the business period — use 'year' for business time.
business_questions: When was this yearly aggregate data snapshot taken?
Is this data fresh relative to other snapshot tables or ETL runs?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string or CAST to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for temporal filters. Use consistent parsing (e.g., TRY_PARSE or STR_TO_DATE) depending on SQL dialect. Do not use created_at as the business period; prefer 'year'.

tracking_event_id.description: Unique identifier for each tracking event (string).
tracking_event_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplication, event-level counts, and ordering events. Use for grouping and retrieving single-event details.
business_questions: Was tracking_event_id TRKxxxxx generated multiple times (duplicates)?
Retrieve full details for a specific tracking event id.
tracking_event_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Although distinct in sample, no PK enforced—use dedup logic if needed. Use = or IN predicates. Avoid numeric casts. Use when selecting specific rows or joining to audit logs if an external events table stores this id.

dispatch_id.description: Identifier for the dispatch/consignment this event belongs to (string).
dispatch_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to connect events to dispatch, order and shipment tables; essential for grouping events per dispatch, computing timelines and final statuses.
business_questions: Show the event timeline for dispatch_id DISPxxxxx.
How many events were recorded per dispatch in the last 7 days?
dispatch_id.nl2sql_hints: Common join column—use equality joins to b2b_dispatches (ensure exact string match). Consider trimming whitespace and consistent casing. If performing window functions (first/last event per dispatch), partition by dispatch_id. Indexing this column improves event-to-dispatch queries.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the event actually occurred, stored as string (appears 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss').
event_ts.analysis_use: Key for temporal analysis: ordering events, computing durations between events, aggregating by day/hour, SLA/latency computations.
business_questions: What time did each dispatch reach DELIVERED status (event_ts)?
How many events occurred per hour/day for a given event_type?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to proper datetime (e.g., CAST(event_ts AS DATETIME) or parse with STR_TO_DATE) before using temporal predicates or date functions. Beware of timezone assumptions. Use BETWEEN or >=/< for ranges after casting. If stored inconsistent formats, normalize on ingestion.

event_type.description: Categorical event label (e.g., PICKED, LOADED, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED).
event_type.analysis_use: Grouping, filtering and pivoting (counts by event_type), computing funnel progression and final statuses per dispatch.
business_questions: How many 'OUT_FOR_DELIVERY' events occurred yesterday?
What proportion of events are DELIVERED vs other statuses?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality—use equality/IN or GROUP BY. Normalize casing/trim. When users ask natural language like 'delivered events', map synonyms/case-insensitive matches. Consider mapping event_type to an ordered status sequence for funnel queries.

gps_lat.description: Latitude coordinate of the event as a string representing decimal degrees.
gps_lat.analysis_use: Spatial analysis: clustering events, mapping routes, distance/time calculations between consecutive events for a dispatch, geofencing checks.
business_questions: Show event coordinates for dispatch DISPxxxx to plot the route.
Are there clusters of failed deliveries around certain lat/long areas?
gps_lat.nl2sql_hints: Cast to FLOAT/DOUBLE before numeric or spatial functions. Handle null/empty strings. For distance calculations use Haversine formula on cast values or load into spatial types if DB supports. Use numeric ranges for bounding-box filters (e.g., gps_lat BETWEEN x AND y).

gps_long.description: Longitude coordinate of the event as a string representing decimal degrees.
gps_long.analysis_use: Paired with gps_lat for mapping, distance computations, and location-based filtering/aggregation.
business_questions: Which events occurred within a specific city bounding box (lat/long)?
Calculate distance between first and last recorded coordinates for a dispatch.
gps_long.nl2sql_hints: Same as gps_lat: cast to numeric before computations. Use both lat/long for spatial predicates. Beware of swapped lat/long; validate sample ranges. For NL2SQL, detect phrases like 'near', 'within X km' and translate to bounding box + Haversine.

scanned_by.description: Name of the person/operator who scanned or recorded the event.
scanned_by.analysis_use: Accountability and operational metrics: events per operator, error/exception analysis, linking to staffing tables for performance metrics.
business_questions: Which scanner recorded the highest number of delivery events last week?
Show events recorded by 'Advika Handa' in the past month.
scanned_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text. Use exact matches or case-insensitive LIKE for partial names. Consider joins to an employees table to get IDs or teams. For NL2SQL, support natural queries like 'by driver John' mapping to scanned_by.

scan_photo_url.description: URL to a scan/photo associated with the event (currently all null in sample).
scan_photo_url.analysis_use: Evidence retrieval for audits and claims; rarely used in aggregations but used in detail views or existence checks (has_photo).
business_questions: Which events have an associated photo?
Retrieve scan photo URL for tracking_event_id TRKxxxxx.
scan_photo_url.nl2sql_hints: Column contains mostly nulls—use IS NOT NULL for existence checks. When selecting, return as-is (string). If always null in production, consider omitting from frequent query projections to reduce payload.

location_name.description: Human-readable location/city name for the event (e.g., Nadiad, Gandhinagar).
location_name.analysis_use: Grouping by city/warehouse, aggregations for regional performance, faceted filtering and mapping human location to gps coords.
business_questions: How many events were recorded in Nadiad this month?
Which locations have the most OUT_FOR_DELIVERY events?
location_name.nl2sql_hints: Use GROUP BY or filter with equality/IN. Be aware of spelling/case variants—apply normalization. Combine with gps filters to disambiguate similar names. Consider mapping to location dimension table for standardized names.

status_note.description: Optional short note about the event status (currently all null in sample).
status_note.analysis_use: Free-text context for exceptions or manual notes; used in detail queries and exception detection rather than numeric aggregation.
business_questions: Show events that contain the note 'damaged' in status_note.
What additional notes were added for DELIVERED events last week?
status_note.nl2sql_hints: Use LIKE '%keyword%' or full-text search (if supported) for phrase queries. Many nulls—use IS NOT NULL to find annotated events. Expect short texts; avoid relying on structured values unless normalized.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the event row was created/ingested into the system, stored as string.
created_at.analysis_use: Measure telemetry/ingestion lag (created_at - event_ts), audit timelines, and filtering by ingestion window.
business_questions: What is the median ingestion delay between event_ts and created_at for the last month?
List events ingested yesterday (created_at date).
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime before temporal operations. Use as ingestion-time filter (e.g., created_at BETWEEN ...). When users ask 'events created on date X', map to created_at cast. Be mindful of potential timezone differences vs event_ts.

device_id.description: Identifier of the device that recorded the event (string, e.g., DEV1108).
device_id.analysis_use: Device-level telemetry: events per device, device health/usage, identifying malfunctioning devices, mapping devices to operators or regions.
business_questions: Which device created the most failed/exception events last month?
Show all events recorded by device DEV1108 in the last 24 hours.
device_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN for filters and GROUP BY for aggregates. May be joinable to device registry tables for metadata. Normalize whitespace/casing. When asked about 'by scanner' clarify whether filter should be by device_id or scanned_by.

vendor_id.description: Logical identifier for the vendor partner (e.g., B2BV2001). Stored as nvarchar; appears unique across rows though not declared primary key.
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary candidate for joins to transactional tables, grouping and deduplication. Useful for exact-match filters, COUNT(DISTINCT vendor_id), and mapping vendor metadata to spend/transactions.
business_questions: Which vendor_id corresponds to a given vendor_name or GST number?
What is the total number of distinct vendors (by vendor_id) by service_type?
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string for equality matches. Prefer exact = vendor_id or IN(...) in WHERE. If joining to other tables, cast consistently (both sides as nvarchar) and trim whitespace. Consider creating an index or declaring PK if used frequently in joins.

vendor_name.analysis_use: Used for display, fuzzy matching, group-by for human reports, and identifying duplicates. Useful in WHERE with LIKE for partial name search or exact equality for reporting.
business_questions: Show contact details for vendor_name = 'Balan Inc'.
List all vendors whose name contains 'Banerjee' or 'Balan' for manual verification.
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: String comparisons may require case-insensitive matching or normalization (TRIM, LOWER). Use LIKE '%term%' for partial match. Avoid using vendor_name as a join key if vendor_id or gst_no is available due to possible duplicates/variants.

service_type.description: Categorical descriptor of vendor service (e.g., Quality Inspection, Transport). Low-cardinality field.
service_type.analysis_use: Group-by and aggregation (counts, distribution) and filtering to identify vendors by service. Useful for pivoting or segmentation.
business_questions: How many vendors provide 'Transport' vs 'Cold Storage'?
Which service_type has the highest vendor count in a specific city?
service_type.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN(...) for filters. Because cardinality is low, GROUP BY service_type is efficient. Consider mapping to a normalized lookup (service_type_id) in NL2SQL generation to avoid spelling variants.

contact_name.description: Primary contact person at the vendor.
contact_name.analysis_use: Used for reporting, contact lookups, and operator workflows. Can be used in WHERE with exact or partial match.
business_questions: Who is the primary contact for vendor_id = 'B2BV2001'?
List vendors where contact_name contains 'Aarna' for outreach.
contact_name.nl2sql_hints: String field — use TRIM/LOWER for robust matching. Expect unique values per vendor; use equals or LIKE for searches. Not recommended as a join key.

phone.description: Vendor contact phone number (string including country code).
phone.analysis_use: Lookup and deduplication (matching same phone across vendors). Filtering for contact outreach, verification and data quality checks (format validation).
business_questions: Provide phone numbers for all vendors in Ahmedabad.
Detect vendors sharing the same phone number (possible duplicates).
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone stored as nvarchar with inconsistent formatting; consider normalizing (remove spaces/symbols) before equality matches. Use LIKE for partial searches. Use phone when matching duplicates if gst_no/vendor_id is absent.

email.analysis_use: Contact retrieval and deduplication. Email domain analysis (GROUP BY SUBSTRING after @) to understand vendor email patterns. Filtering for communication lists.
business_questions: List vendor emails for logistics partners for outreach.
Which vendors use corporate partner.in email addresses vs other domains?
email.nl2sql_hints: Email may contain typos or commas (observed). Clean/validate email format before using in equality comparisons. Use LOWER() for case-insensitive comparisons and LIKE '%@domain' for domain grouping.

credit_terms.description: Payment terms offered to vendor (NET 7, NET 15, NET 30). Low cardinality (3 values).
credit_terms.analysis_use: Group-by and filters to analyze payment term distribution, aging analysis when joined with invoices/payments, and to segment vendors in financial reports.
business_questions: How many vendors are on NET 7 vs NET 30?
What is the average invoice aging for vendors with NET 7 compared to NET 30? (requires join to invoices)
credit_terms.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Use equality or IN(...) in WHERE. When joining to payment/invoice tables, correlate credit_terms to payment aging. Standardize terms (e.g., trim whitespace) because small variants would break matches.

gst_no.description: Tax identification number for the vendor (GST number). High-cardinality and suitable for legal/entity-level identity.
gst_no.analysis_use: Authoritative deduplication/join key across systems, regulatory reporting, and grouping by legal entity. Useful for joins to external vendor registries.
business_questions: Are there duplicate vendor records sharing the same gst_no?
Retrieve vendor details by gst_no for compliance checks.
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; remove non-digit formatting if present before matching. Prefer gst_no for cross-system joins over vendor_name. Index gst_no if used frequently for lookups.

city.description: City where the vendor is located.
city.analysis_use: Geographic segmentation, group-by counts, filtering for location-based vendor selection and logistics planning.
business_questions: How many vendors are located in Vadodara vs Ahmedabad?
Which cities have the most Cold Storage partners?
city.nl2sql_hints: Use equality/IN and GROUP BY city. Standardize city names (case, spelling) to prevent fragmentation. Consider joining to a cities/regions lookup if available for hierarchical queries.

created_at.description: Ingestion or creation timestamp stored as nvarchar; currently appears to hold a single repeated value across rows (2025-12-02 11:03:52), indicating batch load metadata rather than per-row creation time.
created_at.analysis_use: If converted to proper datetime, it can be used to track when vendor records were added or updated. Currently useful to identify ingestion batch/time; not useful for temporal analysis until normalized and populated per row.
business_questions: When was the vendor master last bulk-loaded or refreshed?
Which vendors were added after a specific date? (requires per-row accurate datetime values)
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — convert/cast to datetime in queries (e.g., TRY_CAST or CONVERT) before temporal filters. Verify value consistency; if it's a constant ingestion timestamp, do not use for per-vendor temporal analysis until corrected. Handle parsing errors for unexpected formats.

slot_id.description: Unique identifier for the delivery slot (string). Appears to be distinct per row (80 distinct).
slot_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for selecting or referencing a specific slot; useful for deduplication, point lookups, and joins to booking/allocations tables if they reference this ID.
business_questions: What are the details (date, time, capacity, availability) for a given slot_id?
Which slots were created within the last X days and what are their slot_ids?
slot_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a stable string key. Use exact matching for identification (WHERE slot_id = '...'). If used for joins, ensure the target column is same-cased and trimmed. Avoid LIKE unless partial matching is intentional. No casting needed but trim whitespace and normalize case if inconsistent.

date.description: Date of the delivery slot stored as nvarchar (format appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD').
date.analysis_use: Common filter and group-by field for time-based reporting; requires casting to DATE for range queries, date arithmetic, ordering, and proper comparison.
business_questions: How many slots are available per centre for a given date?
Show availability trend across dates (available_orders summed) for the next 7 days.
date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to a DATE type in NL2SQL conversions (e.g., CAST([date] AS DATE) or PARSE([date] USING 'yyyy-MM-dd')). Watch out for nulls and malformed strings. Because 'date' is a SQL reserved word in some dialects, ensure proper quoting/escaping in generated SQL (e.g., "date" or [date]). Use date ranges (>=, <=) after casting.

start_time.description: Start time of the slot stored as nvarchar (format 'HH:MM'). Low cardinality (5 distinct).
start_time.analysis_use: Filter or group by time window; order by start_time to find earliest slots. Often combined with date for ordering and availability searches. May require casting to TIME or using string sort if zero-padded HH:MM.
business_questions: What are the earliest start times with available capacity for a specific date?
Which start_time windows have the most total capacity across centres?
start_time.nl2sql_hints: If consistent HH:MM zero-padded format, string ordering matches chronological order; otherwise cast to TIME (CAST(start_time AS TIME)). Normalize values (trim). For queries like 'earliest slot' order by CAST(date AS DATE), CAST(start_time AS TIME).

end_time.description: End time of the slot stored as nvarchar (format 'HH:MM'). Paired with start_time to describe the time window.
end_time.analysis_use: Used with start_time to define the window length and to filter by windows overlapping a requested delivery time. Can help compute slot duration after casting to TIME.
business_questions: Which slots have a duration longer than X hours?
Show all slots that overlap a requested time range (e.g., 10:00-12:00) on a given date.
end_time.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIME to compute durations or check overlaps (e.g., CAST(end_time AS TIME) > CAST(:requested_start AS TIME)). Ensure consistent formatting; treat NULLs carefully. Use both start_time and end_time when ranking/ordering slots or checking overlaps.

capacity_orders.description: Declared capacity (maximum orders) for the slot stored as nvarchar numeric string.
capacity_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate measure for total capacity calculations (SUM, AVG), compute utilization (capacity - available or booked), and filter for slots with large capacity. Must be cast to integer/numeric before numeric operations.
business_questions: What is the total capacity across all centres for a given date or date range?
Which slots have capacity greater than N orders?
capacity_orders.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer/decimal before aggregation (e.g., CAST(capacity_orders AS INT)). Handle non-numeric or NULL values defensively (TRY_CAST or CASE WHEN ISNUMERIC()). Consider normalizing and storing as integer in upstream ETL to simplify queries.

available_orders.description: Remaining number of orders that can still be booked for the slot, stored as nvarchar numeric string.
available_orders.analysis_use: Primary field for availability queries (filtering slots with availability, sorting by availability). Used to compute booked = capacity - available (after casting). Useful for alerts (availability below threshold) and capacity planning.
business_questions: Which slots have available_orders > 0 for today or a user-selected date?
List slots with the lowest availability to prioritize capacity increases.
available_orders.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INT for comparisons and arithmetic. Use safe casting (TRY_CAST / ISNUMERIC checks) to avoid runtime errors. Beware of negative or zero values; confirm business semantics of 0. When answering NL queries like 'available slots', interpret as available_orders > 0 unless user asks for capacity as well.

centre_id.description: Human-readable identifier/name of the fulfilment centre (e.g., 'Rajkot Packaging Unit'), stored as nvarchar. Only 3 distinct values in the sample.
centre_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering dimension for location-based reporting. Join key candidate to centre metadata tables to enrich with centre codes, addresses, or region hierarchy.
business_questions: How does slot availability vary by centre for a given date or across dates?
Show all available slots for 'Surat Distribution Centre' for the next X days.
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality filters (WHERE centre_id = '...') or IN lists. If external centre master exists, join on the normalized centre identifier; beware of differences in naming (case/typos). If centre names are long, consider normalizing to centre_code in ETL. Use GROUP BY centre_id for aggregates.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the slot row was created/inserted, stored as nvarchar (format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'). Distinct per row.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and temporal analysis: detect when slots were added or updated (if separate updated column exists), filter recent additions, or compute latency between creation and slot date. Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons and ordering.
business_questions: Which slots were added in the last 7 days and what centres/dates do they affect?
Show slots created most recently to verify recent schedule changes.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for range filters (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Handle timezone considerations if applicable. Ensure consistent format; use TRY_CAST to gracefully handle malformed strings. Use ORDER BY CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) DESC to get newest rows.

delivery_id.description: Identifier for the delivery instance. Multiple event rows per delivery_id represent the lifecycle of that delivery.
delivery_id.analysis_use: Group and filter to get per-delivery timelines, counts, and final status. Use in dedup/aggregation to compute first/last event per delivery.
business_questions: What is the final status distribution (DELIVERED/FAILED) per delivery_id?
How many events are recorded on average per delivery?
delivery_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; treat as a string key. Expect many repeated values (multiple events per delivery). Use WHERE delivery_id = 'DEL9001' for point lookups and GROUP BY delivery_id for per-delivery aggregates. Consider casting/normalizing when joining to a deliveries table if that table uses a different type.

shipment_id.description: Identifier for the shipment that this delivery event is part of. May group multiple deliveries or relate to carrier tracking.
shipment_id.analysis_use: Filter and group to analyze shipment-level progress and correlate with carrier or dispatch tables. Useful for joining to shipment metadata.
business_questions: Which shipments have the highest number of failed delivery events?
What percent of shipments reach DELIVERED status within SLA?
shipment_id.nl2sql_hints: String key. Join candidates: shipments or dispatch tables. Use exact string equality for joins. If other tables use different capitalization or types, cast or LOWER both sides. Index on shipment_id beneficial for frequent joins/filters.

order_id.description: Identifier for the order associated with this delivery event. Connects events back to the originating order.
order_id.analysis_use: Join to orders to attribute delivery performance to customers, revenue, or products. Group to get order-level delivery outcomes or to find orders with multiple delivery events.
business_questions: How many orders ended with a failed delivery versus delivered?
Which high-value orders experienced delivery delays or failures?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: String key; commonly joined to ecom_orders/b2b_orders/orders tables on the order_id column. Be aware event_ts is nvarchar: use proper casting for time-based joins. Use equality filters; if multiple order id formats exist, normalize before joining.

event_ts.description: Timestamp of the delivery event as a string (nvarchar). Records when the event occurred.
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal field for ordering events, computing durations between events (e.g., LOADED -> DELIVERED), building time series, and filtering by time windows. Requires casting to datetime/timestamp for correct temporal operations.
business_questions: What is the average time from LOADED to DELIVERED by location?
How many delivery events occurred per hour/day over a given time range?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar (high cardinality). Always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type in queries for range filters, ordering, and interval arithmetic (e.g., CAST(event_ts AS datetime) or TRY_CAST to avoid errors). Use explicit timezone handling if needed. For NL2SQL, map natural-language time spans to proper CAST + BETWEEN clauses.

event_type.description: Categorical label for the kind of event recorded (e.g., LOADED, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, FAILED_ATTEMPT, DELIVERED).
event_type.analysis_use: Group and count events by type for distribution analysis, transition sequence analysis, and SLA monitoring. Useful for event-sequence queries (e.g., find LOADED then FAILED_ATTEMPT).
business_questions: What proportion of events are OUT_FOR_DELIVERY vs IN_TRANSIT vs DELIVERED?
Which event types commonly precede a FAILED_ATTEMPT?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string; use equality/in-list filters (event_type IN (...)). Consider normalizing capitalization. For sequencing queries, use window functions partitioned by delivery_id ordered by CAST(event_ts AS datetime).

location.description: Named location (city/region) where the event took place (e.g., Surat, Vadodara).
location.analysis_use: Group and filter to measure location-level delivery performance and volumes. Combine with GPS for finer geospatial analysis.
business_questions: Which cities have the highest failed delivery rates?
How does average delivery time vary across locations?
location.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality string—good for GROUP BY. Beware inconsistent naming; consider trimming/normalizing. Use equality or IN-list filters. For mapping, join to a location dimension if available.

status.description: Current high-level delivery status label for the event (e.g., FAILED, IN_TRANSIT, DELIVERED).
status.analysis_use: Primary aggregation dimension for success/failure metrics and KPIs. Useful to compute end-state (final status per delivery) and trend analysis over time.
business_questions: What percent of events are in FAILED status over the last 7 days?
How has the delivered rate trended month-over-month?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string; often used in WHERE or GROUP BY. To get final status per delivery, use ROW_NUMBER() over partition by delivery_id ordered by CAST(event_ts AS datetime) DESC and take first row. Normalize string values for consistent filtering.

updated_by.description: Name of the user/operator or system that recorded the event.
updated_by.analysis_use: Operator-level performance analysis (failure rates, event volumes per operator), auditing, and identifying training opportunities. High-cardinality; useful for top-n queries.
business_questions: Which operators record the most failed attempts?
Who are the top contributors in terms of events logged per day?
updated_by.nl2sql_hints: High-cardinality string; use GROUP BY for top-N and aggregates. For fuzzy matching or partial names, use ILIKE/LIKE after normalizing. Consider mapping to an operator_id if a users table exists for robust joins.

gps_lat.description: Latitude coordinate of where the event was recorded, stored as nvarchar.
gps_lat.analysis_use: Geospatial clustering, radius queries (e.g., deliveries within X km of a point), heatmaps, and verifying location vs claimed city. Must cast to numeric for calculations.
business_questions: What delivery clusters have the highest failure concentration when mapped geographically?
Are there repeated GPS coordinates associated with failed deliveries?
gps_lat.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—CAST to FLOAT/DECIMAL (e.g., CAST(gps_lat AS FLOAT)) before any numeric or geospatial operation. Handle parsing errors with TRY_CAST. For NL2SQL, convert natural-language geo requests (e.g., 'within 5km') into Haversine or spatial queries after casting.

gps_long.description: Longitude coordinate of where the event was recorded, stored as nvarchar.
gps_long.analysis_use: Pair with gps_lat for geospatial analysis (heatmaps, distance calculations, clustering, route reconstruction).
business_questions: What is the route density for deliveries between warehouse and customer clusters?
Do failed deliveries occur near certain road segments or coordinates?
gps_long.nl2sql_hints: Same considerations as gps_lat: CAST to numeric type before use. Use paired casting and validate both coordinates are non-null and numeric before spatial calculations. Consider storing as geography/geometry in a normalized table for performant spatial queries.

photo_url.description: URL or filename to a photo captured at the event (e.g., delivery proof). Currently 100% NULL in this dataset.
photo_url.analysis_use: When populated, used for proof-of-delivery audits, QA, and dispute resolution. Could be used to sample images for quality checks.
business_questions: What fraction of DELIVERED events have a delivery photo attached?
Can we sample photos for quality control of deliveries at specific locations?
photo_url.nl2sql_hints: Column is all NULL currently—treat as optional. Use IS NOT NULL to filter populated records. If populated, ensure URLs are normalized and consider storing in a separate media table to avoid sparse columns.

notes.description: Short free-text notes attached to the event (comments from driver/operator). Currently 100% NULL in this dataset.
notes.analysis_use: When populated, used for qualitative context (reasons for failure, customer instructions). Can be mined for keywords / NLP to categorize reasons for failure.
business_questions: What are the common note keywords associated with FAILED deliveries?
Can notes help explain repeated failed attempts for a specific order?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Free text and sparse. Use full-text search or LIKE/ILIKE for keyword queries. Currently empty—do not rely on it until populated. Consider moving to a text/blob store if notes become large.

test_id.description: Unique identifier of the A/B test (experiment). Appears distinct for each row (80 distinct).
test_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for grouping, deduplication, selecting single experiments, and joining to event/assignment logs if those contain the same id.
business_questions: What are the details (start/end/status/winner) for a given test_id?
Which test_ids had the largest conversion uplift between variants?
test_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string in SQL unless matched by other tables. Use exact equality (WHERE test_id = '...') for lookups. If joining to event tables, ensure the event table's experiment id uses the same format/casing; consider trimming and normalizing whitespace. Consider adding an index on test_id or making it a primary key if duplicates don't exist.

test_name.description: Human-readable name of the experiment (e.g., 'Pricing Banner Test'). Low cardinality (3 unique names here).
test_name.analysis_use: Grouping and aggregation by experiment type for macro-level summaries; filtering to analyze a specific experiment category across multiple runs.
business_questions: How many runs of the 'Checkout Flow Experiment' have been executed and what were their outcomes?
Which test_name category yields the highest average conversion rate improvement?
test_name.nl2sql_hints: Use GROUP BY test_name or WHERE test_name = '...'. Watch for name normalization (case/whitespace). Low cardinality is good for grouping; consider creating a dimension table if richer metadata is needed.

variant_a.description: Label for variant A in the test (string). In this dataset it is uniformly 'Control'.
variant_a.analysis_use: Used primarily for documentation; not useful for distinguishing experiments here because it's constant. In other datasets it can be used to map labels to treatment definitions.
business_questions: Is the A variant consistently used as Control across experiments?
Are there tests where variant_a label changes (indicating different control definitions)?
variant_a.nl2sql_hints: Generally a string label; not a join key. No aggregation needed beyond DISTINCT to validate labels. If variant labels vary in other datasets, normalize before using them in comparisons.

variant_b.description: Label for variant B in the test (string). In this dataset it is uniformly 'Treatment'.
variant_b.analysis_use: Like variant_a, mainly descriptive. Can be used to map B to treatment metadata in other tables if available.
business_questions: Is variant_b consistently named 'Treatment' or are there variant-specific labels to join to config tables?
Which experiments used a particular treatment label and how did they perform?
variant_b.nl2sql_hints: Treat as descriptive string. If enriching with variant metadata, ensure consistent labels and consider trimming/case normalization. Not useful for calculations unless variant-level metadata is present elsewhere.

start_ts.description: Experiment start timestamp stored as nvarchar (ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'). Distinct per experiment.
start_ts.analysis_use: Filter experiments by start date, calculate experiment duration (with end_ts), trend analysis over time, and time-window based cohorting. Often needs CAST/CONVERT to datetime to use date functions and indexes.
business_questions: Which experiments started in August 2025?
How many experiments started per week/month and how does that trend change over time?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Important to CAST/CONVERT to a native datetime type before comparisons: e.g., CAST(start_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST for heterogeneous values. When building NL2SQL queries, prefer canonical ISO format assumptions; include timezone handling if present. For performance, recommend storing as DATETIME in production and indexing on start_ts for time-range queries.

end_ts.description: Experiment end timestamp stored as nvarchar (ISO-like). Distinct per experiment.
end_ts.analysis_use: Used with start_ts to compute experiment duration, filter completed experiments by end date, and analyze recency. Needs casting to datetime for arithmetic and date functions.
business_questions: Which experiments ended in the last 30 days?
What is the distribution of experiment durations (end_ts - start_ts)?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to datetime for date math. Handle NULLs for experiments that are still RUNNING or CANCELLED without an end date. Use TRY_CAST to avoid errors from malformed strings. Consider creating computed columns with proper datetime types for efficient querying.

status.description: Current lifecycle state of the experiment: RUNNING, CANCELLED, or COMPLETED.
status.analysis_use: Filter experiments by lifecycle stage, aggregate counts by status, and analyze correlations between status and other metrics (e.g., cancelled tests vs short durations).
business_questions: How many experiments are currently RUNNING vs COMPLETED vs CANCELLED?
Are cancelled experiments more likely to have low sample conversion rates?
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Use equality or IN filters (e.g., WHERE status = 'COMPLETED'). Normalize casing and trim spaces. Consider indexing status for frequent status-based queries and ensure allowed values are validated at ingestion to avoid inconsistencies.

winner_variant.description: Which variant won at the experiment conclusion: 'A', 'B', or 'NONE' (no winner determined).
winner_variant.analysis_use: Aggregate counts of winners across experiments, compute win rates by test_name, and analyze effect sizes where winners are declared.
business_questions: Across completed experiments, how many were won by variant B vs A?
Which test types (test_name) produce the most B-wins?
winner_variant.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use WHERE winner_variant IN ('A','B','NONE'). For NL2SQL, watch for synonyms ('NONE','NO_WIN') and ensure queries filter only completed experiments if winner determination only applies to COMPLETED status. Consider deriving a boolean column (is_winner_B) for simpler filters.

conversion_rate_a.description: Observed conversion rate metric for variant A, stored as nvarchar. Values represent percentage or proportion (e.g., '3.25', '7.34'), but stored as text with decimal artifacts.
conversion_rate_a.analysis_use: Numeric comparisons with conversion_rate_b, average conversion for A across experiments, uplift calculations, and statistical prechecks. Must be cast to numeric/decimal for aggregation and arithmetic.
business_questions: What is the mean and median conversion_rate_a across completed experiments?
Which experiments had conversion_rate_a above a target threshold (e.g., >5%)?
conversion_rate_a.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST or TRY_CAST to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., TRY_CAST(conversion_rate_a AS DECIMAL(10,4))). Handle malformed strings and NULLs with TRY_CAST and COALESCE. Be explicit about units (percent vs proportion). When comparing A vs B, ensure both columns are cast to the same numeric type.

conversion_rate_b.description: Observed conversion rate metric for variant B, stored as nvarchar. Similar format considerations as conversion_rate_a.
conversion_rate_b.analysis_use: Compare B against A to compute uplift, aggregate statistics for treatment performance, and feed into significance/AB test evaluation.
business_questions: Which experiments show a positive uplift (conversion_rate_b - conversion_rate_a > 0)?
What is the average uplift (B - A) for completed experiments?
conversion_rate_b.nl2sql_hints: Same casting/validation guidance as conversion_rate_a. Use TRY_CAST and define numeric precision. When calculating uplift, explicitly CAST both columns and consider NULL and non-numeric handling. If conversion rates are percentages, ensure consistent interpretation (e.g., 3.25 means 3.25%).

created_at.description: Timestamp when the experiment metadata row was created, stored as nvarchar.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion tracking, filter experiments created within a time window, and determine latency between creation and start.
business_questions: How long after creation do experiments typically start (start_ts - created_at)?
How many experiments were created in the last month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to datetime for time-based filters and calculations. Use TRY_CAST to protect against malformed strings. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit date range filters (WHERE CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) BETWEEN ...). Consider creating a native datetime column for production use for performance.

agent_id.description: Unique identifier for the acquisition agent (e.g., 'ECOMAG3001').
agent_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup key for the agent; use for grouping by agent, joining to transactional tables to attribute actions/revenue, filtering for specific agents.
business_questions: Which agent (agent_id) generated the most acquisitions or orders (requires join to order table)?
Get full contact and status for a given agent_id.
agent_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string (nvarchar). Although distinct values indicate uniqueness, there is no declared PK. Exact equality and IN filters are common (agent_id = 'ECOMAG3001'). agent_id has a stable prefix — use pattern matching (LIKE 'ECOMAG%') if searching ranges. Consider recommending an index/PK for faster joins.

name.description: Full name of the acquisition agent.
name.analysis_use: Primarily for display, reporting and human-readable results; can be used in GROUP BY if you want reports by agent name (but prefer agent_id).
business_questions: List active agents' names and contacts in a specific area.
Find agents by partial name search (e.g., search for all agents with 'Anvi' in their name).
name.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar; prefer case-insensitive searches and LIKE '%term%' for partial matches. Avoid using name for joins — use agent_id or email/phone instead.

phone.description: Agent phone number including country code and formatting (stored as text).
phone.analysis_use: Contact list extraction, de-duplication analysis, and potential join key to systems that store phone as contact identifier.
business_questions: Provide a phone contact list for all ACTIVE agents.
Are there duplicate phone numbers across agents?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored with spaces and '+'; normalize (remove non-digits, standardize country code) before joins or equality comparisons. Use TRIM/REPLACE/REGEXP to normalize in NL2SQL-generated queries.

email.analysis_use: Contact outreach lists, de-duplication, and potential join key to user or CRM tables that store email as identifier.
business_questions: List emails for agents in a given acq_channel for an email campaign.
Find agents whose records were created but have missing/invalid email addresses.
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; normalize case (LOWER) and trim whitespace for joins. Validate format if used for deduplication. Use equality or LIKE for domain-based queries (e.g., '%@dailygreens.in').

acq_channel.description: Acquisition channel through which the agent was onboarded (e.g., 'Social Media', 'Influencer').
acq_channel.analysis_use: Categorical dimension for grouping and aggregation to measure channel contributions (counts of agents by channel) and to join/compare channel performance across datasets.
business_questions: How many agents came from each acquisition channel?
Which channels provide agents concentrated in specific areas?
acq_channel.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — use equality, IN lists, or GROUP BY aggregation. Normalize synonyms/case (e.g., 'Facebook Ads' vs 'facebook ads'). Consider mapping channels into standardized categories in queries.

area.description: Geographic area or city for the agent (e.g., 'Surat', 'Rajkot').
area.analysis_use: Group and filter to analyze geographic distribution of agents, map to operational regions, or join to geographic tables for region-level rollups.
business_questions: Which areas have the most acquisition agents?
List active agents in a specific area for operations or routing.
area.nl2sql_hints: Categorical text — normalize casing and common misspellings. Use GROUP BY and ORDER BY for top-area queries. If integrating with a locations table, consider standardizing naming to enable clean joins.

join_date.description: Agent join date stored as a string in YYYY-MM-DD format.
join_date.analysis_use: Cohort and tenure analysis (after casting to DATE), hires-over-time reporting, and filtering agents who joined before/after a certain date.
business_questions: How many agents were onboarded each month or quarter?
Which agents joined before a certain date (e.g., prior to 2022-01-01)?
join_date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — convert to DATE (CAST/TRY_CONVERT) before temporal comparisons or grouping. The ISO-like format makes casting straightforward, but queries should defensively handle invalid/NULL strings. Use DATE_TRUNC/YEAR/MONTH on the cast value for period grouping.

status.description: Operational status of the agent (ACTIVE or INACTIVE).
status.analysis_use: Filtering for active workforce, calculating active/inactive counts, churn or retention analysis when combined with join_date/created_at and transaction history.
business_questions: How many agents are currently ACTIVE vs INACTIVE?
What is the distribution of ACTIVE agents by acq_channel or area?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with only a few values — use equality checks (status = 'ACTIVE') and GROUP BY status for counts. Normalize case and possible synonyms ('active', 'ACTIVE'). Consider adding a boolean computed column for quick filtering in NL2SQL.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the agent record was created in the system, stored as a string 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and recency queries (e.g., newest records), time-window filters for incremental syncs, and ordering for recent changes after casting to DATETIME.
business_questions: Which agent records were created in the last 30/90 days?
Show newly added agents (sorted by created_at) for onboarding follow-up.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for range filters and ordering. Confirm timezone semantics if mixing with other timestamped tables. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to safely handle malformed strings; prefer comparisons on the casted value.

audit_id.description: Unique identifier for the audit log entry. Presently distinct for all rows sampled.
audit_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for individual audit records; useful for retrieving or deduplicating specific log entries. Not typically aggregated but can be used to count unique events or enforce uniqueness.
business_questions: Retrieve the full audit record for audit_id = 'AUD004001'.
How many unique audit entries exist for a given time window?
audit_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique string key (nvarchar). Use equality filters (WHERE audit_id = '...') for precise retrieval. Consider adding a PK/index in production. Expect no joins on this column.

entity_type.description: Type/category of the entity that the action applies to (examples: PAYMENT, CUSTOMER, ORDER, PRODUCT).
entity_type.analysis_use: High-value grouping/filtering dimension used to segment logs by domain. Useful for aggregations, conditional joins (to different tables depending on type), and trend analysis per domain.
business_questions: How many actions were logged per entity_type over the past week?
Which entity_type has the highest proportion of DELETE actions?
entity_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; use exact equality or IN(...) for filters. When joining to other tables choose target table based on entity_type value. Avoid blind joins — use CASE or separate queries per entity_type or a conditional JOIN using entity_type predicates.

entity_id.description: Identifier of the specific entity instance that was acted upon. Values contain prefixed IDs (e.g., CUST..., EORD...).
entity_id.analysis_use: Key for entity-level auditing: filter to history of a particular object, compute last action per entity, aggregate action counts per entity. Potential join key to domain tables if formats align.
business_questions: What is the audit history (all actions) for entity_id = 'EORD2086'?
Which entities had the most status changes in the last 90 days?
entity_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; equality matches are common (WHERE entity_id = '...'). If target tables use numeric IDs, you may need to STRIP prefix (e.g., remove 'CUST' or 'EORD') and cast to integer before joining. Use LIKE 'prefix%' to filter by entity type if entity_type is missing or inconsistent. Indexing entity_id improves entity-specific lookups.

action.description: Type of action performed on the entity (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, STATUS_CHANGE).
action.analysis_use: Primary categorical variable for aggregations, filtering suspicious actions, and change-type analysis. Useful to compute counts, rates, and to pivot by action type.
business_questions: How many DELETE actions were recorded in the last month and which entities were affected?
What is the ratio of CREATE vs UPDATE actions for orders?
action.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Use equality or IN for filters. Consider normalizing if new action types are added. For time-series, GROUP BY action and time bucket (after casting action_ts).

action_ts.description: Timestamp for when the action occurred, stored as an nvarchar string in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format in the sample.
action_ts.analysis_use: Critical for ordering, time-based aggregations, window functions (e.g., last action per entity), and trend analysis. Must be cast to a native datetime type for correct comparisons and efficient range scans.
business_questions: Show actions per hour/day for the last 7 days to identify usage peaks.
What was the last action time for each entity_id?
action_ts.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, the sample uses ISO-like format — safe to CAST/CONVERT to datetime in queries (e.g., CAST(action_ts AS datetime) or PARSE). For NL2SQL, explicitly interpret as timestamp and use time-based functions (DATE_TRUNC, BETWEEN). Recommend creating a persisted computed column of type datetime or converting and indexing action_ts for efficient time-range queries.

performed_by.description: Identifier of who performed the action (examples observed: system, support_agent, admin).
performed_by.analysis_use: Used for user/activity attribution, counting actions per actor, permission/audit checks, and detecting anomalous actor behavior. Useful in joins to user/admin tables for enrichment.
business_questions: Which actor performed the most STATUS_CHANGE actions in the previous month?
List DELETE actions performed by 'support_agent' in the last 30 days.
performed_by.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; use equality or IN filters. If mapping to a users table, confirm the performed_by values match user identifiers — may require mapping table or normalization. Consider indexing for frequent actor-based queries.

ip_address.description: Source IP address of the actor when the action was performed (IPv4 format observed).
ip_address.analysis_use: Security and anomaly detection: identify unusual or repeated IPs, geo-location enrichment, clustering by IP, or rate-limiting analysis. Can be aggregated to count distinct IPs or flagged for suspicious patterns (many deletes from same IP).
business_questions: Which IP addresses performed the highest number of DELETE actions in the last 90 days?
Are there IPs that performed actions across many different entity_ids in a short time window (possible automated/scripted activity)?
ip_address.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; use equality, GROUP BY, DISTINCT. For partial matching or ranges, use LIKE or split into octets if needed. For geolocation lookups, join or call external IP-to-geo functions. Consider normalizing/storing as varchar(45) if IPv6 support is required.

notes.description: Optional short notes about the action. In the sample this column is fully null.
notes.analysis_use: Free-text enrichment for human-readable context; can be used for filtering when populated, or for basic keyword search. Low utility in this sample due to all NULLs.
business_questions: Show actions where notes mention a manual override or reason for deletion.
Which actions have non-empty notes and what do they say (for audit review)?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Nullable nvarchar; use WHERE notes IS NOT NULL or notes <> '' to find entries. For NL2SQL, prefer exact or keyword searches (LIKE '%term%') and beware of case-sensitivity depending on DB collation. If notes remain unused, consider dropping or expanding length and adding structured fields for common reasons.

browse_id.description: Unique identifier for the browsing event/row (string). Appears to be an event-level id.
browse_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for de-duplication and sessionization. Useful for counting events, deduplicating, and as a grouping key when reconstructing user sessions.
business_questions: How many distinct browsing events occurred in a period?
Are there duplicate or reprocessed events (identify duplicate browse_id)?
browse_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as event-level unique id. Use in COUNT(DISTINCT browse_id) for event counts. May be nullable; check and filter NULLs. If present as unique, it can be a primary key candidate. No CAST needed (string), but trim whitespace and ensure consistent casing when matching.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who generated the event (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and group by customer for per-user metrics, compute unique-user counts, join to customer profile tables for attributes (segment, LTV). Useful for sessionization and per-customer funnels.
business_questions: Which customers performed the most searches or product views?
How many unique customers were active in the last 7/30 days?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Nullable and medium cardinality; use COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) for unique user metrics. Common join key to ecom_customers (ensure same format/casing). Use COALESCE/NULL filters and trim; if inconsistent, normalize (UPPER/LTRIM/RTRIM) before joins/filters.

sku.description: Product SKU related to the event (string). For VIEW_PRODUCT and possibly other events.
sku.analysis_use: Join to products/product_variants to get product attributes (name, category, price). Group and aggregate to find top viewed products and interest signals. Filter to look at events for a specific product.
business_questions: Which SKUs receive the most views?
Which SKUs are frequently searched for or clicked from specific referrers?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — good for GROUP BY. Join to product tables on SKU (ensure consistent SKU key naming). Be mindful of NULLs for events that are not product-specific (e.g., CATEGORY_CLICK). Normalize casing and trim when matching to product tables.

event_ts.description: Event timestamp stored as nvarchar (string) representing when the event occurred.
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary field for time-based filtering, aggregations (hourly/daily), windowing for sessionization and funnels. Important for ordering events per user and computing time-to-conversion.
business_questions: What is the hourly trend of browsing events?
How long between a user's first view and subsequent search or purchase (requires joins)?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — CAST to DATETIME (or equivalent) for accurate time computations: e.g., CAST(event_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST depending on engine. Be explicit about timezone assumptions. Use WHERE CAST(event_ts AS DATETIME) BETWEEN '2025-10-01' AND '2025-10-31'. Indexes on parsed datetime or a materialized/normalized datetime column will speed queries. Handle malformed/null values with TRY_CAST or safe parsing.

event_type.description: Type of browsing event (enumeration): e.g., CATEGORY_CLICK, SEARCH, VIEW_PRODUCT.
event_type.analysis_use: Segment behaviour by event type (funnels, conversion steps). Good low-cardinality grouping for counts and percentages.
business_questions: What proportion of events are SEARCH vs VIEW_PRODUCT vs CATEGORY_CLICK?
Which event_type most often leads to product detail views or purchases (needs join)?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical column — ideal GROUP BY. Use equality filters (event_type = 'SEARCH'). Normalize values (UPPER/TRIM) if user-supplied. Consider mapping to an integer enum in ETL for performance if used heavily in WHERE clauses.

search_query.description: The user's search text for SEARCH events; often NULL for non-search events.
search_query.analysis_use: Aggregate popular search terms, compute search-to-view conversions, build keyword-driven merchandising. Used for full-text or pattern matching analysis and mapping to normalized queries.
business_questions: Which search queries are most frequent and which lead to product views?
Are there common searches with no matching products (zero-results) that need catalog updates?
search_query.nl2sql_hints: Text field — use exact grouping for cleaned terms or full-text / LIKE for partial matches. Normalize (lowercase, trim) and possibly map synonyms/stemmed forms.
