Join to normalized search keyword table if available. Handle NULLs for non-SEARCH events. Use COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE search_query IS NOT NULL) or WHERE event_type='SEARCH'.

device_type.analysis_use: Segment events by device for UX/marketing insights, device-specific funnels, and A/B testing analysis.
business_questions: Which devices generate the most browsing events and views?
Do conversion rates differ by device type (requires join to orders)?
device_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical column. Use in GROUP BY and WHERE filters. Normalize strings (lowercase) and coalesce NULLs into 'unknown'. Good candidate for pivoting. Be careful of typos or variants (e.g., 'iOS' vs 'ios').

referrer_source.description: Traffic source/referrer (FB Ads, WhatsApp, Google, Direct).
referrer_source.analysis_use: Attribution and channel performance: group by referrer to understand which channels drive searches, views, and later conversions when joined with orders.
business_questions: Which referrer sources drive the highest volume of product views and searches?
Which referrers bring higher-intent users (more VIEW_PRODUCT events per search)?
referrer_source.nl2sql_hints: Categorical column for GROUP BY and attribution analysis. Normalize names (e.g., 'FB Ads' vs 'Facebook Ads') and coalesce nulls. When combining with marketing campaign data, ensure consistent naming or map to canonical channel ids.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the record was created or ingested into the raw table (string). May differ from event_ts.
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for ingestion latency analysis, debugging reprocessing, and incremental ETL (e.g., selecting rows WHERE created_at > last_run). Also can serve as a fallback for event ordering if event_ts missing or malformed.
business_questions: What is the ingestion latency between event_ts and created_at?
Which events were ingested in the last ETL window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar — CAST/TRY_CAST to DATETIME for time comparisons. Use created_at for incremental pulls: WHERE CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) > @last_checkpoint. Normalize timezone and handle malformed values. Prefer indexing or storing a parsed datetime column for frequent incremental queries.

cart_item_id.description: Surrogate identifier for the cart line item (unique id per item row).
cart_item_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de‑duplicating rows, tracking lifecycle of a specific cart entry, and joining back to raw logs. Useful in dedup and change-tracking queries.
business_questions: How many unique cart item records were created in a period?
Are there duplicate or repeated cart_item_id values indicating ingestion issues?
cart_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Use equality joins and distinct counts. Since it appears unique, it can be used as a row identifier. No casting required, but trim whitespace; beware NULLs if present.

cart_id.description: Identifier for the shopping cart that contains this item (links the item to a multi‑item cart).
cart_id.analysis_use: Group and aggregate items to produce cart-level metrics (items per cart, total cart value). Join key to ecom_carts and ecom_orders for customer, session and order enrichment.
business_questions: What is average number of items per cart (group by cart_id)?
Which carts have the highest nominal cart value at add time?
cart_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; commonly used in GROUP BY and joins. Expect many-to-one (multiple items per cart). When joining to ecom_carts use exact string match; consider normalizing case and trimming if mismatches occur.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit code identifying the product model at a SKU level (product-level identifier).
sku.analysis_use: Categorical grouping for popularity, frequency, and share-of-cart analyses; join to products/ecom_products for attributes (category, name). Useful for top-N queries.
business_questions: Which SKUs are added to carts most frequently?
Which SKUs contribute most to cart-added revenue (price_at_add * qty)?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text and highly categorical; use equality or IN filters. Join to product tables on sku (string equality). Consider mapping synonyms or trimming. Use GROUP BY sku for top-N queries.

qty.description: Quantity of the SKU added to the cart for this cart_item. Stored as nvarchar but represents an integer.
qty.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to compute total units added, average qty per item, and compute revenue when multiplied by price_at_add. Filter for extreme quantities or outliers.
business_questions: What is the total quantity added per SKU or per cart?
What is the distribution of quantities chosen at add time (e.g., proportion of single-item adds)?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to integer for numeric operations (e.g., CAST(qty AS INT) or SAFE_CAST). Handle non-numeric or NULL values defensively (use TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC patterns). Use in GROUP BY after casting when necessary.

added_at.description: Timestamp (string) when the item was added to the cart.
added_at.analysis_use: Time-series and funnel analyses: sessions of add events, hourly/daily patterns, lag from add to order, retention of cart items. Filter by date range for period analyses.
business_questions: What times of day / days of week see the most cart-add activity?
How long on average do items remain in a cart before being updated or converted?
added_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; parse to a datetime type (e.g., CAST(added_at AS DATETIME) or PARSE/TO_TIMESTAMP depending on DB). Ensure consistent format and handle malformed/missing strings. Use timezone awareness if required.

updated_at.description: Timestamp (string) of the last update to this cart item (quantity change, reservation change, etc.).
updated_at.analysis_use: Track modifications to cart items, compute time-to-update, detect churn or abandonment patterns, and deduplicate by latest update per cart_item_id.
business_questions: How often are cart items updated after initial add (qty changes, price changes)?
What is the typical time between item add and item update?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Same as added_at — parse to datetime before temporal comparisons. Use to identify latest state: e.g., SELECT ... WHERE updated_at = (SELECT MAX(updated_at) ...). Handle nulls and invalid formats.

reserved_flag.description: Binary flag stored as text indicating whether the item is reserved (values '1' or '0').
reserved_flag.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate by reservation state (counts and conversion comparisons), segment carts/items by reservation status, and compute reservation rates.
business_questions: What proportion of cart items are reserved versus not reserved?
Do reserved items convert to orders at a higher rate than non-reserved items?
reserved_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical/text but often castable to boolean/integer (CASE WHEN reserved_flag = '1' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). Normalize values (trim, check for other representations). Use explicit comparisons to '1'/'0' to avoid truthiness issues.

price_at_add.description: The price recorded for the item at the time it was added to the cart (stored as nvarchar; integer-like values observed).
price_at_add.analysis_use: Compute nominal cart value (SUM(price_at_add * qty)), price bucket analyses, and monitor price changes by comparing to product price records. Useful for lost revenue and price sensitivity analyses.
business_questions: What is the total value added to carts by SKU/variant or over time?
How often do prices in carts differ from current product pricing (indicating price changes or promotions)?
price_at_add.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to numeric (INT or DECIMAL) before arithmetic. Beware of currency symbols or commas—clean strings first. Use COALESCE to handle NULLs. When joining to product_pricing, prefer casting both sides consistently.

variant_id.description: Identifier for the specific product variant (size/color/pack) added to the cart.
variant_id.analysis_use: Granular product-level analysis: variant popularity, inventory joins, SKU-to-variant mappings. Grouping by variant for detailed sales funnel metrics.
business_questions: Which variants are most frequently added and which variants have the highest cart conversion?
Are there variants with high add rates but no conversions (potential inventory or UX issues)?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to variant or inventory tables (product_variants, product_inventory). Stored as text — match exactly. Check for variant_id nulls and trim. When both sku and variant_id exist, prefer variant_id for variant-level joins.

notes.description: Free text notes for the cart item (user or system notes). Currently fully NULL in this dataset.
notes.analysis_use: When populated, can be used for qualitative analysis, text mining for reasons for notes (e.g., gift messages, special instructions) and anomaly detection. Currently no analytic value due to 100% nulls.
business_questions: Are customers leaving notes that correlate with conversion (e.g., gift messages leading to higher purchases)?
Do particular note patterns indicate friction or helpdesk issues?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nullable text; use LIKE/full-text search if populated. Expect many nulls—filter nulls out when scanning. May require trimming/normalization for text analysis.

cart_id.description: Unique identifier for a cart snapshot. Appears unique per row (distinct 100).
cart_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key for cart-level aggregations, join key to cart_items and order tables for conversion/funnel analysis, used in de-duplication and event correlation.
business_questions: How many unique carts were created in a time window?
Which carts converted to orders (by joining to ecom_orders)?
What is the distribution of cart sizes and values per cart_id?
cart_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key in SQL. Use equality or IN predicates for lookups. Recommend adding an index or primary key on cart_id in production. When generating joins, explicitly join on zuna_seed_raw.ecom_carts.cart_id = ecom_cart_items.cart_id.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who owns or created the cart; distinct per row in sample.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group/filter to compute per-customer metrics (average cart value, abandonment rate), join to customer profile tables for segmentation and LTV analysis, and to measure repeat cart behaviour.
business_questions: Which customers have the highest average cart value?
How often do customers create carts but not convert (abandonment by customer)?
Which customer segments use coupons most frequently?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; join to ecom_customers on customer_id. Watch for guest/anonymous carts (customer_id could be empty in other datasets). Consider deduplicating multiple carts per customer when computing per-customer metrics (e.g., COUNT(DISTINCT cart_id)).

session_id.description: Identifier for browser/app session associated with the cart; useful to tie cart activity to session-level behaviours.
session_id.analysis_use: Join to browsing/session tables to correlate pageviews or product impressions with cart creation; group by session to find multi-cart sessions; filter to examine session-level abandonment.
business_questions: How many carts are created per session on average?
Do specific session behaviours (pageviews/time on site) correlate with abandonment?
session_id.nl2sql_hints: String type; join to ecom_browsing_history or session tables using exact equality. Sessions may span multiple carts—use COUNT(DISTINCT cart_id) when aggregating by session. Avoid implicitly casting session_id.

created_at.description: Timestamp (as text) when the cart was created. Pattern appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'.
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trends, grouping (daily/weekly/monthly), time-based filters, cohort definitions and funnel time calculations (time-to-abandon). Requires casting to datetime/timestamp for date functions.
business_questions: How many carts were created per day/hour?
Are there day-of-week or hour-of-day patterns in cart creation?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; always CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP in NL2SQL queries (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or use TO_TIMESTAMP with format). If filtering by date ranges, convert literals to datetime to avoid string-comparison pitfalls. Recommend migrating to a proper datetime type for production and indexing on created_at for time-range queries.

last_updated_at.description: Text timestamp for the latest update to the cart (item added/removed or other changes).
last_updated_at.analysis_use: Useful to calculate activity windows, session activity recency, and time between updates and abandonment/checkout. Use for ordering by recent behavior and for identifying stale carts.
business_questions: What is the average time between cart creation and last update?
How many carts were updated in the last X hours/days?
last_updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar; CAST to datetime for date arithmetic and window functions. When calculating durations, compute DATEDIFF between CAST(last_updated_at AS datetime) and CAST(created_at AS datetime).

total_items.description: Text field indicating the total count of items in the cart; low cardinality (8 distinct values) and small max length.
total_items.analysis_use: Aggregation and distribution analysis (SUM, AVG, histograms), segmentation (small vs large carts), and correlation with abandonment and total_amount. Convert to INTEGER for numeric operations.
business_questions: What is the distribution of cart sizes (item counts)?
Do larger carts have higher or lower abandonment rates?
total_items.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; CAST(total_items AS INT) before aggregations. Watch for malformed values or nulls. For grouping, consider bucketing (e.g., CASE WHEN CAST(total_items AS INT) >= X THEN 'large' ...).

total_amount.description: Monetary total for the cart stored as text with decimals (many distinct values).
total_amount.analysis_use: Key metric for revenue analysis at cart level: AVG, SUM, median, top-N carts. Used to compute ARPU, AOV, and to compare coupon impacts. Must be cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for safe aggregation.
business_questions: What is the average order value (AOV) at the cart stage?
How much potential revenue is held in abandoned carts?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: Text type—CAST(total_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2)) or appropriate precision before SUM/AVG. Be careful with locale/formatting (decimal point vs comma); dataset appears to use period. Null or empty strings should be handled (NULLIF/TRY_CAST) to avoid query errors. Consider storing currency-normalized amounts if multi-currency support is added.

coupon_code.description: Applied coupon identifier (e.g., NEWUSER, FRESH10). Low cardinality (2 values) with ~37% nulls (no coupon).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Segment carts by coupon usage to measure lift in cart value or reduction in abandonment. Filter to calculate counts/percentages of coupon usage and revenue impact.
business_questions: How does coupon usage affect average cart value and abandonment?
Which coupon codes drive the most incremental cart additions?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Nullable string. Use COALESCE or explicit IS NULL checks for 'no coupon' segments. When comparing, watch for case-sensitivity depending on DB collation. JOINs are rare; treat as categorical in GROUP BY. For NL2SQL, allow synonyms like 'no coupon', 'null coupon', or specific codes.

currency.description: Currency code for the cart amounts (INR for all rows in this sample).
currency.analysis_use: Used to validate/normalize monetary aggregations and for cross-region comparisons. If single currency, can be ignored for numeric aggregations; otherwise requires normalization to a base currency.
business_questions: Are carts across currencies present and how should amounts be normalized?
Is there any regional pattern in currency usage tied to device or coupon?
currency.nl2sql_hints: Constant in this sample (INR). Still include in GROUP BY if mixing currencies. When currency is multi-valued, ensure total_amount is normalized before SUM across currencies.

device_type.description: Platform where cart was created: web, ios, android. Small number of categories.
device_type.analysis_use: Segment metrics (abandonment, AOV, conversion) by device to detect platform-specific issues or opportunities.
business_questions: Which device has the highest abandonment rate?
Does average cart value differ significantly between web and mobile?
device_type.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string—use GROUP BY device_type. Normalize variants (e.g., 'iOS' vs 'ios') in queries or preprocessing. When users ask 'mobile' vs 'desktop', map device_type values accordingly in NL2SQL translation.

abandoned_flag.description: Binary indicator (stored as string) denoting whether the cart was abandoned: '0' = not abandoned, '1' = abandoned in this sample.
abandoned_flag.analysis_use: Primary filter for abandonment analyses, computing abandonment rate, and joining to abandoned_at for timing. Use as boolean by mapping '1'/'0' or casting when appropriate.
business_questions: What proportion of carts are abandoned vs completed?
What is the average value of abandoned carts vs non-abandoned carts?
abandoned_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—treat '1' as abandoned. When writing NL2SQL, explicitly compare to string literal ('1') or convert to BIT/INTEGER using CAST. Be careful of other values (e.g., 'true'/'false') in different datasets. Recommend normalizing to a boolean type in ETL and indexing if frequently filtered.

abandoned_at.description: Timestamp (text) when the cart was marked abandoned. Many nulls (~59%) for carts not abandoned.
abandoned_at.analysis_use: Compute time-to-abandon (abandoned_at - created_at), abandonment timing distributions, and window-based abandonment counts. Requires casting to datetime and careful NULL handling.
business_questions: What is the distribution of time between cart creation and abandonment?
When (hour/day) do abandonments peak?
abandoned_at.nl2sql_hints: Text timestamp—CAST to datetime for DATEDIFF and time-based grouping. Handle NULLs: use WHERE abandoned_flag = '1' AND abandoned_at IS NOT NULL for abandonment calculations. Normalize timestamp formats and consider timezone implications if present.

coupon_usage_id.description: Identifier for the coupon usage record (string). Intended as a unique identifier for each redemption event, but not enforced as a PK.
coupon_usage_id.analysis_use: Use for deduplication, identifying individual usage events, and linking external audit logs. Useful for COUNT DISTINCT and row-level diagnostics.
business_questions: Are there duplicate coupon usage records (same coupon_usage_id repeated)?
How many unique usage events exist in a given period?
coupon_usage_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text. Use coupon_usage_id for DISTINCT/COUNT to deduplicate. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace. Do not assume uniqueness—check COUNT(*) vs COUNT(DISTINCT coupon_usage_id). When filtering by id use exact match; when matching patterns use LIKE. If used as a join key in downstream tables, confirm normalization and data cleanliness first.

coupon_id.description: Identifier of the coupon that was applied (string). Links a usage event to a coupon definition.
coupon_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping key to compute redemptions per coupon, total/average discount by coupon, and join key to coupon metadata (discount rules, start/end dates, campaign).
business_questions: Which coupons generate the most redemptions?
What is the average discount amount provided by each coupon?
coupon_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—use exact matches for joins to ecom_coupons. GROUP BY coupon_id for aggregation. Consider normalizing casing/whitespace. For NL2SQL, map user-friendly coupon names to coupon_id via a join to ecom_coupons if available.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who used the coupon (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Used for user-level aggregation: count of coupon uses per customer, identifying repeat redeemers, and joining to customer profile tables for segmentation analysis.
business_questions: Which customers redeemed coupons most frequently?
What is the total discount received by a specific customer during a period?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Text field—join to ecom_customers on customer_id for demographics. For NL2SQL: when user asks about 'customers' ensure to join to ecom_customers to retrieve names/emails. Beware of nulls and potential guest/anonymous IDs. Use GROUP BY customer_id for counts; use DISTINCT when counting unique customers.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order where the coupon was applied (string).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders to validate redemptions against order totals/status; used to compute coupon impact on order revenue or to exclude cancelled/refunded orders.
business_questions: How much discount was applied on each order that used a coupon?
Which orders with coupons were later refunded or cancelled?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Text field—join to ecom_orders on order_id to get order_amount, status, refund info. For NL2SQL, combine with order status filters (e.g., WHERE ecom_orders.status = 'completed') to compute net impact. Ensure order_id normalization (trim/upper) matches orders table.

applied_ts.description: Timestamp (string) when the coupon was applied to the order (format appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
applied_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal dimension for time series: use after casting to datetime to compute trends by hour/day/week/month, session timing analyses, and latency between coupon creation and use.
business_questions: What is the hourly/daily distribution of coupon redemptions?
How long after coupon issuance are coupons typically claimed?
applied_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—must CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP/TRY_PARSE to datetime. Typical safe SQL patterns: TRY_CAST(applied_ts AS DATETIME) or use TO_TIMESTAMP(applied_ts, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') depending on dialect. Handle parsing failures with safe casts/COALESCE. Use applied_ts for time-based GROUP BY (DATE_TRUNC or CAST to date). If timezone not present, assume server timezone; clarify if user asks for timezone-aware results.

discount_given.description: Recorded discount amount applied for the coupon usage (stored as text, numeric-looking values).
discount_given.analysis_use: Aggregate metric: SUM and AVG of discounts by coupon/customer/order/time. Also useful for validating coupon rules (percent vs fixed amount) when joined to coupon metadata.
business_questions: What is the total discount given in a period (sum of discount_given)?
What is the average discount provided per coupon redemption?
discount_given.nl2sql_hints: Text field representing numeric values—use CAST/TRY_CAST(discount_given AS DECIMAL/NUMERIC) before SUM/AVG. Handle non-numeric or null strings with TRY_CAST/NULLIF/REGEXP_REPLACE to strip commas. Pay attention to precision (use DECIMAL with suitable scale). When joining to coupon rules, reconcile percent vs absolute discounts (may need additional logic if discount_given stores absolute amounts only).

created_at.description: Timestamp (string) when the usage record was created in the system (format appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Use to audit ingestion time, measure lag between event occurrence (applied_ts) and record creation, and for CDC/ETL incremental loads when selecting new rows.
business_questions: What is the lag between coupon application and creation of the usage record?
How many coupon usage records were created in a given ingestion window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Text field—cast to datetime similarly to applied_ts. For incremental queries, use created_at as the watermark (after casting). For lag calculations use TIMESTAMPDIFF or equivalent between CAST(applied_ts AS DATETIME) and CAST(created_at AS DATETIME). Ensure consistent parsing and handle nulls or malformed timestamps with TRY_CAST/ISDATE checks.

coupon_id.analysis_use: Unique identifier for grouping and joining; primary key candidate for this table. Used in lookups, de-dup checks and joining to usage tables if they reference coupon_id.
business_questions: How many distinct coupons have been created (count of coupon_id)?
Show coupon details for a specific coupon_id.
coupon_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as identifier string. Prefer exact equality matches. If other tables reference numeric ids, confirm whether they store coupon_id or coupon_code. No PK enforced—queries should handle possible duplicates (use DISTINCT).

coupon_code.description: Human-readable code customers enter to redeem the coupon.
coupon_code.analysis_use: Primary human-facing key for joins with orders/usage records; grouping for redemptions and effectiveness; filtering by code or pattern.
business_questions: How many times was the coupon_code 'DIWALI80' used (join with usage table)?
List coupons by coupon_code along with their discount_type and validity.
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Often used in WHERE clauses; watch for case sensitivity depending on DB. Cast joins to the same type/format as in usage tables. Support LIKE for pattern matching (e.g., codes starting with 'FRESH').

description.description: Human-readable description of the coupon (e.g., 'Percentage Discount' or 'Flat Discount').
description.analysis_use: Categorical grouping (count coupons by description) and filtering for operational reports or marketing summaries.
business_questions: How many percentage vs flat discounts are available?
Show all coupons with description 'Flat Discount'.
description.nl2sql_hints: Small set of values—safe to GROUP BY. Values may duplicate discount_type; prefer discount_type for logic and description for display.

discount_type.description: Logical type of discount: PERCENT or FLAT.
discount_type.analysis_use: Used to determine calculation method during analytics and joins; grouping to compare performance between percent vs flat promotions.
business_questions: Which discount_type yields more redemptions (requires join with usage)?
How many coupons of type PERCENT vs FLAT exist?
discount_type.nl2sql_hints: Categorical column with few values—use in WHERE/GROUP BY. When computing amounts, branch logic: if discount_type='PERCENT' apply discount_value as percent; else treat as absolute. Ensure discount_value is cast to numeric when calculating.

discount_value.description: The discount magnitude; stored as text but represents a numeric value (percent or flat amount depending on discount_type).
discount_value.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (min/avg/max), filtering (e.g., discounts >= X), and calculation of potential discount amounts (when combined with order totals or max_discount_amount).
business_questions: List coupons with discount_value >= 20 (including percent and flat depending on type).
What is the average discount_value for percent discounts?
discount_value.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/CONVERT to numeric (INT/DECIMAL) in NL2SQL. Take care to apply business logic based on discount_type. Null/empty strings should be handled. For percent discounts, ensure value is interpreted as percentage (e.g., 20 => 20%).

max_discount_amount.description: Upper cap on discount amount (applies usually to percent discounts), stored as string but represents numeric currency amount.
max_discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute capped discount amounts and to filter coupons that have a high cap. Useful for estimating maximum liability per redemption.
business_questions: Which coupons allow a maximum discount above 100?
For percent coupons, what is the distribution of max_discount_amount?
max_discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST to numeric for comparisons/aggregations. Null means no cap—confirm business semantics. When calculating actual discount on an order, apply min(calculated_percent_amount, max_discount_amount).

valid_from.description: Start of coupon validity window (timestamp stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
valid_from.analysis_use: Filter coupons active at a given time, compute durations, and identify upcoming or expired promotions.
business_questions: Which coupons become active in the next 7 days?
Show coupons that were valid on 2025-10-22.
valid_from.nl2sql_hints: Parse/CAST to a datetime type for range filters and comparisons. Watch timezone assumptions. Because stored as string, use consistent format parsing (ISO-like) or string comparisons if guaranteed format.

valid_to.description: End of coupon validity window (timestamp as nvarchar).
valid_to.analysis_use: Same as valid_from: used to determine currently valid coupons, expiry reports, and to filter coupon lists for a date range.
business_questions: List coupons expiring within 30 days.
Which coupons were valid on a specific transaction date?
valid_to.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for correctness. For 'open-ended' coupons, null might indicate no expiry—handle accordingly. When checking 'is valid now', use valid_from <= now AND valid_to >= now.

min_order_amount.description: Minimum order value required to apply the coupon; stored as text but numeric in nature.
min_order_amount.analysis_use: Filter coupons by threshold, calculate how many orders would qualify based on order totals (requires join), and segment coupons by restriction levels.
business_questions: Which coupons have min_order_amount = 0 (no minimum)?
How many coupons require a minimum order of at least 200?
min_order_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST to numeric for comparisons. Some values are '0' or small set—grouping is useful. If blank/null, treat as 0 only if business defines that; confirm semantics.

usage_limit.description: Maximum number of times a coupon can be used (string representing small integer).
usage_limit.analysis_use: Filter high-limit vs limited coupons, and when joined with usage table compute remaining uses (usage_limit - times_used).
business_questions: Which coupons are single-use (usage_limit = 1)?
List coupons with usage_limit <= 2.
usage_limit.nl2sql_hints: CAST to integer. If usage_limit is null, interpret per business rules (e.g., unlimited). Use in arithmetic with aggregated usage counts from ecom_coupon_usage.

active_flag.description: Indicator if coupon is active (stored as string, appears to be '1' for active).
active_flag.analysis_use: Quick filter for active/inactive coupons. Combined with valid_from/valid_to to determine usable coupons.
business_questions: Show all active coupons.
How many inactive coupons exist (if any)?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean: CAST('1'/'0') to boolean or integer. In this dataset it is constant '1'—check for historical/inactive rows in full dataset. Combine with validity window for accurate 'usable now' queries.

created_at.description: Timestamp when coupon was created (nvarchar timestamp).
created_at.analysis_use: Trend analysis of coupon creation over time, cohorting by creation date and auditing changes.
business_questions: How many coupons were created each month?
Which coupons were created before a campaign start date?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse/cast to datetime for grouping by day/month/year. Be aware that created_at may equal valid_from for some records; handle nulls. Use ISO-like format parsing or string compare if guaranteed format.

updated_at.description: Timestamp of last update to the coupon row (nvarchar timestamp).
updated_at.analysis_use: Audit when rules changed, determine recent edits, and compute time since last modification. Useful to detect churn in coupon definitions.
business_questions: List coupons updated in the last 30 days.
Which coupons have never been updated since creation (created_at = updated_at)?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: CAST to datetime for recency filters. Use updated_at for change detection and to order recent modifications. Ensure consistent parsing; handle nulls.

address_id.description: Unique identifier for the address record (string).
address_id.analysis_use: Use as a record identifier for deduplication, joins to shipping/addresses in other tables (if present), and counting distinct addresses. Good for exact-match filters.
business_questions: How many unique address records exist in the dataset?
Which address_ids are referenced by orders or shipments (after joining)?
Are there duplicate address entries for the same customer (detect by comparing addr_line1/addr_line2/city)?
address_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical string. Use equality or IN filters for lookups. When generating SQL, do not CAST unless needed; if comparing against other tables, ensure formats match (trim whitespace). Consider recommending a unique constraint or primary key in DDL if appropriate.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who owns this address (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to customer/account tables. Grouping and aggregation (addresses per customer), filtering for specific customers, joining to orders/payments to attribute deliveries.
business_questions: How many addresses does a given customer_id have?
Which customers have addresses in more than one city?
What are the addresses for a customer to display in user profile or checkout?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use for joins to ecom_customers and ecom_orders. Expect exact-match joins; ensure same casing/trimming. Consider indexing this column for join/filter performance. If customer_id appears in other schemas as a different type, cast consistently.

label.analysis_use: Low-cardinality field used for grouping/filtering. Useful for segmentation (Home vs Work) and counting address types.
business_questions: What percentage of addresses are labeled 'Home' vs 'Work'?
Do 'Work' addresses cluster differently by city compared to 'Home' addresses?
label.nl2sql_hints: Expect small set of values; use equality or IN. Handle case normalization and possible variants (e.g., 'home', 'Home'). Provide mapping/aliasing if users use synonyms. Good candidate for a filtered index if queries often restrict by label.

addr_line1.description: Primary street/address line (building number, street name).
addr_line1.analysis_use: Textual matching and grouping by common patterns (e.g., same street). Used for deduplication and geocoding checks; filtered with LIKE or full-text if supported.
business_questions: Which top streets/areas (addr_line1) have the most customers?
Are there multiple address records referring to the same physical location (similar addr_line1 + pincode)?
addr_line1.nl2sql_hints: Use LIKE or full-text functions for substring matching. Be careful with variable formatting; normalize (upper/lower, trim) when matching. Avoid equality for free-form addresses unless values are sanitized.

addr_line2.description: Secondary address information (landmarks, unit number); has ~27% nulls and only a few distinct values.
addr_line2.analysis_use: Useful for grouping by landmark phrases, filling missing location context, and improving geocoding. Null-handling necessary in queries.
business_questions: What are the most common landmark phrases used in addresses?
How many addresses rely on landmarks (non-null addr_line2) vs exact street addresses?
addr_line2.nl2sql_hints: Expect many nulls; use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL in filters. Values are repetitive — consider normalization into a lookup table if used frequently. Use TRIM and case normalization for comparisons.

city.description: City/city-level administrative area for the address.
city.analysis_use: Primary geographic grouping field. Useful for aggregation, heatmaps, routing, and regional KPIs. Often used in WHERE clauses and GROUP BY.
business_questions: Which cities have the highest number of customer addresses?
How does order volume or returns vary by city (when joined to orders)?
city.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical text. Consider indexing for aggregation/filters. Normalize common variants/spellings before matching. Since there are ~10 distinct values, GROUP BY city will be efficient.

state.description: State/region for the address (constant in this dataset: 'Gujarat').
state.analysis_use: Regional grouping; in this dataset it provides little variance but is useful for future multi-state datasets and validation.
business_questions: How many addresses are in each state (for multi-state deployments)?
Are there addresses outside expected states that indicate data quality issues?
state.nl2sql_hints: Often constant here — queries filtering by state might be redundant. Still include in joins/filters when working across datasets. Watch for abbreviations vs full names; normalize before comparisons.

pincode.description: Postal code / ZIP code for the address (stored as nvarchar, 99 distinct values).
pincode.analysis_use: Granular geographic grouping, routing, delivery area analysis, and joins to external geographic tables. Good for aggregations, frequency counts, and mapping to serviceability zones.
business_questions: Which pincodes have the highest customer density?
Which pincodes fall outside our deliveryable zones or have higher failed deliveries (when joined to delivery data)?
pincode.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — treat as categorical. If numeric operations are needed (range checks), CAST to integer. Consider indexing for frequent pincode lookups. Normalize leading zeros if present.

country.analysis_use: Top-level geographic filter; in multi-country deployments used for global segmentation. Here it's constant and mainly for data completeness.
business_questions: How many addresses are in each country (for multi-country datasets)?
Are there any records with unexpected or missing country values?
country.nl2sql_hints: Usually constant — treat as categorical. Avoid casting. Validate against expected country list if present.

latitude.description: Latitude coordinate for the address (stored as nvarchar representing decimal coordinates).
latitude.analysis_use: Spatial queries (proximity, bounding boxes), distance calculations (after casting), clustering by location, and map visualizations. Can be used with longitude to compute delivery radii.
business_questions: Which addresses fall within X km of a warehouse or store location?
What is the average delivery distance for customers in a city (after joining to warehouse/store coords)?
latitude.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — CAST to numeric (DECIMAL/DOUBLE) before spatial arithmetic. Be mindful of precision and malformed values. For NL2SQL, detect requests like 'within X km' and generate haversine or spatial-index-aware SQL. Consider adding a spatial index or storing as geography/geometry types for efficient queries.

longitude.description: Longitude coordinate for the address (stored as nvarchar representing decimal coordinates).
longitude.analysis_use: Paired with latitude for spatial analyses, route optimization, and mapping. Needed for distance calculations and geospatial indexing.
business_questions: Which customer addresses are within a certain mile/km radius of a point of interest?
Are there clusters of customers in underserved areas by longitude/latitude?
longitude.nl2sql_hints: Same as latitude: CAST to numeric type before calculations. Use combined lat/long in spatial functions. Normalize formatting (decimal separator) and handle null/malformed coordinates.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the address record was created (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal analyses: new addresses over time, cohorting customers by when they added addresses, and temporal joins with orders. Must be parsed/CAST to datetime for time-based SQL operations.
business_questions: How many addresses were added per week/month?
Do customers add more addresses during specific seasons or promotions?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — CAST/CONVERT to a datetime type for comparisons, grouping by date parts, and range filters. Handle timezone or format inconsistencies. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit parsing logic (e.g., TRY_PARSE) and fallbacks for malformed strings.

customer_id.description: Unique identifier for a customer in this segmentation snapshot (string, max len 8 in sample).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for joins to master customer and transactional tables; used for deduplication, building target lists and granular customer-level reporting; grouping and counting distinct customers.
business_questions: Which customers are in the 'Champions' RFM segment?
Provide the list of customer_ids to target for a reactivation campaign (e.g., Hibernating segment with avg_order_value > X).
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the join key to ecom_customers / other customer tables. Watch for datatype mismatches: other tables may use varchar/nvarchar or different lengths — use explicit CAST/CONVERT if necessary. Because there is no declared PK, queries that assume uniqueness should use DISTINCT or GROUP BY to tolerate duplicates. Use fully qualified table names in cross-schema joins.

rfm_segment.description: Categorical RFM segment assignment for each customer (e.g., Hibernating, Regulars, At Risk, Champions).
rfm_segment.analysis_use: Low-cardinality categorical grouping for counts, proportions and segment-level aggregations (avg loyalty_score, avg_order_value). Useful as a filter for campaign targeting and cohort analysis.
business_questions: How many customers are in each RFM segment?
What is the average avg_order_value per RFM segment?
rfm_segment.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical text. Use GROUP BY rfm_segment for aggregation. Consider COALESCE to handle NULL or unexpected categories. For NL2SQL, when user asks for segment proportions, generate COUNT(*) and COUNT(*) FILTER or compute COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*)).

loyalty_score.description: Customer loyalty score stored as text but representing a numeric score (likely integer or small numeric scale).
loyalty_score.analysis_use: Numeric aggregations (AVG, MIN, MAX), bucketization for cohorts (e.g., high/medium/low loyalty), correlation with avg_order_value and segments, and threshold-based filters for targeting.
business_questions: What is the average loyalty_score across all customers and by RFM segment?
Which customers have loyalty_score above a given threshold (e.g., > 50)?
loyalty_score.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST/TRY_CAST to integer/decimal before numeric operations: e.g., TRY_CAST(loyalty_score AS INT) or CAST(...) with guarded WHERE to exclude non-numeric values. Handle NULLs and non-numeric strings. Use safe conversion functions supported by the target SQL dialect (TRY_CONVERT, TRY_CAST, NULLIF, ISNUMERIC checks).

avg_order_value.description: Average order value per customer stored as string (decimal-like values).
avg_order_value.analysis_use: Monetary aggregations and ranking (AVG, SUM), segment revenue contribution analysis, identifying high-value customers, and combining with orders to validate totals.
business_questions: Who are the top 10 customers by avg_order_value?
What is the total and average avg_order_value for the 'Champions' segment?
avg_order_value.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before aggregation: e.g., CAST(avg_order_value AS DECIMAL(18,2)) or use TRY_CAST to avoid errors. Be careful with formatting/locale; values appear in plain dot-decimal format. For comparisons use numeric cast, not string comparison. If joining to order-level totals, prefer recalculating from orders for accuracy.

purchase_frequency.description: Average purchase frequency per customer (stored as string, likely numeric representing purchases per period).
purchase_frequency.analysis_use: Used for behavior analysis (frequency cohorts), calculating expected LTV, and filtering customers by activity level. Combine with last_purchase_date to find active vs dormant customers.
business_questions: Which customers have purchase_frequency above 1 (frequent buyers)?
What is the average purchase_frequency for each RFM segment?
purchase_frequency.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for numeric filtering and aggregation. Beware of string comparisons causing incorrect ordering. If the user asks for rate calculations, ensure correct units are known (per month/year) or document assumptions. Consider normalizing to a consistent numeric type in query or ETL.

last_purchase_date.description: Timestamp of the customer's last purchase as a string in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format in the sample data.
last_purchase_date.analysis_use: Recency calculations, filtering customers inactive since X, cohorting by last interaction, and ordering customers by last activity. Useful for building 'n-days since last purchase' metrics and triggers.
business_questions: List customers who have not purchased in the last 180 days.
When was the last purchase for each customer and which customers are most recently active?
last_purchase_date.nl2sql_hints: Convert to proper DATETIME/TIMESTAMP using TRY_CAST/CONVERT based on SQL dialect (e.g., TRY_CAST(last_purchase_date AS DATETIME)). Use safe casts to handle malformed strings. When using date arithmetic, ensure timezone assumptions are clear; if times are stored in UTC, document that. Use ISNULL/COALESCE for missing values, and consider filtering out nulls before date operations. For NL2SQL, translate 'inactive since X days' to expressions like WHERE CAST(last_purchase_date AS DATETIME) < DATEADD(day, -X, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP).

created_at.description: Timestamp when this segmentation snapshot or the customer record was created in the segmentation table (string, sample shows 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Tracking when segmentation snapshots were generated, auditing freshness, and comparing changes over time across snapshots (if multiple snapshots exist). Also used to filter snapshot recency and create temporal slices.
business_questions: Which segmentation records are newer than a given date?
How many customers had segmentation created in the last 30 days?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for temporal filters and comparisons. If multiple snapshot runs exist, created_at can be used to pick the latest snapshot per customer (e.g., use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) DESC)). Handle NULLs and malformed timestamps with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT. When generating NL2SQL, use fully qualified table name and explicit casting for date math.

customer_id.description: Internal unique identifier for a customer (e.g., CUST1001). Stored as nvarchar(26) and currently distinct across rows.
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup key for joins to orders/addresses/events, grouping and counting unique customers, deduplication. Use for WHERE filters and joins.
business_questions: How many unique customers do we have?
Retrieve a customer's profile and associated transactions by customer_id.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as the canonical join key. Watch for NULLs (column is nullable). Use equality joins (e.g., ON e.customer_id = o.customer_id). Recommended to trim whitespace and normalize case if inconsistencies exist. Consider adding PK/index in production for performance.

external_customer_code.description: External or partner-facing customer code (nvarchar(24)). Distinct per row in this sample.
external_customer_code.analysis_use: Alternate key for joins when external systems reference customers; dedup checks against customer_id; grouping by partner codes.
business_questions: Which external partner codes correspond to which internal customers?
Are there customers referenced only by external_customer_code in partner data?
external_customer_code.nl2sql_hints: May be used instead of customer_id when joining external datasets. Normalize case/trim and handle NULLs. In NL2SQL, ask explicitly which identifier to use (customer_id vs external_customer_code).

first_name.description: Customer's given name (nvarchar(28)). Moderately high cardinality (81 distinct of 100).
first_name.analysis_use: Grouping for simple name-based reporting, fuzzy matching/deduplication, personalization (email templates). Not useful for identity reliably on its own.
business_questions: How many customers have a given first name?
List customers with name starting with a specific prefix for targeted communications.
first_name.nl2sql_hints: Treat as free-text. Use LOWER()/UPPER() for case-insensitive matching and LIKE for prefix/suffix searches. Avoid joins on names alone.

last_name.description: Customer's family name (nvarchar(36)). High cardinality (90 distinct).
last_name.analysis_use: Similar to first_name — grouping for demographic looks, fuzzy matching. Useful for customer-facing displays.
business_questions: How many customers share a common last name?
Find customers by last name for customer service lookups.
last_name.nl2sql_hints: Use LOWER()/RTRIM(LTRIM()) normalize before comparisons. Avoid using for joins. Consider combining first_name+last_name for higher-quality match queries.

email.description: Customer email address (nvarchar(70)). Distinct across rows in sample and suitable for contact and login operations.
email.analysis_use: Identity lookup, deduplication, marketing contact lists, filtering opted-in customers, pattern analysis (domains).
business_questions: How many customers use gmail.com vs other email domains?
List opted-in customers' emails for a campaign.
email.nl2sql_hints: Treat as unique identifier in many workflows. Use LOWER(email) for case-insensitive matches and LIKE '%@domain.com' to filter domains. Validate/clean emails before joins. Beware of NULLs and duplicates in broader datasets.

phone.description: Customer phone number as nvarchar(38). Distinct per row in sample.
phone.analysis_use: Contact method for SMS campaigns, identity lookup, validation and deduplication. Can be parsed to country code for geo-analysis.
business_questions: How many customers have a valid phone number for SMS marketing?
Which customers have phones in a specific country code (e.g., +91)?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Normalize by removing spaces, punctuation and standardizing country codes before matching. Use LIKE '+91%' or substring functions for country-level filters. Watch for formatting inconsistency since stored as text.

dob.description: Date of birth stored as nvarchar(30) in YYYY-MM-DD format in samples.
dob.analysis_use: Calculate age, age buckets, demographic segmentation by age, target offers by age group.
business_questions: What is the age distribution of customers?
How many customers are in a specific age bracket (e.g., 18-25)?
dob.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE or use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT in SQL (e.g., TRY_CAST(dob AS date)). Handle NULLs and malformed values. Use date arithmetic against current_date to compute age. Clarify exact stored format in NL2SQL prompt ('dob is YYYY-MM-DD').

gender.description: Self-reported gender (nvarchar(22)) with low cardinality: Other, Male, Female (Other is 40%).
gender.analysis_use: Segmentation and demographic analysis; grouping and proportion calculations.
business_questions: What percent of customers identify as Male/Female/Other?
How does marketing opt-in vary by gender?
gender.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical; use equality filters. Normalize casing/spelling (e.g., 'male' vs 'Male'). Map values explicitly in NL2SQL requests if synonyms exist.

created_at.description: Timestamp when customer profile was created stored as nvarchar(48) (samples show 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Cohort analysis, acquisition rate over time, new customer counts by time period, funneling new users into behavior analyses.
business_questions: Count of new customers by month/week/day for a given period.
What is the conversion trend of customers created per acquisition channel?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT. Use date_trunc or DATEPART functions for grouping by month/week/day. Ensure timezone considerations if present. Use BETWEEN for ranges; require explicit format in NL2SQL when using string timestamps.

last_login.description: Last login timestamp recorded as nvarchar(48). Samples indicate full timestamps up to 2025.
last_login.analysis_use: Engagement/recency metrics, active user counts, churn detection (e.g., last_login older than X days), segmentation for re-engagement campaigns.
business_questions: How many customers logged in within the last 30 days?
Which customers have not logged in for more than 90 days?
last_login.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP using TRY_CAST. For recency use current_timestamp - CAST(last_login AS datetime). Handle NULLs for never-logged-in. Use indexes on parsed timestamps in production for speed.

marketing_opt_in.description: Indicator whether customer opted into marketing; stored as nvarchar(20) but values are '1' or '0' (63% '1').
marketing_opt_in.analysis_use: Filter targetable audiences, compute opt-in rates, and use as a boolean flag in joins for campaign lists.
business_questions: What percent of active customers have opted into marketing?
How many opted-in customers came from each acquisition channel?
marketing_opt_in.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean: map '1' -> TRUE and '0' -> FALSE in NL2SQL. Use CASE or CAST to int when counting (e.g., SUM(CAST(marketing_opt_in AS int))). Watch for unexpected values; use IN ('1','0') or explicit mappings.

preferred_language.description: Preferred language for communication (nvarchar(26)). Low cardinality: Hindi (37%), Gujarati (34%), English (29%).
preferred_language.analysis_use: Localization and segmentation for messaging, grouping, and A/B test stratification.
business_questions: How many customers prefer Hindi vs English vs Gujarati?
Which channel performs best for customers with a specific preferred language?
preferred_language.nl2sql_hints: Use equality filters and GROUP BY. Normalize values (trim/case) before comparisons. Use this column to join with localized content tables if available.

status.analysis_use: Filter active customer base, measure reactivation, compute retention metrics and segment by status for marketing.
business_questions: How many customers are active vs inactive?
What is the reactivation rate over time (requires join with events/orders)?
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use explicit equality checks (status = 'ACTIVE'). Consider historical status changes not present in this table; join to events tables for status transitions.

source_channel.description: Acquisition channel (nvarchar(30)) with values like Organic, Referral, Website, Mobile App (4 distinct).
source_channel.analysis_use: Attribution analysis, acquisition funnel, grouping by channel for channel-level cohort and conversion metrics.
business_questions: Which acquisition channel brought the most customers in the last month?
How does marketing opt-in rate vary by source_channel?
source_channel.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — use GROUP BY and ORDER BY counts. Normalize textual variations (e.g., 'Mobile App' vs 'MobileApp'). Use this field in joins with campaign/channel performance tables; ensure consistent naming across datasets.

map_id.description: A unique identifier for the recommendation mapping/row. Likely a mapping key (human-readable code like 'FRQ007001') used to reference the specific frequently-bought pair.
map_id.analysis_use: Used for deduplication and as a stable row identifier (filtering by map_id). Not typically used for aggregation except counts of unique mappings. Useful when auditing updates or deleting/updating specific mappings.
business_questions: When was the mapping FRQ007001 last updated and what SKU pair does it represent?
How many mapping records exist (distinct map_id) for a given time range or SKU?
map_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key (nvarchar). Use equality filters (WHERE map_id = 'FRQ007001') or IN lists. Avoid numeric casts. Consider creating a primary key or unique constraint if map_id is intended to be unique. When writing NL2SQL, map_id is typically used exactly as provided; escape special characters if present.

sku.description: The base SKU for which recommendations are listed. Represents the product that triggers the recommendation ("customers who bought this...").
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering key for analytics and retrieval of recommendations (GROUP BY sku, WHERE sku = ...). Used to join against product metadata and inventory tables. Useful for counts (number of recommendations per sku) and aggregations (average co_purchase_rate for recommendations of a sku).
business_questions: What are the recommended SKUs for sku 'ONI003'?
How many recommendation pairs exist for each sku?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; treat as categorical string. Use equality or IN filters. When joining to products, join ON ecom_products.sku = ecom_frequently_bought.sku (confirm candidate column name in product tables). Consider indexing/partitioning on sku if the table grows. Watch out for case sensitivity depending on DB collation—normalize casing in queries if necessary.

freq_with_sku.description: The SKU that is frequently bought with the sku column (the co-purchased or recommended SKU).
freq_with_sku.analysis_use: Used in tandem with sku to identify pairs (sku, freq_with_sku). Useful for pair-level metrics, deduplication, and joins to product metadata for the recommended item. Can be used for asymmetric vs symmetric analyses (A->B vs B->A).
business_questions: For the recommendation pair (sku, freq_with_sku) = ('TOM001','COW005'), what is the co_purchase_rate?
Which freq_with_sku items appear most often across all sku recommendations?
freq_with_sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nvarchar and as a join key to product tables (e.g., products.sku). When constructing NL2SQL, be explicit about which side of the pair you mean (base vs recommended). If asking for mutual pairs, include logic to check both (sku = X AND freq_with_sku = Y) OR (sku = Y AND freq_with_sku = X).

co_purchase_rate.description: Numeric metric indicating how often the freq_with_sku is bought together with the sku. Currently stored as text (nvarchar) but contains decimal-like values (e.g., '11.24'). Likely represents a percentage, ratio, or score (domain should be clarified).
co_purchase_rate.analysis_use: Central measure for ranking and aggregation: ORDER BY CAST(co_purchase_rate AS DECIMAL) DESC, compute averages, medians, thresholds, and distribution summaries. Use for filtering high-confidence recommendations (WHERE co_purchase_rate >= X after casting).
business_questions: Which SKU pairs have a co_purchase_rate above 10%?
What is the average co_purchase_rate for recommendations of SKU 'COW005'?
co_purchase_rate.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to a numeric type for numeric comparisons/aggregations (e.g., CAST(co_purchase_rate AS DECIMAL(10,4)) or TRY_CAST depending on SQL dialect). Be defensive: handle malformed or NULL values with TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC/SAFE_CAST. Clarify unit (percentage vs absolute score) in NL2SQL prompts to choose correct thresholds. When generating SQL, include explicit ORDER BY CAST(co_purchase_rate AS FLOAT) DESC if sorting.

last_updated.description: Timestamp string indicating when the mapping/co-purchase rate was last refreshed. Stored as nvarchar but contains datetime-like values (e.g., '2025-11-02 13:31:04').
last_updated.analysis_use: Used to filter recent/stale mappings (WHERE last_updated >= ...), compute age of recommendations, and to select the latest version of a mapping if multiple records exist. Useful in time-based audits and scheduling refreshes.
business_questions: Which recommendation pairs were updated in the last 30 days?
What is the most recent update timestamp for recommendations related to SKU 'POT002'?
last_updated.nl2sql_hints: Cast or parse to a proper datetime type before date comparisons (e.g., CAST(last_updated AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE depending on SQL dialect). Be careful with timezone semantics: the stored strings may be naive timestamps. Use safe casts and filter out invalid/NULL strings. In NL2SQL, specify the intended time window and format to ensure proper conversion.

notification_id.description: Unique identifier for each notification event (string).
notification_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication, tracing a single notification. Useful for counting unique notifications and linking logs across systems if IDs are exported elsewhere.
business_questions: How many unique notifications were sent in the last 30 days?
Show the full record for notification_id = 'NOTF005001'.
notification_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; treated as opaque string. Use equality filters for single-row lookups (WHERE notification_id = '...'). Consider creating a primary key or unique index for faster point lookups if table grows.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who was targeted by the notification.
customer_id.analysis_use: Group or filter notifications by customer to measure frequency, failure rates and per-customer timelines. Join key to customer master (ecom_customers) to enrich with attributes (segment, region, lifecycle).
business_questions: Which customers received the most notifications and how many failed deliveries do they have?
Show notification history for customer_id = 'CUST1054' ordered by sent_ts.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Values are strings (nvarchar). When joining, ensure target customer table uses the same identifier type and normalization (case, prefix). Consider indexing customer_id for frequent group-by/filters. Handle NULLs and trim whitespace. Cast sent_ts when ordering by time.

notification_type.description: Channel used to send the notification (e.g., PUSH, EMAIL, SMS).
notification_type.analysis_use: Categorical grouping for volume, success/failure rates and channel performance. Useful for segmentation of delivery metrics by channel.
business_questions: Which channel has the highest delivery success rate?
How many EMAIL notifications were sent last week?
notification_type.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality; safe to GROUP BY. Use exact string matches (e.g., WHERE notification_type = 'EMAIL'). Normalization might be needed if values appear in different cases. Consider indexing if queries frequently filter by channel.

title.description: High-level subject or title of the notification (e.g., Order Update, New Offer).
title.analysis_use: Group and aggregate to measure performance per campaign or message type (volume, delivery outcomes). Use text grouping for A/B test comparisons.
business_questions: Which notification titles are most frequently sent?
Does the 'New Offer' title have a different delivery failure rate than 'Order Update'?
title.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality strings; GROUP BY and COUNT are common. Titles may have variants/spelling differences — consider canonicalization for accurate aggregations. Use exact-match filters for quick lookups (WHERE title = 'Order Update').

message.description: Body or template of the notification message (short text).
message.analysis_use: Analyze which message content is associated with higher engagement or failures. Useful for campaign analytics and deduplicating repeated messages.
business_questions: Which message text has the highest proportion of FAILED deliveries?
How often is the 'A special discount is available.' message sent and through which channels?
message.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality preset messages in this snapshot. For free-text messages, consider text normalization. For NL2SQL, exact string matches or IN lists work; beware of long text and substring matching (LIKE) which can be slower. If message templates map to campaign IDs, prefer storing template_id for easier joins/filters.

sent_ts.description: Timestamp when the notification was sent; stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
sent_ts.analysis_use: Time series analysis (volume by hour/day/week), latency calculations (sent_ts vs created_at), and filtering by period. Convert to proper datetime for ordering and date arithmetic.
business_questions: How many notifications were sent per day last month?
What is the hourly delivery failure rate during peak hours?
sent_ts.nl2sql_hints: Field is a string; convert to a datetime/timestamp type in queries (e.g., CAST(sent_ts AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP(sent_ts,'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS')) before time-based grouping or range filters. Be robust to NULLs and invalid formats. If queries filter by date ranges frequently, consider storing a native datetime column or a computed column for indexability.

delivery_status.description: Outcome of delivery attempt (e.g., DELIVERED, FAILED, SENT).
delivery_status.analysis_use: Key metric for channel health. Use for aggregation (counts, rates) and to identify customers/orders with repeated failures. Combine with notification_type and title to analyze effectiveness.
business_questions: What percentage of PUSH notifications failed in the last 7 days?
List notifications with delivery_status = 'FAILED' and their customer_id and sent_ts.
delivery_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string with few values — groupable. Normalize case and spelling. Consider mapping to booleans or enums in application logic for easier comparisons. Indexing is beneficial if filtering on status is common.

linked_order_id.description: Optional order identifier associated with the notification (ties notifications to order events).
linked_order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders to analyze order-related communications, correlate notification outcomes with order lifecycle (shipping, delivery), and measure per-order notification counts.
business_questions: For order EORD2089, what notifications were sent and were they delivered?
Which orders have the most notification failures associated with them?
linked_order_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; use equality joins to order table (e.g., ecom_orders). Confirm exact column name in the orders table (order_id / linked_order_id / external_order_id) and type match. Handle NULLs for non-order notifications. Index linked_order_id if many queries join by order.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the notifications system (stored as nvarchar). May represent when the notification record was created in DB or when it was queued.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for audit, pipeline latency (created_at -> sent_ts), and data ingestion/time-based filtering. Useful for debugging and timeline reconstruction.
business_questions: What is the typical latency between created_at and sent_ts for notifications?
Show notifications created during a specific deployment window or campaign.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string; cast to datetime for time comparisons and difference calculations (e.g., DATEDIFF between CAST(sent_ts AS DATETIME) and CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Ensure consistent timezone interpretation. If frequent queries use created_at ranges, convert to native datetime or add an indexed computed column.

order_id.description: Internal unique identifier for the order (string).
order_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for grouping/filtering; de-duplication key; join key to order-level events and external raw sources.
business_questions: How many distinct orders occurred in a period?
Which order_ids correspond to cancelled or refunded transactions?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Use for exact equality joins and GROUP BY. Expect high cardinality; avoid range filters. Check for duplicates since no PK enforced.

order_number.description: Human-readable order reference (string) shown to customers; may contain date/sequence info.
order_number.analysis_use: Reporting label, grouping, searching and joins to external systems (invoices/shipments); useful for human-facing queries and lookups.
business_questions: Find the order details for a given order_number.
List orders in chronological order using order_number pattern if contains time component.
order_number.nl2sql_hints: Also high-cardinality string. Prefer exact match for retrieval. If order_number encodes date, do not assume numeric ordering—use order_ts/created_at for time filters.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who placed the order (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key to customer profile tables for segmentation; grouping for customer-level metrics like repeat purchases, ARPU, LTV proxies.
business_questions: Which customers generated the most revenue in a period?
How many orders per customer and distribution of order frequency?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to ecom_customers or b2b_customers. Nullable/less distinct than order_id. Consider aggregating by customer_id for cohort or repeat analysis.

order_ts.description: Timestamp when the order was placed (stored as nvarchar).
order_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time series (orders over time), time bucketing (day/week/month/hour), peak hour analysis and TTL metrics.
business_questions: How many orders per day/hour/week were placed?
What are peak ordering hours and days by channel?
order_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—convert/cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP for filtering, grouping and ordering (e.g., CAST(order_ts AS datetime) or use to_date). Watch format consistency (appears 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss'). Use safe parsing and handle nulls.

channel.description: Sales channel where order was placed (e.g., Web, Mobile App).
channel.analysis_use: Group/filter for channel-level performance (orders, revenue, conversion), channel mix and trend analysis.
business_questions: Which channel drives higher average order value and revenue?
Is the channel mix shifting over time?
channel.nl2sql_hints: Categorical low-cardinality. Use equality filters and GROUP BY. Consider controlling for casing/whitespace if joins or comparisons fail.

order_status.description: Current lifecycle status of the order (PLACED, CONFIRMED, PACKED, SHIPPED, CANCELLED, etc.).
order_status.analysis_use: Filter for active vs cancelled orders, compute fulfillment KPIs, status transition analysis and funnel metrics.
business_questions: What percentage of orders get cancelled vs shipped?
How many orders are in each status for a given date range?
order_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical; use IN lists for filtering multiple statuses. Be careful if status semantics change; consider normalizing mapping for analysis. Use updated_at or order_events for time of status transitions when available.

payment_status.description: Payment state for the order (PAID, PENDING, REFUNDED).
payment_status.analysis_use: Revenue recognition, identify refunds, reconcile payments, filter to paid orders for valid revenue calculations.
business_questions: How much revenue is in PAID vs PENDING vs REFUNDED states?
How many refunded orders and their total refund amount over time?
payment_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical. For revenue queries restrict to payment_status = 'PAID' to avoid overcounting. Cross-check with ecom_payments table for payment timestamps and amounts if available.

delivery_slot_id.description: Identifier for the delivery time slot selected for the order (e.g., SLOT9).
delivery_slot_id.analysis_use: Group by slot to optimize logistics, compute volumes per slot, and identify slot-level profitability or delays.
business_questions: Which delivery slots have the highest order volume?
Is there a correlation between delivery slot and order cancellations or returns?
delivery_slot_id.nl2sql_hints: Moderate cardinality. Use equality filters or GROUP BY. Map slot ids to delivery_slots table for human-readable times and capacity data.

delivery_address_id.description: Identifier of the delivery address for the order (string).
delivery_address_id.analysis_use: Join to address table for geospatial analysis, compute delivery density, zone-level revenue and logistics planning.
business_questions: Which postal areas or addresses have the most orders?
What is average order value by delivery zone or pin code?
delivery_address_id.nl2sql_hints: High cardinality string. Join to ecom_customer_addresses to resolve geolocation or pin codes. Avoid grouping by this column unless doing address-level analysis.

total_amount.description: Order gross amount before tax/discounts/shipping (stored as nvarchar).
total_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to calculate gross sales, averages, and combine with net_amount, tax and discounts for reconciliation.
business_questions: What is total gross sales in a period?
How does average total_amount compare across channels or campaigns?
total_amount.nl2sql_hints: Numeric values stored as strings—must CAST/CONVERT to DECIMAL/NUMERIC to aggregate. Be careful with punctuation and precision (e.g., trailing decimals). Handle nulls and non-numeric artifacts.

tax_amount.description: Tax component of the order amount (stored as nvarchar).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liabilities, compute effective tax rates (tax_amount / total_amount), and verify tax rounding/consistency.
business_questions: What total tax was collected per month?
What is the effective tax rate by product category or fulfillment centre?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before SUM/AVG. Watch for negative/zero values for refunds. Align with financial reporting periods by casting order_ts/created_at.

discount_amount.description: Discounts applied to the order (stored as nvarchar).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure promotional costs, coupon effectiveness and discounts as % of gross sales. Group by coupon_code or source_campaign.
business_questions: How much discount was given per campaign or coupon?
What is average discount per order and its effect on net_amount?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Consider negative sign conventions. When combining with coupon_code, filter nulls carefully.

shipping_charge.description: Shipping/fulfillment charge applied to the order (stored as nvarchar).
shipping_charge.analysis_use: Calculate logistics revenue/expense per order, net contribution after shipping, and evaluate free-shipping impact.
business_questions: What are total shipping charges collected vs shipping cost (if available) per centre?
Do certain delivery slots or zones have higher shipping charges?
shipping_charge.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Joining to shipment/fulfillment tables may be required to compute true shipping cost vs charge.

net_amount.description: Net payable amount after taxes, discounts and shipping (stored as nvarchar).
net_amount.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric when filtered to PAID orders; used for topline revenue, average order value, and financial KPIs.
business_questions: What is total net revenue by day/channel/campaign?
How does net_amount change for repeat vs new customers?
net_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before aggregation. Use in conjunction with payment_status = 'PAID' for realized revenue. Validate that net_amount ≈ total_amount + shipping_charge + tax_amount - discount_amount.

coupon_code.description: Applied coupon identifier for the order (nullable; e.g., NEWUSER, FRESH10).
coupon_code.analysis_use: Group/filter to measure coupon conversion, lift in AOV, discounts incurred and campaign attribution.
business_questions: Which coupon codes drive the most revenue and orders?
What is average discount per coupon and resulting net lift in orders?
coupon_code.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality with nulls; treat nulls as 'no coupon' in analysis. Use equality and GROUP BY. Join to coupons table (ecom_coupons) for coupon metadata and validity.

source_campaign.description: Acquisition/source campaign attribution for the order (e.g., Google, Organic, Referral, FB Ads).
source_campaign.analysis_use: Marketing attribution and ROI calculations when joined with cost data; group by campaign to compare conversion and revenue.
business_questions: Which source_campaign yields highest ROI and highest average order value?
How does conversion and revenue per campaign change over time?
source_campaign.nl2sql_hints: Categorical with some nulls. For accurate attribution, consider last-touch vs multi-touch models and join to acquisition tables. Use GROUP BY and filter on known campaign labels.

fulfillment_centre_id.description: Identifier/name of the fulfillment/warehouse handling the order (string).
fulfillment_centre_id.analysis_use: Fulfillment throughput, centre-level revenue, average fulfilment times (when joined with events), and capacity planning.
business_questions: Which fulfilment centre processes the most orders and generates the most revenue?
Are cancellation or refund rates different across fulfilment centres?
fulfillment_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality. Use GROUP BY and join to centre metadata (pos_stores or a centres table) for region and capacity data.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the raw table (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: ETL tracking, incremental ingestion queries, record-level audit and near-duplicate detection. Can be used as time filter when order_ts missing or unreliable.
business_questions: How many orders were created/ingested each day (ingest trend)?
Are there delays between order_ts and created_at indicating ingestion latency?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Text timestamp—cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP. Prefer order_ts for business time; use created_at for ingestion/audit queries. Use for incremental sync (WHERE created_at > last_run).

updated_at.description: Last update timestamp for the order record (stored as nvarchar).
updated_at.analysis_use: Track status changes, detect late updates, and incremental delta pulls. Useful to determine when order status or payment_status changed.
business_questions: Which orders were updated in the last 24 hours and what fields changed?
What is the lag between created_at and updated_at for fulfilled orders?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIMESTAMP/DATE for time-based filtering. For state-change analysis, combine with order_events or a history table if available to get granular transitions.

Unnamed: 19.description: Legacy or imported column probably indicating customer type (Repeat/New) with many nulls; appears values 'Repeat' and 'New'.
Unnamed: 19.analysis_use: Segmentation marker for repeat vs new customer analysis when populated; helps split cohorts and compute repeat purchase metrics.
business_questions: What share of orders are from repeat vs new customers and how do their AOVs differ?
Are repeat customers more likely to use certain coupons or channels?
Unnamed: 19.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical; values and null proportion should be validated. Consider deriving repeat/new from customer order history instead of relying on this column. Standardize values and map nulls to 'unknown' when needed.

log_id.description: Unique identifier for the log/event row capturing a status change attempt for a payment (string).
log_id.analysis_use: Row-level identifier — useful for de-duplication and retrieving specific events. Can be used in SELECT, WHERE to fetch single events; not usually aggregated except counts of distinct log_id.
business_questions: Show the audit trail for log_id = 'PLOG14001'.
How many distinct log entries were generated for a given payment_id?
log_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as identifier string. Although distinct = 100, there is no PK; consider using it as unique key for row-level retrieval. Use equality filters (WHERE log_id = '...') and SELECT DISTINCT log_id when needed. Do not assume numeric ordering; ordering must be by parsed timestamps not log_id.

payment_id.description: Identifier of the payment that this status log refers to. Matches payments table payment id domain (e.g., EPAY6001).
payment_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to enrich with payment/order/customer details. Grouping/filtering by payment_id to count attempts, compute retries per payment, find final status per payment.
business_questions: Which payment_ids have the highest number of failed status attempts?
For a payment_id, what was the sequence of status changes and final status?
