payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Use payment_id to join to ecom_payments (LEFT JOIN ecom_payments p ON p.payment_id = log.payment_id). Treat as nvarchar; use exact string match. Consider indexing payment_id for frequent joins/filters. If NL asks for latest event per payment, use partition by payment_id ordered by parsed updated_at DESC or MAX(parsed updated_at).

status_before.description: Payment status before the logged attempt (categorical: PENDING, PAID, INITIATED).
status_before.analysis_use: Group and filter to analyze transitions (e.g., from PENDING -> FAILED), compute transition matrices and funnels, filter specific starting states.
business_questions: How often do transitions start from PENDING and move to FAILED?
From which prior status do most successful transitions originate?
status_before.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality — use IN/=`'PENDING'` filters and GROUP BY status_before. When constructing transition queries, pair status_before with status_after in GROUP BY. Beware of case sensitivity depending on DB collation; normalize if needed (UPPER/LTRIM).

status_after.description: Payment status after the logged attempt (categorical: FAILED, PENDING, PAID). Indicates result of attempt.
status_after.analysis_use: Key target for success/failure metrics. GROUP BY status_after to compute counts, conditional aggregates for success rates, and filter for FAILED rows for troubleshooting.
business_questions: What percentage of attempts ended in FAILED vs PAID?
Which status_after values are most common for manual attempts vs webhook attempts?
status_after.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use WHEN/CASE or FILTER expressions for success/failure calculations (e.g., SUM(CASE WHEN status_after = 'PAID' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)). Low cardinality makes it efficient to aggregate. Combine with parsed updated_at for time series.

updated_at.description: Timestamp when the status change attempt was processed/updated (stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
updated_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-series analyses: trend charts, rate calculations, latency calculations vs created_at, windowing for latest-event-per-payment.
business_questions: How many failures occurred per day/week/month?
What is the distribution of time-of-day for payment failures?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to a datetime type for time-based filters and grouping (e.g., TRY_CAST(updated_at AS datetime) or TO_TIMESTAMP(updated_at, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') depending on DB). Use indexed datetime column for performant time range queries — consider materializing a proper datetime column. For latest-event logic use ORDER BY parsed updated_at DESC or MAX(parsed updated_at) in window functions.

source.description: Origin of the status change attempt (Manual or PG Webhook).
source.analysis_use: Group/filter to compare performance between manual operations and payment gateway webhooks, segment failure rates by source.
business_questions: Do PG Webhook attempts fail more often than Manual attempts?
How many attempts originated from Manual source per day?
source.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string (two values). Use simple equality/IN filters (WHERE source = 'PG Webhook'). Watch for whitespace/case; normalize if inconsistencies exist. Consider indexing if frequently filtered.

response_code.description: HTTP or gateway response code returned for the attempt (stored as string but numeric-like: 200/402/500).
response_code.analysis_use: Used to cluster error types and correlate with status_after. Aggregate counts by response_code, compute which codes most often lead to FAILED.
business_questions: Which response_code is most correlated with status_after = FAILED?
What is the distribution of response_codes over time or by source?
response_code.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, treat as small-set categorical or cast to integer for numeric aggregates (TRY_CAST(response_code AS int)). When writing NL2SQL, map natural language mentions of errors (e.g., '500 errors') to response_code = '500'.

response_body.description: Body of the response from the gateway or system (TEXT), but currently 100% null in this dataset.
response_body.analysis_use: Would hold detailed error messages for debugging but is empty here — not useful unless populated. If populated, useful for text-search/keyword filters to surface specific error patterns.
business_questions: Which error messages appear most often in response_body for failed attempts?
Are there recurring error strings indicating a persistent gateway bug?
response_body.nl2sql_hints: Currently null; NL2SQL should handle absent data gracefully (IS NULL). If populated in future, treat as free text — use LIKE/ILIKE or full text search where supported. Avoid joining on this field. Consider truncation length and indexing strategy for text search.

retry_count.description: Number of retry attempts performed for this particular log entry (stored as string but small integer values 0-3 in sample).
retry_count.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute average retries per payment, identify payments with many retries, and analyze effectiveness of retries (does retry lead to success?).
business_questions: What is the average retry_count for attempts that eventually succeed?
Which payment_ids had more than N retries?
retry_count.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for numeric aggregates (TRY_CAST(retry_count AS int)). If stored as nvarchar, use WHERE retry_count = '3' for simple filters but prefer numeric comparison after casting for ranges. Consider storing as tinyint for performance.

processed_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the log entry has been processed by downstream consumers (stored as '1' or '0' strings).
processed_flag.analysis_use: Filter for unprocessed vs processed events, measure backlog of unprocessed logs, and ensure completion of downstream flows.
business_questions: How many log entries remain unprocessed?
What is the processed_flag ratio by source or status_after?
processed_flag.nl2sql_hints: Map '1'/'0' to boolean (processed = CASE WHEN processed_flag = '1' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). Use equality checks WHERE processed_flag = '0' to find pending items. Consider normalizing to BIT/BOOLEAN type and indexing if used heavily in WHERE clauses.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the log entry was created/inserted (stored as nvarchar, appears in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format).
created_at.analysis_use: Compute latency (updated_at - created_at), ingestion timing, and time to process. Useful for SLA and pipeline monitoring.
business_questions: What is the average time between created_at and updated_at for attempts that failed?
How many events were created in a given date range?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: CAST to datetime for time arithmetic and grouping (TRY_CAST(created_at AS datetime)). For latency calculations use TIMESTAMPDIFF or equivalent on parsed datetimes. Ensure consistent timezone handling if present.

notes.description: Free-text notes associated with the log entry (currently 100% null in sample).
notes.analysis_use: When populated, useful for manual operator annotations or debugging context. Currently not usable due to nulls.
business_questions: Are there operator notes associated with failed attempts describing manual fixes?
Which logs have notes and what categories of notes appear?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Handle as nullable text. NL2SQL should allow searching notes via LIKE/ILIKE or full-text functions when not null. Because it's empty in this dataset, queries should be resilient to notes IS NULL.

payment_id.description: Unique identifier for the payment attempt/record (external/internal payment reference).
payment_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplication, row-level counts, linking payment events; used for uniqueness checks and audit trails.
business_questions: Is payment_id unique or are there duplicate payment attempts for the same payment_id?
Show payment details for payment_id = ? for troubleshooting.
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. When grouping or selecting a single payment use exact string equality. Could be promoted to primary key/index for performance. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace; use TRIM in comparisons if data messy.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the payment.
order_id.analysis_use: Used to join payments to order-level tables (orders, order_lines) for revenue attribution, to detect multiple payment attempts per order, and to compute per-order payment status.
business_questions: Which orders have multiple payment attempts and what were their outcomes?
List orders that have no successful payment (order_id join with ecom_orders).
order_id.nl2sql_hints: String join key. Ensure consistent formatting with orders table (cast/trimming). Consider indexing when performing joins. Use equality joins (payments.order_id = orders.order_id).

payment_ts.description: Timestamp when the payment occurred (stored as nvarchar).
payment_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-based aggregation (daily/hourly), sequencing events, computing latency to settlement and trend analysis. Must be cast to proper datetime for correct filtering/sorting.
business_questions: How many payments occurred per day/hour over a date range?
Show payments in the last 7 days ordered by payment_ts.
payment_ts.nl2sql_hints: Convert to native datetime (e.g., CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP) before date arithmetic or GROUP BY date parts. Expect format like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' — use explicit format parse. Use indexed/casted computed columns in production for performance.

amount.description: Payment amount captured for the transaction (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
amount.analysis_use: Aggregate revenue (SUM), average payment, min/max, currency checks and revenue per gateway/mode/status. Must be cast to numeric/decimal for correct math and rounding.
business_questions: What is total payment volume (SUM(amount)) by payment_mode/gateway for a period?
What is the average amount for failed vs successful payments?
amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC in SQL (e.g., CAST(amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Beware of any non-numeric values; use safe conversion or filtering (ISNUMERIC or TRY_CAST). Consider storing as numeric in upstream schema.

payment_mode.description: Customer-facing payment channel (e.g., COD, NETBANKING, UPI, CARD).
payment_mode.analysis_use: Categorical grouping/filtering to compare volumes, success rates and settlement speeds across modes.
business_questions: Which payment modes generate the highest revenue?
Are failure rates higher for a specific payment_mode?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; use direct equality or IN filters. Consider normalizing into a lookup dimension if used frequently. Ensure case consistency (UPPER/TRIM) when matching.

payment_gateway.description: Processor/gateway handling the payment (e.g., Razorpay, Paytm, BankTransfer).
payment_gateway.analysis_use: Group by gateway to measure success/failure rates, settlement performance and fee/reconciliation analysis.
business_questions: Which gateway has the highest success rate and lowest average time-to-settlement?
What is the revenue processed by each payment_gateway?
payment_gateway.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string. Use equality/IN in filters. Join to gateway-specific logs by gateway + gateway_ref_no if gateway-specific tables exist.

gateway_ref_no.description: Reference/transaction ID assigned by the payment gateway (external reference).
gateway_ref_no.analysis_use: Used for reconciliation with gateway reports, tracing gateway transactions, and joining gateway logs. Also useful for deduplication of gateway-side events.
business_questions: Find gateway transaction details for gateway_ref_no = ? to reconcile with gateway reports.
How many payments have missing or duplicate gateway_ref_no values?
gateway_ref_no.nl2sql_hints: String key; often unique per payment. Use exact matches. May need to join with gateway-specific tables that use this as primary key. Trim/normalize before join.

status.description: Outcome status of the payment attempt (PENDING, FAILED, SUCCESS).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational dashboards (e.g., show pending payments), compute success and failure rates, segment analysis by status over time or by gateway/mode.
business_questions: What percentage of payments are SUCCESS vs FAILED vs PENDING this month?
Show failed payments in the last 24 hours for reconciliation.
status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical string. Use IN ('SUCCESS','FAILED','PENDING') filters. Consider mapping to boolean flags (is_success) for simplified queries. Watch for spelling/case variations.

settlement_status.description: Whether the payment has been settled to merchant accounts (SETTLED or UNSETTLED).
settlement_status.analysis_use: Used to compute settled revenue vs unsettled exposure, reconciliation with bank statements, and to trigger settlement follow-ups.
business_questions: What proportion of collected payments are still UNSETTLED?
What is the monetary value of UNSETTLED vs SETTLED payments?
settlement_status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string; pair with settlement_date for settlement timing. Convert to boolean (is_settled) for simpler conditions. When settlement_status = 'UNSETTLED', settlement_date may be null—guard queries accordingly.

settlement_date.description: Timestamp when the payment was settled (stored as nvarchar).
settlement_date.analysis_use: Compute time-to-settlement (settlement_date - payment_ts), settle-date-based reporting, and detect late settlements. Must be cast to datetime for arithmetic.
business_questions: What is average time-to-settlement by payment_gateway?
List payments settled on a particular date.
settlement_date.nl2sql_hints: Parse same as payment_ts: convert to datetime before date math. Handle NULLs for unsettled records. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_PARSE to handle invalid formats. Consider indexing parsed/normalized settlement_date.

refund_eligible_flag.description: Indicator whether the payment is eligible for refund (1 or 0 stored as nvarchar).
refund_eligible_flag.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate refund-eligible payments, estimate potential refund exposure, combine with status to find refundable successful payments.
business_questions: How many successful payments are refund-eligible and what is their total amount?
What fraction of total payments are refund-eligible?
refund_eligible_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean/bit after casting (e.g., CAST(refund_eligible_flag AS INT) or map '1'/'0' to true/false). Beware of string values; use normalized comparison (='1').

processed_by.description: System or user that processed the payment record (metadata). Current data shows 'system' for all rows.
processed_by.analysis_use: Audit/tracing of automated/manual processing. Low analytic value if constant; useful for filtering non-system actions if present in other snapshots.
business_questions: Which payments were processed manually vs automatically?
Show payments processed_by = 'system' in a window to confirm automated pipeline behavior.
processed_by.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string. If constant, can be ignored in queries but kept for provenance. Normalize case and trim before comparisons.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the ingestion system (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit/ETL monitoring, measure data latency (created_at vs payment_ts), and filter recently ingested records. Cast to datetime for time-based queries.
business_questions: What is the ingestion latency distribution (created_at - payment_ts)?
Show payments created in the last 24 hours.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Convert to native datetime for comparisons. Use created_at for ETL/backfill detection and incremental ingestion filters (e.g., WHERE created_at > @last_run).

notes.description: Free-text notes related to the payment record (currently all NULL).
notes.analysis_use: Potentially holds exceptions or manual comments; currently no analytic value in this snapshot due to 100% NULLs. If populated, useful for classification of exceptions.
business_questions: Are there any manual notes for failed payments to explain root causes?
Filter payments where notes IS NOT NULL to review exceptions.
notes.nl2sql_hints: Text column; may contain sparse values. Use IS NOT NULL filters to find rows with content. Consider dropping/archiving if always NULL to reduce storage and simplify schema.

event_id.description: Unique identifier for each product event record (string).
event_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for de-duplication and tracing; used in filters when referencing specific events. Not typically aggregated (high cardinality). Useful when joining to logs that reference event ids.
business_questions: Can we retrieve the full details for a particular event by event_id?
Are there any duplicate event_id values (data quality check)?
event_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string (nvarchar). Because it's high-cardinality and likely unique, avoid grouping by event_id unless returning a single event. Use simple equality filters (WHERE event_id = '...') or IN lists. If enforcing uniqueness, recommend adding primary key/index. No casting needed.

sku.description: Product stock-keeping unit affected by the event; de-facto product key.
sku.analysis_use: Common grouping and filtering dimension (counts per SKU, joins to product/catalog tables). Useful for per-product aggregations and time-series.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest frequency of OUT_OF_STOCK events?
How many BACK_IN_STOCK events occurred for a given SKU over a time window?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—use exact matches. Use sku to join to product tables (products, ecom_products, product_variants, product_inventory). Consider indexing sku for faster lookups. When users refer to 'product name' in NL, map to sku via product metadata joins.

event_ts.description: Timestamp when the event was recorded/occurred in source systems (stored as nvarchar).
event_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-series, windowing, recency, and trend analysis. Use for filtering by date ranges, grouping into time buckets, and computing event sequences.
business_questions: How many PRICE_DROP events happened per day/week/month?
What is the distribution of events over business hours vs off-hours?
event_ts.nl2sql_hints: Values are stored as strings—CAST/TRY_CAST to datetime (or appropriate DB timestamp) before using date functions. Expect format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'; include TRY/CHECK to avoid failures if malformed. For performance, consider a persisted/virtual column of parsed timestamp or an indexed converted column. Use event_ts for time-based filters rather than created_at if it represents occurrence time.

event_type.description: Categorical type of event (e.g., BACK_IN_STOCK, PRICE_DROP, OUT_OF_STOCK).
event_type.analysis_use: Low-cardinality grouping and filtering dimension for aggregations, ratios, and trend analysis by event type.
business_questions: What percentage of events are PRICE_DROP vs OUT_OF_STOCK vs BACK_IN_STOCK?
Has the rate of OUT_OF_STOCK events increased recently?
event_type.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Good candidate for GROUP BY and filters. Consider using a small dimension table for event_type to standardize values if integrating across systems. Because cardinality is small, queries grouping by event_type are efficient.

old_value.description: Prior value before the event (e.g., previous price or inventory marker). Currently all NULL in the sample.
old_value.analysis_use: When populated, can be used to compute deltas (e.g., old price vs new price) or detect magnitude of changes. In the current dataset it is empty—useful to flag as missing and avoid assumptions.
business_questions: What was the previous price before each PRICE_DROP event?
For price changes, how large are typical decreases (old_value - new_value)?
old_value.nl2sql_hints: Column is nvarchar and currently null across sample—check for population in broader data. If populated, CAST to numeric or appropriate type before arithmetic. In NL2SQL, guard expressions with IS NOT NULL and use TRY_CAST to avoid errors.

new_value.description: New value after the event (e.g., updated price or inventory marker). Currently all NULL in the sample.
new_value.analysis_use: When present, used with old_value to measure change magnitude and direction. Can support business rules (e.g., significant price drops).
business_questions: What new price was applied after each PRICE_DROP event?
How often do BACK_IN_STOCK events change inventory status to 'in_stock' versus other states?
new_value.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; if used for numeric comparisons CAST/TRY_CAST is required. Because current sample shows nulls, NL2SQL must handle absent values safely (COALESCE or IS NOT NULL checks).

triggered_by.description: Source that triggered the event (e.g., system, pricing_bot, inventory_bot).
triggered_by.analysis_use: Categorical filter/grouping to understand automation vs manual/system triggers; attribution of event sources and workload by agent.
business_questions: Which trigger source generates the most PRICE_DROP events?
Are inventory_bot-generated OUT_OF_STOCK events correlated with actual sales drops?
triggered_by.nl2sql_hints: Categorical nvarchar. Low cardinality—useful for GROUP BY and segmentation. Normalize values if multiple synonyms exist (e.g., 'system' vs 'System'). For NL2SQL, map natural language references like 'by pricing bot' to triggered_by = 'pricing_bot'.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the event record was created in this table or ingestion pipeline (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for measuring ingestion latency (created_at - event_ts), auditing, and ordering records by arrival time. May differ from event_ts (occurrence vs ingestion).
business_questions: What is the average/median lag between event occurrence (event_ts) and record creation (created_at)?
Are there spikes in ingestion delays at particular times or for particular trigger sources?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string—CAST/TRY_CAST to datetime before time arithmetic. When calculating lag, ensure both event_ts and created_at are parsed and handle timezone assumptions. Consider creating a derived datetime column for efficient querying.

date.description: The sales date for the row (stored as nvarchar). Likely in YYYY-MM-DD format given sample values.
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering, grouping, and ordering. Used to build time series, aggregate over ranges (day/week/month), and align with calendar tables or other daily metrics.
business_questions: What is the daily net_sales for product X over the last 90 days?
Which dates had anomalously high discount_amount relative to gross_sales?
date.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a date/timestamp in NL2SQL queries: cast/convert to DATE (e.g., CAST(date AS DATE) or TRY_CAST) before grouping by month/week. Support natural language like 'last 30 days' by mapping to date comparisons. Be robust to malformed values and NULLs; use TRY_CAST/ISDATE checks. Normalize timezone or format if necessary.

product_id.description: Identifier for the product (string code, low cardinality).
product_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering dimension for product-level analysis and joins to product master tables for descriptive attributes (name, category). Useful for top-N queries and product comparisons.
business_questions: Which product_ids contribute the most to gross_sales this month?
For product_id ONI003, how have total_orders trended over the past 6 months?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a categorical filter or group key. Joinable to products/product_variants tables; ensure consistent casing/whitespace. Because there is low cardinality, consider IN-list or top-N language mapping. Use exact matches; provide fuzzy matching only if product codes vary.

total_orders.description: Number of orders for the product on that date (stored as text). Could be integer count but currently nvarchar.
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) to compute total orders over ranges, AVG for per-day averages, GROUP BY product or date. Used in rate calculations (orders per customer) and identifying order volume spikes.
business_questions: How many orders did product X receive last month?
Which days had the highest order volume for the top 5 products?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Column must be cast to numeric (INT) before SUM/AVG. Handle non-numeric or NULL values with TRY_CAST/COALESCE(0). NL2SQL should map phrases like 'total orders' to SUM(CAST(total_orders AS INT)) and support filters like 'orders > 100' by casting.

total_customers.description: Count of distinct customers who purchased the product on that date (stored as text).
total_customers.analysis_use: SUM over time to estimate reach, compute average customers per day, and calculate conversion/repurchase metrics when compared to orders. Useful for unique-customer-based KPIs.
business_questions: How many unique customers bought product Y in the last quarter?
Are increases in total_orders matched by increases in total_customers (i.e., new demand vs repeat purchases)?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before aggregation. Watch out for misinterpretation vs distinct customer IDs — this is a pre-aggregated count. For NL2SQL, clarify whether the user wants distinct customers at transaction level (which would require joining raw orders) or to use this pre-aggregated column.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the product on that date (stored as text).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: SUM to calculate volume sold over time, compute average kg per order/customer, and compute yield metrics. Useful for inventory planning and fulfillment sizing.
business_questions: What is the total kg sold for product Z this month?
Which products have the highest average kg per order?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/FLOAT as values may be fractional. Normalize units if other tables store alternative units. NL2SQL should allow unit-aware queries (e.g., 'in kg') and handle conversions if needed. Use TRY_CAST/COALESCE to handle non-numeric entries.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount/pre-fees) for the product on that date (stored as text).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for trend analysis, top-product ranking, contribution analysis. SUM/AVG and time-series aggregations; used with discount/tax/delivery to reconcile net_sales.
business_questions: Which product had the highest gross_sales last week?
What is the gross_sales trend for seasonal products over the past year?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to numeric (DECIMAL). Watch currency formatting (commas, currency symbols) and negative values (returns/refunds). NL2SQL should map 'revenue' or 'sales' to gross_sales by default but allow synonyms to be specified. Validate that gross_sales ≈ net_sales + discount_amount + delivery_fee + tax_amount where applicable.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied to the product on that date (stored as text).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to compute net impact of promotions, discount rate (discount/gross_sales), and to analyze promotion effectiveness. Aggregated over time and by product.
business_questions: How much discount was given on product X during the sale period?
What percentage of gross_sales was lost to discounts for each product last month?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric and handle negative/positive semantics (discounts likely positive representing reduction). Support ratio calculations and guard against division by zero. NL2SQL should interpret 'discounts' as SUM(CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL)).

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees charged for the product on that date (stored as text). Could be zero if delivery fee applied at order-level and allocated to product rows.
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Analyze contribution of delivery fees to revenue, examine fee trends, and compute net profit adjustments. Aggregate by product/date and compare to gross/net sales.
business_questions: How much delivery fee revenue did product X generate last quarter?
Do delivery fees materially affect net_sales for high-volume low-margin products?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Understand allocation methodology: delivery fees at product-level may be apportioned; NL2SQL assistant should not assume per-order fees unless documented. Use COALESCE for missing values and ensure the syntax allows summing small decimal amounts.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the product on that date (stored as text).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liability per product/date, compute effective tax rates (tax/gross_sales), and reconcile with financial reporting. Useful for compliance and margin calculations.
business_questions: What was the total tax collected for product category A last month?
What is the effective tax rate for product X over the year?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Watch for different tax schemes (inclusive vs exclusive). NL2SQL should support tax rate calculations and be resilient to zero gross_sales (avoid division by zero).

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the product on that date (likely gross_sales - discount_amount + delivery_fee + tax_amount or similar; stored as text).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary metric for revenue-based rankings, trend analysis, and financial KPIs. Summation across time gives realized revenue after adjustments; compare net_sales to gross_sales to understand discounts/fees impact.
business_questions: What are net_sales by product for the last fiscal quarter?
Which products improved net_sales month-over-month after promotional periods?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL. Verify consistency between net_sales and component columns; inconsistencies may indicate data-quality issues. NL2SQL should prefer net_sales for revenue questions but allow decomposition into gross/discounts/delivery/tax upon user request. Support filters like 'net_sales > X' using numeric cast.

rating_summary_id.description: A unique identifier for the summary row (string). Acts as a stable ID for this snapshot/record of a rating summary.
rating_summary_id.analysis_use: Used as a surrogate identifier for selecting or updating specific summary rows; useful for point lookups or deduplication. Not typically aggregated; can be used to join to external metadata if other systems reference this ID.
business_questions: Can we fetch the exact rating summary record (including when it was updated) for a given rating_summary_id?
Has a specific rating_summary_id been updated within a given time window?
rating_summary_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. Because it's stored as nvarchar, exact equality matching is safe. Use WHERE rating_summary_id = 'RATE07001' for point lookups. Consider trimming whitespace in case upstream data contains padding. If implementing change detection, use it in WHERE/ORDER BY clauses but do not expect it to be numeric or sortable beyond lexicographic order.

sku.description: Stock-keeping unit (product identifier) representing the product to which these ratings belong.
sku.analysis_use: Primary column for grouping and joining. Commonly used in GROUP BY to aggregate across SKUs, ORDER BY to rank SKUs, and JOINs to product catalog and reviews. Low cardinality (7 distinct) in this sample makes it efficient for group-level summaries.
business_questions: What is the average rating and total reviews for SKU 'GUR010'?
Which SKU has seen the greatest drop/increase in average rating over time (when combined with historical snapshots)?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use exact matches or IN lists for filters (WHERE sku = 'GUR010' or WHERE sku IN (...)). For joins, match sku to products.sku or product_variants.sku; ensure consistent casing and trimming since this is nvarchar. If SKU formats differ across tables, normalize (UPPER/TRIM) in the NL2SQL conversion.

avg_rating.description: The average rating value for the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar) but representing a numeric value (e.g., 4.53).
avg_rating.analysis_use: Requires casting to numeric for meaningful aggregation, sorting, comparisons and threshold filters. Used for ranking (ORDER BY avg_rating DESC), computing weighted averages, grouping by rounded rating bands, and deriving insights like percent above/below thresholds.
business_questions: Which SKUs have an average rating below 3.5 and need quality intervention?
What is the average of avg_rating across all SKUs (i.e., mean of per-SKU averages)?
avg_rating.nl2sql_hints: Always convert to a numeric type before numeric operations: e.g., TRY_CAST(avg_rating AS FLOAT) or TRY_CONVERT(DECIMAL(5,2), avg_rating). Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to avoid failures on malformed strings. Do not use raw string comparison (it sorts lexicographically). When using GROUP BY on numeric buckets, cast then apply FLOOR or ROUND. For NULLs or invalid strings, coalesce (COALESCE(TRY_CAST(avg_rating AS FLOAT), 0)) if appropriate.

total_reviews.description: Total number of reviews for the SKU, stored as text (nvarchar) but represents a small integer count.
total_reviews.analysis_use: Used for aggregation (SUM/COUNT), filtering (e.g., only SKUs with at least N reviews), weighting average ratings (weight by total_reviews), and ranking (order by review count). Must be cast to integer for numeric semantics.
business_questions: Which SKUs have more than 50 reviews?
What is the total number of reviews across all SKUs in the table?
total_reviews.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer: TRY_CAST(total_reviews AS INT) or TRY_CONVERT(INT, total_reviews). Use in WHERE clauses like WHERE TRY_CAST(total_reviews AS INT) >= 50. Be mindful of empty strings or non-numeric values; use TRY_CAST and COALESCE to handle invalids. Because max length is 2, values >99 are unexpected; validate when converting.

last_review_ts.description: Timestamp of the most recent review for the SKU, stored as text in format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'.
last_review_ts.analysis_use: Critical for freshness checks and time-based filtering (e.g., find SKUs with last_review_ts older than 30 days). Use for ordering by recency and for joins where you need the most recent review timestamp. Must be cast to datetime for date arithmetic.
business_questions: Which SKUs have not received any reviews in the last 90 days?
For each SKU, when was the most recent review posted?
last_review_ts.nl2sql_hints: Convert to datetime using TRY_CAST(last_review_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_CONVERT(DATETIME, last_review_ts, 120) if using SQL Server. Use date functions (DATEDIFF, DATEADD) after casting. Handle NULLs and invalid formats with TRY_CAST and COALESCE. Avoid lexicographic date comparisons on the raw string unless format is strictly ISO; better to cast for safety.

updated_at.description: Timestamp when this summary row was last updated in the system, stored as text in format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'.
updated_at.analysis_use: Used to determine recency of summary data, schedule refresh logic, or filter for incremental processing (e.g., select rows updated since last ETL). Useful for auditing and syncing with downstream consumers.
business_questions: Which rating summaries have been updated within the last 24 hours?
When was the last time the ratings summary table was refreshed for a specific SKU?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime before time comparisons: TRY_CAST(updated_at AS DATETIME). Use in WHERE clauses for incremental extraction: WHERE TRY_CAST(updated_at AS DATETIME) > @last_job_time. If using this column for change-detection in NL2SQL, ensure timezone assumptions are known; strings lack timezone info. Use COALESCE/TRY_CAST to avoid errors from malformed values.

refund_id.description: Unique identifier for the refund event (string).
refund_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for deduplication and row-level reporting; used to count refunds, link audit logs and trace payment gateway references.
business_questions: How many distinct refunds occurred in a period?
Are there duplicate refund_ids indicating data issues?
refund_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string key. Use equality or GROUP BY on refund_id for dedupe. Expect uniqueness but no PK constraint—queries that assume uniqueness should validate distinct count. No casting required.

order_id.description: Identifier of the related order (string), ties refund to an order.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders table for customer, order value and order date; used to group refunds by order, customer or sales channel.
business_questions: Which orders had refunds and what was the refunded amount per order?
What customers or channels have the highest refunded totals (requires join to orders/customers)?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: This is a natural join key to ecom_orders. Use LEFT JOIN when you want refunds regardless of whether order record exists. Watch for casing/format differences and trailing spaces; column stored as nvarchar — compare as string. Consider indexing in production.

payment_id.description: Identifier of the payment associated with the refund (string).
payment_id.analysis_use: Join key to payment records to reconcile refunds with payment transactions and gateway outcomes; used to analyze refund success by payment method/provider.
business_questions: Which payment transactions resulted in refunds?
What payment methods/gateways have the highest refund rates (requires join to payments)?
payment_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to ecom_payments on string equality. Nulls are possible; cast not needed for equality but required if joining to numeric PKs. If searching by payment attributes, join then filter on payment_method or provider.

refund_ts.description: Timestamp when the refund was requested/initiated, stored as nvarchar (string).
refund_ts.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate refunds over time (daily/weekly/monthly trends), compute time-based KPIs. Requires casting/parse to datetime for proper ordering and range filtering.
business_questions: How many refunds were initiated per day/week/month?
Are refunds increasing or decreasing over recent periods?
refund_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — always CAST/CONVERT to a datetime/timestamp in queries (e.g., TRY_CONVERT/TO_TIMESTAMP depending on SQL dialect). Use normalized UTC if timezone information missing. Beware of malformed strings; use safe parsing or TRY_CAST to avoid query failures.

refund_amount.description: Monetary amount refunded, currently stored as nvarchar with numeric-looking values.
refund_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM, AVG), compute totals per period/reason/mode/status, and reconcile with payments. Must be cast to numeric/decimal for arithmetic.
business_questions: What is the total monetary impact of refunds in a given period?
What is the average refund amount by reason or status?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DECIMAL/NUMERIC using CAST or TRY_CAST before SUM/AVG. Be cautious of currency and precision; if multi-currency exists, join to order/payment currency or normalize. Handle nulls and malformed values (use TRY_CAST or filter non-numeric rows).

refund_mode.description: Mode of refund: categorical values like ACCOUNT_CREDIT or PG_REFUND.
refund_mode.analysis_use: Group and filter refunds by mode to understand operational flows and cost centers; analyze amounts and counts per mode.
business_questions: What share of refunds are issued as account credit vs payment gateway refunds?
Which refund mode has higher failure or retry rates?
refund_mode.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string — use equality or IN lists. Low cardinality is good for GROUP BY. Normalize values if inconsistent casing/spaces. Consider mapping to business-friendly labels in NL2SQL.

gateway_ref_no.description: Reference number returned by the payment gateway for the refund (string).
gateway_ref_no.analysis_use: Reconciliation with gateway logs, tracing refunds in external systems, and deduplicating gateway events.
business_questions: Which refunds have a gateway reference and which failed to produce one?
Can we reconcile refund amounts with gateway transaction IDs for auditing?
gateway_ref_no.nl2sql_hints: Use for joins/text matching to payment gateway tables/logs. Treat as string; may be null for ACCOUNT_CREDIT refunds. Pattern matching (LIKE) sometimes needed if prefixes vary (e.g., RPG...).

status.description: Operational status of the refund: COMPLETED, INITIATED, FAILED (categorical).
status.analysis_use: Filter/group for success/failure rates, SLA and operational monitoring, and identifying refunds needing attention.
business_questions: What percentage of refunds are completed vs failed?
Which reasons or modes have higher failure rates?
status.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — ideal for GROUP BY. Use explicit status lists in NL queries (e.g., status = 'FAILED'). Consider status transitions: join with event logs if time-series status evolution is needed.

processed_by.description: Identifier of the team or user who processed the refund (string).
processed_by.analysis_use: Operational ownership, audits, and SLA measurement by team or user; can be grouped to measure throughput and performance.
business_questions: Which teams process the most refunds and how quickly?
Are specific operators associated with higher failure/retry rates?
processed_by.nl2sql_hints: Values appear to be team names (finance_team in sample). Join to users/teams table if available to enrich. Column may have low variance — prefer grouping when useful. Treat as string; watch for nulls or generic values.

processed_at.description: Timestamp when the refund was processed/handled, stored as nvarchar (string).
processed_at.analysis_use: Used to compute processing latency (processed_at - refund_ts), SLA adherence, daily load and processing time distributions. Requires casting to datetime.
business_questions: What is the median processing time for refunds?
How many refunds were processed each hour/day?
processed_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for arithmetic. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to avoid parse errors. Subtract refund_ts (after parsing) to compute latency; ensure both timestamps are in the same timezone/format.

reason.description: Business reason for the refund (e.g., Quality Issue, Order Cancelled, Customer Return).
reason.analysis_use: Group and filter to identify dominant refund drivers, prioritize quality or process improvements, and compute cost by reason.
business_questions: What are the top reasons driving refund volume and value?
How do reasons vary by product category or vendor (requires joins)?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical string — good for GROUP BY. Normalize values (trim/case) if user input allowed. When joining to returns/return_items, ensure mapping between reason text and return codes is consistent.

notes.description: Free-text notes about the refund (string). In sample all values are NULL.
notes.analysis_use: Potentially useful for auditing or manual investigation if populated; not useful for aggregate analytics in current sample due to 100% nulls.
business_questions: Are there manual notes explaining failed refunds (requires populated notes)?
Can notes help identify systemic issues outside structured reason codes?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Mostly null in sample — include IS NOT NULL filters to find meaningful entries. Free text may require full-text search or LIKE patterns; performance considerations apply. Avoid relying on notes for automated metrics.

return_item_id.description: Unique identifier for the returned item record (row-level id for a returned line item).
return_item_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplicating and linking single-row return-item operations. Useful for row-level counts, deduplication, and as a join key if used by other systems.
business_questions: How many unique return_item records exist in a time window?
Are there duplicate return_item_id values indicating data quality issues?
return_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a unique identifier string. In NL2SQL expect exact-match filters (WHERE return_item_id = 'RIT11001') and existence checks. Consider making it a primary key or indexed for fast point lookups. No casting needed, but check for leading/trailing whitespace.

return_id.description: Identifier of the return transaction this item belongs to (groups multiple return_item rows under one return event).
return_id.analysis_use: Group-level aggregations (items per return), join to returns table for return-level metadata (return date, customer). Filter to narrow to a particular return or set of returns.
business_questions: How many returned items are associated with a specific return (items per return)?
Which returns have the largest total returned quantity or largest number of items?
return_id.nl2sql_hints: Commonly used in GROUP BY and JOINs to returns or returns metadata. Expect equality and IN filters. Not currently defined as FK in DDL, but should be mapped to the returns table for richer queries.

order_item_id.description: Identifier of the originating order line/item that was returned (links returned item back to the sold order line).
order_item_id.analysis_use: Join key to order_items/order_lines to fetch order_id, product_id, sold price and return rates by original sale. Useful for tracing returned quantities back to sales and computing return rate per order item.
business_questions: Which sold order items (or orders) have the highest return counts or quantities?
Are there specific order items that are returned frequently (repeat returns)?
order_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a join key to order items table (ORDER BY or GROUP BY after join). Stored as nvarchar; treat as string in equality joins. Watch for inconsistent prefixes/formatting; consider normalizing if joins fail.

sku.description: Stock keeping unit of the item returned (product-level identifier).
sku.analysis_use: Group and aggregate returns by product SKU (counts, sum qty), join to product dimension tables to enrich with category/brand. Filter for SKU-level diagnostics.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the highest returned quantity or frequency?
What dispositions and restockability rates exist per SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Often used in GROUP BY and WHERE clauses. Join to products/product_variants on sku. Stored as nvarchar; ensure consistent case/whitespace when matching. Because SKU cardinality is low here, aggregations will be small and fast.

qty.description: Quantity of units returned for this return_item record. Currently stored as nvarchar.
qty.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to compute total returned units by SKU/return/disposition. Use in weighted calculations like returned value when multiplied by unit price (after joining price data).
business_questions: What is the total quantity returned by SKU, return, or time period?
Which returns contain the largest total returned quantity?
qty.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/integer before aggregations (e.g., CAST(qty AS INT) or TRY_CAST depending on SQL engine). Watch for non-numeric values or NULLs. In NL2SQL prompts prefer numeric aggregation verbs ('sum returned qty') and hint to cast qty.

inspected_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the returned item has been physically inspected (1/0 stored as nvarchar).
inspected_flag.analysis_use: Filter to inspected vs uninspected items, compute inspection coverage rates, and measure outcomes (disposition/restockable) conditioned on inspection status.
business_questions: What percentage of returned items have been inspected?
Of inspected items, how many are restockable vs discarded?
inspected_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat '1'/'0' strings as booleans. Convert to boolean or integer for boolean logic (CASE WHEN inspected_flag = '1' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END). Expect straightforward WHERE filters (WHERE inspected_flag = '1') and GROUP BY for counts.

inspected_at.description: Timestamp when the return item was inspected; stored as nvarchar (timestamp-like strings present).
inspected_at.analysis_use: Temporal analyses: inspection timing, SLA/latency from return creation to inspection (when joined to return or order timestamps), hourly/daily inspection trends. Can compute inspection lag and peak inspection periods after casting to datetime.
business_questions: How long after a return is an item typically inspected (inspection turnaround time)?
When are inspections most commonly performed (hour of day / day of week)?
inspected_at.nl2sql_hints: Must cast to a datetime type for time-based comparisons and grouping (e.g., CAST(inspected_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE). Handle invalid/NULL values. NL2SQL should map temporal phrases like 'last 7 days' or 'month of November' to proper WHERE inspected_at BETWEEN ... after casting.

restockable_flag.description: Flag indicating if the inspected returned item is suitable to return to inventory (1/0 stored as nvarchar).
restockable_flag.analysis_use: Compute proportion of returns that can be restocked, estimate inventory reinstatement volumes, and feed to inventory replenishment decisions. Combine with disposition for reconciliation.
business_questions: What percentage of returned items are restockable overall and by SKU?
How many restockable units can be returned to inventory per day/week?
restockable_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean-like string. Use WHERE restockable_flag = '1' or convert to integer for aggregations. Consider combining with inspected_flag in filters to ensure restockable only considered after inspection.

disposition.description: Outcome assigned to the returned item post-inspection (e.g., RESTOCK, DISCARD, RETURN_TO_SUPPLIER).
disposition.analysis_use: Group by disposition to measure frequency, compute counts/qty per disposition, and analyze downstream impact (inventory, supplier returns, write-offs). Low cardinality makes it ideal for categorical grouping.
business_questions: What proportion of returned items are restocked vs discarded vs sent back to supplier?
Which SKUs are most commonly discarded or returned to supplier?
disposition.nl2sql_hints: Used in GROUP BY and WHERE filters. No casting necessary. Expect exact-match categorical filters (WHERE disposition = 'DISCARD') or GROUP BY disposition for distribution. Watch for inconsistent spelling/casing.

notes.description: Free-text notes about the return item (currently 100% NULL in the sample).
notes.analysis_use: If populated, used for qualitative context, manual review, or text analytics (keyword search, sentiment). Currently not useful in numeric aggregates and may be dropped if always NULL.
business_questions: Are there recurring textual reasons logged for returns (e.g., damaged, wrong item) that indicate product or fulfillment issues?
Can notes help triage exceptions that are not captured by structured disposition codes?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nullable text. In NL2SQL, text search predicates (LIKE, ILIKE, CONTAINS) may be required. Given current nulls, include logic to handle NULL safely (e.g., WHERE notes IS NOT NULL AND notes LIKE '%damaged%'). Consider excluding from default SELECT * views for performance if always NULL.

review_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplication, row-level filtering and linking to external review systems or logs. Use in COUNT(DISTINCT) or as GROUP BY when wanting review-level details.
business_questions: How many unique reviews exist in a period?
Fetch the full review record by review_id for moderation or display.
review_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. When selecting a specific review use equality on review_id. Because no PK is enforced, recommend deduplicating by review_id if ingest issues suspected. Use exact match predicates rather than LIKE for performance.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who wrote the review.
customer_id.analysis_use: Join key to customer dimension (ecom_customers) for enrichment; group by to find reviewers with multiple reviews; filter to get reviews by a given customer.
business_questions: Which customers have submitted the most reviews?
Retrieve all reviews submitted by a particular customer_id.
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; join should match naming convention in ecom_customers.customer_id. Use equality joins; watch for whitespace/casing differences. Consider casting or trimming if joins fail.

sku.description: Product SKU the review is associated with.
sku.analysis_use: Natural join/link to product tables to aggregate reviews per product/SKU; group by to get avg rating, review counts, and helpful sums by SKU.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the most 5-star reviews?
What is the average rating per SKU over the last 30/90 days?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality (10 values) — grouping is efficient. Join to product tables may require different column names (sku, product_id, variant_sku). Use LEFT JOIN when product may be missing. Ensure exact matches and trim spaces if needed.

rating.description: Rating given by customer (stored as text, values like '3','4','5').
rating.analysis_use: Aggregate (AVG, distribution counts), filter for N-star reviews, compute NPS-like metrics, binning and trend analysis by time or SKU. Must CAST/CONVERT to integer for numeric ops.
business_questions: What is the average rating for a SKU in the last month?
How many 3-star vs 4-star vs 5-star reviews did we receive last quarter?
rating.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; always CAST(rating AS INT) or TRY_CAST to avoid errors if dirty data appears. When grouping use the casted numeric value for correct ordering. Handle unexpected values (NULLs/non-numeric) with SAFE_CAST/TRY_CONVERT.

title.description: Short headline of the review (categorical; limited distinct values).
title.analysis_use: Categorical aggregation for quick sentiment buckets, display in UI, group by title for counts, identify common complaint/success themes.
business_questions: What are the most common review titles for a SKU?
Which titles correspond to lower ratings (e.g., 'Too ripe')?
title.nl2sql_hints: Text column with low distinct values — group by title works well. Titles may contain punctuation; use exact match or simple LIKE for partial matching. Consider mapping common title strings to sentiment tags in SQL or downstream preprocessing.

review_text.description: Full short review text/feedback provided by the customer.
review_text.analysis_use: Text analysis and sentiment classification, keyword search, manual moderation filtering, grouping by exact phrase (here limited set of distinct values).
business_questions: Show reviews that mention 'packaging' or 'overripe' for product quality investigations.
Which review texts have the highest helpful_count?
review_text.nl2sql_hints: Despite nvarchar, values are short. For NL2SQL expect use of LIKE '%keyword%' or full-text search if available. Escape single quotes in user input. For aggregated sentiment, map common phrases to categories before aggregation.

review_ts.description: Timestamp of the review (string representation, ISO-like).
review_ts.analysis_use: Primary time field for time-series analysis (trends, rolling windows) after casting to datetime. Use for filtering by date ranges and aligning with order/fulfillment timelines.
business_questions: How many reviews were posted each day last month?
Show review activity peak hours for the platform.
review_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; must CAST/CONVERT to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CONVERT or PARSE depending on DB). NL2SQL should produce SQL that converts this column before date arithmetic, e.g., CAST(review_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE. Validate time zone assumptions if present.

verified_purchase_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the review is from a verified purchase (string '1' in current data).
verified_purchase_flag.analysis_use: Filter or segment reviews by purchase verification. Compute proportions of verified vs unverified reviews. However current data has no variability (all '1'), limiting usefulness.
business_questions: What percent of reviews are from verified purchases?
Do verified purchase reviews have higher average ratings than unverified ones?
verified_purchase_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text; treat '1' as true. Use equality comparisons like verified_purchase_flag = '1'. If other values possible (0/NULL/'Y'), include normalization logic (CASE WHEN ...). Because data currently constant, queries should guard against empty result sets when filtering for '0'.

helpful_count.description: Number of users who found the review helpful (stored as nvarchar).
helpful_count.analysis_use: Rank and surface most helpful reviews, sum helpful counts per SKU or customer, filter for reviews with helpful_count >= threshold. Must CAST to integer for numeric operations.
business_questions: List top 10 most helpful reviews across the platform.
What is the total helpful_count received by reviews for SKU X?
helpful_count.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to INT before aggregation or ordering. Watch for non-numeric or NULL values. Use COALESCE(TRY_CAST(helpful_count AS INT),0) for safe numeric handling. Use ORDER BY CAST(helpful_count AS INT) DESC for top-N queries.

responded_by.description: Identifier of staff/agent who responded to the review (NULL for all current rows).
responded_by.analysis_use: Identify which reviews have agent responses, track response assignment and workload once populated. Currently all NULL so no analysis until data present.
business_questions: Which agents responded to the most reviews last month?
Show reviews that have not received a response from staff.
responded_by.nl2sql_hints: Nullable text field; use IS NOT NULL / IS NULL to detect responses. When joining to staff/user tables ensure matching key names. Guard NL2SQL for empty responses by checking both NULL and empty-string cases (responded_by IS NOT NULL AND responded_by <> '').

response_text.description: Text of the staff response to the review (NULL for all current rows).
response_text.analysis_use: Moderation and quality-of-response review; search responses for standard templates or escalation. Currently no values present.
business_questions: Show reviews with staff responses containing 'apolog' or 'refund'.
How long after review creation do staff typically respond (when response exists)?
response_text.nl2sql_hints: Text field; when present use LIKE for keyword search. Combine with review_ts/response_ts (if available) to compute response latency. Treat NULL and empty strings similarly when filtering.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the reviews table (string form).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time tracking; compare created_at and review_ts to detect delayed loads or backfilled reviews. Cast to datetime for ordering and time-range filters.
business_questions: When were these review records ingested into the system?
Are there reviews with review_ts much older than created_at (late arrivals)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as nvarchar; CAST/CONVERT to datetime for comparisons. Use created_at when filtering by ingestion date; be explicit in NL2SQL whether user wants review_ts (customer time) vs created_at (system ingestion time).

date.description: Reporting date for the aggregated metrics (stored as nvarchar, values look like 'YYYY-MM-DD').
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for filtering, grouping, ordering, and time-series calculations. Use for window functions, date range WHERE clauses, aggregations by day/week/month after casting to DATE.
business_questions: What are sales and orders on 2024-01-05?
How do daily net sales compare month-over-month?
date.nl2sql_hints: Cast this nvarchar to DATE (e.g., CAST(date AS DATE) or TRY_CAST) for correct sorting, range filtering and grouping. Accept common YYYY-MM-DD formats; handle NULLs and malformed strings with TRY_CAST/ISDATE. Prefer indexing or partitioning on the proper DATE-converted column for performance.

order_channel.description: Source channel for the orders aggregated in the row (examples: Mobile App, WhatsApp, Website).
order_channel.analysis_use: Low-cardinality dimension for grouping and filtering to compare channel performance and compute channel shares. Useful as a join key to channel dimension tables if available.
business_questions: Which channel drives the most daily orders?
How does average order value differ by channel?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Use equality or IN filters for fast selective queries. Normalize values (trim/case) if joins to canonical channel master are required. Consider creating a small lookup/dimension when joining to channel-level metadata.

total_orders.description: Number of orders on that date for that channel (stored as nvarchar, small integers).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation and arithmetic (SUM, AVG), denominator for rate metrics (e.g., AOV = sales / total_orders). Grouping and time-series of order counts.
business_questions: How many orders were placed on a specific date or channel?
What is the trend in daily order volume over the last 30 days?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER/SMALLINT before aggregation (e.g., TRY_CAST(total_orders AS INT)). Handle NULLs and non-numeric strings. Avoid SUM on nvarchar directly; explicit cast needed for correct NL2SQL generation. Consider validating zero or negative values.

total_customers.description: Number of distinct customers (or customer count) represented in that day's orders for that channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_customers.analysis_use: Aggregations and ratios (orders per customer), measuring reach and daily unique customers. Useful for retention/engagement metrics when joined with customer tables.
business_questions: How many unique customers purchased via Website last week?
What is the orders-per-customer ratio by channel?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER before arithmetic. Clarify whether this is unique customers or total customer entries; use caution when comparing with distinct counts from transactional data. Use TRY_CAST and coalesce to avoid query failures on malformed values.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the day/channel (stored as nvarchar, decimal formatted strings).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation (SUM/AVG) to track volumes. Useful for logistics/planning, average kg per order, and correlation with revenue.
business_questions: What was the total kg sold yesterday through Mobile App?
What is the average kg per order by channel?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (appropriate precision) before aggregation, e.g., TRY_CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4)). Watch for locale issues (commas vs dots). Use safe-casting to avoid exceptions.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount/fees/taxes) for the date and channel (stored as nvarchar decimal strings).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue metric for SUM, trend, comparison and percentage calculations (channel share). Used as numerator for discount and net comparisons.
business_questions: What were daily gross sales by channel last month?
Which days had abnormal spikes in gross sales?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)). Avoid string-based comparisons. Use TRY_CAST to guard against invalid formats. For NL2SQL, prefer explicit numeric types to allow aggregations and arithmetic operations.

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount applied on that day/channel (stored as nvarchar decimal strings).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation to compute total discounts and discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales). Useful for promotions analysis and margin impact.
business_questions: How much discount did we give last week and what percentage of gross sales was that?
Which channel has the highest discount per order?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before use. Ensure division by zero is handled when computing rates. Null/empty strings should be treated as 0 or handled explicitly.

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fee collected (or charged) for that date/channel (stored as nvarchar decimal strings).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Aggregation for revenue/fee contribution, net revenue reconciliation, and profitability analysis when combined with cost data.
business_questions: How much delivery fee was collected by channel yesterday?
Do delivery fees offset delivery costs (requires join to cost data)?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for sums and arithmetic. Clarify whether fees are included in gross/net sales. Handle NULLs appropriately (coalesce to 0).

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for that day/channel (stored as nvarchar decimal strings).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregation for compliance and net revenue calculations. Useful in calculating net-of-tax revenue and tax rate analyses.
business_questions: What was the total tax collected last quarter by channel?
What is the effective tax rate (tax_amount / gross_sales) over time?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before aggregations. Avoid dividing by zero. Be aware of tax granularity—this is an aggregate and may not map perfectly to transaction-level tax logic.

net_sales.description: Net sales after discounts, taxes, and fees for the date/channel (stored as nvarchar decimal strings).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line revenue metric for trend, comparison, aggregation and profitability calculations. Used as output for dashboards and KPI calculations.
business_questions: What were the daily net sales per channel last month?
How much did net sales change vs the prior period?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before math. Validate that net_sales equals gross_sales - discount_amount - tax_amount +/- delivery_fee (if that relationship is expected). Use TRY_CAST to avoid exceptions on bad input.

created_at.description: Ingestion or row creation timestamp (stored as nvarchar, two distinct timestamp values present). Likely indicates when the dataset snapshot was loaded.
created_at.analysis_use: Metadata field to filter by ingest batch, track when rows were added/updated, or for debugging data freshness. Not used as primary time dimension for business reporting.
business_questions: When was this data snapshot loaded?
Which ingestion batch contains a given date range?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/DATETIME2 where needed (TRY_CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Use for metadata filtering but prefer the 'date' column for reporting. Because it has only two distinct values, it is low-selectivity — avoid indexing solely on this column.

Unnamed: 11.description: Empty import artifact column — contains only NULLs.
Unnamed: 11.analysis_use: No analytic value. Can be dropped to simplify schema.
business_questions: Is there any data in this column? (No)
Can this column be removed to streamline the table? (Yes)
Unnamed: 11.nl2sql_hints: Ignore or drop. NL2SQL systems should exclude entirely from SELECT lists by default. If present, treat as NULL.

Unnamed: 12.description: Empty import artifact column — contains only NULLs.
Unnamed: 12.analysis_use: No analytic value. Safe to drop.
business_questions: Does this column contain useful information? (No)
Should queries reference this column? (No)
Unnamed: 12.nl2sql_hints: Ignore or drop. Exclude from generated queries and type inference.

Unnamed: 13.description: Sparse import artifact column with very high null rate (~99.8%) and a few repeated short string codes (e.g., 'da','df','gs','ns','ta').
Unnamed: 13.analysis_use: Likely no business relevance in current form; investigate origin. If it maps to a known code (region, dataset, or source), it could be used as a low-volume filter or join key, otherwise drop.
business_questions: What do the codes in this column represent and how do they map to business entities?
Are rows with a non-null value significant for a specific analysis or data partition?
Unnamed: 13.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical but verify semantics before using. Use IS NOT NULL filters to find the few non-empty rows. Validate and map codes to meaningful values before exposing in NL2SQL interfaces.

Unnamed: 14.description: Sparse import artifact numeric-like column (mostly NULLs) containing a small set of repeated large numeric values; likely an aggregated identifier or legacy metric.
Unnamed: 14.analysis_use: Investigate origin — could be an external ID, cumulative metric, or erroneous import. Not reliable for general analytics until provenance is confirmed.
business_questions: What does this numeric value represent and why is it present only for a few rows?
Should these values be merged into a known metric or removed?
Unnamed: 14.nl2sql_hints: Handle as DECIMAL/NUMERIC if used, but first validate meaning. If kept, CAST using TRY_CAST to numeric; otherwise drop to avoid confusing NL2SQL models. Because it's sparse, avoid using it in predicates expecting broad coverage.

year.description: Calendar year for the monthly aggregation (stored as nvarchar). In this dataset only '2024' appears.
year.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering for time-based queries; used to build year-over-year or single-year reports and to join with other tables that have year columns or date ranges.
business_questions: Show KPIs for year 2024 by month and channel.
Filter the dataset to a specific year for reporting.
year.nl2sql_hints: Treat as an integer or smallint in NL2SQL filters (e.g., WHERE CAST(year AS INT) = 2024). Normalize to numeric type when performing comparisons or ranges. If multiple years are expected, ensure casting before grouping.

month.description: Calendar month number (1-12) represented as nvarchar. Distinct values indicate full monthly coverage.
month.analysis_use: Primary time grain for grouping and ordering. Combine with year to form a date period or to sort months chronologically. Useful to filter a specific month or range.
business_questions: What were the net sales in month 10 across channels?
Show month-over-month change in gross_sales for the year.
month.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for numeric comparisons and sorting (e.g., ORDER BY CAST(month AS INT)). If formatting is required (e.g., '2024-01'), build a date using year and month after casting. Beware of leading zeros or non-numeric values.

order_channel.description: The channel through which the order was placed (e.g., Mobile App, Website, WhatsApp). Low cardinality categorical field.
order_channel.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering to compare performance across channels; good for pivoting KPIs by channel.
business_questions: Which channel contributed most to total_orders in July?
Compare average order value across order channels for the year.
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Use as a GROUP BY or filter predicate. Because cardinality is small, consider indexing or enumerating values in NL2SQL suggestions. Joins on channel are possible if other tables have a matching channel column (ensure consistent naming and values).

total_orders.description: Total number of orders in the month for the channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregate metric for counts, used in trend analysis, average order value calculations (net_sales/total_orders) and per-customer metrics.
business_questions: How many total orders did the Website have in March?
What is the month with maximum total_orders across channels?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer (e.g., CAST(total_orders AS INT) or TRY_CAST). Avoid string comparisons. When summing across months, use numeric conversion. Check for non-integer or null values before aggregating.

total_customers.description: Number of unique customers contributing to orders in that month and channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_customers.analysis_use: Used to compute per-customer metrics (orders per customer, net_sales per customer), retention/cohort approximations at monthly granularity.
business_questions: What is net_sales per unique customer by channel in May?
Which month had the highest number of distinct customers?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for aggregations (CAST/TRY_CAST). If combining with other customer dimensions, be careful—this is an aggregated count, not a direct customer_id for joins.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the month and channel (stored as nvarchar representing a decimal).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate measure for volume-based analytics (kg per order, kg per customer) and unit economics. Useful for supply chain and inventory planning correlations.
business_questions: What was the total quantity sold (kg) for the Mobile App in August?
What is the average kg per order by channel and month?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to a decimal/float (e.g., CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4)) or TRY_CAST). Beware of locale-specific formatting and trailing precision. Use appropriate numeric type for arithmetic.

gross_sales.description: Total gross sales (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the month and channel (stored as nvarchar representing a decimal).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Core revenue metric for trend analysis, before discounts and taxes. Useful for YoY, MoM growth, and as denominator for discount percentage calculations.
business_questions: How did gross_sales trend across months in Q3?
What percentage of gross_sales was given away as discounts in a given month?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal (e.g., CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Use numeric casts for SUM, AVG and percentage calculations. Ensure no currency symbols or commas are present before casting.

discount_amount.description: Total discount value applied for the month and channel (stored as nvarchar representing a decimal).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Measure promotional impact and margin pressure (discount/gross_sales). Used in profitability and campaign effectiveness analysis.
business_questions: How much discount was applied on the Website in December?
What is the average discount per order by channel?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal/float before aggregations. When computing discount percentage, divide by CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL) and handle divide-by-zero. Check for negative values if returns or reversals are encoded.

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees collected for the month and channel (stored as nvarchar representing a decimal).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Used to analyze delivery revenue vs. delivery cost (if available) and contribution to net sales. Useful for logistics and pricing decisions.
business_questions: Which month had the highest delivery_fee collected on WhatsApp orders?
What is the delivery fee per order by channel?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal for arithmetic. If delivery fees are sometimes negative/refunded, ensure handling of signs. Combine with total_orders for per-order metrics.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected for the month and channel (stored as nvarchar representing a decimal).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used for compliance/revenue recognition and to compute net sales. Useful to analyze tax burden trends and effective tax rates relative to gross sales.
business_questions: What is the effective tax rate (tax_amount / gross_sales) by month?
How much tax was collected through the Mobile App in January?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal before use. When calculating rates, guard against zero gross_sales. Tax rounding effects may exist—treat as numeric with appropriate precision.

net_sales.description: Net revenue after discounts, taxes and delivery fee contributions as recorded in the monthly aggregation (stored as nvarchar representing a decimal).
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary KPI for revenue performance; used for trend analysis, channel comparisons, and calculating per-order or per-customer revenue.
business_questions: Which channel delivered the highest net_sales in the year?
Compute month-on-month growth in net_sales for the Website.
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal for SUM/AVG and growth calculations. Consider validating that net_sales ≈ gross_sales - discount_amount + delivery_fee - tax_amount (if that is the intended formula); inconsistencies should be handled in data cleaning steps.

created_at.description: Snapshot timestamp indicating when the aggregation row(s) were created or exported (stored as nvarchar). In this dataset it appears constant across all rows.
created_at.analysis_use: Metadata for freshness/versioning of the aggregated table. Useful to filter or assert dataset currency, but not typically used in numeric analysis.
business_questions: When was this sales monthly snapshot created?
Is the aggregated table up-to-date as of a given date?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Treat as datetime when needed: CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE depending on SQL dialect. Note that created_at is not a row-level event timestamp for orders; it is a snapshot marker. Because it is constant here, avoid using it for grouping.

year.description: Calendar year for the aggregated metrics (stored as nvarchar).
year.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering key for year-level reports. Use to join/compare with monthly/daily tables by year. Often used in WHERE clauses (e.g., year = '2024').
business_questions: What are the sales totals for a specific year and channel?
How do annual totals change year-over-year (when multiple years exist)?
year.nl2sql_hints: Currently a string; treat as categorical. Prefer casting to INT if numeric year comparisons or range filters are required (CAST(year AS INT)). Expect exact-match filters (e.g., year = '2024'). Be careful of whitespace/padding; trim before casting.

order_channel.analysis_use: Group-by and filter dimension for channel performance comparisons and channel mix visualizations. Good join key to channel-level schemas or to filter dashboards.
business_questions: Which channel generated the most net sales this year?
How does average order value differ between channels?
order_channel.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string used in GROUP BY and WHERE. Be mindful of inconsistent casing/whitespace in source (use TRIM/UPPER/LOWER normalization). Join with other channel-dimension tables on normalized channel name.

total_orders.description: Total number of orders in the year for the channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_orders.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM) across years/channels, or direct display. Used with gross/net sales to compute average order value, orders per customer, growth rates. Filterable as numeric thresholds after casting.
business_questions: How many orders were placed through each channel this year?
What is the orders-per-customer ratio by channel?
total_orders.nl2sql_hints: Numeric semantic but stored as string — cast to INTEGER (CAST(total_orders AS BIGINT) or INT) before numeric comparisons or arithmetic. Handle NULLs and non-numeric values robustly (TRY_CAST where supported).

total_customers.description: Count of distinct customers who transacted in the year for the channel (stored as nvarchar).
total_customers.analysis_use: Used to compute per-customer metrics (ARPU, transactions per customer). Grouping/filtering for customer-focused KPIs. Combine with total_orders to compute repeat rates.
business_questions: How many unique customers purchased via each channel this year?
What is revenue per customer by channel?
total_customers.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to integer for calculations. If the value represents unique customers in an aggregated source, avoid joining to user tables assuming exact counts; prefer drill-down to base tables for exact deduplication.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
