total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Used for volume-based KPIs, unit economics, and yield analyses. Useful to compute revenue per kg and to compare volume vs value across channels.
business_questions: What is the total kg sold per channel this year?
What is gross/net sales per kg by channel?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Decimal numeric stored as string — cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (e.g., CAST(total_qty_kg AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Watch for localization issues (commas) and trailing precision. Use NULL-safe casts and aggregates (SUM) on numeric type.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar with decimals).
gross_sales.analysis_use: Primary revenue measure for top-line reporting, share-of-sales calculations, and comparisons against net_sales and discounts. Aggregated or used to compute percentages and growth rates.
business_questions: How much gross sales did each channel generate this year?
What percentage of total gross sales does each channel contribute?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Monetary value stored as string — cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC before arithmetic (e.g., CAST(gross_sales AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Beware of rounding/precision; prefer NUMERIC for financial accuracy. Use COALESCE to handle NULLs.

discount_amount.description: Total discounts applied (monetary) for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Used to calculate discount rate (discount_amount / gross_sales), analyze promotional cost, and net impact on revenue. Useful for channel-level promotion efficiency analysis.
business_questions: What is the total discount given per channel this year?
What is the discount as a percent of gross sales by channel?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL before computing rates. When computing ratios, protect against division by zero. Consider rounding and formatting for presentation. Ensure negative/positive sign conventions are known (discounts likely positive amounts reducing gross).

delivery_fee.description: Total delivery fees collected or charged for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar).
delivery_fee.analysis_use: Used to compute net revenue contributions, delivery fee share of gross/net sales, and to offset delivery costs. Useful for channel cost analysis when combined with delivery costs dataset.
business_questions: How much did channels collect in delivery fees this year?
What share of net sales is attributed to delivery fees by channel?
delivery_fee.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for numeric operations. Clarify whether delivery_fee is revenue or expense in downstream joins. Normalize sign conventions before summation.

tax_amount.description: Total tax collected/recorded for the year and channel (stored as nvarchar).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Used to calculate tax rate versus gross sales and for compliance/reporting. Useful in computing net-of-tax revenues if needed.
business_questions: What is the total tax collected per channel this year?
What is the effective tax rate (tax / gross_sales) by channel?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to DECIMAL for arithmetic. For percent calculations use safe division. Ensure tax treatment consistency when joining to invoice-level tax data.

net_sales.description: Net sales amount for the year and channel (likely gross minus discounts plus/minus fees/taxes) stored as nvarchar.
net_sales.analysis_use: Primary bottom-line sales metric for reporting. Used to compute channel profitability proxies, revenue per order/customer, and for trend comparisons. Often used in dashboards as the key KPI.
business_questions: What are the net sales by channel for the year?
Which channel has the highest net sales per customer?
net_sales.nl2sql_hints: Treat as DECIMAL for calculations (CAST(net_sales AS DECIMAL(...))). Validate that net_sales = gross_sales - discount_amount + delivery_fee +/- tax_amount if reconciliation is required. Use consistent precision/rounding.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the aggregated row was created or last updated (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit metadata: used to determine freshness of the aggregate and to filter for the latest snapshot. Not typically used in business aggregations but important for ETL monitoring.
business_questions: When was this yearly snapshot generated?
Is this data up-to-date compared to the reporting cut-off?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string — cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Because the value is identical across rows in this sample, queries that fetch the latest snapshot should use MAX(created_at) after casting. Watch for timezone formats and parsing errors; use TRY_PARSE/TRY_CAST if available.

keyword_id.description: Unique identifier for each keyword record (string). Appears unique across the rows; formatted like 'KEYW1001'.
keyword_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for deduplication and record-level operations. Useful when updating/deleting single rows and tracking source records during ETL. Not typically used for aggregations other than counting distinct entries.
business_questions: How many unique keyword records exist in the dataset?
Has a specific keyword_id been updated or created within a given time range (audit/ETL tracking)?
keyword_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; use equality filters for single-record retrieval. Not a numeric id—do not cast to int. Since there is no PK, queries that assume uniqueness should still use keyword_id = '...' or COUNT(DISTINCT keyword_id). For performance, consider indexing keyword_id if point lookups will be frequent.

keyword.description: The search term text entered by users (e.g., 'potato', 'organic veg'). Low cardinality here implies aggregation/grouping by keyword is meaningful.
keyword.analysis_use: Primary grouping dimension for analytics (GROUP BY keyword). Useful for text analysis, keyword popularity, and detecting synonyms or keyword clusters. Filtering and fuzzy matching (LIKE, ILIKE, full-text) useful for NL2SQL intent mapping.
business_questions: What are the top N keywords by search volume?
Which keywords have the lowest conversion rate despite high search volume?
keyword.nl2sql_hints: Keywords are stored as nvarchar—match case-insensitively (LOWER/ILIKE) when parsing user intent. Because cardinality is low, GROUP BY keyword is efficient. For fuzzy matches use LIKE '%term%' or full-text search when supported. Watch out for trailing/leading spaces—use TRIM in predicates.

search_volume.description: Reported search volume for the keyword; stored as nvarchar but represents an integer-like metric (values like '101', '1030').
search_volume.analysis_use: Numeric aggregation and ranking (SUM, AVG, MAX), filtering (e.g., search_volume > X), and bucketing (high/medium/low demand). Requires casting to an integer/decimal type to perform numeric operations.
business_questions: Which keywords exceed a given search volume threshold (e.g., > 1000 searches)?
What is the total search volume for a set of keywords or for the top SKUs?
search_volume.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric: CAST(search_volume AS INT) or TRY_CAST depending on SQL dialect. Handle NULLs and malformed strings (use TRY_CAST or CASE WHEN ISNUMERIC(...) THEN ...). For performance, consider persisting as INT in a cleaned table to avoid runtime casting.

conversion_rate.description: Conversion rate associated with the keyword, stored as nvarchar but semantically numeric (percent or fraction—values like '2.48', '11.82').
conversion_rate.analysis_use: Numeric metric for performance analysis (AVG, MIN, MAX), filtering (e.g., conversion_rate < x), and identifying optimization targets. Combine with search_volume to compute weighted metrics (weighted conversion). Requires casting to decimal for safe calculations.
business_questions: Which keywords have the highest/lowest conversion rates?
Which high-volume keywords have conversion rate below a target threshold?
conversion_rate.nl2sql_hints: Cast to decimal/float: CAST(conversion_rate AS DECIMAL(10,4)) or TRY_CAST. Be explicit about percent vs fraction in consumer queries (e.g., 2.48 implies 2.48%?). Handle outliers and string artifacts. When computing weighted metrics, join or aggregate after casting and coalescing NULLs to zero when appropriate.

top_sku.description: The SKU that most often appears or is clicked/sold for the corresponding keyword (string like 'APP009'). Low cardinality suggests a small set of top SKUs map to many keyword rows.
top_sku.analysis_use: Join key to product tables for enrichment (product name, category, price). Grouping by top_sku to measure SKU-level exposure in search and to find which products drive conversions for certain keywords.
business_questions: Which SKUs are the top result for the most high-volume keywords?
Do certain SKUs show consistently higher conversion for the keywords they top?
top_sku.nl2sql_hints: No declared FK—use top_sku to join with products.product_sku or product_variants.sku. Use equality joins and consider trimming/case normalization. Since cardinality is low, grouping by top_sku is inexpensive. Consider indexing top_sku for join performance.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the record was created, stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' format.
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal analysis (trends, recency filters). Use for filtering recent keywords or grouping by day/week/month after casting. Useful for incremental ETL (new rows since last run).
business_questions: How have search volumes and conversion rates changed over time for a given keyword?
Which keywords were added or observed in the most recent period?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime: CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_CAST depending on dialect. Confirm timezone semantics. Use DATE_TRUNC/FORMAT functions after casting for grouping. For incremental queries, compare CAST(created_at AS DATE) >= 'YYYY-MM-DD'. Normalize/validate malformed timestamps with TRY_CAST to avoid failures.

ticket_id.description: Unique identifier for the support ticket (nvarchar(30)). In this dataset every row is distinct (100 distinct values).
ticket_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for ticket-level analytics, used for joins to ticket messages or logs; grouping rarely needed since one row per ticket. Useful for counting distinct tickets and deduplication.
business_questions: How many unique tickets exist in a period?
Show the full ticket thread for a given ticket_id
ticket_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Use equals or IN for lookup. Consider declaring a primary key or unique index if truly unique. When joining to ecom_ticket_messages use exact match on ticket_id. Avoid LIKE unless supporting fuzzy matching.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who raised the ticket (nvarchar(26)). ~60 distinct values in 100 rows.
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter/group to find tickets per customer, join to ecom_customers for demographics and CLV, aggregate counts to find frequent complainers.
business_questions: Which customers have the highest number of tickets?
How do ticket counts correlate with customer lifetime value?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: String key used in joins to ecom_customers. Use equality/IN for filtering. Consider creating an index on customer_id for frequent lookups. Watch for inconsistent formatting (leading/trailing spaces) — trim during comparisons.

subject.description: High-level ticket category like 'Delivery Delay' or 'Order Issue' (nvarchar(44)). Only 4 distinct values.
subject.analysis_use: Group and count to get distribution of ticket types; useful for trend analysis and prioritization of product/process fixes.
business_questions: What proportion of tickets are related to Delivery Delay vs Refund Query?
Which subjects generate the most HIGH priority tickets?
subject.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field — use GROUP BY or CASE for bucketing. Exact matches or IN lists suffice. Consider mapping to a normalized subject lookup if the taxonomy grows.

description.description: Short textual description of the issue (nvarchar(60)). Values are repetitive and correlate with subject (4 distinct descriptions).
description.analysis_use: Used for simple categorization and counts. For deeper analysis use full-text search or NLP to cluster free-text if descriptions become diverse.
business_questions: How many tickets state 'Item received damaged.'?
Which descriptions correspond most often to CLOSED vs RESOLVED statuses?
description.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; avoid expensive LIKE '%...%' scans on large data — use full-text index if supported. For current dataset, equality checks are sufficient. Consider standardizing common phrases.

status.description: Ticket lifecycle status (e.g., OPEN, IN_PROGRESS, RESOLVED, CLOSED) (nvarchar(32)). 4 distinct values with distribution provided.
status.analysis_use: Filter/group to measure backlog, resolution rates, SLA compliance; monitor transitions over time when combined with timestamps.
business_questions: How many tickets are currently OPEN or IN_PROGRESS?
What is the weekly resolved rate (RESOLVED vs total created)?
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical field. Use equality/IN for filters and GROUP BY for aggregations. For trend queries combine with created_at/updated_at. Consider indexing status for status-based dashboards.

priority.description: Priority level of the ticket (LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH) (nvarchar(22)). 3 distinct values with LOW most frequent.
priority.analysis_use: Group and aggregate to prioritize workload, compute SLA breaches by priority, and route high priority tickets to specific agents.
business_questions: What is the distribution of ticket priorities?
What is the average resolution time for HIGH vs LOW priority tickets?
priority.nl2sql_hints: Categorical. Use GROUP BY or CASE to compute SLA metrics. Consider mapping to numeric severity for weighted aggregations. Indexing priority can help if frequently filtered.

created_at.description: Ticket creation timestamp stored as nvarchar(48) with values like '2025-08-04 14:03:22' (distinct=100).
created_at.analysis_use: Key for time-series analysis (tickets by day/week/month), cohorting customers, SLA calculations (created_at -> updated_at), and trend detection.
business_questions: How many tickets were created each day over the last 30 days?
What is the peak hour for ticket creation?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as string, values appear to follow ISO-like datetime format — cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP before grouping or date truncation (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Normalize timezone if needed. Ensure consistent format; use BETWEEN for range filters. Consider converting column type to native datetime for performance.

updated_at.description: Last update timestamp for the ticket stored as nvarchar(48) with full cardinality (distinct=100).
updated_at.analysis_use: Used with created_at to compute resolution time (updated_at - created_at), to detect recent activity, and to measure response intervals.
business_questions: What is the average time to last update or resolution?
Which tickets were updated in the past 24 hours?
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for calculations (DATEDIFF). If updated_at equals last activity rather than resolution, clarify semantics. Ensure proper handling of nulls for unresolved tickets before computing durations.

order_id.description: Associated order identifier if the ticket refers to a specific order (nvarchar(26)). 53 distinct values in dataset.
order_id.analysis_use: Join to ecom_orders to analyze ticket distribution by order value, order items, shipping method; filter tickets related to high-value orders or specific SKUs via order joins.
business_questions: Which orders have associated tickets and how many per order?
Are certain products or order types generating more tickets?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to ecom_orders. Treat as string; trim/normalize before joining. Use equality/IN filters. Consider indexing order_id if many queries join on it.

assigned_to.description: Identifier of the support agent or role assigned to the ticket (nvarchar(40)). Low cardinality (3 distinct: ops_manager, support_agent_1, support_agent_2).
assigned_to.analysis_use: Agent-level metrics: ticket load, resolution time per agent, backlog and performance comparisons. Useful for routing analytics and SLA adherence.
business_questions: How many tickets is each agent currently handling and what are their average resolution times?
Which agent resolves the highest proportion of HIGH priority tickets?
assigned_to.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality field; GROUP BY and aggregate easily. Join to a users/support_agents dimension table for richer agent attributes. Use equality/IN. If agent values are roles (ops_manager) vs user ids, standardize to unique agent id for joins.

ticket_message_id.description: Unique identifier for the message row (string). Intended to identify each message event.
ticket_message_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for deduplication and linking individual messages. Useful for counting messages, ensuring uniqueness, and ordering messages if it encodes sequence. Not currently defined as a primary key in DDL.
business_questions: Are there duplicate message IDs indicating ingestion issues?
How many unique messages exist in a time window (using ticket_message_id to dedupe)?
ticket_message_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key. Use COUNT(DISTINCT ticket_message_id) for deduplication. If system scales, recommend defining a primary key or unique index. For NL2SQL, avoid assuming any numeric ordering inside this id — use timestamp columns for ordering instead.

ticket_id.description: Identifier for the ticket/conversation this message belongs to.
ticket_id.analysis_use: Grouping and joining key to aggregate messages by ticket (messages per ticket, conversation threads). Primary column for joins to ticket metadata in ecom_ticket and through that to customers, orders, etc.
business_questions: Which tickets have the most messages (hot tickets)?
What is the average number of messages for resolved vs unresolved tickets (requires join to ticket metadata)?
ticket_id.nl2sql_hints: String column used for joins. Use exact match (e.g., WHERE ticket_id = 'TICK001002'). Normalize case if inconsistent (LOWER(ticket_id)). When joining in NL2SQL plans, explicitly reference the ecom_ticket table: JOIN zuna_seed_raw.ecom_ticket t ON m.ticket_id = t.ticket_id. Consider indexing ticket_id for larger volumes.

message_ts.description: Timestamp when the message was sent as stored text (format appears to be 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
message_ts.analysis_use: Key for temporal analyses: ordering messages, calculating inter-message or response times, detecting peak times and trends. Must be cast/converted to datetime for accurate temporal functions.
business_questions: What is the average time between customer message and support reply (compute response times by ordering message_ts per ticket)?
During which hour(s) of the day are most messages sent?
message_ts.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — cast before temporal operations: e.g., TRY_CAST(message_ts AS datetime) or CAST(message_ts AS datetime) depending on SQL dialect. Use this converted value for GROUP BY DATEPART / DATE_TRUNC. Handle malformed or null values with TRY_CAST or ISDATE checks. Use message_ts for ordering within a ticket (ORDER BY CAST(message_ts AS datetime)).

sender.description: Indicates who sent the message (categorical: 'support' or 'customer').
sender.analysis_use: Filter and group by sender to compute sender-specific volumes, ratios, and timing (e.g., support response counts vs customer messages). Useful for identifying message initiators and measuring support activity.
business_questions: What share of messages is sent by support vs customers?
How quickly does support respond after a customer message (requires ordering and timestamps)?
sender.nl2sql_hints: Categorical string; values appear normalized ('support', 'customer'). Use equality filters (sender = 'support') or lower(sender) to be case-insensitive. Consider mapping variants (e.g., 'agent', 'system') if present in other data. If expanding to multi-role senders, treat as dimension for joins or grouping.

message_text.description: The textual content of the message. In this sample it's low-cardinality and appears templated.
message_text.analysis_use: Content analysis, frequency counts, template detection, sentiment proxies (limited here due to templated values). Grouping by message_text reveals common responses and common customer requests. Can be used for simple keyword filters (LIKE '%photo%').
business_questions: Which canned responses are used most by support?
How often do customers ask for photos or escalate (keyword search in message_text)?
message_text.nl2sql_hints: Text stored as nvarchar. For NL2SQL, support equality and LIKE/ILIKE searches. Because message_text is low-cardinality in sample, GROUP BY message_text is meaningful; in broader data, consider full-text search or tokenization. Use LENGTH/LEN to find long messages. Beware of trailing spaces and case — use TRIM and LOWER for robust matching.

attachment_url.description: URL to any attachment included with the message. Sample shows 100% nulls, indicating attachments may not be used or not populated.
attachment_url.analysis_use: Indicator of attachments presence; can be used to compute share of messages with attachments. Potentially used to fetch or verify attachments in downstream processes.
business_questions: What proportion of messages include attachments?
Do messages with attachments correlate with longer resolution times or specific issue types?
attachment_url.nl2sql_hints: Currently all null in sample. Use attachment_url IS NOT NULL or COALESCE to detect presence. If non-null values appear, treat as string; may need URL validation. For NL2SQL, avoid JOINs based on attachment_url. If attachments are expected, create an attachments table for metadata instead of embedding URLs.

created_at.description: Row creation timestamp stored as text. Likely indicates ingestion time into this table rather than message send time (message_ts).
created_at.analysis_use: Useful to detect ingestion lag (created_at - message_ts), audit row creation, and to detect late-arriving messages. Use for temporal filtering when message_ts is absent or unreliable.
business_questions: Is there a lag between when a message was sent and when it was ingested into the system (created_at - message_ts)?
How many messages were ingested on a particular date or within a maintenance window?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Also nvarchar — convert to datetime with TRY_CAST/CAST before temporal operations. Distinguish created_at (ingestion) from message_ts (event). Use COALESCE(CAST(message_ts AS datetime), CAST(created_at AS datetime)) when message_ts may be missing. For NL2SQL, prefer DATE(CAST(created_at AS datetime)) or equivalent for daily aggregations.

vendor_id.description: Canonical identifier for a vendor (string). Appears unique per row and intended to be the primary lookup key.
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary join key to transactional tables; exact-match filters; group-by for vendor-level aggregations; deduplication checks.
business_questions: What is the vendor_name and contact info for vendor_id = 'ECOMV3001'?
How many transactions exist for each vendor_id (requires join with orders/payments)?
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text exact-match key. Prefer equality or IN lists (WHERE vendor_id = '...' OR vendor_id IN (...)). Use as join key to other vendor-aware tables. Consider enforcing PRIMARY KEY / unique index on vendor_id for faster joins. Beware of nulls; handle trimming and consistent casing if joins fail.

vendor_name.analysis_use: Used for display, grouping (e.g., top vendors), fuzzy matching and deduplication. Useful in LIKE/full-text searches and reporting labels.
business_questions: List vendors with names containing 'Tara' or 'Bajaj'.
Which vendors have duplicate or very similar names (data quality)?
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: Support both exact and pattern matches: use LOWER(vendor_name) and LIKE '%term%' for case-insensitive matches. Watch for punctuation and commas in stored names; consider normalizing (trim, collapse multiple spaces) when matching. Avoid using vendor_name for join keys when vendor_id is available.

vendor_category.description: Categorical classification of vendor type (Ad Agency, Courier, Influencer, Marketing, Tech).
vendor_category.analysis_use: Group-by and filter for category-level aggregations and segmentation. Good for small-dimension joins (categorical filters).
business_questions: How many vendors are in each vendor_category?
How many active vendors exist per vendor_category?
vendor_category.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality: use equality or IN (...) in WHERE clauses for efficient filtering. Consider mapping synonyms (e.g., 'Marketing' vs 'Ad Agency') in NL2SQL logic. Avoid vague natural language like 'marketing vendors'—map to exact stored category values.

phone.description: Vendor phone number stored as text, including country code and spacing.
phone.analysis_use: Contact retrieval, deduplication by phone, pattern searches, and operational notifications. Can be used to validate presence of contact info.
business_questions: Provide phone numbers for all active vendors in Ahmedabad.
Which vendors are missing phone numbers?
phone.nl2sql_hints: Phone numbers are strings with formatting; normalize by removing spaces, plus signs and punctuation for comparisons. Use LIKE for partial matches (e.g., area codes). Treat as PII—queries returning phone numbers may require masking depending on policies.

email.analysis_use: Contact retrieval, uniqueness checks, validation and automated communications. Useful for joins if external systems use email as an identifier (less ideal than vendor_id).
business_questions: List emails for vendors in the 'Tech' category.
Which vendors have non-company or malformed emails (data quality)?
email.nl2sql_hints: Emails are text—use LOWER(email) for case-insensitive comparisons. Use LIKE '%@domain%' for domain-based filtering. Treat as PII and validate pattern with simple regex or LIKE patterns in NL2SQL-generated queries.

billing_address.description: Vendor billing/street address stored as free-form text.
billing_address.analysis_use: Address extraction for logistics/settlement; geocoding preprocessing; partial matching by locality or pin codes; KYC/manual verification.
business_questions: Which vendors have billing addresses in a given postal code or locality?
Show vendors whose billing_address contains 'Master Nagar'.
billing_address.nl2sql_hints: Free-form text—prefer LIKE '%term%' or full-text search where available. Consider extracting structured fields (street, city, postal code) into separate columns for easier NL2SQL parsing. Avoid relying on exact matches.

gst_no.description: Vendor Goods and Services Tax Identification number (string).
gst_no.analysis_use: Compliance checks, matching to financial records, and deduplication of corporate entities. Used in KYC and vendor onboarding validations.
business_questions: Which vendors are missing gst_no?
Are there multiple vendors sharing the same gst_no (potential duplicates)?
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Exact-match comparisons are typical. GST numbers are sensitive—treat as PII and mask in outputs when required. Validate by length/pattern; use equality or IS NULL checks in NL2SQL.

pan_no.description: Vendor PAN (Permanent Account Number) string used for tax identification.
pan_no.analysis_use: KYC/compliance, vendor identity verification and deduplication. Useful for financial reporting and regulatory checks.
business_questions: List vendors without PAN recorded.
Find vendors with a given PAN number (fraud/duplicate detection).
pan_no.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text—use exact equality for matches. Validate format/length. Mask PAN values in outputs unless required. Use IS NULL / !='' checks to find missing values.

city.description: City associated with vendor billing address (normalized to a short string).
city.analysis_use: Geographic segmentation, group-by aggregations (counts, proportions), region-based filtering and reporting.
business_questions: How many vendors are in Anand, Bharuch and Ahmedabad respectively?
What proportion of active vendors are located in Nadiad?
city.nl2sql_hints: City is low-to-moderate cardinality—use equality/IN for filters and GROUP BY city for aggregates. Consider canonicalization (trim/case) for consistent matching. If NL input uses 'near' or 'around', map to city list or lat/long if available from another table.

status.description: Operational status of the vendor (e.g., ACTIVE, INACTIVE).
status.analysis_use: Primary filter for operational vs non-operational vendors, segmenting active vendors for business workflows and reporting.
business_questions: How many active vendors are there by category?
Show inactive vendors and their contact details for re-engagement.
status.nl2sql_hints: Binary categorical values—use WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' or status IN ('ACTIVE','INACTIVE'). Normalize case and trim. Ensure NL2SQL maps phrases like 'active vendors' to status = 'ACTIVE'.

created_at.description: Load or record creation timestamp stored as nvarchar (string). Sample shows a single repeated timestamp indicating a load-time value rather than true creation datetime.
created_at.analysis_use: Temporal analysis of vendor onboarding if converted to a proper datetime. Currently useful to identify ingestion time or to detect stale/invalid metadata.
business_questions: Show vendors created after 2025-01-01 (requires casting to datetime).
When was vendor metadata last loaded into the system?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—cast to appropriate DATE/TIMESTAMP type before range filters or date functions (e.g., CAST(created_at AS datetime) or TRY_PARSE). Validate consistent format; if value is constant across rows treat as ingestion timestamp. NL2SQL should detect and cast, or warn user if created_at appears non-informative.

wishlist_id.description: Unique identifier for the wishlist entry (string, nvarchar). Appears unique across the dataset but not declared as a primary key.
wishlist_id.analysis_use: Used for deduplication, counting distinct wishlist entries, and as a stable row identifier in joins/updates. Useful when grouping by entry-level or when measuring unique wishlist events.
business_questions: How many unique wishlist entries exist in a date range?
Are any wishlist_id duplicates present (data quality check)?
wishlist_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; safe to use in COUNT(DISTINCT wishlist_id). When writing NL2SQL, if a uniqueness assumption is needed prefer COUNT(DISTINCT) rather than COUNT. If updating rows use wishlist_id as the key. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace in nvarchar values.

customer_id.description: Identifier for the customer who created the wishlist entry (string).
customer_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering field for customer-level metrics (wishlist count, recency). Join key to ecom_customers to enrich with demographics, lifetime value, or segmentation.
business_questions: Which customers have added the most items to their wishlist?
What is the distribution of wishlist counts per customer?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; when joining to ecom_customers ensure both sides use same case/trim. Use LEFT JOIN if customers might be missing from master table. For performance, filter on customer_id early in queries. Consider casting or normalizing if other tables use different types or formats.

sku.description: Product SKU for the wished item (string). Low cardinality here; contains top sku frequencies.
sku.analysis_use: Group and aggregate to find popular SKUs, compute top-N lists, and join to product tables to attach category, price and inventory information.
business_questions: Which SKUs are most frequently added to wishlists?
Which SKUs have the highest ratio of priority_flag=1?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use sku as join key to product/product_variants tables. Be careful with SKU normalization (case, whitespace). Because cardinality is low, GROUP BY sku is cheap. If SKU appears in multiple catalogs, disambiguate with vendor/product id when joining.

added_at.description: Timestamp when the wishlist item was added, stored as nvarchar in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' format.
added_at.analysis_use: Primary temporal axis for trend analysis, recency calculations, cohorting by add date, and windowing (daily/weekly aggregations). Requires casting/parsing to native datetime for efficient time operations.
business_questions: How many wishlist adds occurred per day/week/month?
What is the time distribution (hour of day/day of week) of wishlist activity?
added_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as text — cast to DATE or DATETIME in NL2SQL (e.g., CAST(added_at AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP depending on dialect). Validate format and handle NULLs or malformed strings. For performance, filter by a casted date field early; consider creating a persisted normalized datetime column or index on converted value in production.

priority_flag.description: Binary-like flag stored as nvarchar indicating item priority (values '0' and '1'). Represents a lightweight user-assigned priority.
priority_flag.analysis_use: Filter/group to measure high-priority wishlist items, compute proportions, and prioritize marketing/notification lists. Can be used in segmentation and weighting conversion predictions.
business_questions: What percentage of wishlist items are marked priority?
Which SKUs have the highest proportion of priority marks?
priority_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical or boolean. Convert to integer/boolean in queries (e.g., CAST(priority_flag AS INT) or priority_flag = '1') for aggregations like SUM(CAST(priority_flag AS INT)). Ensure consistent handling of '0'/'1' strings and NULLs. Indexing on this low-cardinality column is rarely useful unless combined with other predicates.

notes.description: Free-text notes associated with the wishlist entry. In this dataset all values are NULL.
notes.analysis_use: Potential source for qualitative signals if populated (search for keywords, sentiment). Currently unusable until populated — acts as a schema placeholder.
business_questions: Are there recurring keywords in wishlist notes indicating intent (e.g., 'gift', 'size')?
Do notes correlate with higher conversion or priority flags?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Currently 100% NULL; NL2SQL should guard against expecting values. If populated, treat as text and use full-text search or LIKE for keyword matches. Avoid joins on this field. Consider trimming and normalizing before text processing.

item_id.description: A string identifier for the inventory item record or lot. Appears unique per row (100 distinct across 100 rows).
item_id.analysis_use: Use as a unique identifier for de-duplicating, row-level joins, batch traceability and linking to external systems if they store item-level references.
business_questions: Which exact inventory record corresponds to a given batch_id or SKU?
Has a particular item_id been updated recently (compare created_at/updated_at)?
item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as opaque string key. Although nearly unique, there is no PK—consider using it as the join key only if external tables reference it. No casting needed. Use equality filters and IN lists; avoid numeric/date functions.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit — product-level identifier shared across batches (low cardinality, 10 distinct).
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping dimension for product-level summaries, joins to product tables (products, product_variants, product_sales) and aggregations (SUM of stock/value by sku).
business_questions: What is the total units on hand per SKU?
Which SKUs have the most near-expiry stock?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use sku for GROUP BY and JOIN. Normalize case/whitespace when matching (e.g., TRIM/UPPER). Expect multiple rows per sku. Use equality or IN filters for targeted SKU queries.

name.description: Human-readable product name associated with the SKU (low cardinality mirroring sku values).
name.analysis_use: Labeling and reporting dimension; grouping for human-friendly reports when sku is not shown. Can be used in text-based filtering (LIKE) for search queries.
business_questions: Show product names and their current stock levels for dashboard display.
Which product names contain a given keyword (e.g., 'Onion') and their stock?
name.nl2sql_hints: Prefer joining on sku for machine joins; use name for display and text filters (use LIKE or full-text if available). Beware of duplicates or inconsistent naming—normalize before joining by name.

total_stock_units.description: Quantity on-hand for the record/batch, stored as nvarchar but numeric in content (distinct 97).
total_stock_units.analysis_use: Key numeric measure for SUM, AVG, comparisons, thresholds, and inventory valuation. Must be CAST/TRY_CAST or converted to an integer/decimal for arithmetic.
business_questions: What is the total quantity in stock across all batches or per SKU?
Which batches exceed a stock threshold (e.g., > 1000 units)?
total_stock_units.nl2sql_hints: Always convert to numeric before aggregation: e.g., TRY_CAST(total_stock_units AS INT) or TRY_CONVERT(DECIMAL(18,2), total_stock_units). Guard against non-numeric values or NULLs using TRY_CAST/ISNUMERIC checks. Use SUM(...) after casting.

batch_id.description: Batch or lot identifier for the inventory receipt. Nearly unique (99 distinct) — useful for batch-level traceability.
batch_id.analysis_use: Filter/group at batch level, identify specific lots for quality checks, link to batch-level shipment/inspection/returns if those tables capture batch_id.
business_questions: Show all SKUs and quantities associated with a given batch_id.
Which batches had quality issues or returned items (when joined to returns/quality tables)?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join/filter key for batch-tracking joins. Normalize whitespace. Batch_id is textual; use equality/IN filters. If batch_id is near-unique, consider indexing for faster lookups.

received_at.description: Timestamp when the batch/item was received; stored as nvarchar typically in 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' ISO-like format (100 distinct).
received_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis (receipts over time), aging calculations, lead-time measurement and filtering by time windows. Must be CAST to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP for comparisons and date-part extraction.
business_questions: How many units were received per day/week/month?
Which receipts occurred within a given time range (e.g., last 30 days)?
received_at.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DATETIME using TRY_CAST(received_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_CONVERT(DATETIME, received_at, 120). Use DATEPART/CAST to date for groupings. Guard against malformed strings with TRY_* functions.

expiry_date.description: Expiry date for the batch in YYYY-MM-DD format stored as nvarchar (29 distinct).
expiry_date.analysis_use: Identify near-expiry/expired batches, run FEFO/FIFO alerts, calculate shelf life remaining and prioritize picking. Must be parsed to DATE for range filters and comparisons.
business_questions: Which batches expire within the next N days?
What quantity is expiring this month by supplier or SKU?
expiry_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE (TRY_CAST(expiry_date AS DATE)). Be careful with empty/NULL or non-standard formats. Use DATE or DATETIME comparisons; consider TIMEZONE not relevant for pure date strings.

supplier.description: Name of the supplier for the batch. Very low cardinality (3 distinct values: Kumar Veg Wholesalers, Desai Fruit Traders, Patel Agro Suppliers).
supplier.analysis_use: Supplier-level aggregation for spend/volume (SUM of units/value), supplier performance, and procurement analysis. Good candidate for GROUP BY and filters.
business_questions: How much inventory (units and value) does each supplier provide?
Which supplier provides the most near-expiry inventory?
supplier.nl2sql_hints: Use equality, IN lists, and GROUP BY on supplier. Normalize names (TRIM) to avoid subtle mismatches. Because cardinality is low, group-by operations are inexpensive.

cost_per_unit.description: Purchase price per unit stored as nvarchar though numeric values appear (distinct 100). Some values show high precision (e.g., 119.70999999999999).
cost_per_unit.analysis_use: Calculate inventory value (cost_per_unit * total_stock_units), average cost per SKU/supplier, and margin calculations against sales data (when joined). Must be converted to DECIMAL/NUMERIC for accurate arithmetic.
business_questions: What is the total inventory value by SKU/supplier?
What is the average cost per unit for a SKU over time?
cost_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to DECIMAL(18,4) or appropriate precision before arithmetic. Watch for localization (commas) or trailing precision artifacts. Use ROUND when presenting results. Use TRY_CAST to avoid errors from malformed strings.

mfg_date.description: Manufacturing/packing date for the batch stored as nvarchar in YYYY-MM-DD format in many rows (68 distinct values).
mfg_date.analysis_use: Calculate shelf life (expiry_date - mfg_date), age at receipt, and compliance checks. Must be converted to DATE for date arithmetic.
business_questions: What is the average shelf life (expiry - mfg) per supplier or SKU?
Which batches have unusually old mfg_date relative to received_at?
mfg_date.nl2sql_hints: CAST to DATE (TRY_CAST(mfg_date AS DATE)) and validate that expiry_date is after mfg_date before calculating shelf life. Handle NULLs and malformed strings gracefully.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the source system, stored as nvarchar with datetime precision (ISO-like).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and data-latency checks (compare to received_at/updated_at), ingestion timelines, and to identify newly added records. Cast to DATETIME for comparisons and windowing.
business_questions: When were inventory records created in the system (ingestion timing)?
Which records were recently added (created in last 7 days)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Convert to DATETIME with TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT. Use created_at for ordering, freshness checks and CDC-type comparisons. Beware of timezone assumptions—strings appear to be naive timestamps.

updated_at.description: Last update timestamp for the record, stored as nvarchar with datetime precision.
updated_at.analysis_use: Detect recent changes to inventory records, implement change-detection queries, and support audit trails. Useful in incremental ETL/CDC to pull changed rows.
business_questions: Which inventory records have been updated in the last N hours/days?
Show changes to batches by comparing created_at and updated_at.
updated_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME using TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT for filter and window operations. Use updated_at > last_run_timestamp for incremental extraction. Handle NULLs for never-updated records.

order_item_id.description: Unique identifier for the order line item (string).
order_item_id.analysis_use: Record-level identifier used for deduplication, identifying specific line items, and as a potential primary key. Rarely aggregated but useful for counts and joins when present.
business_questions: How many unique order items were created in a given period?
Are there duplicate order_item_id values indicating ingestion issues?
order_item_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string; use for equality joins/filters. Good candidate for a primary key or unique index. For NL2SQL, map 'order item id' to this column; avoid casting. Use COUNT(DISTINCT order_item_id) for unique counts.

order_id.description: Identifier of the parent order containing this line item (string).
order_id.analysis_use: Grouping to roll up line-level metrics to order-level (e.g., order totals), joining to orders table for customer, status and order-level metadata.
business_questions: What is the average number of items per order?
How much revenue does each order generate (sum of line_total grouped by order_id)?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as the primary join key to orders/ecom_orders/b2b_orders. Stored as nvarchar — cast only if joining to a differently typed column. When users reference 'orders', map to order_id and join accordingly.

sku.description: Stock keeping unit code for the product sold (string).
sku.analysis_use: Grouping and filtering to compute SKU-level sales, top-sellers, price variance and inventory demand. Low cardinality makes it efficient for group-by.
business_questions: Which SKUs are top sellers by quantity and revenue?
What is the average unit_price and tax per SKU?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Common filter column in NL2SQL queries. Join to product master on sku or normalize case/whitespace. Use equality and IN lists. If user asks 'product code' map to sku.

product_name.description: Descriptive name of the product tied to the SKU/variant (string).
product_name.analysis_use: Readable label for reports and grouping when SKU description is required; useful for display and user-facing results. Not a reliable join key.
business_questions: What are the top product names by revenue?
How does sales performance differ across products with similar names?
product_name.nl2sql_hints: Prefer grouping by sku/variant_id instead of product_name to avoid duplicates. For NL2SQL, when users ask by 'product name' map to product_name; for aggregations prefer sku to avoid text inconsistencies.

variant_id.description: Identifier for the specific product variant (string), often SKU with a suffix.
variant_id.analysis_use: Granular grouping below SKU level for variant-level pricing and quantity analysis, linking to product_variants or product_pricing tables.
business_questions: Which variants of a product sell better (quantity and revenue)?
Are prices consistent across variants?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to variant/product master if available. Stored as nvarchar; ensure joins respect type. When NL2SQL references 'variant' or 'size/pack', map here.

qty_units.description: Quantity in units for the line item; stored as string but represents integer counts.
qty_units.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM), filters (e.g., qty_units > X), and average quantity per order or per SKU calculations after casting to numeric.
business_questions: How many units of each SKU were sold in the period?
What is the average units per order item for top SKUs?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer/decimal (e.g., CAST(qty_units AS INT)) before aggregations. NL2SQL should detect numeric semantic despite nvarchar type. Handle NULLs/empty strings and non-numeric values defensively (e.g., TRY_CAST).

qty_kg.description: Quantity in kilograms for the line item; stored as string but represents numeric weight.
qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate total weight sold, convert between units, and compute yield or weight-based pricing metrics after casting to numeric.
business_questions: What total weight (kg) of each SKU was sold?
What is revenue per kg for weight-based products?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL/NUMERIC. When both qty_units and qty_kg exist, clarify which to use for aggregation in NL2SQL prompts. Use TRY_CAST or COALESCE to avoid runtime errors on bad strings.

unit_price.description: Unit price for the item as stored (string). Likely price per unit or per kg depending on product.
unit_price.analysis_use: Compute revenue consistency (unit_price * qty), average price, price variance across orders and SKUs. Must be cast to numeric prior to arithmetic/aggregation.
business_questions: What is the average unit price per SKU or variant?
Are there large unit_price deviations for the same SKU across orders?
unit_price.nl2sql_hints: CAST(unit_price AS DECIMAL(12,2)) before arithmetic. Be explicit in NL2SQL translations about currency and decimal precision. Watch for currency symbols or commas in data and strip them if present.

line_total.description: Line-level total amount (string) representing quantity * unit_price (before/after tax depending on ingestion).
line_total.analysis_use: Primary money metric for revenue aggregations (SUM, AVG), order-level rollups, and SKU revenue computations after casting to numeric.
business_questions: What is total revenue by SKU, order, or day?
Which lines contribute the most to revenue and which orders have largest line_totals?
line_total.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST to decimal before SUM/AVG. Verify if line_total includes tax/discount — check against unit_price*qty and tax_amount. Use TRY_CAST and handle NULL/empty strings. Map 'revenue'/'line total' to this column in NL2SQL.

tax_amount.description: Tax charged for the line item (string representing numeric).
tax_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate total tax, calculate tax rate relative to line_total, and validate tax calculations. Cast to numeric for arithmetic.
business_questions: How much tax was collected per period or per SKU?
What is the effective tax rate per product or order line?
tax_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST(tax_amount AS DECIMAL). When user asks for 'tax' map to this column. Compute tax_rate = tax_amount / NULLIF(line_total,0) after casting. Handle decimals and possible trailing zeros.

discount_amount.description: Discount applied to the line (string). Current snapshot shows all zeros.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate discounts, check promotion effects, and compute net revenue. If always zero, can be ignored until non-zero values appear but still include in validation checks.
business_questions: How much discount value was given in the period (total and by SKU)?
Which SKUs or batches received discounts?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL). Account for constant-zero columns in query generation (optimize away when known). For NL2SQL, present as 'discount' and allow exclusion if it's zero everywhere.

batch_id.description: Identifier for the picking/batching group that handled this order item (string).
batch_id.analysis_use: Join to picking_batches for fulfillment analytics, group-by to measure throughput, and filter to inspect specific batches. Useful for operational KPIs like items per batch and batch pick duration.
business_questions: Which batches processed the most order items and revenue?
Are there batches with repeated picking delays or errors?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Use for joins to picking/picking_batches tables. Stored as nvarchar — ensure join types match. Support IN lists or equality filters in NL2SQL when users refer to 'batch'. Consider indexing for operational queries.

picked_at.description: Timestamp when the line item was picked in fulfillment (string representation of datetime).
picked_at.analysis_use: Time-based analyses of picking latency, SLA compliance, distribution of pick activity across hours/days, and computing time differences between created_at and picked_at.
business_questions: What is the average time from item creation to pick (fulfillment latency)?
What are hourly/daily picking volumes and peaks?
picked_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse/CAST to proper datetime (e.g., CAST(picked_at AS DATETIME) or TRY_PARSE). For NL2SQL, when users ask for 'picked date' or 'pick time', map to picked_at and apply DATE_TRUNC or DATE functions. Handle timezone assumptions and nulls when not picked yet.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the order item record was created/ingested (string representation of datetime).
created_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for order item creation trends, cohorting, and time-series aggregations. Also used to compute processing times vs picked_at.
business_questions: How many order items were created per day/week/month?
How long, on average, does it take for an item to be picked after creation?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime with TRY_CAST/TRY_PARSE. Map queries with 'created date'/'order date' to created_at. Use DATE_TRUNC/DATE functions for grouping. If system uses a timezone, document it or convert explicitly.

summary_id.description: Unique identifier for each daily summary row (e.g., 'SUM1000').
summary_id.analysis_use: Row-level identity for de-duplication and tracing; can be used to reference or filter specific summary rows. Not required for aggregations but useful for audit and joins to metadata tables if such exist.
business_questions: Which summary record corresponds to an anomalous entry for a given date and store?
When was a particular summary row created or updated (trace by summary_id)?
summary_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string. Often unique in this dataset — can be used in WHERE or IN clauses. Not a reliable join key to other transactional tables. If used in NL2SQL generation prefer equality filters (summary_id = 'SUM1000') rather than range filters.

store_id.description: Identifier of the physical POS store (e.g., 'STORE_AHD_001').
store_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping/filtering dimension for reporting and comparisons across locations. Low cardinality makes it good for GROUP BY and indexed filtering.
business_questions: What are total daily sales for STORE_AHD_001 over the last 30 days?
Which store has the highest refunds as a percentage of sales?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: String typed; commonly used in WHERE/GROUP BY. Prefer exact matches or IN lists (e.g., store_id IN ('STORE_AHD_001','STORE_RJT_001')). Join to pos_stores on store_id; ensure target table has same string format. Consider normalizing/canonicalizing store_id if other tables use different casing or formats.

date.description: Date of the summary (stored as nvarchar, format appears 'YYYY-MM-DD').
date.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analysis, time-window filters (day/week/month), grouping and joins to calendar tables or other daily snapshots. Used for ORDER BY in time-series queries.
business_questions: What were total sales on 2025-11-16 across all stores?
How did sales change week-over-week for each store?
date.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as string, values appear in ISO 'YYYY-MM-DD' format—safe for lexical ordering but cast to DATE for date arithmetic. NL2SQL should generate CAST(date AS DATE) or TO_DATE(date, 'YYYY-MM-DD') when performing range filters or date functions. Ensure timezone considerations if joining to timestamp-based tables.

total_txns.description: Total number of transactions recorded for the store on the date (stored as nvarchar).
total_txns.analysis_use: Key activity metric for counting operations, used in aggregations (SUM, AVG), denominators for per-transaction metrics, or to compute throughput and conversion metrics. Useful for anomaly detection (sudden drops/spikes).
business_questions: How many transactions did each store process yesterday?
Which days had unusually low transaction counts compared to historical average?
total_txns.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string; must CAST/CONVERT to integer (e.g., CAST(total_txns AS INT)) before numeric aggregation or arithmetic. Watch for NULLs or non-numeric characters. When generating SQL, prefer safe casts or TRY_CAST semantics if available to avoid errors.

total_sales.description: Sum of sales (currency) for the store on the date; stored as nvarchar and contains decimal values.
total_sales.analysis_use: Primary financial metric for revenue aggregation, trend analysis, ranking stores by sales, computing growth rates, and calculating ratios (e.g., tax/sales, refunds/sales).
business_questions: What were the daily revenue totals by store for the past month?
Which store had the highest total sales last week?
total_sales.nl2sql_hints: Strings representing decimals—must be CAST/CONVERT to numeric/decimal (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2) or FLOAT) prior to SUM()/AVG()/ratios. Numeric precision is important; choose DECIMAL for monetary calculations. Watch out for scientific or malformed values; prefer TRY_CAST where supported.

total_tax.description: Total tax collected on the day's sales for the store (stored as nvarchar decimals).
total_tax.analysis_use: Used to compute effective tax rate (total_tax / total_sales), to verify tax reporting, and to aggregate tax liabilities by period or store.
business_questions: What is the effective tax rate per store for the given period?
How much tax was collected across all stores last month?
total_tax.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—CAST to DECIMAL for arithmetic. When computing rates, guard against division by zero (total_sales = 0). Use consistent numeric scale to avoid rounding errors (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)).

total_refunds.description: Total monetary value of refunds issued that day for the store (stored as nvarchar decimals).
total_refunds.analysis_use: Used to compute refund rates, impact on net sales, trending returns, and identifying stores/dates with high refunds for quality checks.
business_questions: What percentage of sales were refunded last quarter per store?
Which dates had spikes in refund amounts that require investigation?
total_refunds.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—convert to DECIMAL for aggregations and ratio calculations. When calculating refund rate use safe divisions and consider net_sales = total_sales - total_refunds as a derived metric. Check for negative values or anomalies in source formatting.

avg_txn_value.description: Average transaction value for the day (stored as nvarchar decimal). May be precomputed as total_sales / total_txns or calculated externally.
avg_txn_value.analysis_use: Used as a performance KPI to assess basket size and sales quality. Can be recomputed for validation by dividing total_sales by total_txns after casting.
business_questions: What is the average basket value for each store over the month?
Are there days where avg_txn_value deviates significantly from the historical average?
avg_txn_value.nl2sql_hints: String decimal—CAST to DECIMAL. Validate precomputed avg_txn_value by recomputing: CAST(total_sales AS DECIMAL)/NULLIF(CAST(total_txns AS DECIMAL),0). NL2SQL should prefer recomputing if source reliability is unknown. Handle division-by-zero and null safely.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the summary row was created (stored as nvarchar, appears 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Useful for auditing, determining freshness of data, incremental loads, and ordering by ingestion time. Can be used to filter recent updates.
business_questions: When was this summary row generated or last updated?
How many summaries were created in the last 24 hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: String timestamp—CAST/CONVERT to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME for range filtering or time arithmetic. Use consistent timezone assumptions. For incremental NL2SQL queries, filter on CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) >= ... . Expect varying precisions; use TRY_CAST if available.

notes.description: Free-text note about the row; currently constant 'Daily summary'.
notes.analysis_use: Primarily metadata; can be used to filter special rows if notes diverge in future (e.g., 'recalculated', 'estimated'). Currently low analytical value.
business_questions: Are there any summary rows marked as 'recalculated' or with special notes?
Which summaries were created with special annotations?
notes.nl2sql_hints: String; low cardinality and often constant. Use equality filters where applicable (notes = 'Daily summary'). Not used as a join key. Watch for trailing spaces or casing differences when matching.

adjustment_id.description: Unique identifier for the inventory adjustment event (external string id like PAD3000).
adjustment_id.analysis_use: Primary event identifier for deduplication and row-level joins. Useful for counts, event-level reporting, and for tracing individual adjustments during audits.
business_questions: How many unique adjustment events occurred in a time window?
Can we trace the full details of a specific adjustment by ID for investigation?
adjustment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string when filtering/equating (e.g., WHERE adjustment_id = 'PAD3000'). Although distinct count equals row count here, there is no enforced PK; consider adding uniqueness constraint/index. Avoid numeric casts.

pos_sku.description: Point-of-sale SKU code identifying the product variant impacted by the adjustment.
pos_sku.analysis_use: Group and aggregate adjustments by product SKU; join to product or pos_products tables to enrich with product metadata; filter to analyze specific SKUs or SKU patterns.
business_questions: Which SKUs have the most quantity adjusted (total absolute qty_change) in the last month?
Are adjustments concentrated in a small set of SKUs (top N)?
pos_sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — use exact equality for joins. Consider normalization of SKU strings (trim whitespace) and ensure case sensitivity matches target product tables. Use IN lists for top-SKU filters or GROUP BY pos_sku for aggregation.

store_id.description: Identifier for the store where the adjustment occurred (e.g., STORE_RJT_001).
store_id.analysis_use: Group by store to compute store-level adjustment counts and quantities; join to store master (pos_stores) for location, region, and ownership info.
business_questions: Which stores have the highest shrinkage or adjustment volumes?
Are certain stores producing abnormal negative adjustments indicative of inventory loss?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as a categorical filter or GROUP BY. Join keys should be string-to-string — ensure store master uses the same identifier format. Low cardinality means GROUP BY on store_id is efficient; consider indexing if querying by store frequently.

adjustment_ts.description: The timestamp when the adjustment event occurred (stored as nvarchar in format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
adjustment_ts.analysis_use: Time-based grouping and windowing (daily/hourly); sequence ordering of adjustments; temporal correlation with sales/transactions after casting to datetime.
business_questions: When (hour of day / day of week) do most adjustments occur?
How many adjustments occurred in a specified date/time range?
adjustment_ts.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to a datetime type for range filters and time bucketing (e.g., CAST or CONVERT using 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS' format). Beware of nulls or malformed strings. Use indexed datetime column if high-volume queries are expected (introduce a persisted datetime column or functional index).

qty_change.description: Quantity delta applied in the adjustment (positive for increases, negative for decreases) stored as nvarchar.
qty_change.analysis_use: Sum/abs-sum for total adjusted quantity, detect negative adjustments (loss), compute average adjustment size, and verify inventory arithmetic with before/after fields.
business_questions: What is the total quantity reduced due to shrinkage in the last month?
Which adjustments exceed a threshold magnitude (e.g., |qty_change| > 5)?
qty_change.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric type (INTEGER) before aggregation or arithmetic (SUM, ABS). Watch for non-numeric values or leading/trailing spaces. Use WHERE TRY_CAST(qty_change AS INT) IS NOT NULL to filter valid rows if DB supports it. Consider adding a numeric column for performance.

reason.description: Categorical reason for the adjustment (e.g., Shrinkage, Stock Count Correction, Damage).
reason.analysis_use: Group by reason to understand causes of inventory movement; filter to focus on specific causes; combine with qty_change sums to quantify impact by reason.
business_questions: What proportion of adjustment quantity is labeled as Shrinkage vs Damage vs Stock Count Correction?
Which reason accounts for the highest count of adjustments in a period?
reason.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality makes GROUP BY efficient. Normalize textual values (trim/case) to avoid accidental duplicates. Use exact match or IN lists for filtering reasons. Consider mapping reasons to standardized categories if joining to analytics tables.

adjusted_by.description: User identifier who performed the adjustment (POS user code).
adjusted_by.analysis_use: Audit who performed adjustments — counts and volumes per user; identify high-frequency adjusters or outliers; join to user/HR tables for role-based analysis.
business_questions: Which employees perform the most adjustments and what is their average adjustment magnitude?
Are certain users more associated with adjustments that require large approvals or cause repeated corrections?
adjusted_by.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string. Join to pos_users or employee tables on user id. Watch for user id format consistency. For NL2SQL, allow filters like WHERE adjusted_by = 'PU1003' and support grouping by adjusted_by.

approval_by.description: User identifier who approved the adjustment.
approval_by.analysis_use: Measure approval coverage and lag between adjustment_ts and approval (if approval timestamp exists elsewhere), analyze approvals by manager to detect patterns or conflicts of interest.
business_questions: What percentage of adjustments are approved by each approver?
Are there cases where the approver and adjuster are the same person?
approval_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. To detect same-person approvals: WHERE approval_by = adjusted_by. If approval timestamps are not present here, join to audit/approval logs if available. Consider null checks for unapproved adjustments.

inventory_before.description: Inventory count for the SKU at the store immediately before the adjustment (stored as nvarchar).
inventory_before.analysis_use: Validate adjustment logic (inventory_after ?= inventory_before + qty_change); compute pre-adjustment stock levels for reconciliation and trend analysis.
business_questions: What were typical inventory levels before adjustments for high-frequency SKUs?
Which adjustments occurred when inventory_before was critically low (below threshold)?
inventory_before.nl2sql_hints: Convert to integer before arithmetic or comparisons. Since this column is nearly unique, it often varies per event. Use CAST/TRY_CAST and guard against non-numeric entries. For range filters (inventory_before < N), ensure numeric type conversion.

inventory_after.description: Inventory count for the SKU at the store immediately after the adjustment (stored as nvarchar).
inventory_after.analysis_use: Post-adjustment inventory trending; cross-check inventory_after against inventory_before + qty_change; detect negative inventory or implausible values.
business_questions: Are there adjustments that result in negative inventory_after?
Which SKUs end up with very high inventory_after values after corrections?
inventory_after.nl2sql_hints: Convert to integer for arithmetic and comparisons. Because inventory_after has high uniqueness, use numeric casts and validate arithmetic consistency. Consider storing as numeric/persisted column for faster analytics.

created_at.description: Ingestion or record creation timestamp (when this row was created in the raw table), stored as nvarchar.
created_at.analysis_use: Distinguish event time (adjustment_ts) from ingestion time for latency analysis; identify late-arriving data; filter by ingestion windows for ETL debugging.
business_questions: What is the typical delay between adjustment_ts and created_at (data latency)?
Were there bulk ingestions at particular times that may affect downstream reporting?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for time-difference calculations (e.g., created_at - adjustment_ts). Use consistent datetime parsing; watch for timezone differences or inconsistent formats. For NL2SQL, prefer queries that explicitly cast both timestamps to datetime before computing intervals.

redemption_id.description: Surrogate identifier for the loyalty redemption event (string).
redemption_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup key for single-event queries. Use for deduplication and event-counting. Grouping by redemption_id is rarely useful beyond uniqueness checks.
business_questions: Is a given redemption_id present and what are its details?
Are there duplicate redemption_ids (data quality)?
How many unique redemptions occurred in a period?
redemption_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as identifier string; use equality filters for direct lookups. Because there's no PK, check for duplicates (SELECT redemption_id, COUNT(*)). Avoid casting to numeric. Use TRIM/UPPER if ingest may have whitespace/case differences.

customer_id.description: Identifier of the customer who redeemed loyalty points (string, format like 'CUST####').
customer_id.analysis_use: Filter and group to compute per-customer redemption counts, total points used, total discount, recency/frequency analysis and join to customer master data for enrichment.
business_questions: Which customers redeemed loyalty points most frequently?
What is the total discount amount attributed to a particular customer?
Which customers should be targeted for loyalty re-engagement based on recent redemptions?
customer_id.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN filters; join condition typically on customer_id to ecom_customers or b2b_customers. Cast nothing — keep as string for joins. Use TRIM to avoid mismatches. For top-n queries use GROUP BY customer_id with SUM(CAST(discount_amount AS DECIMAL(12,2))).

pos_txn_id.description: POS transaction identifier associated with the redemption (string), links redemption to the transactional record.
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Join to POS transactions to derive total basket value, items redeemed against, and to compute redemption as percent of transaction value. Can also be used for auditing and reconciliation.
business_questions: Which transactions contained loyalty redemptions and what were their totals?
What proportion of a transaction's value was covered by the loyalty discount?
Are there transactions with multiple redemptions or mismatches between transaction and redemption records?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to pos_transactions on pos_txn_id (string equality). Ensure to TRIM and match case. When filtering by transaction date, prefer using the pos_transactions.redeemed_at if available; otherwise cast this table's redeemed_at to DATETIME.

loyalty_points_used.description: Number of loyalty points consumed for the redemption, stored as nvarchar but representing integer values.
loyalty_points_used.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to measure total points redeemed, average points per redemption, distribution of points used, and segment customers by points usage.
business_questions: How many loyalty points were redeemed over a period?
What is the average number of points used per redemption?
Which customers or stores consume the most points?
loyalty_points_used.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer before aggregation: CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(loyalty_points_used),'') AS INT) or TRY_CAST depending on engine. Handle non-numeric/null values defensively. Use GROUP BY after casting for numeric aggregations.

discount_amount.description: Monetary value of discount applied due to the redemption, stored as nvarchar with decimal values (string representation of float/decimal).
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute total financial impact of loyalty redemptions, average discount per redemption, percentage of sales discounted, and revenue leakage estimations.
business_questions: What is the total discount value granted from loyalty redemptions in a given period?
What is the average discount per redemption and per store?
How does loyalty discounting affect overall gross sales?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL for aggregations: CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(discount_amount),'') AS DECIMAL(12,2)) or TRY_CAST to safely handle malformed strings. Be mindful of floating-string artifacts (e.g., '148.05000000000001'); rounding may be required in results. When joining to financial tables, ensure consistent currency and scale.

redeemed_at.description: Timestamp of when the loyalty was redeemed, stored as nvarchar but formatted like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'.
redeemed_at.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for time-series analysis (hour, day, week, month) of redemptions. Use for recency calculations and for aligning with transaction timestamps.
business_questions: How many redemptions happened per day/week/month?
What are hourly peak times for loyalty redemptions?
What is the recency distribution of customer redemptions?
redeemed_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP before filtering or grouping: CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(redeemed_at),'') AS DATETIME) or use TRY_PARSE/TRY_CAST depending on DB. If timezone is relevant, ensure conversion. When users supply natural-language date ranges, convert those to the same datetime type and granularity. For date bucketing, use CAST(... AS DATE) or DATE_TRUNC equivalents.

store_id.description: Identifier for the store where the redemption occurred (string like 'STORE_XXX_001').
store_id.analysis_use: Group and filter to compare redemption counts and monetary impact between stores. Useful for per-store KPI calculations and capacity/resource planning.
business_questions: Which store has the highest total discount value from loyalty redemptions?
What is the redemption rate per store relative to footfall or transactions?
Are some stores over/under-utilizing loyalty redemptions?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Join to pos_stores or store master table on store_id. Low cardinality — consider using store_id in GROUP BY and as a filter. Normalize TRIM and case for safe joins. For geo/regional analysis, join store master for region attributes.

created_by.description: User or POS user id who created/processed the redemption (string, e.g., 'PU1001').
created_by.analysis_use: Attribution to staff for auditing and performance; detect training needs or anomalous activity by user. Group by created_by to measure number and value of redemptions processed by each staff.
business_questions: Which staff processed the most loyalty redemptions?
Are there any users with anomalously high discount allocations?
Is there a correlation between staff and approval_id patterns?
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Join to pos_users on created_by for staff metadata. Use GROUP BY created_by to compute per-user aggregates. Low cardinality — include when filtering or when auditing exceptions.

approval_id.description: Identifier of approval record associated with redemption (string). May reference an approval workflow or authorizer.
approval_id.analysis_use: Audit and control analysis: link redemptions to approval flows, find redemptions lacking approvals, and measure approval times if approvals are timestamped elsewhere.
business_questions: Which redemptions required and received approvals versus those that didn't?
What is the distribution of approvals by approver or approval code?
Are there approvals associated with anomalous discount amounts?
approval_id.nl2sql_hints: If an approvals table exists, join on approval_id. Because this table shows many distinct approval_ids, treat as dimension for audit queries. If no approvals table is available, use approval_id for grouping or to detect NULL/missing values. TRIM strings before joins.

notes.description: Free-text note about the redemption; in sample data it's constant ('Loyalty redemption').
notes.analysis_use: Usually not useful for aggregations due to low/constant cardinality. May contain operational comments in other datasets — useful for searches or anomaly detection when non-standard notes appear.
business_questions: Are there redemption records with special notes that require follow-up?
Do any notes indicate manual overrides or exceptions?
notes.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text; use LIKE/ILIKE or full-text search when present. Since current sample is constant, exclude from aggregates. Trim and normalize when searching for keywords.

date.description: The sales date for the aggregated metrics in the row. Stored as nvarchar, typically in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format.
date.analysis_use: Used for time-based filtering (WHERE date BETWEEN ...), grouping/aggregation (GROUP BY date, date_trunc equivalents), trend analysis and joins to date/calendar dimension tables. Convert to native date/datetime for accurate range queries and date functions.
business_questions: What were total daily net sales across all products for a date range?
Show monthly trends of net sales for product X.
date.nl2sql_hints: Always CAST/TRY_CAST or parse to DATE (or use a date dimension). Expect ISO-formatted strings but validate; use TRY_CAST/TRY_PARSE to avoid failures. Avoid string comparisons for ranges (lexical may work for ISO but not robust). Consider normalizing this column to a DATE type in a view or ETL to improve performance and enable date functions.

product_id.description: Identifier for the product (string). Low cardinality in this table and used to identify which product the metrics belong to.
product_id.analysis_use: Primary grouping and filtering key for product-level analytics (GROUP BY product_id, filter WHERE product_id = 'XYZ'). Use for joining to product master and cross-schema sales tables to bring in names, categories, vendor, or SKU metadata.
business_questions: Which product_ids are the top contributors to net sales this month?
How does product X's daily quantity sold compare to product Y?
product_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a join key to product master tables. Watch for inconsistent casing/leading/trailing whitespace—use TRIM/UPPER if needed. Because product_id cardinality is low, queries filtering by product_id are selective only if combined with date filters. Consider indexing or materialized views on (product_id, date) if queries commonly filter by both.

total_qty_kg.description: Total quantity sold in kilograms for the product on the date. Stored as nvarchar; values expected to be numeric (may include decimals).
total_qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate (SUM) to get total kilos sold over time, compute average daily sales (AVG), calculate unit price per kg (gross_sales / total_qty_kg) after casting. Useful for operational inventory and supply planning analyses.
business_questions: How many kilograms of product X were sold in the last 30 days?
What is the average daily quantity sold per product over a month?
total_qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to a numeric type (DECIMAL/NUMERIC/FLOAT) and cleaned for thousands separators or non-numeric characters. Use SAFE_CAST/TRY_CAST in NL2SQL to avoid errors. Guard against division by zero when computing unit prices. Consider precomputing numeric column in a cleaned view for frequent calculations.

gross_sales.description: Gross sales amount (pre-discount, pre-tax) for the product on the date. Stored as nvarchar; expected currency/number format.
gross_sales.analysis_use: Aggregate with SUM to obtain total gross revenue; compute averages, per-kg or per-unit prices; used with discount and tax to reconcile to net_sales. Useful for revenue trend, promotion effectiveness and price analysis.
business_questions: What was the gross sales amount for each product last quarter?
How much gross revenue was lost to discounts for a promotion period?
gross_sales.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric and strip currency symbols/commas. Validate consistency between gross_sales - discount_amount + tax_amount and net_sales. Use TRY_CAST or regex cleansing when generating NL2SQL to avoid runtime errors. For aggregation performance, prefer storing as DECIMAL in a transformed schema.

discount_amount.description: Total discount amount applied to the product's gross sales on the date. Stored as nvarchar; expected numeric.
discount_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute total discounts given, measure promotion impact (SUM(discount_amount)), compute discount rates (discount_amount / gross_sales) and trend analysis for markdowns.
business_questions: How much discount was applied to product X during the last promotion?
What percentage of gross sales was discounted for each product last month?
discount_amount.nl2sql_hints: Convert to numeric and handle nulls as zeros for aggregations. Be careful with negative/positive sign conventions. When computing percentages, guard against divide-by-zero. NL2SQL should prefer explicit CAST and coalesce to 0.
