tax_amt.analysis_use: Aggregate tax liability (SUM(tax_amt)), tax per SKU/transaction. Validate tax computation relative to line_total and tax rules. Identify rounding issues.
business_questions: What is the total tax collected for the recorded lines?
Which SKUs incur the most tax or highest average tax per unit?
tax_amt.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DECIMAL with appropriate precision (e.g., DECIMAL(12,2)) to normalize floating-string artifacts. Use rounding to 2 decimals for presentation. When validating arithmetic (tax as percentage of line_total), guard against division by zero and small floating differences.

variant_info.description: Holds variant-level metadata about the product (e.g., size, color). In this dataset the column is 100% null.
variant_info.analysis_use: When populated, this is valuable for variant-level sales analysis (size/color). Currently unusable until data appears; keep schema mapping for future enrichment.
business_questions: Which size/color variants of a product sell best?
Do variant-specific promotions impact sales?
variant_info.nl2sql_hints: Treat as nullable string. When present, trim and normalize values before grouping. Use left joins to product_variants if needed. For NL2SQL, avoid assuming non-null—always include null-safe comparisons.

expiry_date.description: Expiry date associated with this line's product batch (if applicable). Currently 100% null in this dataset.
expiry_date.analysis_use: When populated, supports expiry-based sell-through analysis and inventory risk (near-expiry promotions). Currently not usable without data.
business_questions: How many sold items were near expiry at the time of sale?
Which batches are expiring soon and how many units sold from them?
expiry_date.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; when used cast to DATE or DATETIME. Handle multiple possible formats and nulls. NL2SQL should include parsing logic or rely on normalized expiry_date in joined inventory tables.

batch_id.description: Identifier for the inventory/manufacturing batch associated with the sold item. Almost unique per line (124 distinct), indicating strong batch-level traceability.
batch_id.analysis_use: Group/slice by batch_id for recall/traceability, compute units sold per batch, and join to inventory_batches or product_inventory for batch metadata (manufacture date, expiry).
business_questions: Which batches generated how many sales and what revenue?
Can we trace which customers/transactions received items from a specific batch?
batch_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string and prefer exact-match joins. Because batch_id is nearly unique, aggregations by batch will often be per-row. Use joins to inventory tables to get batch metadata; handle missing foreign key constraints and potential casing/whitespace mismatches.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the line record was created (transaction time). Stored as nvarchar strings in a datetime format.
created_at.analysis_use: Time-series analysis: filtering by date ranges, grouping by day/week/month for sales trends, and aligning with business calendar. Use to compute time-based KPIs and to join with date dimension after casting.
business_questions: What are daily/weekly/monthly sales trends from these POS lines?
Which hours of day have the highest unit sales or revenue?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)) before temporal grouping/filters. Expect ISO-like format but validate parsing and timezone context. Use date_trunc or equivalent after casting for grouping. In NL2SQL, recommend explicit date parsing to avoid string comparisons.

pos_txn_id.description: Unique identifier for the POS transaction (string).
pos_txn_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for joins to transaction-line/detail tables; grouping or counting transactions; deduplication and data-quality checks.
business_questions: How many unique transactions occurred in a date range?
Which transactions correspond to a specific external reference for reconciliation?
pos_txn_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as an identifier string. Use equality filters or IN lists. For joins to pos_transaction_lines or other tables use exact match on this column. Avoid numeric casting. Check for leading/trailing whitespace in filters.

terminal_id.description: Identifier of the POS terminal where the transaction occurred (string).
terminal_id.analysis_use: Group/filter to compute per-terminal sales volumes and counts; join to terminal metadata for location or store-level aggregations.
business_questions: Which terminal generated the most revenue today?
How many transactions per terminal occurred per shift?
terminal_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality; recommended to index for filtering. When users ask for 'store' or 'terminal', map directly to terminal_id. Be careful that terminal values are strings like 'TERM-001-4'.

pos_user_id.description: Identifier of the POS user/cashier who performed the transaction (string).
pos_user_id.analysis_use: Aggregate by cashier for productivity, average ticket value per cashier, or to join to pos_users for names/roles.
business_questions: What is the total sales per cashier today?
Which cashier has the highest average transaction value?
pos_user_id.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field; use for GROUP BY and filters. Join conditions to pos_users should be exact string matches. Watch for multiple user id formats; normalize when necessary.

txn_ts.description: Transaction timestamp (string stored in ISO-like format).
txn_ts.analysis_use: Primary time dimension for trend analysis, time-window filters, time bucketing (hour/day/week/month) after casting to a datetime type.
business_questions: Show hourly sales for the last 7 days.
How many transactions occurred between 9am-12pm yesterday?
txn_ts.nl2sql_hints: Must cast/convert to a datetime type in SQL (e.g., CAST(txn_ts AS DATETIME) or TRY_CONVERT depending on engine). Use consistent timezone assumptions. When generating time buckets use the converted datetime. Be defensive about invalid or NULL strings.

txn_type.description: Type of transaction (e.g., SALE). In this dataset it is uniform (SALE).
txn_type.analysis_use: Filter for transaction types (SALE, REFUND, etc.) if present in larger data; exclude non-sales for revenue calculations.
business_questions: Filter to only sales transactions when computing revenue.
What proportion of transactions are refunds vs sales (if refunds present)?
txn_type.nl2sql_hints: Often a small categorical set. Use equality filters. If currently constant, queries should still explicitly filter txn_type='SALE' for safety.

txn_total.description: Total monetary amount for the transaction (stored as string with decimals).
txn_total.analysis_use: Key measure for revenue aggregations, average ticket size, and financial KPIs after casting to numeric/decimal.
business_questions: What is the total revenue for a period (sum of txn_total)?
What is the average transaction value (AVG txn_total) by payment_mode?
txn_total.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — always CAST or CONVERT to DECIMAL/NUMERIC in SQL to aggregate. Use TRY_CAST/TRY_CONVERT to avoid errors on bad strings. Consider rounding rules when summing; specify precision (e.g., DECIMAL(18,2)).

tax_total.description: Total tax applied to the transaction (string containing numeric value).
tax_total.analysis_use: Aggregate tax collected, compute tax as percentage of txn_total, or validate tax calculations at transaction level after casting to numeric.
business_questions: How much tax was collected in a period?
What is the average tax rate (tax_total / txn_total) across transactions?
tax_total.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric before arithmetic. Be mindful of division by zero when computing rates. Use consistent rounding and tolerances for float-like strings.

discount_total.description: Total discount applied to the transaction (string). In this sample it is always '0'.
discount_total.analysis_use: Aggregate discounts, compute net revenue, identify transactions with discounts. May be used for promotion analysis when non-zero values exist.
business_questions: What is the total discount given over the last month?
Which shifts or terminals have the highest discount usage?
discount_total.nl2sql_hints: Currently constant; still cast to numeric when used in calculations. If values are small integers or booleans, ensure correct casting. Consider interpreting '0' vs NULL differently in filters.

payment_mode.description: Method of payment for the transaction (CASH, UPI, CARD).
payment_mode.analysis_use: Group by payment mode to analyze payment mix, fee calculations, or settlements; filter for specific payment types for reconciliation.
business_questions: What percentage of revenue is via CARD vs CASH vs UPI?
How many CARD transactions happened yesterday?
payment_mode.nl2sql_hints: Categorical low-cardinality field. Map natural language queries like 'card sales' to payment_mode='CARD'. Consider normalizing values (upper-case) and handling synonyms ('credit card' -> 'CARD').

external_txn_ref.description: External system transaction reference (string) used for reconciliation with payment processors or external systems.
external_txn_ref.analysis_use: Join/reconcile with payment provider data, detect duplicate external refs, and support audit trails.
business_questions: Which transactions failed reconciliation with the payment gateway?
Find details for external reference EXTXXXXX.
external_txn_ref.nl2sql_hints: Treat as opaque string for equality joins and lookups. Use exact matches or LIKE for partial queries. Be aware of uniqueness — may not be strictly unique.

shift_id.description: Identifier of the operational shift during which the transaction took place (e.g., SHIFT1).
shift_id.analysis_use: Aggregate sales and counts by shift, compare shift performance, or join to shift metadata for start/end times.
business_questions: Which shift generates the most revenue on average?
Compare transaction counts between SHIFT1 and SHIFT2.
shift_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality categorical field — use for GROUP BY. If joining to a shift dimension, match on exact string. Some queries may convert shift_id to an ordered dimension for shift sequencing.

cashier_note.description: Free-text note entered by cashier for the transaction. In this dataset it is fully NULL.
cashier_note.analysis_use: When populated, used for qualitative analysis, tagging exceptional transactions, or searching for incidents; low utility in current dataset due to 100% nulls.
business_questions: Show transactions with non-empty cashier notes in the last week.
Search cashier notes for lapses or incident keywords.
cashier_note.nl2sql_hints: Text field — use IS NOT NULL and LIKE for text search. May require full-text search on larger datasets. Handle empty strings vs NULLs explicitly.

status.description: Business status of the transaction (e.g., COMPLETED). Currently uniform 'COMPLETED'.
status.analysis_use: Filter to completed/active transactions for revenue; exclude cancelled/voided transactions if present.
business_questions: How many transactions are completed vs voided?
Exclude non-completed transactions when calculating revenue.
status.nl2sql_hints: Categorical status — include status='COMPLETED' in revenue queries for safety. Map synonyms (VOID/CANCELLED) to a normalized set for consistent filtering.

created_at.description: Row creation timestamp (string); often similar to txn_ts and useful for ingestion/time of record creation.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ETL diagnostics (when row entered), compare created_at vs txn_ts for latency analysis, or use as an alternative time dimension if txn_ts invalid.
business_questions: What is the ingestion latency between txn_ts and created_at?
How many transactions were created in the last 24 hours?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for time-based operations. Use for data freshness checks. If txn_ts and created_at differ, queries should choose the appropriate one and document timezone assumptions.

synced_flag.description: Flag indicating whether transaction has been synced to downstream systems (string '1' in this sample).
synced_flag.analysis_use: Filter to unsynced transactions for retry; count unsynced rows; monitor sync-health metrics.
business_questions: How many transactions remain unsynced?
List transactions that failed sync for reconciliation.
synced_flag.nl2sql_hints: Often stored as string '1'/'0' or 'Y'/'N' — normalize to boolean in queries (e.g., synced_flag = '1'). Beware of inconsistent values; use COALESCE and canonical mapping.

pos_user_id.description: Unique identifier for the POS user (alphanumeric id like 'PU1001').
pos_user_id.analysis_use: Primary stable identifier for joins to transaction and shift tables, grouping and counting users, deduplication. Use for filters and joins.
business_questions: Which transactions were handled by a given pos_user_id?
How many active users exist (count distinct pos_user_id) per store?
pos_user_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string in SQL; commonly used as join key to pos_transactions, pos_shift_closures, pos_sales tables. Although not declared PK, assume uniqueness in queries (use COUNT(DISTINCT pos_user_id) if unsure).

name.description: Human-readable full name of the POS user.
name.analysis_use: Display label in result sets, grouping for human reports, fuzzy matching for de-duplication or searching.
business_questions: Show full names of users for a given store or role.
List user names and last login times for an audit report.
name.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; often used in SELECT and WHERE (LIKE) clauses. Be cautious of duplicate/variant names; prefer pos_user_id for joins/aggregations.

role.description: The role/title of the user within POS (e.g., Cashier, Supervisor).
role.analysis_use: Filter and group to measure counts and performance by role; join to role-based permission logic or access audits.
business_questions: How many Cashiers vs Supervisors are there per store?
Which Supervisors have not logged in in the last 30 days?
role.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical column—good for GROUP BY and WHERE role = 'Cashier'. Normalize casing/spelling in NL2SQL mappings. Consider mapping role synonyms ('cashier' vs 'Cashier').

phone.description: Contact phone number for the user (stored as string, includes country code).
phone.analysis_use: Used for contact lists, identity verification, or matching to other systems. Can be used as an alternate identifier for deduplication.
business_questions: Provide contact numbers for all active users at a given store.
Find users with missing or malformed phone numbers.
phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar including characters like '+' and spaces—use normalization functions when matching (remove non-digits). Be careful with formatting differences in filters.

email.description: Email address of the POS user.
email.analysis_use: Contact/notification routing, matching to identity systems, grouping by domain for organizational analysis.
business_questions: List emails for supervisors for notification sends.
How many users use corporate vs personal email domains?
email.nl2sql_hints: String column—use LOWER(email) for case-insensitive comparisons and LIKE '%@domain.com' to filter domains. Validate formats if used for messaging.

store_id.description: Identifier of the store the user is assigned to (e.g., STORE_AHD_001).
store_id.analysis_use: Primary column for grouping users by store, joining to pos_stores for store metadata, scoping user-level analyses to a store or region.
business_questions: Which users are assigned to STORE_AHD_001?
How many users per store and role?
store_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string; commonly used as a foreign key to pos_stores—map this join in NL2SQL. Use exact equality for joins and filters. Consider indexing in production for joins and group-by performance.

active_flag.description: Flag indicating whether the user account is active (stored as string, sample shows '1').
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter active vs inactive accounts, compute active user counts, audit account statuses.
business_questions: How many active users are there per store?
Which users are inactive and may need deprovisioning?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; canonicalize to boolean in NL2SQL layer (e.g., active_flag = '1' => active). Support common representations ('1','0','Y','N','true'). If missing values exist, treat as inactive by default or explicit NULL-handling.

created_at.description: Account creation timestamp stored as a string (format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' in sample).
created_at.analysis_use: Used for cohorting, hire-date reports, tenure calculations, filtering by creation date. Requires casting to datetime for range filters and date arithmetic.
business_questions: Which users were created in the last 90 days?
What is the distribution of user creation dates by month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar—cast to a datetime/timestamp in SQL (e.g., CAST(created_at AS datetime) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Ensure consistent format and handle parsing errors. Index on parsed datetime in production if used frequently in filters.

last_login.description: Last login timestamp for the user stored as a string (format 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' in sample).
last_login.analysis_use: Measure recency of activity, identify inactive users, calculate time since last login for SLA/audit. Requires casting to datetime for date math.
business_questions: Which users have not logged in for more than 30 days?
Who are the most recently active users across stores?
last_login.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons (e.g., WHERE CAST(last_login AS datetime) < DATEADD(day, -30, GETDATE())). Handle NULLs and malformed strings. Consider indexing parsed datetime for large datasets.

pin_hash.description: Hashed representation of the user's PIN or authentication secret (sensitive security data).
pin_hash.analysis_use: Not used for analytics; only relevant for authentication systems and security audits (e.g., checking whether a hash exists). Avoid selecting or exposing in business reports.
business_questions: Do all active users have a pin_hash set (i.e., is a PIN configured)?
How many users are missing a pin_hash (potential security gap)?
pin_hash.nl2sql_hints: Sensitive data—avoid returning raw values in NL2SQL responses by default. Use anonymized existence checks (pin_hash IS NOT NULL) rather than selecting the hash. Store as string; do not attempt to interpret or reverse. Exclude from default SELECT * outputs in NL2SQL mappings or enforce role-based access control.

vendor_id.description: Surrogate or natural identifier for the vendor (string). Appears unique per row (40 distinct).
vendor_id.analysis_use: Primary lookup/join key for vendor metadata; grouping and counting vendors; filtering to a specific vendor. Useful for linking to transactional tables if they use the same id.
business_questions: How many transactions or invoices are associated with vendor_id = 'POSV1001'?
List vendor details for a given vendor_id
Which vendor_ids have missing or duplicate GST numbers?
vendor_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a string key (nvarchar). Use equality filters (vendor_id = '...') for exact lookups; avoid LIKE unless fuzzy matching is needed. Consider normalizing case/whitespace. Recommend creating an index/primary key on vendor_id and ensuring consistent format across source tables to support joins.

vendor_name.analysis_use: Used for display, grouping, fuzzy searches and reporting; can be aggregated for counts of unique vendors by name; useful for manual lookup and duplicate detection.
business_questions: Show all vendors whose name contains 'Bobal' or 'Boase'.
Which vendor names appear to be duplicates or near-duplicates?
vendor_name.nl2sql_hints: Free-text field; queries often use LIKE or full-text search for partial matches. Beware of spelling variations—consider soundex/levenshtein fuzzy matching in NL2SQL translations. Use exact match when joined by vendor_id rather than vendor_name.

phone.description: Vendor contact phone number (string, appears to include country code).
phone.analysis_use: Used for contact lists and completeness checks; can be grouped to find vendors missing phone numbers; used to filter vendors by contact availability.
business_questions: Which vendors do not have a phone number listed?
Provide phone numbers for vendors in Ahmedabad for outreach.
phone.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar; may contain formatting characters (+, spaces). Normalize (trim, remove non-digits) when doing equality or deduplication. Use IS NULL/='' checks for missing values. Do not assume strict length—use LIKE '%...' when searching partial numbers.

email.analysis_use: Used for communication lists, validation checks, grouping by domain (e.g., vendor.in), and filtering vendors who can receive electronic documents.
business_questions: Which vendors have email addresses in the 'vendor.in' domain?
Which vendors are missing email addresses?
email.nl2sql_hints: Text field—queries often use LIKE '%@vendor.in' for domain grouping or IS NULL/='' checks for missing. Email may contain commas or malformed values; validate/clean during NL2SQL generation. Use LOWER() when performing case-insensitive matches.

address.description: Street address lines including locality and postal code.
address.analysis_use: Used for display, geocoding/enrichment, and identifying vendors by locality; can be used for string-based grouping or extracting postal codes/city when city column is missing or inconsistent.
business_questions: Provide full mailing addresses for all vendors in Navsari.
Which vendor addresses appear to be incomplete or missing postal codes?
address.nl2sql_hints: Free-form text; use LIKE for substring searches or pattern extraction functions (e.g., SUBSTRING, PATINDEX) for postal codes. Avoid exact matches. When joining or grouping by location, prefer the structured city column over address.

city.description: City where the vendor is located (low cardinality).
city.analysis_use: Important grouping/filtering dimension for regional vendor analysis and counts; good candidate for indexes or materialized aggregation (city-level counts).
business_questions: How many vendors are located in Gandhinagar, Anand and Vadodara respectively?
Which cities have the largest share of our vendors?
city.nl2sql_hints: Categorical nvarchar; use equality or IN lists for filters (city IN ('Gandhinagar','Anand')). Watch for inconsistent capitalization or trailing spaces—normalize with UPPER()/LTRIM()/RTRIM() in queries. City is efficient for GROUP BY and ORDER BY due to low cardinality.

gst_no.description: Tax identifier (GST number) for the vendor (string).
gst_no.analysis_use: Used for tax validation, uniqueness checks, regulatory reporting and joins to finance systems; grouping and de-duplication by gst_no helps detect the same legal entity registered under multiple vendor_ids.
business_questions: Which vendors are missing GST numbers?
Are there any duplicate GST numbers across vendor records (possible duplicate vendors)?
gst_no.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Normalize (remove spaces, punctuation) before equality checks. Consider enforcing uniqueness in the data model. Use LENGTH() and pattern checks to validate GST format. Use gst_no as an alternate key for joins to financial tables if vendor_id is absent there.

business_type.description: Categorical classification of vendor's business (e.g., Equipment Repair, Hardware).
business_type.analysis_use: Dimension for segmentation and supplier-category analysis; useful for grouping vendors and filtering suppliers by service/product type for sourcing decisions.
business_questions: How many vendors are classified as 'Equipment Repair' and how does that compare to 'Hardware'?
List vendors by business type for supplier onboarding in Cleaning Services.
business_type.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality categorical field—use equality, IN and GROUP BY for aggregation. Ensure consistent category labels; consider mapping synonyms to canonical values prior to query generation. Good candidate for an indexed column or a small lookup/dimension table.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp stored as text (nvarchar). All rows currently have the same value in this snapshot.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for temporal filtering (new vendors, onboarding windows) once converted to a proper datetime type; currently limited because it's stored as string and not variable
business_questions: Which vendors were added after a specific date?
How many vendors were created in the last 30/90 days?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Currently nvarchar with identical value; NL2SQL should CAST/CONVERT to a DATETIME/TIMESTAMP before doing range filters or ordering (e.g., TRY_CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)). Guard against invalid formats and NULLs. Recommend altering schema to use a native datetime type for efficient comparisons and indexing.

inventory_id.description: Unique identifier for this inventory record (appears to encode SKU and centre in the value).
inventory_id.analysis_use: Record-level identifier for deduplication, linking inventory metadata, grouping unique inventory entries; useful when listing inventory records or joining to inventory transaction tables if mapped.
business_questions: Which inventory records correspond to a given SKU-centre combination?
Are there duplicate or multiple inventory records for the same SKU at a centre?
inventory_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Often parse inventory_id to extract sku/centre if SKU/centre fields are missing or inconsistent. inventory_id appears unique (distinct=40) so it can be used as a primary key in queries (e.g., WHERE inventory_id = 'INV-APP009-AHMEDABAD'). Watch for case/whitespace; use exact match or normalized equality.

sku.description: Product SKU code (business product identifier).
sku.analysis_use: Primary attribute for grouping and aggregating inventory metrics (SUM qty, available, reserved). Join key to product master tables to obtain product name, category, pricing, etc.
business_questions: How much stock (units/kg) do we have for SKU 'APP009' across all centres?
Which SKUs are most frequently below reorder point?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR: treat as categorical string and use as join key to product tables. Normalize case and trim. When users ask for product name, map to sku -> products table. Low cardinality; queries grouping by sku are inexpensive on this dataset but join expansions may increase cost.

centre_id.description: Name/identifier of the physical centre/warehouse/storage location.
centre_id.analysis_use: Filter and group inventories by location; used to compute per-centre totals, alerts, or to join to centre/store dimension tables for region, type or capacity attributes.
business_questions: What is the available stock by centre (e.g., Ahmedabad Collection Centre)?
Which centres have most items below reorder points?
centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Join candidate to store/centre master tables (e.g., pos_stores) after normalization. Values are descriptive (not numeric). Use exact or LIKE matching if users may use partial names.

qty_units.description: Quantity measured in units (text field containing numeric values).
qty_units.analysis_use: Aggregation (SUM, AVG) and comparisons to express inventory counts; used in calculations like available vs reserved ratio, total units per SKU/centre.
business_questions: What is the total unit count of a given SKU across all centres?
How many units are available vs reserved for sale for SKU X?
qty_units.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR containing numbers — CAST/CONVERT to numeric (INT or DECIMAL) before arithmetic: e.g., CAST(qty_units AS INT). Handle non-numeric/null values and trimming. Use COALESCE to treat NULLs as 0 in aggregations.

qty_kg.description: Quantity measured in kilograms (string field containing numeric values).
qty_kg.analysis_use: Aggregate kilograms per SKU/centre, convert between units if needed, compute weight-based reorder or expiry prioritization.
business_questions: What is the total kg on hand for SKU Y by centre?
Which SKUs have low kg-availability compared to safety stock?
qty_kg.nl2sql_hints: CAST to numeric (DECIMAL) for SUM and comparisons (e.g., CAST(qty_kg AS DECIMAL(10,2))). Watch for integer-like strings; handle commas/formatting. Use consistent units (kg) and document precision.

reserved_stock.description: Stock quantity reserved (string) — items allocated to orders or holds.
reserved_stock.analysis_use: Subtract from qty to compute available stock, analyze reservation rates, and compute fulfillment capacity.
business_questions: How much stock is reserved for orders for SKU Z at centre A?
What proportion of total stock is reserved vs available?
reserved_stock.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR — CAST to INT/DECIMAL before arithmetic. Consider NULL->0. When computing available_stock, verify if available_stock column already accounts for reservation or if calculation required (qty - reserved).

available_stock.description: Available stock quantity (string) — inventory that can be allocated to new orders.
available_stock.analysis_use: Primary field for fulfillment and replenishment decisions; aggregate per SKU/centre, compute stockouts and compare to reorder_point/safety_stock.
business_questions: Which SKUs have zero available stock at any centre?
How much available kg remains for urgent orders for SKU X?
available_stock.nl2sql_hints: Stored as NVARCHAR — CAST to numeric for filters (e.g., WHERE CAST(available_stock AS INT) < reorder_point). Confirm whether available_stock is redundant or needs recalculation (qty - reserved). Use COALESCE for NULLs.

reorder_point.description: Threshold (string) below which an automatic/manual reorder should be triggered.
reorder_point.analysis_use: Compare available stock to reorder_point to produce reorder alerts; group by SKU/centre for ordering plans.
business_questions: Which inventory items currently sit below their reorder point?
How many orders are needed to bring SKUs back above reorder point per centre?
reorder_point.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR storing small integers — CAST to INT for comparisons. Some records may have different units (units vs kg) — ensure reorder_point unit alignment before comparison. Use explicit unit-based comparisons in NL2SQL generation.

lead_time_days.description: Supplier/transfer lead time in days (string field with small set of integer values).
lead_time_days.analysis_use: Used to compute expected replenishment arrival (today + lead_time_days), to prioritize orders and calculate reorder quantities using demand forecasts.
business_questions: Which SKUs have lead time > X days and need earlier reordering?
What proportion of SKUs have short (1 day) vs longer lead time?
lead_time_days.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality NVARCHAR. CAST to SMALLINT/INT for arithmetic (e.g., DATEADD with parsed expiry or current date). If generating dates, ensure proper date arithmetic and handle null/invalid values.

safety_stock_kg.description: Configured safety stock level in kilograms (string field).
safety_stock_kg.analysis_use: Compare available kg to safety_stock_kg to detect stock risk, compute buffer requirements, and inform reorder quantities.
business_questions: Which SKUs are below their safety stock (kg) and are at risk of stockouts?
How much additional stock (kg) is needed to meet safety stock levels per centre?
safety_stock_kg.nl2sql_hints: NVARCHAR storing numeric values — CAST to DECIMAL for accurate kg arithmetic. Ensure both available_stock (kg) and safety_stock_kg are in same unit; if available_stock is in units, map conversion or use qty_kg instead.

expiry_ts.description: Expiry timestamp for the inventory batch, stored as string (looks like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
expiry_ts.analysis_use: Compute days-to-expiry, filter items expiring within X days, prioritize shipments, and generate expiry risk reports.
business_questions: Which inventory batches expire within the next 30 days?
What is the distribution of expiry dates by SKU and centre?
expiry_ts.nl2sql_hints: Parse/CAST to DATETIME/TIMESTAMP (e.g., CAST(expiry_ts AS DATETIME) or TO_TIMESTAMP depending on DB). Expect ISO-like strings but validate format and timezone. Use date functions for RANGE filters (e.g., WHERE CAST(expiry_ts AS DATETIME) BETWEEN CURRENT_DATE AND DATEADD(day, 30, CURRENT_DATE)). Handle NULLs and malformed strings gracefully.

storage_temp_c.description: Recommended or actual storage temperature in degrees Celsius (string with small set of values).
storage_temp_c.analysis_use: Filter or group inventory by storage temperature needs (cold chain management), identify items requiring refrigerated storage, and plan storage allocation.
business_questions: Which SKUs require refrigerated storage (e.g., storage_temp_c = 4) vs ambient (10)?
How many kg of refrigerated vs ambient stock exist per centre?
storage_temp_c.nl2sql_hints: Small set of numeric-looking strings — CAST to INT for numeric comparisons or treat as categorical. Use in WHERE/GROUP BY clauses. If joining to storage capacity tables, normalize values and units before joining.

price_id.description: Identifier for the pricing row/version (appears to be structured like PR-<SKU>-<n>), likely intended as a unique id per price record.
price_id.analysis_use: Used as a stable identifier for deduplication, auditing, or selecting a specific price record. Good for retrieving a single record or counting pricing versions. Not used for numeric/time comparisons.
business_questions: How many distinct price versions exist (count of price_id) across all SKUs?
Show the full record for price_id = 'PR-APP009-1'.
price_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Although distinct per row in this dataset, the table lacks a declared primary key—do not assume enforcement. Use price_id for exact-match filters. Avoid using it for joins unless other tables store it explicitly. If used in grouping, group by the raw string.

sku.description: Product stock-keeping unit identifier used to associate the price with a product.
sku.analysis_use: Primary grouping and join key for per-product pricing analysis, aggregations (avg/min/max price per sku), filtering (price history for a SKU), and joining to orders/product tables to apply pricing.
business_questions: What is the historical pricing schedule for SKU 'APP009'?
Which SKUs have the highest average price_per_unit in the last 3 months?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string and canonicalize (trim/upper) if source systems vary. Common join target: products.product_id or products.sku or order_items.sku. When writing NL2SQL, prefer equality joins on sku and include casting of start_ts/end_ts for temporal joins.

start_ts.description: Start timestamp (string) from which this price is effective.
start_ts.analysis_use: Used for temporal filtering (which price was active at a given time), interval overlap detection, ordering price versions, and calculating duration of a price version (with end_ts). Requires casting to a timestamp/datetime for correct comparisons and ordering.
business_questions: Which price was effective for SKU X at 2024-11-15 12:00:00?
How long did each price_version (start_ts to end_ts) remain active on average?
start_ts.nl2sql_hints: Contains 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss' strings; always CAST or PARSE to datetime/timestamp in NL2SQL (e.g., CAST(start_ts AS TIMESTAMP) or TO_TIMESTAMP). Be careful about inclusive vs exclusive semantics when pairing with end_ts (decide whether end_ts is inclusive). Use start_ts for ordering price versions and temporal BETWEEN checks.

end_ts.description: End timestamp (string) until which this price is effective.
end_ts.analysis_use: Used together with start_ts to define effective intervals, detect overlaps/gaps, determine if a price was active at a given order timestamp, and to compute duration of a price period.
business_questions: Are there any SKUs with overlapping price intervals (start_ts/end_ts overlap) indicating conflicting price definitions?
Which prices expire within the next N days?
end_ts.nl2sql_hints: Also stored as string; CAST to datetime/timestamp before comparisons. If end_ts can be NULL (open-ended pricing), interpret NULL as indefinite. When checking if a price applies to an order at time T use: CAST(T AS TIMESTAMP) BETWEEN CAST(start_ts AS TIMESTAMP) AND CAST(end_ts AS TIMESTAMP) (or use <=/>= with chosen inclusivity).

price_per_unit.description: Unit price for the SKU during the specified interval, stored as string with decimal precision.
price_per_unit.analysis_use: Primary metric for aggregation (min/avg/max), trend analysis, price-change percent calculations, revenue estimation when joined to quantity in orders. Must be cast to numeric/decimal for accurate math.
business_questions: What is the average price_per_unit for SKU Y over the past 6 months?
Which SKU had the largest percentage price change between consecutive price versions?
price_per_unit.nl2sql_hints: Stored as nvarchar — always CAST/CONVERT to DECIMAL/NUMERIC in NL2SQL prior to arithmetic or aggregation (e.g., CAST(price_per_unit AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Watch for localized formatting (commas) though sample shows plain dot decimals. Avoid string-based ORDER BY if numeric ordering is required.

currency.description: Currency code for the price (three-letter ISO-like string). In this dataset the value is constant 'INR'.
currency.analysis_use: Useful for multi-currency support, conversion/normalization, and filtering by currency. In this dataset it has no variance but is important when integrating across datasets with different currencies.
business_questions: Are there any price records not in the base currency (INR)?
When prices exist in multiple currencies, what is the USD-equivalent average price_per_unit per SKU?
currency.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Typically used as a filter or to drive currency conversion joins. If always single-valued in practice, queries can omit it or assert currency='INR'.

min_order_qty.description: Minimum order quantity required for this price to apply (stored as string representing small integers like '1','5','10').
min_order_qty.analysis_use: Used to segment pricing by order quantity tiers (filtering/grouping), to calculate effective revenue thresholds, and to enforce business rules in joins to order data (e.g., apply price only if order quantity >= min_order_qty). Should be cast to integer for numeric comparisons.
business_questions: How many price versions require a minimum order quantity of 10?
What is the average price_per_unit for prices that require min_order_qty >= 5?
min_order_qty.nl2sql_hints: Cast to INTEGER in NL2SQL (e.g., CAST(min_order_qty AS INT)). Expect low cardinality — good candidate for grouping. Be mindful of NULLs or non-numeric values; include safe CAST/TRY_CAST where available.

created_by.description: Audit column indicating who created the pricing record (string). In this dataset it is constant 'pricing_admin'.
created_by.analysis_use: Used for audit queries, filtering changes by user, and workflow analysis of who created prices. If multiple creators exist, can be grouped to show contributions by user.
business_questions: Which admin created the most price entries in the last quarter?
Show records created by 'pricing_admin' in October 2024.
created_by.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string. Joinable to user/employee tables if a user directory exists. If constant, it can be ignored for performance unless auditing is required.

created_at.description: Timestamp (string) when the pricing record was created in the system.
created_at.analysis_use: Used for auditing, sequencing inserts, measuring latency between creation and effective start_ts, and filtering records by creation date. Should be cast to timestamp for date arithmetic.
business_questions: How many price records were created per month by the pricing team?
What is the average lag between created_at and start_ts for new price versions?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to TIMESTAMP/DATETIME before comparisons or grouping by date parts (day/month). Use CAST/TO_TIMESTAMP functions and consider timezones if relevant. Be careful: created_at and start_ts are both strings — compare only after casting.

sku.description: Product stock keeping unit identifier used to attribute sales metrics to a product SKU.
sku.analysis_use: Used as the primary grouping key for per-product aggregations, top-N queries, joins to product master tables and filters (e.g., WHERE sku = 'APP009').
business_questions: Which SKUs contribute most to gross and net sales?
For a given SKU, what are the gross sales, discounts, tax and net sales?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a text key. Values appear unique in this sample. Use exact-match joins to product master tables on sku. Ensure case-sensitivity rules match the target DB. If the app expects numeric comparisons, note sku is not numeric. When generating SQL, quote/bracket the column when used (e.g., [sku] or "sku"). Consider adding an index or primary key if this table becomes large and sku is unique.

Gross Sales.description: Total billed sales amount (before discounts, fees and taxes) for the SKU, stored as text.
Gross Sales.analysis_use: Primary measure for revenue analysis: aggregations (SUM, AVG), ranking, ratio calculations (e.g., discount rate = Discount / Gross Sales). Used for filters (top grossing SKUs) after casting to numeric.
business_questions: What is the total gross sales by SKU or across all SKUs?
Which SKUs have the highest growth or concentration of gross sales (when combined with time or orders)?
Gross Sales.nl2sql_hints: Column name has space — quote/bracket it (e.g., [Gross Sales]). Stored as nvarchar: convert to DECIMAL/NUMERIC (TRY_CAST or CAST) before SUM/AVG to avoid errors. Handle NULLs and malformed strings with TRY_CAST or ISNUMERIC checks. Consider casting once in a CTE to simplify downstream NL2SQL expressions.

Discount.description: Total discounts applied to the SKU (monetary amount) stored as text.
Discount.analysis_use: Used to compute discount dollars, discount rate (Discount / Gross Sales), and to measure promotional impact. Aggregateable after numeric conversion, useful in comparisons and reconciliation against gross/net sales.
business_questions: What absolute and percentage discounts are applied per SKU?
Which SKUs are most impacted by discounts (largest discount amount or highest discount rate)?
Discount.nl2sql_hints: Name contains no special characters but column is nvarchar — cast to numeric before arithmetic or aggregation. Use COALESCE to treat NULL as 0 when computing rates. Beware division by zero when Gross Sales is zero or NULL; include safe guards in generated SQL (CASE WHEN GrossSales<>0 THEN Discount/GrossSales END).

Delivery Fee.description: Total delivery fee associated with sales of the SKU, stored as text.
Delivery Fee.analysis_use: Can be aggregated to understand shipping contribution per SKU, included in revenue reconciliation, or analyzed as a per-order/per-SKU cost contributor when joined with order counts/quantities.
business_questions: How much delivery fee revenue is attributed to each SKU?
What portion of gross or net sales is composed of delivery fees for top SKUs?
Delivery Fee.nl2sql_hints: Column name has a space — quote/bracket it (e.g., [Delivery Fee]). Cast to numeric for SUM/AVG. Delivery fee may be zero or NULL; use COALESCE(TRY_CAST([Delivery Fee] AS DECIMAL(...)),0) in generated SQL. If delivery fee should be stored per-order rather than per-SKU, users should be warned about potential double counting when joining to order-level data without proper keys.

Tax.description: Tax amount applied to sales of the SKU, stored as text.
Tax.analysis_use: Used in revenue reconciliation, tax liability reporting per SKU, and to calculate effective tax rate (Tax / Gross Sales). Aggregateable after numeric conversion.
business_questions: What is the tax collected per SKU and in total?
Which SKUs have the highest effective tax rate relative to gross sales?
Tax.nl2sql_hints: Quote/bracket the name when used. Convert nvarchar to numeric using TRY_CAST or CONVERT in a safe manner. When computing ratios, guard against zero gross sales. Consider join to tax/rate master data if more granularity is required; currently the table contains only the monetary tax amount, not tax rates or jurisdictions.

Net Sales.description: Net sales amount for the SKU after discounts, fees and tax adjustments as recorded, stored as text.
Net Sales.analysis_use: Primary target metric for performance (SUM, AVG, ranking). Used in reconciliation checks (Gross Sales - Discount + Delivery Fee + Tax ?= Net Sales). Useful for computing margins when cost data is joined.
business_questions: What are the net sales by SKU and which SKUs drive net revenue?
Are net sales consistent with the components (gross, discount, delivery, tax) or are there discrepancies indicating data quality issues?
Net Sales.nl2sql_hints: Column name has space — use brackets (e.g., [Net Sales]). Cast to numeric before aggregations and arithmetic. Use a data-quality SQL pattern to compare computed_net = GrossSales - Discount + DeliveryFee + Tax against [Net Sales] with an allowance/tolerance for rounding. Consider normalizing types in ETL: store this column as DECIMAL(18,2) to avoid repeated casts in NL2SQL translations.

variant_id.description: Internal identifier for the product variant (string). Appears unique per row in this sample.
variant_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for variant-level lookups and deduplication. Useful for joins when other systems reference variant_id. Grouping/filtering by variant identity.
business_questions: Which variant_id corresponds to a given variant_sku or barcode?
How many transactions are attributed to a specific variant_id (when joined with transactions)?
variant_id.nl2sql_hints: Although stored as nvarchar, treat variant_id as an identifier (no numeric casting). Prefer exact equality joins (WHERE variant_id = 'VAR3001'). If another table references variant_id, use it as the join key; otherwise fallback to sku or variant_sku.

sku.description: Product-level SKU to which the variant belongs (string). Lower cardinality relative to variant_sku (10 distinct in sample).
sku.analysis_use: Group variants under a product. Useful for aggregations at product level (e.g., total variants per sku), and joining with products/product_pricing/product_sales tables by product SKU.
business_questions: How many variants does SKU 'APP009' have?
What are the average retail and wholesale prices across variants for a given sku?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Use sku as a common natural join key to product master tables (products, product_pricing). Matches should be exact; consider case/whitespace normalization. When users request product-level aggregates, group by sku. If a user references 'product', clarify whether they mean sku or variant_sku.

variant_name.description: Human-readable variant descriptor (e.g., 'Loose', 'Packed 500g'). Low cardinality in sample (2 values).
variant_name.analysis_use: Categorical grouping to report on packaging types or variant naming. Useful for filtering (e.g., show only 'Loose' variants) and for UI labels.
business_questions: How many sales come from 'Loose' variants vs 'Packed 500g' (when joined with sales)?
Which SKUs offer 'Packed 500g' packaging?
variant_name.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string. Use equality or IN filters (WHERE variant_name = 'Loose'). Be mindful of synonyms/variants in naming; NL2SQL should map user terms like 'packaged' or 'packed' to variant_name values.

variant_sku.description: Variant-level SKU combining product SKU and variant identifier (e.g., APP009-LOOSE). Appears unique per row.
variant_sku.analysis_use: Primary natural key for joins with transaction lines, inventory, pricing and barcode lookups. Useful for direct sales attribution and SKU-level reporting.
business_questions: Show price and barcode for variant_sku 'APP009-PACKED500G'.
Which variant_sku has the highest retail price?
variant_sku.nl2sql_hints: Common join key to order_lines/pos_transaction_lines/order_items. Use exact matches. When users refer to 'SKU' ambiguity may arise (product SKU vs variant SKU) — NL2SQL should confirm which is intended. If variant_sku contains hyphens or special chars, ensure literal quoting in generated SQL.

uom.description: Unit of measure for the variant (e.g., kg, 250g, 500g). Low cardinality.
uom.analysis_use: Grouping and conversion logic for weight/volume calculations. Filter variants by uom for packaging/fulfillment and analytics that depend on unit normalization.
business_questions: List all variants measured in kg.
How many variants use 500g packaging?
uom.nl2sql_hints: Because uom values may be non-standard ('250g' vs '0.25 kg'), NL2SQL should normalize units for numeric comparisons or conversions (e.g., convert '250g' to 0.25 kg). Use string equality for simple filters; include mapping functions for unit conversions when needed.

units_per_pack.description: Number of units per pack or pack size represented as string (e.g., '0.5', '1.0'). Stored as nvarchar but represents numeric quantity.
units_per_pack.analysis_use: Convert to numeric to compute total units across inventory or orders, to derive case-pack quantities and to compute per-unit pricing.
business_questions: What is the units_per_pack for variant_sku 'BRN007-LOOSE'?
Calculate price per unit for variants given price_retail and units_per_pack.
units_per_pack.nl2sql_hints: Must be CAST/CONVERT to decimal/float for arithmetic (e.g., CAST(units_per_pack AS DECIMAL(10,4))). Handle NULLs and non-numeric strings. NL2SQL should add casts implicitly when users ask for calculations (e.g., price/unit).

weight_kg.description: Weight of the variant in kilograms stored as text (e.g., '0.5', '1.0').
weight_kg.analysis_use: Used for shipping estimate calculations, volumetric/weight-based logistics costs, and weight-based pricing analyses after casting to numeric.
business_questions: What is the total shipment weight for a set of variants (when joined to quantities)?
Which variants exceed 1.0 kg in weight?
weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric (DECIMAL/FLOAT) before comparisons or aggregations. Normalize units if other weight units exist. When generating SQL for weight thresholds, include CAST(weight_kg AS DECIMAL(...)) and handle invalid strings.

barcode.description: Barcode associated with the variant used for scanning/fulfillment (string). Distinct per variant in sample.
barcode.analysis_use: Lookup for point-of-sale and fulfillment matching; detect duplicate barcodes; join to scanning/fulfillment logs.
business_questions: Which variant corresponds to barcode 'BAR1631897'?
Are there duplicate barcodes across variants?
barcode.nl2sql_hints: Treat as string for equality searches. For fuzzy matching or partial scans, use LIKE. When joining to scan logs, ensure exact normalization (trim/upper) since barcodes may have formatting differences.

price_retail.description: Retail price for the variant stored as nvarchar (string) containing numeric-like values, sometimes with floating point artifacts.
price_retail.analysis_use: Key metric for revenue calculations, price comparisons and margin analyses when cast to numeric. Use for sorting (most/least expensive) and aggregations (avg, min, max) after conversion.
business_questions: What is the retail price for each variant and which is most expensive?
Compute retail price per kg or per unit for variants.
price_retail.nl2sql_hints: Must CAST to DECIMAL or FLOAT before arithmetic/aggregation (e.g., CAST(price_retail AS DECIMAL(18,4))). Be aware of floating representation strings (e.g., '13.199999999999999')—rounding formatting may be desired. Handle NULLs and non-numeric values robustly. When users ask for ranges or comparisons, convert in WHERE clauses (WHERE CAST(price_retail AS DECIMAL(18,4)) > 20).

price_wholesale.description: Wholesale price for the variant stored as nvarchar. Lower cardinality than price_retail in sample.
price_wholesale.analysis_use: Used to compute wholesale-retail spreads/margins and to support B2B pricing reports when converted to numeric.
business_questions: What is the wholesale price for variant_sku 'COW005-LOOSE'?
What is the average wholesale price across variants of a given SKU?
price_wholesale.nl2sql_hints: Same as price_retail: CAST to numeric for calculations. When computing margins, ensure both retail and wholesale are cast and rounded appropriately. NL2SQL should prefer DECIMAL types for monetary calculations to avoid floating point imprecision.

created_at.description: Timestamp string indicating when the variant record was created (stored as nvarchar, appears ISO-like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS').
created_at.analysis_use: Used for temporal analyses (new variant introductions over time), filtering recent variants, and ordering by creation date after casting to datetime.
business_questions: List variants created after 2024-01-01.
Show most recently added variants.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: CAST/CONVERT to DATE or DATETIME (e.g., CAST(created_at AS DATETIME)) before temporal comparisons, ordering or date truncation. Validate string format and handle parsing errors. NL2SQL should produce WHERE CAST(created_at AS DATETIME) >= '2024-01-01' when users provide date filters.

active_flag.description: Indicator of whether the variant is active (stored as nvarchar; sample contains '1' only).
active_flag.analysis_use: Filter to active variants for reporting and joins; support soft-delete semantics. Use to exclude inactive SKUs in product catalogs and analytics.
business_questions: Show all active variants available for sale.
How many inactive variants exist (if any)?
active_flag.nl2sql_hints: Treat as boolean-like flag; normalize values ('1','0','Y','N') if present. Use equality checks (WHERE active_flag = '1'). If user asks for 'active' or 'inactive', map to appropriate flag values. Consider casting to BIT/BOOLEAN in transformations for simpler NL2SQL generation.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit: unique identifier for the product in this catalog. nvarchar(22) in raw data.
sku.analysis_use: Primary lookup/join key to link product master to orders, inventory, pricing and sales. Also used for grouping and counting unique products and de-duplication.
business_questions: List product details for a given SKU or set of SKUs.
Which SKUs have the highest inventory or sales when joined to inventory/sales tables?
Are there duplicate SKUs or inconsistencies across channels?
sku.nl2sql_hints: Treat as text key. Prefer exact equality joins (sku = '...') or IN lists. Normalize case / trim whitespace. Add index on sku for performance. Validate uniqueness before assuming PK. When generating SQL, avoid LIKE for exact SKU matches.

product_name.description: Human readable product title (e.g., 'Apple - Kinnaur (1kg)'). nvarchar(58).
product_name.analysis_use: Used for search, display, text matching, grouping by product label, and fuzzy matching in NL2SQL when user queries by partial name. Useful for deduplication and to generate UI lists.
business_questions: Show product names and SKUs matching 'apple' or other search terms.
Which product names contain size or packaging info inconsistently?
How many variants share the same base product name?
product_name.nl2sql_hints: Support case-insensitive contains/ILIKE or LOWER(product_name) LIKE '%term%'. Prefer exact or prefix matches if user supplies SKU. Watch for size units embedded in name; consider normalization rules or tokenization for advanced NL2SQL. Return both product_name and sku for clarity.

product_type.description: High-level classification such as 'Vegetable' or 'Fruit'. nvarchar(28).
product_type.analysis_use: Group and filter products by type for assortment and category reporting, allocation of storage/marketing rules, and channel segmentation.
business_questions: How many products fall into each product_type?
Filter product lists to show only 'Vegetable' or 'Fruit' items.
product_type.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality field - use direct equality or IN (e.g., product_type = 'Vegetable'). Consider mapping synonyms (veg vs vegetable) in NL2SQL normalization. Indexing not critical but helpful for filtering.

category.analysis_use: High-level grouping for catalog overviews, permissioning, and reporting. Useful when joining to category-level marketing or assortment tables.
business_questions: Show all products in the 'Fresh Produce' category.
Count products per category for assortment planning.
category.nl2sql_hints: Often a low-cardinality filter. Expect exact match filters. Consider canonicalization (trim/case). If category is always 'Fresh Produce' in source, NL2SQL should handle that as a constant.

subcategory.analysis_use: Useful for grouping, drill-downs in reports, SKU selection and planning. Combine with product_type for more granular segmentation.
business_questions: List SKUs under the 'Brinjal' subcategory.
Which subcategory has the most SKUs?
subcategory.nl2sql_hints: Use equality or IN for filters. Provide suggestions/autocomplete from distinct values for NL2SQL disambiguation. Be aware that subcategory can include size descriptors in this dataset—normalize if needed.

brand.analysis_use: Group products by brand for supplier/brand performance, vendor reporting and promotion targeting.
business_questions: Which products belong to 'Daily Greens'?
How many SKUs does each brand have?
brand.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality here. Use direct equality filters. If schema has vendor tables, prefer joining on vendor/brand id (not present here) or use brand string for lookups.

uom.description: Unit of measure for the product (e.g., 'kg', '250g'). nvarchar(18).
uom.analysis_use: Normalize units for quantity calculations, convert weights for aggregation, and filter products by packaging size.
business_questions: Show all products sold by 'kg' vs fixed-pack sizes.
How many SKUs are packaged as 250g or 500g?
uom.nl2sql_hints: Small set of values—use equality/IN. When aggregating across UOMs, convert to a common base (requires casting and conversion rules). NL2SQL should be able to map user phrases like 'per kg' to uom = 'kg'.

gross_weight_kg.description: Gross weight of the packaged product in kilograms, stored as nvarchar (e.g., '1.0').
gross_weight_kg.analysis_use: Numeric operations: convert to numeric and use for weight-based aggregations, shipping cost estimates, and palletization calculations.
business_questions: List products with gross weight > 0.5 kg.
What is the average gross weight by subcategory?
gross_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal (e.g., TRY_CAST(gross_weight_kg AS DECIMAL)) before comparisons/aggregations. Handle nulls and malformed strings. Include unit normalization checks if uom differs.

net_weight_kg.description: Net (product-only) weight in kilograms, stored as nvarchar.
net_weight_kg.analysis_use: Used for pricing per kg calculations, yield/loss computations and per-weight promotions.
business_questions: Compute price per kg when joined with pricing table.
Which products have net weight < gross weight (packaging overhead)?
net_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric for math. When both net and gross are present, consider computing packaging_weight = gross - net with numeric cast and null handling. NL2SQL should warn users about string numeric types and apply TRY_CAST.

pack_size.description: Pack quantity indicator, stored as nvarchar (value '1' in sample).
pack_size.analysis_use: Used to compute total units per transaction or convert between unit/pack dimensions. Low variability in this dataset.
business_questions: Which SKUs have a pack_size greater than 1?
Aggregate units sold by pack_size when joined with sales.
pack_size.nl2sql_hints: Stored as string—cast to integer when filtering or aggregating. If constant, consider ignoring or normalizing out of product master.

hs_code.description: Harmonized System code for customs/regulatory classification. nvarchar(16). Empty in this snapshot (100% null).
hs_code.analysis_use: When populated, used for international trade reporting, duties calculation, and shipping compliance checks.
business_questions: Which SKUs are missing HS codes and need enrichment?
Group products by HS code for customs reporting.
hs_code.nl2sql_hints: Expect many nulls. Use IS NULL checks to find missing values. When present, HS codes may need normalization (leading zeros). Treat as text for joins to regulatory tables.

shelf_life_days.analysis_use: Critical for perishable inventory management, FIFO/HOT handling, spoilage projections and promotion scheduling.
business_questions: List products with shelf life <= 3 days (high perishability).
How many SKUs fall into shelf-life buckets (0-3, 4-7, 8+ days)?
shelf_life_days.nl2sql_hints: Cast to integer for comparisons and bucketization. Handle nulls and strings like 'N/A'. Use numeric ranges in NL2SQL and provide suggested bucket operations.

storage_temp_c.description: Recommended storage temperature in Celsius (nvarchar) e.g., '4', '10', '15'.
storage_temp_c.analysis_use: Used to determine cold-chain requirements, warehouse zone assignments, and shipping method selection.
business_questions: Which products require cold storage (e.g., temp <= 10°C)?
Group SKUs by storage_temp_c to plan pick/pack locations.
storage_temp_c.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric. Consider threshold-based filters (<= 4°C, <= 10°C). Use ranges in NL2SQL prompts and map verbal queries ('cold', 'ambient') to numeric conditions.

is_perishable_flag.description: Flag indicating perishability (stored as nvarchar '1' in sample).
is_perishable_flag.analysis_use: Binary filter to separate perishable vs non-perishable items for routing, promotions, and SLA logic.
business_questions: Show all perishable products that require expedited shipping.
Count perishable vs non-perishable SKUs in the catalog.
is_perishable_flag.nl2sql_hints: Normalize to boolean (e.g., is_perishable_flag IN ('1','true','yes') => TRUE). Use explicit casting or CASE expressions. Since sample is all '1', check for variability in production data.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the product record was created (stored as nvarchar, e.g., '2023-10-21 07:04:58').
created_at.analysis_use: Used for temporal analyses (new product cadence), auditing, and determining recency for enrichment or archival.
business_questions: List products created in the last 30 days.
How many products were added per month?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Parse to DATE/TIMESTAMP using TRY_CAST/CONVERT; handle inconsistent formats. Use date_range filters (>=, BETWEEN) after parsing. For NL2SQL, allow natural language time phrases (e.g., 'last month') and map to explicit date filters.

default_image_url.description: URL to default product image. nvarchar(16) but 100% null in this snapshot.
default_image_url.analysis_use: Used to check product content completeness and drive UI displays or enrichment workflows.
business_questions: Which SKUs are missing a default image and need an image assigned?
List products with available image URLs for catalog export.
default_image_url.nl2sql_hints: Expect many nulls. Use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL in filters. If populated, treat as text and validate URL patterns. Consider providing a fallback image in query outputs when null.

return_id.description: Unique identifier for the return request (string).
return_id.analysis_use: Primary row identifier for de-duplication, counting distinct returns, and linking to external return-specific systems. Use in filters or as grouping key when listing returns.
business_questions: How many unique returns exist in a given period?
Show full details for return RET10001.
return_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as a categorical/string key. No casting required. Use equality or IN filters (e.g., WHERE return_id = 'RET10001'). It is effectively unique in this dataset but not declared primary key—avoid relying on DB-level constraints.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the return.
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders tables to attribute returns to customers, order date, items and revenue. Useful for counting returns per order and computing per-order return rates.
business_questions: Which orders have more than one return?
List returns and corresponding customer/order data for order EORD2089.
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Cast/join as string to b2b_orders or ecom_orders on order_id. Ensure fully qualified table names when multiple orders tables exist (e.g., b2b_orders vs ecom_orders). When computing return rate, join with orders table and count distinct order_id.

request_ts.description: Timestamp when the return was requested (stored as nvarchar in ISO-like format).
request_ts.analysis_use: Primary temporal attribute for time-based analyses (daily/weekly/monthly trends), filtering by date range, and calculating latencies relative to other timestamps.
business_questions: How many returns were requested each day?
What is the trend of return requests over the last 30 days?
request_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to proper datetime (e.g., CAST(request_ts AS datetime) or TO_TIMESTAMP) before date arithmetic or grouping. Use explicit format parsing if DB requires it. Support WHERE request_ts BETWEEN '2025-11-01' AND '2025-11-30' only after casting or normalizing string format.

reason_code.description: Categorical reason for the return (e.g., QUALITY, WRONG_ITEM, NOT_AS_DESCRIBED, DAMAGED).
reason_code.analysis_use: Group and filter to understand return drivers, calculate reason-specific rates and refunds, and prioritize quality issues.
business_questions: What percentage of returns are due to QUALITY issues?
Which reason_code is associated with the highest average refund_amount?
reason_code.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string; ideal for GROUP BY. Be mindful of casing/whitespace—normalize (UPPER/TRIM) to avoid duplicates. When building NL2SQL, allow synonyms (e.g., quality vs QUALITY) and map user phrases to these codes.

requested_items_json.description: JSON field that should list items requested for return; in this sample it contains '[]' for all rows.
requested_items_json.analysis_use: When populated, parse to extract returned SKUs, quantities and unit-level reasons for item-level analysis. Currently offers no item-level detail because it's empty.
business_questions: Which SKUs are being returned most frequently (requires non-empty JSON)?
What is the breakdown of quantities returned per return?
requested_items_json.nl2sql_hints: Use JSON functions (JSON_VALUE, JSON_QUERY or equivalent) to extract item arrays. If empty array, queries should handle empty/NULL gracefully. NL2SQL should detect when user asks for SKU-level answers and warn/auto-join to a dedicated return_items table if this field is empty or insufficient.

status.description: Lifecycle status of the return (REQUESTED, APPROVED, REJECTED, COMPLETED).
status.analysis_use: Filter and aggregate to show open vs closed returns, compute approval and completion rates, and SLA monitoring.
business_questions: How many returns are currently REQUESTED vs COMPLETED?
What is the completion rate of approved returns?
status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical. Use exact string matches but also support synonyms (e.g., closed includes REJECTED and COMPLETED). When users request 'open returns', translate to status IN ('REQUESTED','APPROVED') depending on business rules.

approved_by.description: Identifier of the approver (ops_manager, quality_head) or NULL if not approved.
approved_by.analysis_use: Performance and workload analysis per approver, approval rate and average refund by approver. Filter to find returns pending approval (NULL).
business_questions: Which approver approved the highest number of returns?
What is the average refund_amount for returns approved by ops_manager?
approved_by.nl2sql_hints: Low-cardinality string with many NULLs. Normalize values. When grouping, treat NULL as 'UNAPPROVED' if business semantics require. Be explicit in NL2SQL when asking about approvers to include/exclude NULLs.

pickup_scheduled_ts.description: Timestamp when the pickup for the return was scheduled (string).
pickup_scheduled_ts.analysis_use: Calculate scheduling latency (time from request to scheduled pickup), measure SLA adherence, and correlate with completed returns.
business_questions: What is the average time from request to pickup scheduled?
How many pickups were scheduled on a particular day?
pickup_scheduled_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for arithmetic. Handle NULLs for returns without scheduled pickup. Use COALESCE or conditional logic when computing average latencies to avoid skew from NULLs.

received_at_centre_ts.description: Timestamp when returned items were received at the central processing centre (string).
received_at_centre_ts.analysis_use: Compute transit time from pickup to reception, end-to-end return cycle time, and identify delays in logistics.
business_questions: What is the average time from pickup_scheduled_ts to received_at_centre_ts?
List returns that took longer than X days to be received at centre.
received_at_centre_ts.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime and handle timezone or format differences. Use WHERE received_at_centre_ts IS NOT NULL for completed receipt-based metrics. Consider negative or inconsistent values—validate chronology (request_ts <= pickup_scheduled_ts <= received_at_centre_ts).

refund_amount.description: Monetary amount refunded for the return, stored as a string representing a decimal number.
refund_amount.analysis_use: Aggregate to compute total refunds, average refund per return, and refunds by reason or status. Reconcile with credit notes via credit_note_id.
business_questions: What is the total refund amount issued in November 2025?
Which reason_code results in the highest total refunds?
refund_amount.nl2sql_hints: Cast to numeric/decimal before SUM/AVG (e.g., CAST(refund_amount AS DECIMAL(18,2))). Handle malformed values and nulls. Apply proper rounding/precision in results. When generating NL2SQL, prefer numeric casting in SELECT and WHERE (e.g., WHERE CAST(refund_amount AS DECIMAL) > 100).

credit_note_id.description: Identifier of the credit note generated for the refund (string), often links to a credit note/invoice table.
credit_note_id.analysis_use: Join to credit notes table for reconciliation and tracing of refund documentation. Check for returns lacking credit notes and reconcile amounts.
business_questions: Which returns do not have an associated credit_note_id?
Match credit note CN1656 to its return and refund_amount for reconciliation.
credit_note_id.nl2sql_hints: Join key to b2b_credit_notes or analogous tables. Treat as string; watch for inconsistent prefixes. Nulls indicate missing credit note. Use LEFT JOIN when listing returns with or without credit notes.

created_at.description: Record creation timestamp in the raw table (string). May be close to request_ts for ingest.
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion-time analyses, identify late-arriving records, and compare created_at vs request_ts for data latency monitoring.
business_questions: How long after request_ts are returns recorded in the raw table on average?
Show returns created after a given ingestion date/time.
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to datetime for comparisons. Be careful: created_at may reflect ingestion time rather than business event time—prefer request_ts for business timelines. Use created_at to filter incremental ingestion windows in NL2SQL-generated ETL queries.

resolution_notes.description: Free-text resolution notes; in this dataset the column is fully NULL.
resolution_notes.analysis_use: When populated, supports qualitative analysis, issue categorization and escalation tracking. Could be used for keyword search (e.g., 'customer refused', 'missing accessory').
business_questions: List returns where resolution_notes mention 'replacement' or 'refund only'.
How many returns include manual notes about investigation outcomes?
resolution_notes.nl2sql_hints: Text column often null—handle NULLs in filters (e.g., WHERE resolution_notes IS NOT NULL). For keyword searches use LIKE or full-text search if available. NL2SQL should allow free-text queries mapping to LIKE '%keyword%'. If analytics need structured resolution reasons, recommend normalizing into a separate coded column.

shipment_id.description: Unique identifier for the shipment record (string).
shipment_id.analysis_use: Primary identifier for de-duplication, grouping, and linking shipment-level events. Useful for counts, dedup checks and joins to tracking/event tables.
business_questions: How many unique shipments were created in a date range?
Are there duplicate shipment_id values indicating data quality issues?
shipment_id.nl2sql_hints: Treat as unique key string; recommend creating an index/PK. Use direct equality filters (shipment_id = '...') and grouping. Watch for leading/trailing whitespace; apply TRIM if needed.

order_id.description: Identifier of the order associated with the shipment (string).
order_id.analysis_use: Join key to orders tables to bring in order totals, customer, location, and items. Useful for order-level aggregation of shipping metrics (cost per order, shipments per order).
business_questions: Which orders had multiple shipments?
What is the shipping_charge per order when joined with order totals?
order_id.nl2sql_hints: Use as join key to b2b_orders or ecom_orders. Cast safely as string; ensure joins handle one-to-many (one order -> many shipments). Trim and normalize case before joining if other tables have different formats.

carrier_id.description: Identifier/name of the shipping carrier (categorical string). In sample values: LocalCourier, ThirdPartyLogistics, InHouseFleet.
carrier_id.analysis_use: Group and filter for carrier performance metrics: average delivery time, cost, status distribution. Good for pivot/group-by due to low cardinality.
business_questions: Which carrier has the shortest average delivery time?
Which carrier accounts for the largest share of shipping_costs?
carrier_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality - good candidate for GROUP BY. Normalize values (trim/upper/lower) for reliable grouping. Prefer equality filters (carrier_id = 'LocalCourier'). If carrier metadata exists, join on carrier_id to vendor tables.

tracking_no.description: Carrier-provided tracking number for the shipment (string).
tracking_no.analysis_use: Linking to external tracking/event feeds and to b2b_shipment_tracking_events for detailed timeline. Useful for troubleshooting and audit queries.
business_questions: Show the full tracking event history for a given tracking_no.
Which tracking numbers have no events (potential lost shipments)?
tracking_no.nl2sql_hints: Often used for equality filters or LIKE patterns. Ensure exact-match comparisons; consider indexing if frequent lookups. Watch duplicates if carriers reuse numbers across periods.

shipment_date.description: Datetime when the shipment was dispatched/created (stored as nvarchar).
shipment_date.analysis_use: Primary temporal dimension for time-series analysis: shipments per day/hour, windowed KPIs. Basis for computing transit time against actual_delivery_date/expected_delivery_date.
business_questions: How many shipments were dispatched per day over the last month?
What is the hourly distribution of shipments for peak planning?
shipment_date.nl2sql_hints: Must be cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP (e.g., TRY_CAST(shipment_date AS datetime)). Handle inconsistent formats and invalid strings. Use indexed date columns (store as proper datetime in ETL) for efficient range queries.

expected_delivery_date.description: Carrier/estimated delivery timestamp for when the shipment is expected to arrive (stored as nvarchar).
expected_delivery_date.analysis_use: Used to compute SLA/on-time metrics and to flag late shipments by comparing to actual_delivery_date.
business_questions: What portion of shipments missed the expected_delivery_date?
Which routes/pickup centres have the highest rate of late deliveries relative to expected dates?
expected_delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP before comparisons. Consider time zone consistency. Null or malformed expected dates should be handled (coalesce or filter).

actual_delivery_date.description: Actual timestamp when the shipment was delivered (stored as nvarchar). Many rows are null (undelivered/pending).
actual_delivery_date.analysis_use: Crucial for computing actual transit times, on-time/late classification, and delivery SLA metrics. Also used to identify undelivered shipments (NULL).
business_questions: Which shipments are still outstanding (actual_delivery_date IS NULL) and how long have they been pending?
What is the median actual delivery time vs expected delivery time?
actual_delivery_date.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP for calculations. Handle NULLs explicitly in NL2SQL (IS NULL checks). When computing transit time, use functions that handle NULLs (e.g., DATEDIFF with null-safe logic).

shipment_status.description: Categorical status of the shipment (PICKED, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED, etc.).
shipment_status.analysis_use: Filter and group for operational dashboards, real-time status counts, and funnels (picked -> in_transit -> out_for_delivery -> delivered). Useful for SLA breach alerts and in-transit monitoring.
business_questions: How many shipments are currently OUT_FOR_DELIVERY vs IN_TRANSIT?
What is the conversion rate from PICKED to DELIVERED within X hours?
shipment_status.nl2sql_hints: Treat as categorical string; normalize cases. Use equality and IN-list filters for dashboards. Beware of status synonyms; map to canonical values in NL2SQL preprocessing or use CASE normalization.

pickup_centre_id.description: Name/identifier of the pickup or distribution centre that originated the shipment (string).
pickup_centre_id.analysis_use: Group by pickup centre for workload, performance and cost analysis. Useful to identify capacity/throughput and regional performance differences.
business_questions: Which pickup centre ships the most orders per day?
Which pickup centre has the highest average shipping_charge or longest transit times?
pickup_centre_id.nl2sql_hints: Low cardinality - good GROUP BY candidate. Normalize naming (trim/case) to avoid false splits. Consider joining to a centres lookup table if available to enrich with region info.

package_weight_kg.description: Weight of the package in kilograms, stored as text but numeric in meaning.
package_weight_kg.analysis_use: Used for weight-based cost analysis, binning (light/medium/heavy), and correlation with shipping_charge. Useful for pricing models and carrier cost comparisons.
business_questions: What is the average shipping_charge per kilogram?
How does shipping_charge vary across weight brackets (0-1kg, 1-5kg, >5kg)?
package_weight_kg.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to numeric (DECIMAL/FLOAT) before aggregations. Handle malformed values and localized decimal separators. Consider creating a numeric persisted column in ETL for fast queries.

package_dimensions_json.description: JSON dimensions for the package (presently '{}' for all rows in sample).
package_dimensions_json.analysis_use: Potentially holds length/width/height for volumetric pricing; currently unused because sample contains empty JSON. Future ETL could extract volume for volumetric weight calculations.
business_questions: What is the volumetric weight distribution of shipments (when dimensions present)?
How many shipments lack dimensional data and need enrichment?
package_dimensions_json.nl2sql_hints: If storing JSON, use JSON functions (JSON_VALUE/JSON_EXTRACT) to extract fields; handle empty JSON as NULL. If always '{}', treat as missing and deprioritize in NL2SQL unless ETL populates it.

shipping_charge.description: Monetary charge billed for the shipment (stored as text).
shipping_charge.analysis_use: Core for cost and revenue analysis: average/median shipping cost, cost-per-order, carrier cost comparison and margin calculations when joined to order revenue data.
business_questions: What is the total shipping_charge by carrier and by pickup_centre?
What is the distribution of shipping_charge for same weight buckets?
shipping_charge.nl2sql_hints: CAST/TRY_CAST to DECIMAL before SUM/AVG. Be mindful of currency units and rounding. Filter out or coalesce malformed/null charges. Consider indexing if used frequently in ORDER BY aggregates.

created_at.description: Timestamp when the shipment record was created in the system (stored as nvarchar).
created_at.analysis_use: Audit and ingestion timeline; used to measure processing delays between record creation and shipment_date or last_update. Useful for ETL incremental loads.
business_questions: How long after order placement are shipment records created?
How many shipments were created by hour/day (system activity)?
created_at.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP for time-series queries. Use for incremental extraction (WHERE created_at > last_run). Ensure consistent time zone and format handling.

last_update.description: Timestamp of the last modification to the shipment record (stored as nvarchar).
last_update.analysis_use: Change tracking for status updates, debugging updates delays, and for incremental syncs. Can identify shipments with frequent updates or long stale periods.
business_questions: Which shipments had status updates in the last 24 hours?
What is the average time between created_at and last_update?
last_update.nl2sql_hints: Cast to DATE/TIMESTAMP for computations. Use for WHERE clauses to find recently changed records. Combine with shipment_status to find recently transitioned shipments.
